That's why the allowance for doubtful accounts is SO important. Think of it like this: you're throwing a party, but you know some people always bail. The allowance is your 'no-show' buffer. Nail this part, and you'll avoid some serious headaches later on. Trust me, dealing with uncollectible accounts sucks way less when you've planned for it.

sections.title: Gross vs. Net: Discount Confusion!
sections.content: A common mistake is immediately applying the cash discount to the initial accounts receivable balance when using the gross method. Students often assume that because the customer *might* take the discount, it should be deducted upfront. However, the gross method specifically records the receivable at the full invoice amount *initially*. Only when the customer actually takes the discount within the discount period is an adjustment made to reduce the receivable and recognize sales discount. Remember, gross means record it all, then adjust if needed!

sections.title: Sarah's Sales Snafu: Discount Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a junior accountant, recorded a $10,000 sale with terms 2/10, n/30 using the gross method. Her boss noticed that after 15 days, no payment was received, and Sarah hadn't adjusted the receivable. This highlights the importance of regularly monitoring accounts receivable and adjusting for discounts *not* taken. Sarah learned to track payment dates closely, ensuring accurate financial reporting by the due date.

sections.title: AR in the Real World: Audit Trails and Aging
sections.content: In practice, you'll be constantly analyzing accounts receivable aging reports. These reports categorize receivables by how long they've been outstanding, which helps you estimate the allowance for doubtful accounts. Auditors will scrutinize these reports and your methodology. You'll also trace individual invoices to supporting documentation to verify existence and accuracy. Mastering the allowance method is essential for accurately reflecting a company's financial health and passing those audits!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: GAAP requires the allowance method—direct write-off is NOT GAAP
Write-offs reduce BOTH A/R and Allowance—no P&L impact
Recoveries: First reinstate receivable, then record cash collection
% of Sales focuses on EXPENSE; % of Receivables focuses on ALLOWANCE BALANCE
Aging method applies different rates to different age buckets—most accurate

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A company sells goods to a customer on credit for $10,000 with terms 2/10, n/30. Using the gross method, what amount should the company debit to Accounts Receivable at the time of the sale?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under the gross method, the accounts receivable is recorded at the full invoice amount. The discount is only considered when payment is received within the discount period.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the discount is applied at the time of initial recognition under the gross method.

reveal.question: Why is the allowance method required by GAAP for bad debts?
reveal.answer: The allowance method is required by GAAP to adhere to the matching principle, ensuring that bad debt expense is recognized in the same period as the related revenue.

rows.label: Discount Recognition
values: Initially Recorded; Adjustment for Lost Discount

knowledgeCheck.question: An increase in the Allowance for Doubtful Accounts will have what effect on the financial statements?
options: Increase in total assets
Decrease in total assets
Increase in net income
Decrease in liabilities
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Allowance for Doubtful Accounts is a contra-asset account. An increase in this account reduces the net realizable value of accounts receivable, thereby decreasing total assets.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the nature of the Allowance for Doubtful Accounts and its relationship to Accounts Receivable.

title: CECL: Current Expected Credit Losses
description: Understand the forward-looking credit loss model under ASC 326

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: CECL (ASC 326) fundamentally changed how companies estimate credit losses. Instead of waiting for losses to be 'probable,' companies now estimate LIFETIME expected losses from Day 1. This is a major exam topic—understand the philosophy and mechanics.

sections.title: CECL Overview
sections.content:
**CECL = Current Expected Credit Losses**

Effective for:
• SEC filers: 2020
• Smaller reporting companies & private: 2023

**Key Change:**
Old model: Recognize losses when "probable" (incurred loss model)
CECL: Recognize **lifetime expected losses** at inception (expected loss model)

**Result:** Earlier recognition of credit losses; larger Day 1 allowances

sections.title: Scope of CECL
sections.content:
CECL applies to financial assets measured at **amortized cost**:

• **Trade receivables**
• **Loans and notes receivable**
• **Held-to-maturity debt securities**
• **Net investment in leases** (lessor)
• **Loan commitments and financial guarantees**

**NOT in scope:**
• Trading securities (fair value through P&L)
• AFS debt securities (separate impairment model)
• Equity securities

sections.title: The CECL Approach
sections.content:
**Estimate losses over the FULL contractual life:**

1. Pool assets with similar risk characteristics
2. Consider:
   • Historical loss experience
   • Current conditions
   • **Reasonable and supportable forecasts**
3. Recognize lifetime expected losses as an allowance
4. Update each reporting period

**Key Innovation:** Forward-looking forecasts are REQUIRED, not optional.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CECL Philosophy
sections.content:
Think of CECL as **"pessimistic from Day 1"**:

• When you originate a loan, some borrowers WILL default
• Don't wait until default is "probable"—estimate it NOW
• Consider the FUTURE, not just the past

**Analogy:** It's like buying insurance on Day 1 for the expected accidents over the loan's life.

content.definition: Apply historical loss rate to amortized cost balance. Most common for trade receivables. Adjust for current conditions and forecasts.
Track losses by origination year. Useful when risk varies by when asset was originated.

content.term: Probability of Default × Loss Given Default
content.definition: PD × LGD × Exposure. Sophisticated approach used by banks and financial institutions.
Compare contractual cash flows to expected cash flows. Used for individually evaluated assets.

sections.title: Trade Receivables: Simplified Approach
sections.content:
For trade receivables without significant financing component:

• Can use **simplified approach**
• Measure at lifetime expected losses (no staging)
• Practical expedient: **Aging schedule** with loss rates

**Example:**
```
Age Bucket          Balance    Loss Rate    Allowance
0-30 days          $100,000      1%         $1,000
31-60 days          $50,000      3%         $1,500
61-90 days          $20,000     10%         $2,000
>90 days            $10,000     30%         $3,000
                                           -------
Total Allowance                             $7,500
```

sections.title: Forecasts in CECL
sections.content:
**Reasonable and Supportable Forecasts:**

• Use available information without undue cost/effort
• Consider macroeconomic factors (GDP, unemployment, housing)
• Forecast period varies—use what's supportable
• Beyond forecast period: Revert to historical losses

**Reversion Methods:**
• Immediate reversion to historical
• Straight-line reversion over time
• Other systematic approaches

sections.title: Journal Entry: Initial Recognition
sections.content:
**Scenario:** Company originates $1,000,000 in receivables. CECL analysis estimates lifetime losses of $25,000.

**Entry at origination:**
Dr Bad Debt Expense (or Provision)    $25,000
    Cr Allowance for Credit Losses          $25,000

**Balance Sheet Presentation:**
Accounts Receivable                 $1,000,000
Less: Allowance for Credit Losses      (25,000)
Net Receivables                       $975,000

**Note:** Full allowance recorded on Day 1!

sections.title: Updates Each Period
sections.content:
**Each reporting period:**

1. Re-estimate expected credit losses
2. Adjust allowance to match new estimate
3. Difference flows through P&L

**If conditions improve:**
• Reduce allowance
• Credit to P&L (recovery of provision)

**If conditions worsen:**
• Increase allowance
• Debit to P&L (additional provision)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: AFS Securities Are Different
sections.content:
CECL does NOT apply to available-for-sale debt securities!

AFS securities have their own impairment model:
• Recognize allowance only if fair value < amortized cost
• Credit loss = amortized cost − present value of expected cash flows
• Non-credit loss = remaining decline (recorded in OCI)

Don't confuse the two models on the exam!

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
sections.content:
CECL requires extensive disclosures:

• **Credit quality** indicators and aging
• **Allowance rollforward** (beginning, provisions, write-offs, recoveries, ending)
• **Methods and assumptions** used
• **Vintage disclosures** (for certain entities)
• **Purchased credit-deteriorated** asset information
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: CECL: Kiss 'Probable' Goodbye!
sections.content: Okay, future CPAs, let's be real. The old 'probable' standard was about as clear as mud. Remember trying to figure out if a loss was *really* probable? A headache, right? CECL's like, 'Nah, we're not doing that anymore.' Now, you gotta look into your crystal ball and estimate lifetime losses *upfront*. It's more work, sure, but at least it's (supposedly) more accurate. Think of it as trading a small headache now for a potentially HUGE one later. Embrace the future, folks!

sections.title: Confusing CECL's Scope: It's Not Everything!
sections.content: A frequent error is applying CECL to *all* assets. Remember, CECL applies to financial assets measured at amortized cost. That means things like held-to-maturity securities, trade receivables, and loans. Students often incorrectly include assets measured at fair value, like trading securities or available-for-sale securities. Those are handled differently! Don't get tripped up by this on the exam. Focus on assets carried at amortized cost and you'll be golden!

sections.title: Sarah's Loan Loss Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a credit analyst at First National Bank, is reviewing a $500,000 commercial loan to a local bakery. Under the old incurred loss model, she only considered the bakery's recent late payment. Now, with CECL, Sarah must also consider the bakery's long-term business plan, the overall economic outlook, and industry trends to estimate potential losses over the *entire life* of the loan. That's a big difference in scope and responsibility!

sections.title: CECL: Day-to-Day Reality
sections.content: In your audit career, you'll encounter CECL when reviewing a client's allowance for credit losses. For example, when auditing accounts receivable, you'll scrutinize the client's methodology for estimating lifetime expected losses. You'll assess whether they've considered factors like historical write-off rates, current economic conditions, and forward-looking forecasts. A poorly documented or unreasonable CECL estimate is a huge red flag and a potential material misstatement. So get comfy with CECL, it's here to stay!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CECL requires recognition of LIFETIME expected losses from Day 1
Forward-looking forecasts (economic conditions) are REQUIRED
Applies to: trade receivables, loans, HTM securities, lease receivables
Does NOT apply to: trading securities, AFS securities, equity securities
Trade receivables can use simplified aging approach with loss rates
Allowance is updated each period with changes flowing through P&L

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following financial instruments is NOT subject to the CECL standard?
options: A) Trade receivables
B) Held-to-maturity debt securities
C) Trading securities
D) Loans and notes receivable
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Trading securities are measured at fair value, with changes in fair value recognized in profit or loss. CECL applies to financial assets measured at amortized cost.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how the financial asset is measured on the balance sheet.

reveal.question: Under the CECL model, when are lifetime expected credit losses recognized?
reveal.answer: Lifetime expected credit losses are recognized at the inception of the financial instrument.

sections.title: Incurred Loss vs. Expected Loss Model

rows.label: Loss Recognition
values: Recognize lifetime expected losses at inception

rows.label: Time Horizon
values: Focuses on past events and current conditions
Considers historical experience, current conditions, and reasonable and supportable forecasts

rows.label: Scope
values: Narrower scope - losses must be probable
Broader scope - includes all expected losses

title: Notes Receivable & Impairment
description: Account for long-term receivables, present value calculations, and impairment

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Notes receivable questions test your understanding of present value concepts and interest recognition. TBS simulations may require you to calculate the present value of a note, determine interest income, and assess impairment. Master the time value of money!

sections.title: Notes Receivable vs. Accounts Receivable
sections.content:
**Notes Receivable:**
• Formal written promise to pay
• Typically longer-term (> 1 year)
• Usually bear interest explicitly
• More enforceable than A/R

**When to Record at Present Value:**
• Notes with **no stated interest** or **below-market interest**
• Notes arising from non-cash transactions
• Notes with terms > 1 year

**Exception:** Trade receivables due within 1 year—record at face value.

sections.title: Present Value Concepts
sections.content:
**Time Value of Money:**
A dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future.

**Present Value Formula:**
PV = FV ÷ (1 + r)^n

Or using tables:
PV = FV × PV factor

**For annuities (equal payments):**
PV = Payment × PV of Ordinary Annuity factor

**Key Insight:** Notes receivable are recorded at the **present value of future cash flows**, not face value (unless interest rate is market rate).

sections.title: Zero-Interest-Bearing Note
sections.content:
**Scenario:** Company sells equipment with FMV of $80,000 and receives a $100,000 note due in 2 years with no stated interest.

**What's happening?**
• The $20,000 difference IS interest, just not stated
• Record the note at present value (FMV of equipment)

**Journal Entry at Sale:**
Dr Notes Receivable (face)            $100,000
    Cr Discount on Notes Receivable          $20,000
    Cr Sales Revenue                         $80,000

**Balance Sheet Presentation:**
Notes Receivable                    $100,000
Less: Discount                       (20,000)
Net Notes Receivable                 $80,000

sections.title: Amortizing the Discount
sections.content:
**Effective Interest Method (Required by GAAP):**

Interest Income = Carrying Value × Effective Interest Rate

**Each period:**
1. Calculate interest income using effective rate
2. Credit Interest Income
3. Reduce the Discount (increase carrying value)

**The discount is amortized to interest income over the note's life.**

sections.title: Discount Amortization Example
sections.content:
**Using previous example:**
Face Value: $100,000
Present Value (carrying value): $80,000
Discount: $20,000
Term: 2 years
Effective Rate: 11.8% (calculated)

**Year 1:**
Interest Income = $80,000 × 11.8% = $9,440
Dr Discount on Notes Receivable    $9,440
    Cr Interest Income                    $9,440

New carrying value: $80,000 + $9,440 = $89,440

**Year 2:**
Interest Income = $89,440 × 11.8% = $10,560 (rounded)
Dr Discount on Notes Receivable    $10,560
    Cr Interest Income                    $10,560

Final carrying value: $89,440 + $10,560 = $100,000 ✓

sections.title: Notes with Below-Market Interest
sections.content:
**Scenario:** Note has stated rate of 2% but market rate is 8%.

**Solution:**
1. Calculate PV of cash flows using MARKET rate (8%)
2. Record note at this present value
3. Difference between face and PV is discount
4. Amortize discount using effective interest method

**The stated interest is cash received; the effective interest (higher) is income recognized.**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Interest Calculations
sections.content:
**Two types of interest to track:**

1. **Cash Interest** = Face Value × Stated Rate
   (This is what you actually receive)

2. **Interest Income** = Carrying Value × Effective Rate
   (This is what you record as income)

**Difference = Discount Amortization**

Interest Income > Cash Interest when note is at a discount

sections.title: Impairment of Notes Receivable
sections.content:
**Under CECL (ASC 326):**
• Recognize expected credit losses at inception
• Update estimate each period
• No separate "impairment" analysis needed—built into allowance

**For individually significant notes:**
• May need specific analysis
• Compare carrying value to PV of expected cash flows
• Shortfall = additional allowance needed

**If borrower restructures (troubled debt):**
• See TDR guidance (ASC 310-40)

sections.title: Impairment Calculation
sections.content:
**Scenario:**
Note receivable carrying value: $100,000
Expected cash flows: $80,000 in 2 years
Original effective rate: 6%

**Calculate impairment:**
PV of expected cash flows = $80,000 ÷ (1.06)² = $71,200

Impairment loss = $100,000 − $71,200 = $28,800

**Entry:**
Dr Credit Loss Expense (or Provision)    $28,800
    Cr Allowance for Credit Losses              $28,800

**Note:** Use ORIGINAL effective rate for discounting!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Carrying Value Changes
sections.content:
When calculating interest income on subsequent periods:

**Use the CURRENT carrying value**, not the original!

Carrying value changes each period as:
• Discount is amortized (increases CV)
• Premium is amortized (decreases CV)
• Impairment is recognized (decreases CV through allowance)

Always ask: What's the carrying value AT THE START of this period?
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Notes Receivable: Not Just Fancy A/R
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, notes receivable can seem like a pain. You're probably thinking, 'Isn't this just accounts receivable with extra steps?' Well, yes and no. Think of it this way: A/R is a handshake, a note receivable is a signed contract. We're talking about larger amounts, longer terms, and legally enforceable interest. If a client offers you a note receivable, especially for a non-cash transaction, get ready to dust off those present value tables. It’s more than just a formality, it's about accurately reflecting the true value of the asset on the balance sheet.

sections.title: Stated vs. Market Interest: The PV Trap
sections.content: A common mistake is ignoring the present value calculation when a note's stated interest rate is *below* the market rate. Students often assume that if there's *any* stated interest, they can skip the PV calculation. Wrong! The market rate reflects the true risk and opportunity cost of lending. If the stated rate is significantly lower, the note is effectively being issued at a discount. Failing to account for this understates the initial value of the note and distorts subsequent interest income recognition. Always compare the stated rate to the prevailing market rate for similar instruments.

sections.title: Sarah's Soggy Software Sale
sections.content: Sarah's software company sold a license for $500,000, receiving a note receivable with no stated interest, due in three years. The market rate for similar loans is 8%. Instead of recording the full $500,000 as revenue immediately, Sarah needs to calculate the present value of that future payment using the 8% discount rate. By discounting the future cash flow, Sarah accurately reflects the economic substance of the transaction and avoids overstating initial revenue and assets. This ensures the financial statements are a true and fair representation.

sections.title: Valuing a Business Acquisition: Notes in the Mix
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter notes receivable frequently during business acquisitions. When performing due diligence, you'll need to analyze any notes receivable held by the target company. If those notes have below-market interest rates or were issued in non-cash transactions, you'll have to adjust their carrying value to reflect their fair value using present value techniques. This adjustment directly impacts the purchase price allocation and the goodwill calculation, making it a critical step in the acquisition process. Ignoring this can lead to a misvaluation of the acquired assets and future financial reporting errors.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Notes receivable recorded at present value if no/below-market interest rate
Discount = Face Value − Present Value; amortized using effective interest method
Interest Income = Carrying Value × Effective Rate (changes each period)
Cash Interest = Face Value × Stated Rate (constant)
Impairment under CECL: estimate lifetime expected losses using original effective rate
Always use current carrying value for interest calculations, not original

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: On January 1, Year 1, ABC Company sold land with a fair value of $500,000 in exchange for a three-year, non-interest-bearing note receivable. The prevailing market rate of interest for notes of this type is 8%. What amount of sales revenue should ABC Company recognize on January 1, Year 1?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The sales revenue recognized should be the present value of the note receivable. PV = FV / (1+r)^n = $500,000 / (1+0.08)^3 = $500,000 / 1.259712 = $396,915. Note that because this note is non-interest bearing, we must use the present value.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to use the present value formula since the note is non-interest bearing.

reveal.question: Under the effective interest method, how is interest income calculated each period?
reveal.answer: Interest Income = Carrying Value of the Note × Effective Interest Rate. This amount is then credited to Interest Income.

rows.label: Interest
values: May or may not bear interest

title: Factoring, Pledging & Transfers of Receivables
description: Understand when transfers qualify as sales vs. secured borrowings

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Companies often monetize receivables before collection. The accounting depends on whether the transfer is a TRUE SALE (receivables off balance sheet) or a SECURED BORROWING (receivables stay, liability recorded). The exam tests your ability to analyze transfer terms and determine the correct treatment.

content.definition: Using receivables as collateral for a loan. Receivables stay on books; loan recorded as liability. No derecognition.
Specific receivables identified as collateral. Similar to pledging but with specific identification. Loan recorded; receivables remain.
Selling receivables to a third party (factor). May qualify as sale or secured borrowing depending on terms.

sections.title: Sale vs. Secured Borrowing
sections.content:
**The Key Question:** Has the transferor surrendered control over the receivables?

**Three conditions for SALE treatment (ASC 860):**

1. **Isolation:** Assets are beyond reach of transferor and its creditors (even in bankruptcy)

2. **Transferee has rights:** Transferee can pledge or exchange the assets without constraint

3. **No effective control retained:** Transferor doesn't maintain control through:
   - Agreement to repurchase
   - Ability to unilaterally cause return

**If ANY condition fails → Secured Borrowing**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Recourse Doesn't Automatically Mean Secured Borrowing
sections.content:
Having recourse doesn't AUTOMATICALLY make it a secured borrowing!

If the three sale conditions are met, it's still a sale—but the transferor records a **recourse liability** for expected payments under the recourse provision.

Analyze the three conditions first, THEN consider recourse.

sections.title: Sale Treatment Accounting
sections.content:
**When transfer qualifies as SALE:**

1. Derecognize receivables transferred
2. Recognize assets received (cash, servicing asset)
3. Recognize liabilities assumed (recourse liability, servicing liability)
4. Record gain or loss on sale

**Gain/Loss Calculation:**
Proceeds received
+ Fair value of servicing asset (if retained)
− Carrying value of receivables
− Fair value of recourse liability
− Fair value of servicing liability (if any)
= Gain or Loss

sections.title: Sale Example: Without Recourse
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Receivables: $100,000 (carrying value)
• Factor pays: $92,000 cash
• Factor charges 3% fee ($3,000)
• Factor holds back 5% ($5,000) until collection
• No recourse provision

**Journal Entry:**
Dr Cash                              $92,000
Dr Due from Factor (holdback)         $5,000
Dr Loss on Sale of Receivables        $3,000
    Cr Accounts Receivable                 $100,000

**The $5,000 holdback is returned after collection.**

sections.title: Sale Example: With Recourse
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Receivables: $100,000 (carrying value)
• Factor pays: $95,000 cash
• Recourse liability estimated: $2,000 (expected losses)
• Qualifies as sale (three conditions met)

**Journal Entry:**
Dr Cash                              $95,000
Dr Loss on Sale of Receivables        $7,000
    Cr Accounts Receivable                 $100,000
    Cr Recourse Liability                    $2,000

**Later, if factor seeks reimbursement:**
Dr Recourse Liability                 $X,XXX
    Cr Cash                                  $X,XXX

sections.title: Secured Borrowing Accounting
sections.content:
**When transfer is SECURED BORROWING:**

• Receivables STAY on transferor's books
• Record LIABILITY for cash received
• Continue to record bad debt expense
• Factor's fee may be treated as financing cost

**Journal Entry:**
Dr Cash                              $XX,XXX
    Cr Liability (Borrowing)               $XX,XXX

**Receivables remain; collections reduce the liability.**

sections.title: Secured Borrowing Example
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Receivables pledged: $100,000
• Cash received: $80,000
• Interest rate: 8%
• Company maintains effective control (fails sale criteria)

**Initial Entry:**
Dr Cash                              $80,000
    Cr Notes Payable                        $80,000

**Receivables stay on books—disclosed as pledged.**

**As collections occur:**
Dr Cash (from customers)             $XX,XXX
    Cr Accounts Receivable                  $XX,XXX

Dr Notes Payable                     $XX,XXX
Dr Interest Expense                   $X,XXX
    Cr Cash (remit to lender)               $XX,XXX

sections.title: Servicing Rights
sections.content:
**Who collects the receivables?**

Often the transferor continues to **service** (collect) the receivables even after sale.

**Servicing Asset:** Fair value of expected servicing fees > adequate compensation

**Servicing Liability:** Expected servicing fees < adequate compensation (below market)

**Adequate Compensation:** Market rate for servicing

If servicing is at market rate → No asset or liability

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
sections.content:
**For transferred receivables:**

• Nature of transfer arrangements
• Continuing involvement (servicing, recourse)
• Key assumptions used in measuring retained interests
• Sensitivity analysis for key assumptions
• Cash flows with transferees

**For pledged receivables:**
• Carrying amount pledged as collateral
• Terms and conditions of pledge

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sale Criteria
sections.content:
**"ITC" for Sale Treatment:**

**I**solation — Assets isolated from transferor
**T**ransferee rights — Can pledge/exchange freely
**C**ontrol surrendered — No repurchase agreements

All three must be met for sale treatment!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Factoring: It's All About Control, Baby!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Factoring receivables can feel like you're juggling chainsaws while riding a unicycle. The key question, 'Has the transferor surrendered control?' is EVERYTHING. Think of it like this: if you can still boss those receivables around after the transfer, it's probably NOT a sale. ASC 860 wants to know who's REALLY calling the shots. Mess this up, and your financial statements are going to look like a toddler finger-painted them. So, focus on those three conditions for sale treatment. Seriously.

sections.title: Confusing Isolation with Collateral
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking that simply securing a borrowing with receivables means the receivables are 'isolated.' Isolation, as defined by ASC 860, means the assets are truly beyond the reach of the transferor and its creditors, even in bankruptcy. Pledging receivables as collateral for a loan doesn't achieve that. The transferor still owns the receivables, and they are subject to the transferor's creditors. If a company defaults on the loan, the lender can seize the receivables, but the company's creditors can also try to get to them, meaning they weren't truly isolated.

sections.title: Sarah's Factoring Fiasco
sections.content: Sarah, a controller at 'Widgets, Inc.', factored $500,000 of receivables to 'First Factor Corp' with recourse. Widgets, Inc. remains responsible for collecting the receivables and bears the credit risk. Even though cash changed hands, this is likely a secured borrowing, not a sale, because Widgets, Inc. hasn't truly surrendered control. The recourse provision essentially means Widgets, Inc. is still on the hook if the customers don't pay. This impacts how Sarah needs to classify the transaction on the balance sheet.

sections.title: Due Diligence: Beyond the Numbers
sections.content: In practice, when auditing a company that factors receivables, it's crucial to go beyond the numbers. You need to carefully examine the factoring agreement. Look for clauses related to recourse, repurchase agreements, and the transferor's continuing involvement. These details will reveal whether the transaction truly qualifies as a sale or is, in substance, a secured borrowing. Don't just rely on management's assertions; perform thorough due diligence to ensure proper accounting treatment. Misclassification can have a material impact on a company's financial position.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Pledging/Assigning: Receivables stay on books; record liability for loan
Factoring: May be sale or secured borrowing—analyze three conditions
Sale requires: Isolation, transferee rights, control surrendered
With recourse can still be a sale—record recourse liability
Without recourse usually qualifies as sale (factor assumes credit risk)
Secured borrowing: Receivables remain; cash is a liability

title: Inventory Cost Flow: FIFO, LIFO, Weighted Average
description: Master the major inventory costing methods and their financial statement impacts

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Inventory costing is fundamental to FAR. Different methods produce different COGS, gross profit, and ending inventory—and the exam loves comparing these effects. Know the mechanics AND the conceptual implications. Also remember: LIFO is prohibited under IFRS!

sections.title: Inventory Basics
sections.content:
**What is Inventory?**
Assets held for sale in ordinary course of business, in production, or consumed in production.

**The Cost Flow Problem:**
When identical items are purchased at different prices, which cost goes to COGS vs. ending inventory?

**The Inventory Equation:**
Beginning Inventory + Purchases = Cost of Goods Available for Sale
Cost of Goods Available − Ending Inventory = Cost of Goods Sold

**Or:** Beginning Inventory + Purchases − COGS = Ending Inventory

sections.title: Inventory Systems
rows: Physical count required to determine ending inventory

sections.title: FIFO: First-In, First-Out
sections.content:
**Concept:** Oldest costs go to COGS; newest costs remain in ending inventory.

**Think:** First items purchased are first items sold.

**Effects in Rising Prices:**
• **Lower COGS** (old, cheaper costs)
• **Higher gross profit**
• **Higher net income**
• **Higher ending inventory** (newer, higher costs)
• **Higher taxes**

**Advantage:** Ending inventory approximates current replacement cost.

**Note:** FIFO produces SAME results under perpetual and periodic systems.

sections.title: LIFO: Last-In, First-Out
sections.content:
**Concept:** Newest costs go to COGS; oldest costs remain in ending inventory.

**Think:** Last items purchased are first items sold.

**Effects in Rising Prices:**
• **Higher COGS** (new, expensive costs)
• **Lower gross profit**
• **Lower net income**
• **Lower ending inventory** (old, lower costs)
• **Lower taxes** (main benefit!)

**Disadvantages:**
• Ending inventory is outdated (old costs)
• LIFO liquidation can distort income
• **PROHIBITED under IFRS!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: FIFO vs. LIFO in Inflation
sections.content:
**"FIFO = FAT"** (in rising prices)
• **F**IFO produces
• **A**mplified (higher)
• **T**axable income

**"LIFO = LEAN"** (in rising prices)
• **L**IFO produces
• **E**conomic
• **A**dvantage via
• **N**et tax savings

In inflation, LIFO reduces taxes but also reduces reported income!

sections.title: Weighted Average Cost
sections.content:
**Concept:** Average all costs together; use average for both COGS and ending inventory.

**Periodic System (Weighted Average):**
Calculate average at END of period
Avg Cost = Total Cost of Goods Available ÷ Total Units Available

**Perpetual System (Moving Average):**
Recalculate average after EACH purchase
New Avg = (Old Inventory Cost + New Purchase Cost) ÷ Total Units

**Effects:**
• Results fall BETWEEN FIFO and LIFO
• Smooths out price fluctuations
• Allowed under both GAAP and IFRS

sections.title: Comparative Example
sections.content:
**Data:**
Beginning inventory: 100 units @ $10 = $1,000
Purchase 1: 150 units @ $12 = $1,800
Purchase 2: 200 units @ $14 = $2,800
Goods available: 450 units, $5,600
Units sold: 300 units
Ending inventory: 150 units

**FIFO:**
COGS: 100 @ $10 + 150 @ $12 + 50 @ $14 = $3,500
Ending Inv: 150 @ $14 = $2,100

**LIFO:**
COGS: 200 @ $14 + 100 @ $12 = $4,000
Ending Inv: 100 @ $10 + 50 @ $12 = $1,600

**Weighted Average:**
Avg cost: $5,600 ÷ 450 = $12.44
COGS: 300 × $12.44 = $3,733
Ending Inv: 150 × $12.44 = $1,867

sections.title: LIFO Reserve
sections.content:
**LIFO Reserve** = Inventory at FIFO − Inventory at LIFO

Companies using LIFO must disclose what inventory would be under FIFO.

**Why it matters:**
• Allows comparison with FIFO companies
• Shows cumulative tax benefit from LIFO
• Required disclosure in notes

**Converting LIFO to FIFO:**
FIFO Inventory = LIFO Inventory + LIFO Reserve

**LIFO Reserve builds up over time in inflationary periods.**

sections.title: LIFO Liquidation
sections.content:
**What is it?**
When a LIFO company sells more units than it purchases, dipping into old LIFO layers.

**Problem:**
• Old, low-cost layers flow to COGS
• Artificially HIGH gross profit
• Higher taxes (defeats LIFO's purpose)
• One-time distortion to income

**Required Disclosure:**
Amount of LIFO liquidation and its effect on income.

**Exam Tip:** Watch for questions about unusual gross profit increases due to LIFO liquidation.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: LIFO Conformity Rule
sections.content:
**IRS LIFO Conformity Rule:**

If a company uses LIFO for TAX purposes, it MUST use LIFO for FINANCIAL REPORTING (book) purposes.

This is unique—normally book and tax can differ!

**Exception:** LIFO Reserve disclosure doesn't violate conformity rule.

sections.title: Specific Identification
sections.content:
**Concept:** Track actual cost of each specific item sold.

**When Used:**
• Unique, high-value items (jewelry, cars, art)
• Items with serial numbers or specific identification

**Advantages:**
• Matches actual cost flow
• Most accurate for unique items

**Disadvantages:**
• Allows income manipulation (cherry-picking)
• Impractical for homogeneous, high-volume items
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: LIFO/FIFO: My First Inventory Nightmare!
sections.content: Okay, so picture this: my first busy season, fresh out of college, and I'm staring at a spreadsheet that looks like it's been attacked by a calculator. Trying to figure out inventory cost flow for a client using LIFO during a period of crazy inflation. Honestly, I spent hours trying to reconcile the ending inventory. Moral of the story? Nail down these concepts now. It'll save you from looking like a deer in headlights when you're under pressure. Trust me, your future self will thank you. Plus, knowing this stuff cold makes you look like a rockstar.

sections.title: FIFO vs. LIFO: The Classic Mix-Up
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming LIFO always results in lower taxes. While it's true during periods of rising prices, remember that LIFO is prohibited under IFRS! Students often forget to consider the accounting standards being applied. Also, many incorrectly assume that if prices are decreasing, LIFO will result in higher net income. Always analyze the price trends. Failing to do so can lead to significant misstatements and incorrect tax planning advice. Always consider the impact of price fluctuations on each method.

sections.title: Sarah's Inventory Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, was tasked with valuing ending inventory using the weighted-average method. She diligently calculated the weighted average cost but forgot to multiply it by the *number* of units remaining in ending inventory, instead applying it to the number of units sold. This significantly understated her company's assets. The lesson? Double-check that you're applying the weighted-average cost to the correct quantity. Accuracy is paramount when dealing with inventory valuation.

sections.title: Inventory Costing: Beyond the Exam
sections.content: In practice, you'll use inventory costing methods not just for financial reporting, but also for internal management decisions. For example, a retailer might use FIFO to manage perishable goods, ensuring older items are sold first to minimize spoilage. Understanding the implications of each method on key financial metrics like gross profit margin is vital for advising clients on optimizing their inventory strategies. You might even be asked to analyze the impact of switching from FIFO to weighted average on a company's financial statements during an audit.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FIFO: Oldest costs → COGS; newest costs → Ending Inventory (highest income in inflation)
LIFO: Newest costs → COGS; oldest costs → Ending Inventory (lowest income/taxes in inflation)
LIFO is PROHIBITED under IFRS—frequently tested!
Weighted Average falls between FIFO and LIFO results
LIFO Reserve = FIFO Inventory − LIFO Inventory (disclosed in notes)
LIFO liquidation distorts income when old layers are sold

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: FIFO vs. LIFO: Impact on Financial Statements

knowledgeCheck.question: During a period of rising prices, which of the following statements is true regarding the impact of FIFO versus LIFO on a company's financial statements?
options: FIFO results in a lower cost of goods sold and a lower net income compared to LIFO.
FIFO results in a higher cost of goods sold and a higher net income compared to LIFO.
FIFO results in a lower cost of goods sold and a higher net income compared to LIFO.
FIFO results in a higher cost of goods sold and a lower net income compared to LIFO.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: In a period of rising prices, FIFO uses the older, cheaper costs for COGS, resulting in a lower COGS. This leads to a higher gross profit and, consequently, a higher net income. LIFO, conversely, uses the newer, more expensive costs, resulting in a higher COGS and lower net income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which costs (oldest or newest) are being assigned to COGS under each method during rising prices.

reveal.question: In a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) inventory system, what assumption is made about which inventory costs are assigned to the cost of goods sold (COGS)?
reveal.answer: LIFO assumes that the *newest* inventory costs (the most recently purchased items) are the first ones sold and therefore assigned to COGS. The *oldest* inventory costs remain in ending inventory.

sections.title: FIFO vs. LIFO: A Side-by-Side Comparison

rows.label: Assumption
values: First items purchased are first items sold.
Last items purchased are first items sold.

title: Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value
description: Apply inventory valuation rules when market value declines

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Inventory must be written down if its value drops below cost. The rules differ slightly for LIFO/retail vs. other methods. This topic combines conservatism, valuation concepts, and calculation skills—all heavily tested on FAR.

sections.title: The Basic Rule
sections.content:
Inventory should be reported at the **lower of cost or market**.

**"Market" depends on cost flow method:**

• **FIFO, Weighted Average, Specific ID:**
  Market = **Net Realizable Value (NRV)**

• **LIFO, Retail Method:**
  Market = **NRV with ceiling and floor constraints**

**ASC 330 simplified the rules for most companies by using NRV directly (not the old ceiling/floor).**

sections.title: Net Realizable Value (NRV)
sections.content:
**NRV = Estimated Selling Price − Costs to Complete − Costs to Sell**

**Example:**
Expected selling price: $100
Costs to complete: $10
Selling costs (commission, shipping): $5
**NRV = $100 − $10 − $5 = $85**

If cost is $90, write down to $85.

**This is the method for FIFO, Weighted Average, and Specific ID.**

sections.title: Lower of Cost or Market (LIFO/Retail)
sections.content:
For LIFO and Retail Method, "Market" is constrained:

**Market = Replacement Cost, BUT:**

• **Ceiling** (maximum) = NRV
• **Floor** (minimum) = NRV − Normal Profit Margin

**Apply the constraints:**
1. If Replacement Cost > Ceiling → Use Ceiling (NRV)
2. If Replacement Cost < Floor → Use Floor (NRV − Profit)
3. If between → Use Replacement Cost

Then compare this "Market" to Cost. Use lower.

sections.title: LCM Calculation Steps (LIFO/Retail)
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Cost: $50
• Replacement Cost: $45
• Selling Price: $60
• Costs to complete/sell: $8
• Normal profit margin: 20% of selling price = $12

**Step 1: Calculate constraints**
• Ceiling (NRV) = $60 − $8 = $52
• Floor = $52 − $12 = $40

**Step 2: Determine Market**
• Replacement cost $45 is between $40 and $52
• Market = $45

**Step 3: Compare to Cost**
• Cost = $50, Market = $45
• LCM = $45 (write down by $5)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: LCM Ceiling and Floor
sections.content:
**"Ceiling is NRV, Floor is NRV minus Profit"**

Think of it as:
• You can't value inventory HIGHER than what you'll get for it (ceiling = NRV)
• You can't value it LOWER than what you'll net after normal profit (floor)

**Squeeze replacement cost between ceiling and floor, then compare to cost!**

sections.title: Application: Item by Item vs. Category
sections.content:
**How to apply LCM/LCNRV:**

1. **Item-by-item** (most conservative, most common)
2. **By category/class** of similar items
3. **In aggregate** (total inventory)

**Item-by-item produces largest write-down** because gains in some items can't offset losses in others.

**Once selected, apply consistently.**

sections.title: Journal Entry for Write-Down
sections.content:
**Direct Method (Reduce Inventory):**
Dr Loss on Inventory Write-Down    $XX
    Cr Inventory                         $XX

**Allowance Method:**
Dr Loss on Inventory Write-Down    $XX
    Cr Allowance to Reduce Inv to NRV    $XX

**Income Statement Presentation:**
• Usually included in **Cost of Goods Sold**
• If material/unusual, may be shown separately

sections.title: Reversal of Write-Downs
sections.content:
**U.S. GAAP:**
• Write-downs are **permanent**
• **NO reversal allowed** even if value recovers
• New lower value becomes the "cost" basis

**IFRS:**
• Write-downs **CAN be reversed**
• But only up to original cost (no gain beyond cost)
• Reversal recognized in P&L

**This is a key GAAP vs. IFRS difference!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Different Rules by Method
sections.content:
**Watch the cost flow method!**

• FIFO/Weighted Average/Specific ID → Use NRV directly
• LIFO/Retail → Use LCM with ceiling/floor

The exam may give you replacement cost, ceiling, and floor when you don't need them (if using FIFO)!

Always check: **What cost flow method is the company using?**

content.definition: Given selling price, costs to complete, selling costs. NRV = SP − CTC − CTS
Compare replacement cost to ceiling (NRV) and floor (NRV − profit). Use middle value.
Cost minus LCM/LCNRV = write-down amount
GAAP: No reversal. IFRS: Reversal allowed up to original cost.

sections.content: <!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: NRV: It's All About What You Actually Get!
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, 'Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value' sounds boring, right? But think of it this way: you're selling your old car. You *think* it's worth $5,000 (that's your cost), but after fixing the dent ($500) and paying for the ad ($100), you'll only get $4,400. That $4,400 is your NRV! You wouldn't value the car on your books at $5,000 when you know you'll only pocket $4,400, would you? Accounting is about being realistic, and NRV forces us to face reality.

sections.title: Forgetting the 'Costs to Sell'!
sections.content: Students often calculate NRV by only subtracting 'Costs to Complete'. Don't forget the 'Costs to Sell'! It's easy to overlook expenses like commissions, shipping, or advertising. Imagine you have inventory that needs minor repairs costing $5, but to sell it, you have to pay a 10% commission on the $100 selling price. NRV is $100 - $5 - $10 = $85, *not* $95. Always factor in all the costs you'll incur to actually get the cash in your hand.

sections.title: Sarah's Seasonal Sweaters
sections.content: Sarah owns a boutique that sells seasonal sweaters. She has 50 sweaters that cost her $30 each. It's late in the season, and she estimates she can sell them for $40 each, but she'll have to discount them heavily and pay $2 per sweater for repackaging. Her NRV is $40 - $2 = $38. Since $38 is higher than her cost of $30, she doesn't need to write them down. However, if her estimated selling price was only $31, her NRV would be $29, and she'd have to write them down to $29 each.

sections.title: Auditing Inventory: Beyond the Spreadsheet
sections.content: As an auditor, you'll be verifying inventory valuations. Don't just blindly accept the client's spreadsheet! Question their assumptions about selling prices, costs to complete, and, crucially, costs to sell. Review sales contracts, shipping agreements, and marketing plans to assess the reasonableness of their NRV calculations. A small change in the estimated selling price can have a significant impact on reported earnings, so be diligent!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FIFO/Weighted Avg: Use NRV directly (simpler rule under ASC 330)
LIFO/Retail: Use LCM with ceiling (NRV) and floor (NRV − normal profit)
NRV = Selling Price − Costs to Complete − Costs to Sell
Apply LCM item-by-item for most conservative result
GAAP: Write-downs are permanent (no reversal allowed)
IFRS: Write-downs can be reversed up to original cost

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Company uses the FIFO inventory method. They have inventory with a cost of $75 per unit. The estimated selling price is $95 per unit, costs to complete are $5 per unit, and selling costs are $3 per unit. At what amount should XYZ Company report the inventory?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With FIFO, Market = NRV. NRV = $95 (Selling Price) - $5 (Costs to Complete) - $3 (Selling Costs) = $87. The inventory should be reported at the lower of cost ($75) or NRV ($87), which is $75.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula for NRV and compare it to the cost.

reveal.question: What is the formula for Net Realizable Value (NRV)?
reveal.answer: Net Realizable Value (NRV) = Estimated Selling Price - Costs to Complete - Costs to Sell

rows.label: Definition
values: NRV (Estimated Selling Price - Costs to Complete - Costs to Sell)

rows.label: Application
values: Replacement cost cannot exceed the ceiling. If it does, use the ceiling.
Replacement cost cannot be lower than the floor. If it is, use the floor.

title: Dollar-Value LIFO
description: Master the complex LIFO pools technique using price indices

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Dollar-Value LIFO is one of the most complex topics in FAR. It appears in TBS simulations that require multi-step calculations. Once you understand the logic and master the steps, these become very doable. The key is practice!

sections.title: What is Dollar-Value LIFO?
sections.content:
**Problem with Unit LIFO:**
Tracking individual units at different costs is impractical for companies with thousands of items.

**Solution: Dollar-Value LIFO:**
• Group inventory into **pools** of similar items
• Measure inventory in **dollars** rather than units
• Use **price indices** to convert between base-year and current-year dollars
• Track **layers** by year, each at its own index

content.definition: Group of similar inventory items treated as one unit for costing purposes
The starting year for DV-LIFO. All calculations reference back to base-year prices.
Ratio of current-year prices to base-year prices. Base year index = 1.00
An increment of inventory added in a specific year, valued at that year's index
Current inventory converted to base-year dollars using the price index

sections.title: The DV-LIFO Process
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Convert ending inventory to BASE-YEAR dollars
Ending Inv at Base = Ending Inv at Current Prices ÷ Current Year Index

**Step 2:** Compare to prior year's ending inventory (at base)

**Step 3:** Determine if there's an INCREMENT or DECREMENT:
• Increment: Current base > Prior base (added a layer)
• Decrement: Current base < Prior base (LIFO liquidation)

**Step 4:** Convert layers to DV-LIFO cost:
• Each layer × its year's price index

**Step 5:** Sum all layers = Ending Inventory at DV-LIFO

sections.title: Year 1 Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Base year (Year 1) inventory at current prices: $100,000
• Price index Year 1: 1.00 (base year)

**Calculation:**
Ending at base = $100,000 ÷ 1.00 = $100,000

**DV-LIFO Inventory:**
Base layer: $100,000 × 1.00 = $100,000

**This becomes the starting point for future years.**

sections.title: Year 2 Example: Adding a Layer
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Ending inventory at current prices: $126,000
• Price index Year 2: 1.05
• Prior year base: $100,000

**Step 1: Convert to base**
$126,000 ÷ 1.05 = $120,000 at base

**Step 2: Compare to prior**
Current base: $120,000
Prior base: $100,000
Increment: $20,000 (at base)

**Step 3: Value layers**
Base layer: $100,000 × 1.00 = $100,000
Year 2 layer: $20,000 × 1.05 = $21,000

**DV-LIFO Inventory: $121,000**

sections.title: Year 3 Example: LIFO Liquidation
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Ending inventory at current prices: $115,500
• Price index Year 3: 1.10
• Prior year base: $120,000

**Step 1: Convert to base**
$115,500 ÷ 1.10 = $105,000 at base

**Step 2: Compare to prior**
Current base: $105,000
Prior base: $120,000
Decrement: $15,000 (at base) — LIFO liquidation!

**Step 3: Peel off layers (LIFO)**
Year 2 layer had $20,000 at base → Reduce by $15,000
Remaining Year 2 layer: $5,000 at base

**Step 4: Value remaining layers**
Base layer: $100,000 × 1.00 = $100,000
Year 2 layer: $5,000 × 1.05 = $5,250

**DV-LIFO Inventory: $105,250**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DV-LIFO Steps
sections.content:
**"DCIVC" — Don't Confuse Inventory Value Calculations**

**D**eflate to base (÷ index)
**C**ompare to prior year base
**I**dentify increment or decrement
**V**alue each layer at its index
**C**ombine layers for total

Always work in BASE-YEAR DOLLARS first, then convert!

sections.title: Calculating the Price Index
sections.content:
**Two Methods:**

**1. Double-Extension Method:**
Extend ending inventory at both current-year AND base-year prices
Index = Total at Current ÷ Total at Base

**2. Link-Chain Method:**
Calculate internal index each year (current vs. prior year)
Cumulative index = Prior index × Current internal index

**The exam typically provides the index.** If not, they'll give you the info to calculate it.

sections.title: Double-Extension Example
sections.content:
**Ending Inventory:**
Item A: 100 units × $15 current = $1,500
Item A: 100 units × $12 base = $1,200
Item B: 200 units × $8 current = $1,600
Item B: 200 units × $6 base = $1,200

**Totals:**
Current prices: $3,100
Base prices: $2,400

**Index = $3,100 ÷ $2,400 = 1.29**

**Inventory at Base = $3,100 ÷ 1.29 = $2,403**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Which Index to Use
sections.content:
**Each layer uses its OWN year's index!**

Common mistake: Using current year index for all layers.

**Correct approach:**
• Base layer: Always index of 1.00
• Year 2 layer: Year 2's index (e.g., 1.05)
• Year 3 layer: Year 3's index (e.g., 1.10)

Layers are "frozen" at the price level when created.

sections.title: Why Use DV-LIFO?
sections.content:
**Advantages:**
• Simplifies LIFO for large inventories
• Reduces LIFO liquidation (pools absorb changes)
• Practical for retail and manufacturing
• Still achieves tax benefits of LIFO

**Disadvantages:**
• Complex calculations
• Requires maintaining historical layer data
• Index calculation can be subjective
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Dollar-Value LIFO: It's All About the Index!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, Dollar-Value LIFO can feel like alphabet soup at first. But trust me, once you get the hang of the price indices, it's smooth sailing. Think of the index as your translator, converting those current-year dollars back to the base-year language. Mess up the translation, and your inventory valuation is toast. I remember one time, I accidentally used the wrong index and the CFO nearly had a heart attack! So, double-check those indices, folks!

sections.title: Confusing Base-Year Dollars with Current-Year Dollars
sections.content: A common mistake is forgetting *why* you're converting to base-year dollars. Students often calculate ending inventory at base-year prices, then immediately compare that number to the *current-year* ending inventory. The point of converting to base-year is to eliminate the effect of inflation. Always compare base-year ending inventory to the *prior year's base-year* ending inventory to determine the real increase (or decrease) in inventory quantity.

sections.title: Sarah's Inventory Puzzle
sections.content: Sarah, a cost accountant at Acme Corp, is calculating Dollar-Value LIFO. Ending inventory at current prices is $500,000, and the current year price index is 1.25. She needs to determine the base-year value. Sarah correctly divides $500,000 by 1.25, getting $400,000. The key takeaway is that the $400,000 represents the inventory value as if prices hadn't changed since the base year. This allows for a true comparison to prior periods.

sections.title: Dollar-Value LIFO in the Real World
sections.content: You'll likely encounter Dollar-Value LIFO when auditing companies with large, diverse inventories, especially retailers or manufacturers. Imagine auditing a department store with thousands of SKUs; tracking each item individually would be a nightmare. Dollar-Value LIFO simplifies this by grouping similar items into pools and using price indices published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) or internally developed indices. Mastering this technique allows you to efficiently verify inventory valuations for even the most complex businesses.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: DV-LIFO measures inventory in dollars using pools and price indices
Always convert ending inventory to BASE-YEAR dollars first (÷ current index)
Compare to prior year to identify INCREMENT (new layer) or DECREMENT (liquidation)
Each layer keeps its original year's price index forever
Liquidation removes most recent layers first (LIFO)
Price index = Inventory at current prices ÷ Inventory at base prices

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: King Company uses the dollar-value LIFO method. At the end of 2023, King's inventory at year-end prices was $360,000. The price index for 2023, using 2022 as the base year, is 120%. What is King's inventory at base-year prices at the end of 2023?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The formula for converting ending inventory to base-year dollars is: Ending Inventory at Base = Ending Inventory at Current Prices ÷ Current Year Index.  Therefore, $360,000 / 1.20 = $300,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula to convert ending inventory at current prices to base-year prices.

reveal.question: Explain the significance of an 'increment' in the context of Dollar-Value LIFO.
reveal.answer: An increment occurs when the ending inventory at base-year prices is greater than the prior year's ending inventory at base-year prices. This means that the inventory has increased in quantity (measured in base-year dollars). The increment is then layered and valued using the price index for the year the increment occurred.

knowledgeCheck.question: Using Dollar-Value LIFO, if the ending inventory at base-year prices is *lower* than the beginning inventory at base-year prices, which of the following is true?
options: A new inventory layer is created.
A decrement has occurred, and the LIFO layers are reduced starting with the most recent layer.
The price index must be recalculated.
The company must switch to unit LIFO.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When the ending inventory at base-year prices is less than the beginning inventory at base-year prices, a decrement has occurred. This requires reducing the existing LIFO layers, starting with the most recently added layer, until the difference is accounted for.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how LIFO works in general, and how the dollar-value method applies it.

title: Inventory Errors & Period Corrections
description: Analyze and correct inventory errors across periods

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Inventory errors have a ripple effect across periods. An error in ending inventory affects COGS, net income, retained earnings, AND the next period! The exam loves multi-period error analysis—understand the self-correcting nature and impact on each financial statement.

sections.title: The Inventory-COGS Relationship
sections.content:
**Remember the equation:**
Beginning Inventory + Purchases − Ending Inventory = COGS

**Rearranged:**
COGS = Beginning Inventory + Purchases − Ending Inventory

**Key insight:**
• Overstate Ending Inventory → Understate COGS → Overstate Net Income
• Understate Ending Inventory → Overstate COGS → Understate Net Income

**Ending inventory and COGS move in OPPOSITE directions!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Error Effects
sections.content:
**"ERIC" — Ending and Revenue move In Correlation**

If Ending Inventory is **OVERSTATED:**
• COGS is **UNDERSTATED** (opposite)
• Gross Profit is **OVERSTATED** (same as ending)
• Net Income is **OVERSTATED** (same as ending)
• Retained Earnings **OVERSTATED**

**Ending Inventory Error = Same direction as Net Income Error**

sections.title: Self-Correcting Nature
sections.content:
**Key Concept:** Inventory errors self-correct over TWO periods.

**Why?** Ending inventory of Year 1 = Beginning inventory of Year 2

**Example:**
Year 1: Ending inventory overstated by $10,000
• Year 1 COGS understated → NI overstated by $10,000

Year 2: Beginning inventory overstated by $10,000 (same error carries over)
• Year 2 COGS overstated → NI understated by $10,000

**Net effect over 2 years: ZERO** (errors offset)

**But each individual year is WRONG!**

sections.title: Two-Year Error Analysis
sections.content:
**Scenario:** 2024 ending inventory overstated by $5,000

**2024 Effects:**
• COGS: Understated $5,000
• Net Income: Overstated $5,000
• Ending RE: Overstated $5,000
• Assets: Overstated $5,000

**2025 Effects (error not corrected):**
• Beginning Inv: Overstated $5,000
• COGS: Overstated $5,000
• Net Income: Understated $5,000
• Ending RE: CORRECT (errors offset)
• Assets: CORRECT (error gone)

**After 2 years, balance sheet self-corrects!**

sections.title: Purchases Errors
sections.content:
**Purchases Recorded but Inventory Not Counted:**
• Purchases: Correct
• Ending Inventory: Understated
• COGS: Overstated
• Net Income: Understated

**Inventory Counted but Purchases Not Recorded:**
• Purchases: Understated
• Ending Inventory: Correct
• COGS: Understated
• Net Income: Overstated
• A/P: Understated

**These errors do NOT self-correct if A/P affected!**

sections.title: FOB Shipping Terms
sections.content:
**FOB Shipping Point:**
• Title passes when goods LEAVE seller
• In transit goods belong to BUYER
• Buyer includes in inventory even if not received

**FOB Destination:**
• Title passes when goods ARRIVE at buyer
• In transit goods belong to SELLER
• Buyer excludes until received

**Common Error:** Buyer includes goods that are FOB Destination but still in transit → Overstates inventory

sections.title: Consignment Inventory
sections.content:
**Consignment goods belong to the CONSIGNOR until sold.**

**Consignor (owner):**
• Includes consignment inventory on its books
• Records sale when consignee sells to customer

**Consignee (holder):**
• Does NOT include in its inventory
• Acts as agent; earns commission

**Common Error:** Consignee includes consignment goods in their inventory → Overstates inventory

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cut-off Errors
sections.content:
**Cut-off errors are common in year-end inventory scenarios:**

• Goods shipped FOB Shipping Point on Dec 31 — include in BUYER's inventory
• Goods shipped FOB Destination on Dec 31, arriving Jan 2 — include in SELLER's inventory
• Consignment goods at retailer — include in CONSIGNOR's inventory

Always ask: **Who has TITLE to the goods on the balance sheet date?**

sections.title: Correcting Entry
sections.content:
**If error discovered in same year:**
Simply correct the inventory count.

**If error discovered in subsequent year (books closed):**

Overstated Ending Inventory (prior year):
Dr Retained Earnings       $X,XXX
    Cr Inventory (if still wrong)    $X,XXX

**If error already self-corrected:**
No entry needed for balance sheet, but may need to restate comparative income statements.

sections.title: Comprehensive Error Analysis
sections.content:
**Given:** Following errors in 2024:
1. Ending inventory overstated $8,000
2. Purchases of $3,000 not recorded (goods were counted)
3. Consignment goods of $2,000 included in ending inventory

**Analysis:**
1. EI overstated $8,000 → NI overstated $8,000
2. Purchases understated $3,000 → COGS understated $3,000 → NI overstated $3,000 (Also A/P understated $3,000)
3. EI overstated $2,000 → NI overstated $2,000

**Total NI Overstatement: $8,000 + $3,000 + $2,000 = $13,000**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Inventory Errors: The Gift That Keeps on Giving (Twice!)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, inventory errors are a pain. But here's the good news: they're usually self-correcting. Think of it like this: you mess up the ending inventory one year, and it automatically becomes the beginning inventory the next. So, while you might have a rough year one, year two is like a free pass to fix it. Just remember that overstatement/understatement dance we talked about. Get that down, and you're golden. Trust me, I've seen it all – even a warehouse manager who 'found' a whole pallet of widgets after the books closed!

sections.title: Net Income Impact: Getting Overstatement & Understatement Flipped
sections.content: A frequent error is confusing the relationship between ending inventory and net income. Students often think that overstating ending inventory *understates* net income. This is incorrect! Remember, an overstated ending inventory leads to an *understated* Cost of Goods Sold (COGS). Since COGS is subtracted from revenue to get gross profit, a lower COGS means a higher gross profit, and thus a higher net income. The reverse is true for understated ending inventory. Practice visualizing the formula: Revenue - COGS = Gross Profit - Expenses = Net Income. This helps to avoid flipping the relationship.

sections.title: Sarah's Spreadsheet Snafu: The $5,000 Mistake
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, accidentally entered $55,000 instead of $50,000 for Year 1 ending inventory. This $5,000 overstatement flowed into Year 2 as beginning inventory. While Year 1's net income was overstated, Year 2's will be *understated* by the same amount. The key takeaway? Don't just fix Year 1; you *must* also adjust Year 2 to fully correct the consolidated financial statements. Always trace the impact across both periods.

sections.title: Inventory Counts: Where the Rubber Meets the Road
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter inventory errors most often during physical inventory counts. Discrepancies between the system and the actual count are common. For example, damaged goods might be included in the count at full price, or obsolete inventory might not be written down. Understanding the impact of these errors on COGS and net income is crucial for accurate financial reporting. This is why thorough inventory reconciliation and valuation are essential components of a strong internal control system.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Ending inventory errors move OPPOSITE to COGS, SAME direction as Net Income
Inventory errors self-correct over two periods (but each year is wrong)
FOB Shipping Point: Buyer owns goods in transit
FOB Destination: Seller owns goods in transit
Consignment goods stay in consignor's inventory until sold
Purchases error with A/P impact does NOT self-correct

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Impact of Inventory Errors on Financial Statements

knowledgeCheck.question: During Year 1, Beta Corp. understated its ending inventory by $25,000. What is the effect of this error on Beta Corp.'s Year 1 financial statements?
options: Net Income is overstated and Cost of Goods Sold is understated.
Net Income is understated and Cost of Goods Sold is overstated.
Net Income and Cost of Goods Sold are both overstated.
Net Income and Cost of Goods Sold are both understated.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Understating ending inventory results in an overstatement of Cost of Goods Sold (COGS). Since COGS is an expense, an overstatement of COGS leads to an understatement of Net Income. Remember the formula: Beginning Inventory + Purchases - Ending Inventory = COGS.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how the ending inventory number directly impacts the calculation of Cost of Goods Sold.

reveal.question: Explain why inventory errors are said to be 'self-correcting' over a two-year period. What is the underlying principle that drives this?
reveal.answer: Inventory errors self-correct over two periods because the ending inventory of one period becomes the beginning inventory of the next period. If Year 1 ending inventory is incorrect, that incorrect amount flows directly into Year 2 as the beginning inventory, offsetting the initial error over the two years. While individual year income statements are misstated, the cumulative income over the two-year period is correct.

knowledgeCheck.question: Gamma Company overstated its Year 1 ending inventory by $50,000. Assuming no other errors, what is the combined effect of this error on Gamma Company's net income for Year 1 and Year 2?
options: Net income is overstated by $50,000 over the two years.
Net income is understated by $50,000 over the two years.
Net income is correctly stated over the two years.
The effect on net income cannot be determined without additional information.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While Year 1 net income is overstated by $50,000, Year 2 net income will be understated by $50,000 because the overstated Year 1 ending inventory becomes the overstated Year 2 beginning inventory, leading to higher COGS in Year 2. Therefore, the combined effect over the two years is zero; net income is correctly stated over the two-year period.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the cumulative effect over both years, considering the beginning inventory impact in the second year.

title: PP&E Acquisition & Capitalization
description: Determine what costs to capitalize vs. expense for property, plant, and equipment

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: PP&E is typically the largest asset on a company's balance sheet. Knowing what to capitalize vs. expense directly impacts net income, total assets, and key ratios. The exam tests your judgment on which costs belong in the asset vs. on the income statement.

sections.title: What is PP&E?
sections.content:
**Property, Plant & Equipment** includes tangible assets that are:

• **Used in operations** (not held for sale)
• **Long-term** (useful life > 1 year)
• **Physical substance** (tangible)

**Examples:**
• Land
• Buildings
• Machinery and equipment
• Furniture and fixtures
• Vehicles
• Leasehold improvements

sections.title: The Capitalization Principle
sections.content:
**Capitalize costs that provide FUTURE BENEFIT:**

• Costs to acquire the asset
• Costs to bring it to its intended location
• Costs to prepare it for its intended use

**Expense costs that benefit only the CURRENT PERIOD:**

• Repairs and maintenance
• Operating costs after asset is ready for use

content.definition: Invoice price less any discounts (trade or cash discounts taken). Include sales tax if applicable.
Transportation costs to bring asset to location. FOB terms determine who pays.
Costs to set up, assemble, test, and prepare equipment for use.
Clearing, grading, or preparing the location for the asset.
Legal fees for title, architectural fees, engineering fees directly related to acquisition.
Costs of trial runs before asset is ready for intended use (net of any revenue from test output).
Routine upkeep that maintains (doesn't enhance) the asset's condition.
Employee training to operate equipment—expense as incurred.
Losses during the break-in period after asset is ready for use.
Costs to move existing equipment to a new location (generally).
General overhead not directly attributable to acquisition.

sections.title: Land Costs
sections.content:
**Capitalize to LAND (not depreciated):**
• Purchase price
• Closing costs (title, legal, recording)
• Existing structures removed to prepare site (net of salvage)
• Grading, filling, draining for permanent improvement
• Assessments for streets, sewers, sidewalks (permanent)

**Capitalize to LAND IMPROVEMENTS (depreciated):**
• Parking lots
• Fencing
• Lighting
• Landscaping with limited life

**Key:** Land has unlimited life; Land Improvements are depreciated.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capitalize or Expense?
sections.content:
**Ask yourself: Is it BEFORE or AFTER the asset is ready for use?**

**BEFORE ready for use → CAPITALIZE**
(Getting the asset to its location and condition for intended use)

**AFTER ready for use → EXPENSE**
(Operating, maintaining, or running the asset)

The "ready for use" moment is the dividing line!

sections.title: Building Costs
sections.content:
**Purchased Building:**
• Purchase price (allocated between land and building)
• Renovation costs to prepare for intended use
• Legal and closing costs

**Constructed Building:**
• Materials, labor, and overhead
• Architect and engineering fees
• Building permits
• **Capitalized interest** during construction (ASC 835)
• Insurance during construction

sections.title: Interest Capitalization
sections.content:
**When to Capitalize Interest (ASC 835-20):**

Capitalize interest on assets constructed or produced for:
• Company's own use
• Sale or lease as discrete projects

**Capitalization Period:**
• Begins when: Expenditures incurred, activities in progress, interest being incurred
• Ends when: Asset substantially complete and ready for use

**Amount to Capitalize:**
Lesser of:
1. Actual interest incurred
2. Avoidable interest (weighted avg expenditures × interest rate)

sections.title: Interest Capitalization Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Building construction expenditures:
  - Jan 1: $500,000
  - Jul 1: $300,000
• Construction period: Full year
• Debt: $1,000,000 @ 8%

**Weighted Average Expenditures:**
Jan 1: $500,000 × 12/12 = $500,000
Jul 1: $300,000 × 6/12 = $150,000
Total: $650,000

**Avoidable Interest:**
$650,000 × 8% = $52,000

**Actual Interest:**
$1,000,000 × 8% = $80,000

**Capitalize:** $52,000 (lesser amount)
**Expense:** $28,000 ($80,000 − $52,000)

sections.title: Lump-Sum Purchases
sections.content:
**Basket Purchase:** Multiple assets acquired for single price.

**Allocation Method:**
Allocate total cost based on **relative fair values**.

**Example:**
Purchase land and building for $500,000
• Land FMV: $150,000
• Building FMV: $350,000
• Total FMV: $500,000

Land: $500,000 × ($150,000/$500,000) = $150,000
Building: $500,000 × ($350,000/$500,000) = $350,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Government Grants
sections.content:
**If government provides grant for PP&E:**

**Two methods (policy choice):**

1. **Reduce asset cost** (net method)
   - Lower depreciation over asset life

2. **Deferred income** (gross method)
   - Asset at full cost
   - Recognize grant income over asset life

**Both methods spread benefit over useful life!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Capitalization: Don't Be a Cheapskate (Too Soon!)
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Nobody *wants* to capitalize stuff. It's way easier to expense it and lower that taxable income right away. But the CPA exam doesn't reward laziness! Think of it this way: If the cost is going to benefit the company for more than just this year, capitalize it. Don't try to sneak in that $5,000 delivery fee for the new machine as an expense. It's part of getting the asset ready, period. The IRS (and your future boss) will thank you for getting it right from the start. Play the long game!

sections.title: Land Improvements: Not Always Part of the Land!
sections.content: Students often lump land improvements (like landscaping or fencing) directly into the cost of the land itself. This is wrong! Land is considered to have an indefinite life and is not depreciated. Land improvements, however, *do* depreciate over their useful lives. The key difference is that land improvements are subject to wear and tear and will eventually need to be replaced. Don't forget to depreciate those fences and sprinkler systems separately! It's a common mistake that can throw off your calculations.

sections.title: Sarah's Shipping Snafu: Getting the Asset Ready
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant at Acme Corp, received an invoice for $12,000 for the delivery and installation of a new printing press. The invoice was broken down into $2,000 for shipping and $10,000 for specialized installation. Sarah initially wanted to expense the shipping, thinking it was a period cost. However, she realized that the printing press couldn't operate without the installation *and* the delivery. Both costs are necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use, and therefore should be capitalized as part of the asset's cost.

sections.title: Audit Trail: Capitalization Decisions in the Real World
sections.content: In a typical month during an audit, you'll encounter this when reviewing a company's fixed asset schedule and supporting documentation. You'll need to verify that the costs capitalized are actually directly related to the acquisition and preparation of the asset. Look for invoices, contracts, and other documentation to support the capitalization decisions. Are the costs reasonable? Are they incremental? This is a key area for potential misstatement, so mastering it now will save you headaches (and potential audit findings) later on.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capitalize costs to acquire, transport, and prepare asset for intended use
Expense costs incurred AFTER asset is ready for use (repairs, training)
Land is NOT depreciated; Land Improvements ARE depreciated
Interest capitalization: Lesser of actual interest or avoidable interest
Basket purchases: Allocate based on relative fair values
Subsequent costs: Capitalize if extends life or improves productivity

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company purchased a machine for $50,000. They also incurred $2,000 in freight charges to get the machine to their factory, $5,000 for installation, and $1,000 for routine maintenance performed after the machine was operational. What amount should ABC Company capitalize as the cost of the machine?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The capitalized cost includes the purchase price ($50,000), freight charges ($2,000), and installation costs ($5,000). Routine maintenance ($1,000) is expensed because it benefits the current period, not the future. $50,000 + $2,000 + $5,000 = $57,000
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which costs are necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use.

reveal.question: List the three key characteristics of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E).
reveal.answer:
1. Used in operations (not held for sale)
2. Long-term (useful life > 1 year)
3. Physical substance (tangible)

rows.label: Examples
values: Repairs and Maintenance, Operating Costs after asset is ready for use

title: Depreciation Methods: SL, DDB, Units, SYD
description: Calculate depreciation using all major methods tested on the CPA exam

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Depreciation calculations appear in both MCQ and TBS. You must know all four methods cold: Straight-Line, Double-Declining Balance, Sum-of-Years-Digits, and Units of Production. The exam also tests partial-year depreciation and changes in estimates.

sections.title: Depreciation Basics
sections.content:
**What is Depreciation?**
Systematic allocation of an asset's cost over its useful life.

**Key Terms:**
• **Cost** = Capitalized amount (original cost)
• **Salvage Value** = Estimated value at end of useful life
• **Depreciable Base** = Cost − Salvage Value
• **Useful Life** = Estimated period of benefit

**Depreciation is an ESTIMATE, not exact science!**

sections.title: Straight-Line (SL) Method
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Depreciation Expense = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life

**Or:**
Depreciation Expense = Depreciable Base × (1 ÷ Useful Life)

**Characteristics:**
• Equal expense each period
• Most common method
• Simple and easy to apply
• Best when asset benefits are constant over time

sections.title: Straight-Line Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
