• Cost: $50,000
• Salvage Value: $5,000
• Useful Life: 5 years

**Annual Depreciation:**
($50,000 − $5,000) ÷ 5 = $9,000 per year

**Depreciation Rate:** 1/5 = 20% per year

**Year 1:** $9,000
**Year 2:** $9,000
**Year 3:** $9,000
**Year 4:** $9,000
**Year 5:** $9,000
**Total:** $45,000 (depreciable base)

sections.title: Double-Declining Balance (DDB)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Depreciation = Book Value × (2 ÷ Useful Life)

**Key Points:**
• **Ignores salvage value** in calculation (but don't go below it!)
• Rate is **DOUBLE** the straight-line rate
• Apply rate to **BOOK VALUE** (cost − accumulated depreciation)
• Accelerated method — higher expense in early years
• Stop when book value = salvage value

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DDB Rules
sections.content:
**DDB = "Double the rate, Book value base"**

• **D**ouble the SL rate (2 ÷ Life)
• Apply to **B**ook value (NOT depreciable base)
• **B**ut don't drop below salvage!

Year 1: Always Cost × DDB Rate
Later years: Book Value × DDB Rate

sections.title: Sum-of-Years-Digits (SYD)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Depreciation = Depreciable Base × (Remaining Life ÷ SYD)

**SYD (Sum of Years Digits):**
SYD = n(n+1) ÷ 2

For 5-year life: SYD = 5(6)/2 = 15
Or: 5+4+3+2+1 = 15

**Fraction each year:**
Year 1: 5/15, Year 2: 4/15, Year 3: 3/15, etc.

**Also accelerated — higher early depreciation**

sections.title: Units of Production
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Depreciation Rate = Depreciable Base ÷ Total Estimated Units

Depreciation Expense = Rate × Units Produced This Period

**Characteristics:**
• Based on OUTPUT, not time
• Good for assets with variable usage
• Common for machinery, vehicles
• Depreciation varies with activity level

sections.title: Units of Production Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Cost: $50,000
• Salvage Value: $5,000
• Estimated Total Output: 100,000 units
• Year 1 Production: 25,000 units
• Year 2 Production: 30,000 units

**Rate:** $45,000 ÷ 100,000 = $0.45 per unit

**Year 1:** 25,000 × $0.45 = $11,250
**Year 2:** 30,000 × $0.45 = $13,500

sections.title: Partial-Year Depreciation
sections.content:
**When asset acquired mid-year:**

Calculate full-year depreciation, then prorate.

**Common Conventions:**
• **Exact days/months** — Most precise
• **Half-year convention** — 6 months in year of acquisition and disposal
• **Mid-month convention** — Half month in month of acquisition
• **Full-month convention** — Full month if owned any part

**Exam Tip:** Read carefully for which convention applies!

sections.title: Partial-Year Example
sections.content:
**Asset purchased April 1 (SL method):**
• Cost: $50,000, Salvage: $5,000, Life: 5 years
• Full-year depreciation: $9,000

**Year 1 (April-December = 9 months):**
$9,000 × 9/12 = $6,750

**Year 2 (full year):**
$9,000

**Year 6 (January-March = 3 months):**
$9,000 × 3/12 = $2,250

Total: $6,750 + $9,000×4 + $2,250 = $45,000 ✓

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Changes in Estimates
sections.content:
**If useful life or salvage value changes:**

• **Prospective treatment** (going forward)
• NO restatement of prior periods
• Calculate new depreciation using:
  - Current book value
  - NEW salvage value
  - NEW remaining life

**Entry:** Adjust depreciation expense going forward

**NOT a prior period adjustment!**

sections.title: Change in Estimate Example
sections.content:
**After Year 2, life revised from 5 to 8 years:**

• Original: Cost $50,000, Salvage $5,000, Life 5 years
• Depreciation Years 1-2: $9,000 × 2 = $18,000
• Book Value at end of Year 2: $50,000 − $18,000 = $32,000

**New calculation (remaining 6 years):**
New annual depreciation = ($32,000 − $5,000) ÷ 6 = $4,500

**Years 3-8:** $4,500 per year
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Depreciation: It's More Than Just Numbers!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk depreciation. It's not just some boring formula you cram into your head. Think of it as accounting for the slow, inevitable decline of your company's shiny toys. You bought a delivery truck for $50,000? Great! But it won't be worth that forever. Depreciation helps us spread the cost over its lifespan. Don't just blindly plug numbers; understand the 'why' behind it. It's about matching expenses to revenue. Trust me, understanding this conceptually will save you headaches later!

sections.title: Salvage Value Stumbling Block!
sections.content: Students often forget to subtract salvage value before calculating depreciation expense. They see 'Cost', they see 'Useful Life', and they just dive right in! But remember, salvage value is the estimated worth *at the end* of the asset's life. Depreciation is about allocating the *depreciable base* (Cost - Salvage Value). For example, if you have a $100,000 machine with a $10,000 salvage value, you're only depreciating $90,000. Don't give away free points – always subtract that salvage value first!

sections.title: Sarah's Spreadsheet Snafu!
sections.content: Sarah, a newly minted staff accountant, was tasked with calculating depreciation on a fleet of vehicles. She used the straight-line method for all of them, but forgot to update the formula when one vehicle's useful life changed due to increased usage. This led to an overstatement of depreciation expense and understated net income. The lesson? Always double-check your assumptions and formulas, especially when dealing with multiple assets and varying circumstances.

sections.title: Depreciation: Daily Life in Public Accounting
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter depreciation calculations constantly, especially during audit season. You'll be reviewing client-prepared depreciation schedules, verifying the accuracy of their calculations, and ensuring they've used appropriate methods. Understanding the nuances of each method (straight-line, double-declining balance, etc.) is critical for assessing the reasonableness of their financial statements. Ignoring this means potentially signing off on materially misstated financials. That's not a risk worth taking!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SL: (Cost − Salvage) ÷ Life — equal expense each year
DDB: Book Value × (2/Life) — ignore salvage but don't go below it
SYD: Depreciable Base × (Remaining/SYD) — accelerated like DDB
Units: (Cost − Salvage) ÷ Total Units × Units This Period
Partial year: Prorate based on months or convention specified
Changes in estimates: Apply prospectively (no restatement)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company purchased equipment on January 1, Year 1, for $500,000. The equipment has an estimated useful life of 5 years and an estimated salvage value of $50,000. Using the straight-line method, what is the depreciation expense for Year 2?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The depreciable base is $500,000 (Cost) - $50,000 (Salvage Value) = $450,000. The annual depreciation expense is $450,000 / 5 years = $90,000. The straight-line method results in the same depreciation expense each year.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula for straight-line depreciation: (Cost - Salvage Value) / Useful Life

reveal.question: What is the key difference in the Double-Declining Balance (DDB) method compared to the Straight-Line (SL) method regarding salvage value in the depreciation calculation?
reveal.answer: The DDB method ignores salvage value in the initial depreciation calculation. However, you should not depreciate the asset below its salvage value. The SL method subtracts the salvage value upfront to determine the depreciable base.

rows.label: Depreciation Expense Pattern
values: Higher expense in early years, lower later

rows.label: Salvage Value Consideration
values: Ignored in initial calculation, but asset not depreciated below it

rows.label: Calculation Complexity
values: More complex due to changing book value

rows.label: Impact on Net Income
values: Potentially lower net income in early years, higher later

title: Asset Impairment: Long-Lived Assets
description: Apply the two-step impairment test for assets held and used

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When an asset's value drops significantly, it must be written down. The CPA exam tests your knowledge of WHEN to test for impairment, HOW to measure the loss, and the key difference between GAAP and IFRS (no reversal under GAAP!). This is a frequently tested advanced topic.

sections.title: When to Test for Impairment
sections.content:
**Impairment testing is required when:**

Events or circumstances indicate the carrying amount may not be recoverable:

• Significant decrease in market price
• Significant adverse change in how asset is used
• Significant adverse change in legal or business climate
• Accumulation of costs significantly exceeding expectations
• Current-period operating or cash flow loss combined with history of losses
• Expectation that asset will be sold or disposed of significantly before end of useful life

sections.title: The Two-Step Test (GAAP)
sections.content:
**Step 1: RECOVERABILITY TEST**
Is carrying value recoverable from undiscounted future cash flows?

If Carrying Value > Undiscounted Cash Flows → Asset is IMPAIRED, go to Step 2

If Carrying Value ≤ Undiscounted Cash Flows → No impairment, STOP

**Step 2: MEASUREMENT**
Impairment Loss = Carrying Value − Fair Value

Write asset down to fair value.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Two Steps
sections.content:
**"Undiscounted to TEST, Fair Value to MEASURE"**

**Step 1 (Recoverability):** Use UNDISCOUNTED cash flows
→ This is a low hurdle—just need to recover the investment

**Step 2 (Measurement):** Use FAIR VALUE
→ If impaired, write down to what it's actually worth

**Only go to Step 2 if Step 1 fails!**

sections.title: Impairment Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Equipment carrying value: $500,000
• Expected undiscounted future cash flows: $450,000
• Fair value: $380,000

**Step 1: Recoverability Test**
Carrying value $500,000 > Cash flows $450,000
→ Asset is IMPAIRED

**Step 2: Measure Impairment Loss**
Loss = $500,000 − $380,000 = $120,000

**Journal Entry:**
Dr Impairment Loss               $120,000
    Cr Accumulated Depreciation       $120,000

OR

Dr Impairment Loss               $120,000
    Cr Equipment                       $120,000

sections.title: Fair Value Determination
sections.content:
**Fair Value Hierarchy (ASC 820):**

**Level 1:** Quoted prices in active markets for identical assets

**Level 2:** Observable inputs other than Level 1 (similar assets, market data)

**Level 3:** Unobservable inputs (company's own estimates)

**If no market price available:**
Use present value of expected future cash flows or other valuation techniques.

sections.title: Asset Groups
sections.content:
**What if asset doesn't generate independent cash flows?**

Test for impairment at the **asset group** level:

• Lowest level of identifiable cash flows
• Largely independent of other asset cash flows
• Group assets that work together

**Example:** A factory with multiple machines—test the factory as a group, not each machine separately.

sections.title: After Impairment
sections.content:
**Post-Impairment Treatment:**

• New carrying value = Fair value (becomes new "cost")
• Continue depreciating over remaining useful life
• Use new, lower depreciable base

**GAAP Rule: NO REVERSAL ALLOWED!**

Even if fair value later increases, you cannot write the asset back up.

**IFRS Difference:** Reversal IS allowed (but not above original carrying amount)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: GAAP vs. IFRS Impairment
sections.content:
**GAAP (ASC 360):**
• Two-step test (recoverability, then measurement)
• Undiscounted cash flows for Step 1
• NO reversal of impairment loss

**IFRS (IAS 36):**
• One-step test (compare to recoverable amount directly)
• Recoverable amount = Higher of fair value less costs to sell OR value in use (PV of cash flows)
• Reversal IS required if circumstances change

**This is a frequently tested difference!**

sections.title: Assets Held for Sale
sections.content:
**Different rules for assets to be SOLD (not used):**

**Classification Criteria:**
• Management commits to a plan to sell
• Asset is available for immediate sale
• Active program to locate buyer has begun
• Sale is probable within one year
• Asset is being actively marketed at reasonable price
• Significant changes to plan are unlikely

**Measurement:**
Lower of carrying value or fair value less costs to sell

**No depreciation while held for sale!**

sections.title: Held for Sale Accounting
sections.content:
**Upon Classification as Held for Sale:**

1. Stop depreciating
2. Measure at lower of:
   - Carrying value, or
   - Fair value less costs to sell
3. Recognize loss if FV − costs to sell < carrying value

**Subsequent Increases:**
• Can reverse prior losses (but not above original carrying value)

**Presentation:**
• Report separately on balance sheet (not in PP&E)
• Current asset if sale expected within one year

sections.title: Comparison: Held for Use vs. Held for Sale
rows: Compare to FV less costs to sell
Lower of CV or FV − costs to sell

sections.content: <!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Impairment: When Your Baby's Ugly
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Impairment testing is like admitting your prized asset isn't as valuable as you thought. Nobody *wants* to do it. But trust me, burying your head in the sand doesn't work. If market prices are tanking, or your shiny new machine is constantly breaking down, trigger that impairment test. It's better to take the hit and reflect reality than to overstate your assets and mislead stakeholders. Plus, the auditors *will* find it eventually!

sections.title: Undiscounted vs. Discounted: A Costly Error
sections.content: A frequent error is using *discounted* future cash flows in Step 1 (the recoverability test). Remember, Step 1 specifically requires *undiscounted* cash flows. Students often instinctively discount because that's how we typically value future cash flows. But the point of Step 1 is to see if the asset can even generate enough raw cash to justify its existence. Discounting comes into play only if you fail Step 1 and need to measure the impairment loss. Mess this up and you'll be calculating impairments where none exist, or missing impairments that do!

sections.title: Sarah's Solar Panel Snafu
sections.content: Sarah's company, SolarShine, invested heavily in solar panels. Then, a new, more efficient panel hit the market, making SolarShine's panels obsolete. Sarah noticed a significant drop in the market price for used panels. She correctly recognized this as a triggering event for impairment testing. However, she hesitated to perform the test, hoping the market would rebound. Because she delayed, the eventual impairment loss was even larger, impacting the company's earnings and potentially triggering debt covenant issues.

sections.title: Impairment: It's More Common Than You Think
sections.content: In practice, you'll often encounter impairment testing during annual audits, especially for companies in industries with rapid technological advancements or volatile markets (think tech, energy, or manufacturing). Imagine a company building a factory. If a cheaper, more efficient production method emerges mid-construction, impairment testing is necessary. Mastering the two-step process is crucial for accurately reflecting the financial health of these companies and ensuring fair financial reporting. It directly impacts key metrics like net income and asset values.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Test for impairment when events indicate carrying value may not be recoverable
Step 1: Compare carrying value to UNDISCOUNTED future cash flows
Step 2: If impaired, write down to FAIR VALUE
Impairment loss = Carrying Value − Fair Value
GAAP: NO reversal allowed; IFRS: Reversal required if conditions change
Held for sale: Lower of CV or FV less costs to sell; no depreciation

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following events or circumstances would indicate that an asset's carrying amount may NOT be recoverable, requiring an impairment test under GAAP?
options: A temporary decline in the market price of the asset.
A significant adverse change in the extent or manner in which the asset is being used.
Routine maintenance performed on the asset.
An expected increase in future cash flows attributable to the asset.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A significant adverse change in the asset's use indicates a potential impairment. A temporary decline in market price might not be significant enough, routine maintenance is normal, and expected increases in cash flows would suggest no impairment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which option directly suggests the asset is no longer as valuable or useful as originally expected.

reveal.question: Explain the purpose and process of Step 1 in the GAAP two-step impairment test for long-lived assets.
reveal.answer: Step 1, the Recoverability Test, aims to determine if an asset is potentially impaired. It involves comparing the asset's carrying value to the undiscounted future cash flows expected to be generated by the asset. If the carrying value exceeds the undiscounted cash flows, the asset is considered impaired, and Step 2 (measurement) is required. If the carrying value is less than or equal to the undiscounted cash flows, no impairment exists, and the process stops.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a machine with a carrying value of $500,000. The undiscounted future cash flows expected to be generated by the machine are $450,000. Under GAAP, what is the next step in determining if an impairment loss should be recognized?
options: Recognize an impairment loss of $50,000 immediately.
Compare the carrying value to the discounted future cash flows.
Proceed to Step 2 to measure the impairment loss.
No impairment loss is recognized.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since the carrying value ($500,000) is greater than the undiscounted future cash flows ($450,000), the asset is considered impaired according to Step 1. Therefore, you must proceed to Step 2 to measure the impairment loss.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the decision rule: Carrying Value > Undiscounted Cash Flows leads to Step 2.

title: Disposals & Nonmonetary Exchanges
description: Account for asset sales, retirements, and exchanges with commercial substance

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Assets don't last forever—eventually they're sold, traded, or scrapped. The exam tests your ability to calculate gains and losses on disposal and to handle the tricky rules for nonmonetary exchanges. The "commercial substance" concept is key!

sections.title: Disposal of PP&E
sections.content:
**Basic Steps:**

1. Update depreciation to date of disposal
2. Remove asset cost from books
3. Remove accumulated depreciation
4. Record proceeds received
5. Recognize gain or loss

**Gain/Loss Formula:**
Proceeds − Book Value = Gain (or Loss)

Book Value = Cost − Accumulated Depreciation

sections.title: Disposal Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Equipment cost: $100,000
• Accumulated depreciation at sale: $70,000
• Sold for: $35,000 cash

**Book Value:** $100,000 − $70,000 = $30,000
**Proceeds:** $35,000
**Gain:** $35,000 − $30,000 = $5,000

**Journal Entry:**
Dr Cash                          $35,000
Dr Accumulated Depreciation      $70,000
    Cr Equipment                      $100,000
    Cr Gain on Sale of Equipment       $5,000

sections.title: Retirement (Scrapping)
sections.content:
**When asset is retired with no sale:**

• Remove asset and accumulated depreciation
• No proceeds (or minimal salvage)
• Usually results in a loss

**Entry (no salvage):**
Dr Accumulated Depreciation      $XX,XXX
Dr Loss on Disposal              $X,XXX
    Cr Equipment                      $XX,XXX

sections.title: Involuntary Conversions
sections.content:
**Asset destroyed or condemned (fire, theft, eminent domain):**

**Treatment:**
• Remove asset and accumulated depreciation
• Record insurance proceeds or condemnation award
• Recognize gain or loss

**Example:** Building destroyed by fire
Dr Cash (insurance proceeds)     $300,000
Dr Accumulated Depreciation      $200,000
    Cr Building                       $400,000
    Cr Gain on Involuntary Conversion $100,000

sections.title: Nonmonetary Exchanges: Overview
sections.content:
**When assets are exchanged (not sold for cash):**

The accounting depends on whether the exchange has **COMMERCIAL SUBSTANCE**.

**Commercial Substance exists when:**
The entity's future cash flows are expected to significantly change as a result of the exchange.

Specifically, if the:
• Configuration (timing, risk, amount) of cash flows of asset received differs significantly from asset given up, OR
• Entity-specific value changes, AND
• The difference is significant relative to fair values exchanged

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Commercial Substance
sections.content:
**"Does the exchange change your business?"**

**HAS Commercial Substance:**
• Exchanging a truck for equipment
• Trading land for a building
→ Different cash flow patterns = recognize gain/loss

**LACKS Commercial Substance:**
• Exchanging one delivery truck for a similar delivery truck
→ Same cash flows, just shuffling similar assets = defer gain

sections.title: Exchange WITH Commercial Substance
sections.content:
**When exchange HAS commercial substance:**

Record asset received at **FAIR VALUE**

• Use FV of asset given up (usually more reliable)
• Or FV of asset received if more clearly evident
• Recognize gain or loss immediately

**Entry:**
Dr New Asset (at FV)             $XX,XXX
Dr Accumulated Depreciation      $XX,XXX
Dr/Cr Cash (boot)                $X,XXX
    Cr Old Asset (at cost)            $XX,XXX
    Cr/Dr Gain or Loss                $X,XXX

sections.title: Commercial Substance Example
sections.content:
**Exchange truck for equipment (HAS commercial substance):**

• Truck cost: $50,000
• Truck accumulated depreciation: $30,000
• Truck book value: $20,000
• Truck fair value: $25,000
• Equipment fair value: $30,000
• Cash paid (boot): $5,000

**Entry:**
Dr Equipment                     $30,000
Dr Accumulated Depreciation      $30,000
    Cr Truck                          $50,000
    Cr Cash                            $5,000
    Cr Gain on Exchange                $5,000

**Gain:** FV of truck ($25,000) − BV of truck ($20,000) = $5,000

sections.title: Exchange WITHOUT Commercial Substance
sections.content:
**When exchange LACKS commercial substance:**

**Losses:** Always recognize immediately

**Gains:** Generally deferred (reduce basis of new asset)

**Exception:** If boot (cash) is received, recognize proportionate gain

**Proportionate Gain Formula:**
Recognized Gain = Total Gain × (Boot Received ÷ Total Consideration Received)

sections.title: No Commercial Substance Example
sections.content:
**Exchange similar trucks (LACKS commercial substance):**

• Old truck book value: $20,000
• Old truck fair value: $25,000
• New truck fair value: $28,000
• Cash paid: $3,000
• Total gain: $5,000 (FV $25K − BV $20K)

**Since paying boot (not receiving), defer entire gain:**

**Entry:**
Dr New Truck (plug)              $23,000
Dr Accumulated Depreciation      $XX,XXX
    Cr Old Truck                      $XX,XXX
    Cr Cash                            $3,000

**New truck basis:** $23,000 (not $28,000 FV)
**Deferred gain:** $5,000 (reduces basis of new asset)

sections.title: Boot Received - Partial Gain Recognition
sections.content:
**Exchange similar assets, RECEIVING boot:**

• Old asset BV: $20,000, FV: $30,000
• New asset FV: $25,000
• Cash received: $5,000
• Total gain: $10,000 ($30K − $20K)

**Proportion received as boot:**
$5,000 ÷ $30,000 = 16.67%

**Gain recognized:**
$10,000 × 16.67% = $1,667

**New asset basis:**
$30,000 − $5,000 − ($10,000 − $1,667) = $16,667

Or: BV of old − boot + gain recognized = $20,000 − $5,000 + $1,667 = $16,667

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Always Recognize Losses
sections.content:
**Regardless of commercial substance:**

LOSSES are ALWAYS recognized immediately!

Conservatism principle: Don't hide losses by deferring them.

**Only GAINS are deferred** when exchange lacks commercial substance.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Disposals: It's Not Just About the Money!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Disposals aren't just about calculating a gain or loss and calling it a day. Think about the story behind the numbers! Was that old machine replaced because it was inefficient, or did it blow up spectacularly? Understanding the 'why' helps you catch errors, like forgetting to write off related spare parts or improperly classifying costs associated with the disposal. Plus, knowing the operational impact makes you a better, more informed advisor, not just a number cruncher.

sections.title: Confusing Proceeds with Fair Value
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming proceeds are always the fair value of the asset received. Remember, proceeds are what the company *actually* receives, regardless of what the other asset is worth. For example, if a company exchanges an old machine (book value $10,000) for a new one and *also* pays $5,000 cash, the proceeds from the old machine are -$5,000 (cash paid out). Don't get tripped up by irrelevant fair value information provided in the problem; focus on the cash or other consideration changing hands!

sections.title: Sarah's Surprise Retirement Loss
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, recorded the retirement of a fully depreciated machine. She thought, 'No problem, it's fully depreciated, so no gain or loss!' However, the company incurred $2,000 in dismantling and removal costs. Sarah initially forgot to include these costs. Remember, even with a fully depreciated asset, costs associated with its retirement create a loss. Failing to account for these costs would understate expenses and overstate net income.

sections.title: Nonmonetary Exchanges: Audit Trail is Key
sections.content: In practice, nonmonetary exchanges require meticulous documentation. Auditors scrutinize these transactions for potential manipulation. For example, management might try to inflate the value of an asset received to avoid recognizing a loss. As an accountant, you need to maintain a clear audit trail, including appraisals, invoices, and any other evidence supporting the fair value assessment. This protects both you and the company from potential scrutiny and ensures accurate financial reporting.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Gain/Loss on disposal = Proceeds − Book Value
Always update depreciation to date of disposal first
Commercial substance: Cash flows change significantly → recognize gain/loss
No commercial substance: Defer gains (reduce new asset basis), recognize losses
Boot received without commercial substance → recognize proportionate gain
LOSSES are always recognized immediately regardless of commercial substance

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company disposed of equipment with an original cost of $500,000 and accumulated depreciation of $350,000. ABC received proceeds of $200,000 from the sale. What is the gain or loss on disposal?
options: Gain of $50,000
Loss of $50,000
Gain of $200,000
Loss of $200,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The book value of the equipment is $500,000 (Cost) - $350,000 (Accumulated Depreciation) = $150,000. Proceeds of $200,000 less Book Value of $150,000 = Gain of $50,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the book value first.

reveal.question: What journal entries are typically made when an asset is retired with no proceeds?
reveal.answer: Debit Accumulated Depreciation, Debit Loss on Disposal, Credit the Asset account.  The loss on disposal is equal to the remaining book value of the asset.

sections.title: Key Differences: Disposal vs. Involuntary Conversion

title: Debt Securities: HTM, AFS, Trading
description: Master the three classifications of debt security investments and their accounting

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Investment classification drives everything: measurement, income recognition, and balance sheet presentation. The exam heavily tests the differences between HTM, AFS, and Trading securities. One key: know where unrealized gains/losses go—P&L or OCI!

sections.title: Three Classifications
sections.content:
**Debt securities must be classified based on INTENT and ABILITY:**

**1. Held-to-Maturity (HTM)**
• Intent and ability to hold until maturity
• Only for DEBT securities (not equity)

**2. Trading**
• Held for short-term profit
• Active buying and selling expected

**3. Available-for-Sale (AFS)**
• Everything else—the default category
• Neither HTM nor Trading

sections.title: Held-to-Maturity (HTM)
sections.content:
**Requirements:**
• Positive intent to hold to maturity
• Ability to hold (no liquidity constraints)
• Only DEBT securities (bonds, notes)

**Accounting:**
• Carry at **amortized cost**
• Amortize premium/discount using effective interest method
• Unrealized gains/losses NOT recognized (market changes ignored)
• Test for credit impairment under CECL

**Why choose HTM?** Avoids earnings volatility from fair value changes.

sections.title: HTM Example
sections.content:
**Purchase $100,000 face value bond at $96,000:**
• Stated rate: 5%
• Market rate at purchase: 6%
• Term: 5 years

**Initial Entry:**
Dr Investment in HTM Debt         $96,000
    Cr Cash                             $96,000

**Year 1 Interest:**
Cash interest: $100,000 × 5% = $5,000
Interest income: $96,000 × 6% = $5,760
Discount amortization: $760

Dr Cash                            $5,000
Dr Investment in HTM Debt            $760
    Cr Interest Income                    $5,760

**New carrying value: $96,760**

sections.title: Trading Securities
sections.content:
**Characteristics:**
• Active and frequent buying/selling
• Profit from short-term price changes
• Typically held by financial institutions, broker-dealers

**Accounting:**
• Carry at **fair value**
• Unrealized gains/losses → **Net Income**
• Changes in fair value affect earnings immediately
• No premium/discount amortization needed (use fair value)

sections.title: Trading Securities Example
sections.content:
**Purchase trading security for $50,000:**

**At Purchase:**
Dr Trading Securities            $50,000
    Cr Cash                            $50,000

**Year-End (FV = $53,000):**
Dr Trading Securities             $3,000
    Cr Unrealized Gain (P&L)            $3,000

**Next Year (FV = $48,000):**
Dr Unrealized Loss (P&L)          $5,000
    Cr Trading Securities               $5,000

**Trading securities: FV changes hit the INCOME STATEMENT!**

sections.title: Available-for-Sale (AFS)
sections.content:
**The "default" classification:**
• Not held for trading
• Not intended to be held to maturity
• May be sold in response to liquidity needs, interest rate changes, etc.

**Accounting:**
• Carry at **fair value**
• Unrealized gains/losses → **OCI** (not P&L)
• Accumulates in **AOCI** on balance sheet
• Interest income: Effective interest method
• Reclassify to P&L when sold ("recycling")

sections.title: AFS Example
sections.content:
**Purchase AFS bond at $100,000; year-end FV = $105,000:**

**At Purchase:**
Dr Investment in AFS Debt        $100,000
    Cr Cash                           $100,000

**Year-End Adjustment:**
Dr Investment in AFS Debt          $5,000
    Cr OCI - Unrealized Gain            $5,000

**When Sold for $108,000:**
Dr Cash                          $108,000
Dr OCI - Unrealized Gain           $5,000
    Cr Investment in AFS Debt         $105,000
    Cr Gain on Sale (P&L)               $8,000

**Total gain hits P&L when SOLD, not before!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Where Do Unrealized G/L Go?
sections.content:
**"TIP" — Trading goes to Income (P&L)**

**T**rading → **I**ncome Statement (**P**&L)

**AFS → OCI** (bypasses income until sold)

**HTM → Nowhere** (stay at amortized cost)

Remember: Trading is most volatile to earnings!

sections.title: Premium and Discount Amortization
sections.content:
**Premium:** Paid more than face value (market rate < stated rate)
• Amortization REDUCES interest income

**Discount:** Paid less than face value (market rate > stated rate)
• Amortization INCREASES interest income

**Effective Interest Method:**
Interest Income = Carrying Value × Market Rate at Purchase
Cash Received = Face Value × Stated Rate
Amortization = Difference

**Required for HTM and AFS; optional for Trading (use FV anyway)**

sections.title: Impairment of Debt Securities
sections.content:
**HTM and AFS Debt Securities:**

Under ASC 326 (CECL), when FV < Amortized Cost:

1. **Determine credit loss portion:**
   Amortized cost − PV of expected cash flows = Credit loss

2. **Record credit loss:**
   Dr Credit Loss Expense
       Cr Allowance for Credit Losses

3. **Non-credit portion (AFS only):**
   Remaining decline recorded in OCI

**Trading securities:** Already at fair value through P&L, so no separate impairment analysis needed.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Reclassifications
sections.content:
**Transfers between categories are RARE and restricted:**

**From HTM:** Taints entire portfolio—questions intent for ALL HTM

**To HTM:** Generally prohibited unless very limited circumstances

**From Trading:** Very rare; generally prohibited

**Between AFS and Trading:** Possible but requires clear change in intent

**Key:** Exam may test consequences of inappropriate transfers!

sections.title: Balance Sheet Presentation
sections.content:
**Classification on Balance Sheet:**

**Current Assets:**
• Trading securities (always)
• HTM/AFS maturing within one year

**Non-current Assets:**
• HTM/AFS with maturity > one year

**Note:** Trading securities are current even if maturity is > 1 year (intent to sell)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: HTM, AFS, Trading: My Real-World Perspective
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. HTM, AFS, and Trading? It sounds like alphabet soup. But understanding these classifications is crucial. In practice, you'll be helping clients determine how to classify these investments, and the wrong classification can significantly impact their financial statements. I've seen companies try to sneak an AFS security into HTM to avoid marking it to market. Auditors, don't let them! Knowing the intent and ability is key. Trust me, nailing this now will save you headaches (and maybe a lawsuit) later.

sections.title: Confusing AFS with HTM: A Costly Error
sections.content: Students often confuse Available-for-Sale (AFS) with Held-to-Maturity (HTM) because both seem like long-term investments. The key difference lies in the 'intent and ability'. HTM *requires* a positive intent and ability to hold until maturity. AFS is the 'catch-all' category - if it's not Trading and doesn't meet the HTM criteria, it's AFS. Misclassifying a $1,000,000 bond from AFS to HTM means unrealized gains/losses bypass the income statement, directly hitting equity. The impact on ratios and investor perception can be massive. Get it right!

sections.title: Sarah's Trading Security Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a financial analyst at GreenTech Innovations, purchased $500,000 of corporate bonds. She initially intended to hold them for a few months to capitalize on interest rate fluctuations. However, after a sudden market downturn, the CFO suggested classifying them as AFS to avoid reporting a loss on the income statement. The learning point here is that initial *intent* is paramount. Even if market conditions change, the initial classification should reflect the initial purpose of the investment. Changing the classification now would violate accounting principles.

sections.title: Debt Securities in the Real World
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently encounter debt security classifications when auditing investment accounts or preparing financial statements for companies holding significant bond portfolios. For example, insurance companies often hold large amounts of HTM securities to match their long-term liabilities. Conversely, hedge funds will primarily use Trading securities to generate short-term profits. Understanding the business model and investment strategy of your client is crucial to properly audit or prepare the debt securities classifications. Failure to do so could result in a material misstatement.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: HTM: Amortized cost; unrealized G/L not recognized; only debt securities
Trading: Fair value; unrealized G/L in P&L (Net Income)
AFS: Fair value; unrealized G/L in OCI (bypasses P&L until sold)
Premium amortization reduces interest income; discount increases it
Effective interest method required for HTM and AFS
Impairment: Credit losses to P&L; non-credit portion to OCI (AFS)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An entity purchased debt securities with the intent to hold them until maturity. However, due to unforeseen liquidity constraints, the entity sold a significant portion of these securities before their maturity date. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate classification for the remaining debt securities?
options: Held-to-Maturity (HTM)
Trading
Available-for-Sale (AFS)
The entity should reclassify all remaining debt securities as Trading, regardless of future intent.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While the original intent was HTM, the demonstrated inability to hold the securities due to liquidity constraints disqualifies the HTM classification. Trading requires active buying and selling for short-term profit, which isn't stated. The remaining securities should be classified as AFS, as this is the default classification when HTM criteria are not met, and Trading is not appropriate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the impact of the entity's actions on their original intent and ability.

reveal.question: How are unrealized gains and losses on Held-to-Maturity (HTM) debt securities treated?
reveal.answer: Unrealized gains and losses on Held-to-Maturity (HTM) debt securities are NOT recognized in the financial statements. HTM securities are carried at amortized cost, meaning market fluctuations are not reflected until the security is sold or matures.

title: Equity Securities: Fair Value Measurement
description: Account for stock investments using fair value method under ASC 321

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Under current GAAP (ASC 321), most equity securities are measured at fair value with changes flowing through Net Income. This is a significant change from the old AFS treatment for equities. Understanding when fair value applies and the exceptions is critical for exam success.

sections.title: Scope: What Are Equity Securities?
sections.content:
**Equity securities include:**

• Common stock
• Preferred stock (non-redeemable)
• Stock warrants and rights
• Call/put options on equity securities

**NOT equity securities:**
• Investments accounted for under equity method
• Investments in consolidated subsidiaries
• Investments in partnerships, LLCs (generally)

sections.title: Default Treatment: Fair Value through Net Income
sections.content:
**Under ASC 321, equity securities are:**

• Measured at **fair value**
• Changes in fair value → **Net Income** (P&L)
• **No OCI option** for equity investments

**Key change from old GAAP:**
Pre-2018, equities could be classified as AFS with unrealized G/L in OCI.
Now ALL fair value changes hit earnings immediately!

sections.title: Fair Value Through Net Income Example
sections.content:
**Purchase 5,000 shares (2% ownership) at $40/share:**

**At Purchase:**
Dr Equity Investment           $200,000
    Cr Cash                          $200,000

**Year-End (FV = $45/share):**
Dr Equity Investment            $25,000
    Cr Unrealized Gain (P&L)          $25,000

**Next Year (FV = $38/share):**
Dr Unrealized Loss (P&L)        $35,000
    Cr Equity Investment              $35,000

**Dividends received ($1/share):**
Dr Cash                          $5,000
    Cr Dividend Income                 $5,000

sections.title: Exception: Practicability Exception
sections.content:
**For equity securities WITHOUT readily determinable fair value:**

**May elect to measure at:**
Cost minus impairment, plus or minus changes from observable price changes

**Qualifications:**
• No readily determinable fair value
• Not required to be measured at fair value (e.g., not a derivative)
• Election is irrevocable

**Common for:** Private company stock, limited partnership interests

sections.title: Practicability Exception Measurement
sections.content:
**Initial measurement:** Cost (acquisition price)

**Subsequent measurement adjustments:**

1. **Impairment** — If qualitative indicators exist
   • Write down to fair value if impaired

2. **Observable price changes** — If same/similar securities sold
   • Adjust to observable transaction price
   • Gain or loss to Net Income

**Annual reassessment:** Review if FV becomes readily determinable

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Equity Investment Accounting
sections.content:
**"FV to NI for Equity"**

**F**air **V**alue changes go to **N**et **I**ncome

**No OCI for Equities!**

Unlike AFS debt securities (which use OCI), equity securities send ALL fair value changes straight to the income statement.

This creates more earnings volatility for equity investors.

sections.title: Dividend Income Recognition
sections.content:
**When to recognize dividends:**

• Recognize when **right to receive** is established
• Usually the **ex-dividend date**
• Record as **Dividend Income** (not investment adjustment)

**Liquidating dividends:**
• Reduce investment account (return of capital)
• Not income
• Recognize when total dividends exceed share of earnings since acquisition

sections.title: Equity Securities Received as Dividends
sections.content:
**Stock dividends received:**

**From investee:**
• Allocate existing cost over more shares
• No income recognized
• Lower cost per share

**Example:**
• Own 100 shares at $50 = $5,000 investment
• Receive 10% stock dividend (10 shares)
• New: 110 shares at $45.45/share = $5,000 total

**Record the new shares at fair value only if measuring at FV through NI**

sections.title: Stock Splits
sections.content:
**Treatment similar to stock dividends:**

**2-for-1 split:**
• Own 100 shares at $50 = $5,000
• After split: 200 shares at $25 = $5,000
• No accounting entry needed (just memo update)

**If measuring at fair value:**
• Adjust shares count
• Fair value reflects post-split market price
• Generally no immediate P&L impact from split itself

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Fair Value vs. Equity Method
sections.content:
**Fair Value Method (< 20% typically):**
• FV changes → Net Income
• Dividends → Income
• No amortization of purchase premium

**Equity Method (20-50% typically):**
• Share of net income → Income
• Dividends → Reduce investment (not income!)
• Amortize FV adjustments

**The dividend treatment is OPPOSITE!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Fair Value Fun: My P&L Rollercoaster
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Fair value accounting for equity securities can feel like a wild ride. You buy stock, it goes up, you're happy, P&L gets a boost. It goes down, you cry a little, P&L takes a hit. It's all about that immediate impact on net income. Forget OCI here, folks! Think of it as your personal stock ticker impacting your firm's bottom line. Embrace the volatility – it's job security, right? Just kidding (mostly). But seriously, nail this down, you'll see it everywhere.

sections.title: OCI Confusion: No OCI Allowed!
sections.content: Students often try to apply the Available-for-Sale (AFS) securities rules from the old GAAP, incorrectly thinking they can elect to report changes in fair value in OCI. Under ASC 321, there is NO option to report changes in fair value of equity securities in OCI. It ALL goes to net income. The key difference is that ASC 321 drastically simplifies the accounting, eliminating the AFS category. If you see a question mentioning OCI related to fair value changes of equity securities, flag it immediately as a trap!

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Surprise
sections.content: Sarah, an analyst at Global Investments, purchased $50,000 of TechCo stock. At year-end, the fair value is $65,000. Sarah's initial reaction was to debit Investment and credit Unrealized Gain (OCI), thinking about debt securities. She quickly realized her mistake: because this is an EQUITY security under ASC 321, the $15,000 gain MUST flow through net income, not OCI. Always double-check the security type before posting entries!

sections.title: Fair Value: The Daily Grind
sections.content: In a typical month, you'll encounter this when preparing financial statements for clients with investment portfolios. Many companies hold small investments in other companies' stock. These investments, if they qualify as equity securities under ASC 321 (meaning they don't give the investor significant influence), must be adjusted to fair value at each reporting period. That’s why mastering this now saves time later when you're working on those 10-Ks and 10-Qs, ensuring accurate and compliant financial reporting.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Equity securities measured at fair value with changes through Net Income
NO OCI option for equity investments (unlike AFS debt)
Dividends recorded as income when right to receive is established
Practicability exception: Cost minus impairment for equities without determinable FV
Stock dividends/splits: Allocate cost over new shares, no income recognized
Fair value method dividends = income; Equity method dividends = reduce investment

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Fair Value Measurement of Equity Securities - Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company acquired shares of XYZ Company, a publicly traded entity. ABC also holds warrants to purchase additional shares of XYZ. How should ABC Company account for these investments under ASC 321?
options: Both the shares and warrants should be measured at fair value, with changes in fair value recognized in OCI.
The shares should be measured at fair value, with changes in fair value recognized in net income, and the warrants should be measured at cost minus impairment.

The shares should be measured at fair value, with changes in fair value recognized in net income, and the warrants should also be measured at fair value, with changes in fair value recognized in net income.
The shares should be accounted for under the equity method, and the warrants should be expensed as incurred.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under ASC 321, both equity securities (like the shares of XYZ) and stock warrants are measured at fair value, with changes in fair value recognized in net income. The equity method is not applicable here, and cost minus impairment is only used when fair value is not readily determinable and the practicability exception is elected.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the default treatment and what types of financial instruments are considered equity securities.

reveal.question: When can an entity elect to use the practicability exception for measuring equity securities?
reveal.answer: An entity can elect to measure equity securities at cost minus impairment, plus or minus changes from observable price changes, when the security does NOT have a readily determinable fair value, it is NOT required to be measured at fair value (e.g., it is not a derivative), and the entity chooses to apply the exception.

rows.label: Measurement
values: Original Cost + Share of Investee's Earnings - Dividends Received

rows.label: Income Statement Impact
values: Changes in fair value recognized in Net Income
Share of investee's earnings recognized in Investment Income

rows.label: Balance Sheet Impact
values: Investment in Investee reported at adjusted cost

title: Equity Method Investments
description: Apply the equity method for investments with significant influence (20-50%)

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The equity method is called 'one-line consolidation' because your investment account reflects your share of the investee's net assets. This is heavily tested—you must understand the income/dividend entries, purchase premium amortization, and intercompany elimination. Getting the details right is essential!

sections.title: When to Apply the Equity Method
sections.content:
**Presumption of significant influence:**

**20-50% ownership of voting stock**

**Indicators of significant influence:**
• Representation on board of directors
• Participation in policy-making processes
• Material intercompany transactions
• Interchange of managerial personnel
• Technological dependency

**Can be rebutted:** If evidence shows no actual influence despite ownership %

sections.title: Basic Equity Method Entries
sections.content:
**Initial purchase:**
Dr Investment in Affiliate    $XXX
    Cr Cash                         $XXX

**Recognize share of investee income:**
Dr Investment in Affiliate    $XXX
    Cr Equity in Earnings           $XXX

**Receive dividends:**
Dr Cash                        $XXX
    Cr Investment in Affiliate      $XXX

**Key:** Dividends REDUCE investment (return of capital, not income!)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Investment Account T-Account
sections.content:
**Investment Account:**

**DEBITS (Increases):**
• Initial purchase
• Share of investee income
• Share of investee OCI

**CREDITS (Decreases):**
• Dividends received
• Share of investee loss
• Amortization of FV adjustments
• Impairment

**Balance = Your share of investee's net assets (adjusted)**

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**Jan 1: Acquire 40% of XYZ for $500,000**
XYZ's book value: $1,000,000 (Your share: $400,000)
Excess paid: $100,000 (attributed to building, 10-year life)

**Dec 31: XYZ reports net income of $200,000:**
Dr Investment in XYZ           $80,000
    Cr Equity in Earnings            $80,000
(40% × $200,000)

**Amortize building excess:**
Dr Equity in Earnings          $10,000
    Cr Investment in XYZ             $10,000
($100,000 ÷ 10 years)

**XYZ pays dividends of $50,000:**
Dr Cash                        $20,000
    Cr Investment in XYZ             $20,000
(40% × $50,000)

sections.title: Purchase Price Allocation
sections.content:
**When purchase price > share of book value:**

1. **Allocate excess to identifiable assets/liabilities**
   • Inventory → Expense when sold (usually Year 1)
   • Fixed assets → Amortize over remaining life
   • Intangibles → Amortize over useful life

2. **Remaining excess = Goodwill**
   • NOT amortized
   • Test for impairment annually

**These adjustments REDUCE equity in earnings!**

sections.title: Intercompany Transactions
sections.content:
**Must eliminate unrealized intercompany profits!**

**Downstream (Investor → Investee):**
• Investor sold goods to investee
• Eliminate **100%** of unrealized profit

**Upstream (Investee → Investor):**
• Investee sold goods to investor
• Eliminate **investor's % share** of unrealized profit

**Realized when:**
• Inventory sold to third party
• Fixed asset fully depreciated

sections.title: Intercompany Example
sections.content:
**40% ownership; intercompany sale:**

**Downstream (Investor sells to Investee):**
• Investor sold inventory for $100,000 (cost $70,000)
• Gross profit: $30,000
• 25% remains in investee's inventory at year-end
• Unrealized profit: $30,000 × 25% = $7,500

**Eliminate 100%:**
Dr Equity in Earnings          $7,500
    Cr Investment in XYZ             $7,500

**If Upstream (Investee sells to Investor):**
• Same facts, but only eliminate 40% (investor's share)
• $7,500 × 40% = $3,000 elimination

sections.title: Share of Other Comprehensive Income
sections.content:
**Record investor's share of investee's OCI:**

**If investee has OCI item:**
Dr Investment in Affiliate    $XXX
    Cr OCI (investor's share)       $XXX

**Example:** Investee has $10,000 unrealized gain on AFS securities
Investor's share (40%): $4,000

Dr Investment                  $4,000
    Cr OCI - Unrealized Gain        $4,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Investment Goes Negative
sections.content:
**What if losses exceed investment balance?**

**General rule:** Stop recognizing losses at $0
• Don't let investment go negative

**Exceptions — continue recognizing if:**
• Investor has guaranteed obligations
• Investor committed to provide financial support
• Imminent return to profitability

**Resume equity method:** Only after unrecognized losses are absorbed by share of subsequent income

sections.title: Loss of Significant Influence
sections.content:
**When ownership falls below 20% (or influence lost):**

1. **Stop equity method** from date influence lost
2. **Measure retained investment** at fair value
3. **Recognize gain/loss** on remeasurement
4. **Apply fair value method** prospectively

**No retroactive restatement!**

sections.title: Acquisition of Significant Influence
sections.content:
**When ownership crosses 20% threshold:**

1. **Remeasure prior investment** to fair value at date
2. **Recognize gain/loss** on remeasurement
3. **Combine with new purchase** for total investment
4. **Apply equity method** from date of significant influence

**Note:** Some view this as a step acquisition requiring retroactive application
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Equity Method: It's All About Control (Sort Of)
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk equity method. You're not buying the whole company, just a big chunk – enough to have some serious sway. Think of it like this: you're not just a shareholder, you're like the cool older cousin who gives 'advice' (that everyone secretly has to follow). That 20-50% range? It's a guideline, not gospel. If you're sitting on the board, whispering sweet nothings into the CEO's ear, you're probably exerting significant influence, even if your ownership is slightly below 20%. Don't get hung up on the numbers alone; look at the *impact*.

sections.title: Confusing Cost vs. Equity: A Costly Error!
sections.content: Students often get tripped up differentiating between the cost method and the equity method. The biggest mistake? Using the cost method when significant influence exists! Remember, the cost method is for *passive* investments (think less than 20% ownership, no board seat, no input). Under the cost method, dividends are income. Under the equity method, dividends are a *reduction* in the investment account. Confuse these, and you'll be adding income when you should be reducing the asset, and vice versa. Read the scenario carefully! Is there significant influence? If so, equity method it is!

sections.title: Sarah's Slippery Slope: Intercompany Sales
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant at ParentCo, noticed a large volume of sales to AffiliateCo, where ParentCo owns 30%. The sales are at a significant markup. Sarah is concerned about the impact of these intercompany sales on the equity method calculation. The learning point: unrealized profits from these intercompany transactions *must* be eliminated when applying the equity method. Failure to do so overstates both the investment in affiliate and consolidated net income. Proper elimination is essential for accurate financial reporting.

sections.title: Equity Method: Due Diligence & Beyond
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter the equity method frequently when dealing with consolidated financial statements, especially in industries with strategic alliances or joint ventures. During due diligence for acquisitions, understanding the equity method is crucial for properly valuing the target company's investments. Furthermore, you'll need to monitor the investee's performance and adjust the investment account accordingly each reporting period. Mastering the equity method now will save you headaches later when analyzing complex financial statements and making informed investment decisions. Think of it as building a solid foundation for your future career.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Equity method = 'one-line consolidation' for 20-50% ownership
Share of income increases investment; dividends decrease investment
Purchase premium: Allocate to identifiable assets → amortize; residual = goodwill
Downstream intercompany: Eliminate 100% unrealized profit
Upstream intercompany: Eliminate investor's % share of unrealized profit
Stop at zero unless guarantees/commitments exist

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Parker Corp. owns 30% of the voting stock of Stockton Co. Parker also has a seat on Stockton's board of directors and actively participates in Stockton's policy-making processes. During the year, Parker sold goods to Stockton at a significant profit. Which of the following best describes the accounting treatment for Parker's investment in Stockton?
options: The investment should be accounted for using the equity method.
The investment should be accounted for as a trading security at fair value.
The investment should be accounted for as an available-for-sale security at fair value.
The investment should be accounted for using the cost method.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The equity method is appropriate because Parker owns between 20% and 50% of Stockton's voting stock and exhibits indicators of significant influence, including board representation, participation in policy-making, and material intercompany transactions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the ownership percentage and indicators of significant influence.

sections.title: Equity Method Journal Entries - Test Your Recall

reveal.question: What is the journal entry to record dividends received from an investee under the equity method?
reveal.answer:
Dr. Cash
Cr. Investment in Affiliate

Dividends received reduce the investor's carrying value of the investment.

knowledgeCheck.question: Salinger Inc. owns 40% of the outstanding common stock of Caulfield Co. During the year, Caulfield Co. reported net income of $500,000. Salinger's share of Caulfield's net income will result in which of the following?
options: An increase in Salinger's Investment in Caulfield account and an increase in Salinger's Equity in Earnings account.
A decrease in Salinger's Investment in Caulfield account and an increase in Salinger's Equity in Earnings account.
An increase in Salinger's Investment in Caulfield account and an increase in Salinger's Retained Earnings account.
No impact on Salinger's financial statements.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under the equity method, the investor recognizes its share of the investee's net income by increasing the Investment in Affiliate account (an asset) and crediting Equity in Earnings (an income statement account).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how the equity method reflects the investor's share of the investee's earnings.

title: Fair Value Hierarchy: Levels 1, 2, 3
description: Apply the three-level fair value hierarchy under ASC 820

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Fair value measurement is used throughout GAAP—investments, impairments, acquisitions, derivatives, and more. ASC 820 establishes a single framework for measuring fair value. The three-level hierarchy tells you which inputs are most reliable. This is foundational knowledge you'll apply across many topics!

sections.title: What is Fair Value?
sections.content:
**ASC 820 Definition:**

"The price that would be **received to sell** an asset or **paid to transfer** a liability in an **orderly transaction** between **market participants** at the **measurement date**."

**Key elements:**
• **Exit price** (not entry price)
• **Orderly** (not forced/distressed)
• **Market participants** (hypothetical buyers/sellers)
• **Measurement date** (current conditions)

sections.title: The Fair Value Hierarchy
sections.content:
**Three levels based on INPUT observability:**

**Level 1:** Quoted prices in ACTIVE markets for IDENTICAL assets/liabilities (most reliable)

**Level 2:** Observable inputs OTHER than Level 1 quotes (similar assets, market data)

**Level 3:** UNOBSERVABLE inputs based on entity's own assumptions (least reliable)

**Priority:** Use highest level available! Level 1 > Level 2 > Level 3

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 1-2-3 Hierarchy
sections.content:
**"Same, Similar, Self"**

**Level 1:** **Same** asset, active market, quoted price
(e.g., NYSE stock quote)

**Level 2:** **Similar** assets or observable market data
(e.g., bond priced using yield curves)

**Level 3:** **Self** — entity's own estimates
(e.g., private company valuation using DCF)

Think: Reliability decreases 1 → 2 → 3

content.definition: Quoted prices in ACTIVE markets for IDENTICAL assets or liabilities
Directly observable, unadjusted, no judgment needed
Closing price on NYSE for shares of Apple
CME exchange price for gold futures
Sufficient frequency and volume of transactions
Generally no adjustments (would move to Level 2)
Observable inputs OTHER than Level 1 quoted prices
Quoted prices for SIMILAR (not identical) assets in active markets
Quoted prices for identical assets in LESS active markets
Interest rates, yield curves, volatilities, credit spreads
Corporate bond valued using Treasury yield curve + credit spread
Option priced using observable volatility inputs
UNOBSERVABLE inputs reflecting entity's own assumptions
Use only when observable inputs are not available
DCF, Monte Carlo, other valuation models using internal data
Valuing startup stock using projected cash flows
Customer relationship valued using internal projections
Enhanced disclosure requirements for Level 3 measurements

sections.title: Determining Overall Level
sections.content:
**Classify based on LOWEST level of SIGNIFICANT input:**

**Example 1:**
All Level 1 inputs → **Level 1** measurement

**Example 2:**
Level 1 + Level 2 inputs → **Level 2** measurement

**Example 3:**
Level 1 + Level 2 + significant Level 3 input → **Level 3** measurement

**"Significant" requires judgment!**

sections.title: Principal Market
sections.content:
**Where to measure fair value?**

**Principal Market:** Market with greatest volume and level of activity
• Use this market if it exists
• Even if price in another market is better

**Most Advantageous Market:** Market that maximizes proceeds (or minimizes payment)
• Use only if no principal market

**Assumption:** Entity has ACCESS to the market

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Transaction Costs
sections.content:
**Transaction costs are NOT part of fair value!**

Fair value measures the asset/liability itself, not the transaction.

**However:**
• Transaction costs DO matter for determining most advantageous market
• **Transportation costs** ARE included if location is an asset characteristic

**Example:** Oil in Oklahoma vs. Gulf Coast—location affects fair value

sections.title: Fair Value Disclosures
sections.content:
**Required disclosures by level:**

**All levels:**
• Fair value measurements by class of asset/liability
• Level classification

**Level 3 specifically:**
• Valuation techniques and inputs used
• Reconciliation of beginning to ending balances
• Sensitivity analysis (reasonably possible changes)
• Transfers in/out of Level 3

**Recurring vs. Nonrecurring:** Different requirements
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Fair Value: Not as Fairytale as it Sounds!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Fair value can feel like chasing unicorns sometimes. ASC 820 wants an 'exit price' in an 'orderly transaction.' Think 'eBay on a good day,' not 'fire sale because the CFO just got arrested.' Level 1? Easy, like looking up Apple's stock price. Levels 2 and 3? Buckle up. You're diving into models and assumptions that can make your head spin. Just remember, it's about what a willing buyer would pay, not what *you* think it's worth. Don't get emotionally attached to your valuations!

sections.title: Confusing Input Observability: The Level Trap
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking that if ANY observable input is used, it automatically becomes Level 2. Not so fast! Level 1 *only* uses quoted prices in active markets for *identical* assets. If you're adjusting that Level 1 price even a tiny bit, or using prices for similar (but not identical) assets, you're in Level 2 territory. Students also often jump to Level 3 too quickly. Exhaust Level 2 options first! Just because it's complex doesn't mean observable inputs don't exist. Don't overcomplicate things!

sections.title: Jane's Tangled Web of Level 3 Assets
sections.content: Jane, a valuation specialist, is valuing a private company's patent. No active market exists, and comparable transactions are scarce. She uses a discounted cash flow model, relying heavily on management's projections for future revenue and market share. Because Jane's model relies significantly on unobservable inputs from management, the patent's fair value would be classified as Level 3. The key takeaway is that the *significance* of unobservable inputs drives the classification, not just their presence.

sections.title: Fair Value in Your Audit Life: Goodwill and Beyond
sections.content: You'll encounter the fair value hierarchy constantly in practice, especially during purchase price allocations in M&A and impairment testing of goodwill. For example, when auditing a company's goodwill impairment test, you'll scrutinize the valuation models used. Are Level 1 inputs used where possible? Are Level 3 inputs reasonable and well-supported? A poorly documented Level 3 valuation is a red flag that could indicate aggressive accounting. Mastering the hierarchy now saves you hours of audit work and potential headaches later.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Fair value = exit price in orderly transaction at measurement date
Level 1: Quoted prices for identical assets in active markets (most reliable)
Level 2: Observable inputs other than Level 1 (similar assets, market data)
Level 3: Unobservable entity-developed inputs (least reliable)
Overall level = lowest level of significant input
Transaction costs excluded; transportation costs may be included

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following inputs would be considered a Level 2 input when determining fair value?
options: Quoted prices in active markets for identical assets.
Prices of similar assets in active markets.
Unobservable inputs based on the entity's own assumptions.
Historical cost less accumulated depreciation.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Level 2 inputs are observable inputs other than quoted prices in active markets for identical assets (Level 1). Prices of similar assets in active markets fall under this category. Level 3 inputs are unobservable.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what 'observable' means in the context of the fair value hierarchy.

reveal.question: Complete the definition: Fair Value is the price that would be received to ______ an asset or paid to ______ a liability in an ______ transaction between ______ at the ______.
reveal.answer: Fair Value is the price that would be received to *sell* an asset or paid to *transfer* a liability in an *orderly* transaction between *market participants* at the *measurement date*.

rows.label: Source
values: Active market quotes for identical assets

title: Intangible Assets: R&D & Software Costs
description: Account for research & development costs and software development under GAAP

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: R&D is the lifeblood of innovation, but GAAP takes a conservative approach—expense most of it! The exception is software development costs, which have specific capitalization rules. Understanding these rules helps you avoid common exam traps about what to capitalize vs. expense.

sections.title: Research & Development: Overview
sections.content:
**General Rule: EXPENSE ALL R&D AS INCURRED!**

**Why?** Future benefits are too uncertain.

**Research:** Planned search for new knowledge
**Development:** Application of research findings to create new or improved products/processes

**Combined into "R&D expense" on income statement**

content.definition: Searching for new knowledge - EXPENSE
Developing alternatives - EXPENSE
Pre-production samples - EXPENSE
Evaluating product/process options - EXPENSE
Before commercial production - EXPENSE
For significantly improved version - EXPENSE
During production - NOT R&D, capitalize as overhead
Minor changes to existing products - NOT R&D
Test marketing - NOT R&D (selling expense)
Capitalize as intangible asset if patent obtained
After product ready - capitalize as manufacturing
Fashion, style changes - NOT R&D

sections.title: R&D Materials & Equipment
sections.content:
**Equipment and facilities used in R&D:**

**HAS alternative future use:**
• Capitalize as PP&E
• Depreciate normally
• Allocate to R&D expense as used

**NO alternative future use:**
• EXPENSE immediately as R&D

**Materials & Supplies:**
• Expense as consumed in R&D

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: R&D Rule
sections.content:
**"If it's R&D, Expense it!"**

**Exceptions (NOT expensed as R&D):**
• Equipment with OTHER uses → Capitalize as PP&E
• Contract R&D for others → Cost of revenue (not R&D expense)
• Software after technological feasibility → Capitalize

**The default is EXPENSE. Exceptions are narrow!**

sections.title: Software Development: For External Sale
sections.content:
**ASC 985-20: Software for sale/lease to customers**

**BEFORE Technological Feasibility:**
• EXPENSE as R&D

**AFTER Technological Feasibility:**
• CAPITALIZE

**Technological feasibility established when:**
• Detailed program design completed, OR
• Working model completed and tested

**Capitalization ends when:** Product available for general release

sections.title: Software Capitalization Diagram
sections.content:
**Timeline:**

[Start] ──────── [Tech Feasibility] ──────── [General Release]
      ↓                  ↓                         ↓
   EXPENSE            CAPITALIZE               AMORTIZE
   as R&D             costs incurred           the capitalized
                      (coding, testing)        software costs

sections.title: Amortization of Capitalized Software
sections.content:
**Amortize capitalized software costs:**

**Use GREATER of:**
1. **Straight-line** over remaining estimated life
2. **Revenue ratio:** Current revenue ÷ Total expected revenue × Capitalized cost

**Report at LOWER of:**
• Unamortized cost, OR
• Net realizable value (if impaired)

**Annual evaluation required!**

sections.title: Software for Internal Use
sections.content:
**ASC 350-40: Software developed for internal use**

**Three stages:**

**1. Preliminary Stage:** EXPENSE
• Conceptual formulation, evaluating alternatives

**2. Application Development Stage:** CAPITALIZE
• Design, coding, installation, testing
• Configuration of purchased software

**3. Post-Implementation Stage:** EXPENSE
• Training, maintenance, data conversion

sections.title: Internal Use Software Example
sections.content:
**Company develops custom ERP system:**

**Preliminary Stage (EXPENSE):**
• Consultant fees for evaluation: $50,000
• Feasibility study: $30,000
Total expensed: $80,000

**Application Development (CAPITALIZE):**
• Programmer salaries: $200,000
• Software design: $100,000
• Testing: $50,000
Total capitalized: $350,000

**Post-Implementation (EXPENSE):**
• User training: $25,000
• Data migration: $40,000
Total expensed: $65,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Software Cost Distinctions
sections.content:
**For SALE (ASC 985-20):**
• Capitalize AFTER technological feasibility
• Tech feasibility = detailed design or working model

**For INTERNAL USE (ASC 350-40):**
• Capitalize DURING application development stage
• Three-stage model

**Key difference:** "Technological feasibility" vs. "Application development stage" are different concepts!

sections.title: Cloud Computing Arrangements
sections.content:
**Hosting arrangements (ASC 350-40):**

**If software license exists:**
• Capitalize implementation costs like internal-use software

**If no software license (service contract):**
• Expense implementation costs as incurred, OR
• Capitalize certain costs similar to internal-use software rules
• Amortize over hosting contract term

**This is an evolving area—check for updates!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: R&D: Expense Now, Party Later (Maybe)
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. R&D accounting feels like a buzzkill. You're pouring money into potential breakthroughs, but GAAP is like, 'Nah, expense it!' It's because predicting the future success of R&D is about as accurate as predicting my golf score. Think of it this way: you're throwing darts at a board. Some darts hit the bullseye (patent!), most don't. So, expense it now, and if you actually get a patent that generates revenue, then we can talk about amortization. Until then, R&D = R.I.P. (to your net income… temporarily!).

sections.title: Confusing Equipment Used in R&D
sections.content: A frequent error is immediately expensing all equipment purchased for R&D. Remember the 'alternative future use' rule! If the equipment can be used for other projects after the R&D, capitalize it and depreciate it over its useful life. Only the depreciation expense is allocated to R&D. However, if the equipment is *specifically* designed and *only* useful for that R&D project, then, and only then, do you expense the entire cost immediately. Don't throw away perfectly good assets just because they're temporarily in a lab!

sections.title: Sarah's Software Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a new staff accountant at TechStart Inc., is unsure how to treat the $50,000 cost of a specialized server purchased solely to develop new software. It can't be used for anything else. Even though it feels like PP&E, because it has NO alternative future use, the entire $50,000 should be expensed immediately as R&D. This is a classic example of a cost directly tied to a specific R&D activity with no lasting benefit beyond that activity.

sections.title: R&D in the Real World: Audit Insights
sections.content: During audits, I've seen companies try to sneak costs into PP&E that are really R&D. Auditors will scrutinize supporting documentation for equipment purchases, looking for evidence of alternative future use. In a typical biotech or tech audit, you'll spend a significant amount of time reviewing R&D expense schedules, ensuring costs are properly classified and documented. Understanding this topic thoroughly will make you a valuable asset during any audit engagement involving companies with significant R&D activities.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: R&D: Expense as incurred (very few exceptions)
R&D equipment with other uses: Capitalize and depreciate
Software for sale: Capitalize AFTER technological feasibility
Software for internal use: Capitalize DURING application development stage
Amortize capitalized software: Greater of straight-line or revenue ratio
Preliminary/post-implementation costs: Always expense

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During 2024, Gamma Corporation incurred the following costs: laboratory research aimed at discovering new knowledge, $300,000; quality control during commercial production, $150,000; and routine ongoing efforts to refine existing product characteristics, $75,000. What amount should Gamma Corporation report as research and development expense in its 2024 income statement?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: According to U.S. GAAP, research and development costs are generally expensed as incurred. Only the laboratory research costs ($300,000) qualify as R&D. Quality control and routine product refinement are not considered R&D.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the definition of R&D: Planned search for new knowledge, and application of research findings.

reveal.question: What is the general rule for accounting for Research and Development (R&D) costs under U.S. GAAP?
reveal.answer: The general rule is to expense all Research and Development (R&D) costs as incurred. This is because the future benefits from R&D activities are considered too uncertain to capitalize as an asset.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Discovering new knowledge or applying research findings

rows.label: Examples
values: Quality control, Routine product design, Market research

rows.label: Accounting Treatment
values: Expensed or capitalized depending on the specific activity

title: Acquired Intangibles & Amortization
description: Account for purchased intangible assets and determine amortization periods

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Unlike internally developed intangibles (usually expensed), PURCHASED intangibles are capitalized at cost. Understanding which intangibles have definite vs. indefinite lives determines whether you amortize or test for impairment. This distinction is heavily tested!

sections.title: Acquired vs. Internally Developed
sections.content:
**The fundamental distinction:**

**PURCHASED (Acquired) Intangibles:**
• Capitalize at fair value (business combination) or cost (direct purchase)
• Meets recognition criteria—third party valued it!

**INTERNALLY DEVELOPED Intangibles:**
• Generally EXPENSE as incurred
• Too uncertain to capitalize (R&D, advertising, brands)

**Exception:** Software development after technological feasibility

sections.title: Recognition in Business Combinations
sections.content:
**In an acquisition (ASC 805):**

Identifiable intangibles recognized separately from goodwill if:

**1. Contractual-legal criterion:**
• Arises from contractual or legal rights
• Examples: Patents, licenses, franchise agreements

**OR**

**2. Separability criterion:**
• Can be separated and sold, transferred, licensed
• Examples: Customer lists, trade names

sections.title: Common Acquired Intangibles
rows: Shorter of legal (20 yrs) or economic life
Creator life + 70 years (usually economic life shorter)

sections.title: Definite vs. Indefinite Life
sections.content:
**Definite Life:**
• Finite useful life
• AMORTIZE over useful life
• Test for impairment when indicators present

**Indefinite Life:**
• No foreseeable limit on cash-generating period
• Do NOT amortize
• Test for impairment at least ANNUALLY

**Reassess each period:** Indefinite → Definite requires prospective amortization

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Definite or Indefinite?
sections.content:
**"Does it expire?"**

**DEFINITE (expires):**
• Patents → 20 years
• Non-competes → Agreement term
• Customer relationships → Estimated period

**INDEFINITE (renewable/perpetual):**
• Trademarks → Renewable indefinitely
• Broadcast licenses → Renewable by law
• Goodwill → No expiration

**Legal life ≠ Useful life.** Use shorter!

sections.title: Amortization Methods
sections.content:
**Method should reflect pattern of benefit consumption:**

**Straight-line:** Most common; use when pattern uncertain

**Accelerated:** When benefits decrease over time (rare for intangibles)

**Revenue-based:** May be used if reliably measurable (e.g., some licenses)

**Residual value:** Usually zero for intangibles (no resale market)

**Period:** Shorter of legal life or estimated useful life

sections.title: Amortization Example
sections.content:
