**Acquire patent for $500,000:**
• Legal life remaining: 15 years
• Estimated useful life: 10 years
• Use shorter: 10 years

**Annual amortization (straight-line):**
$500,000 ÷ 10 = $50,000/year

**Entry:**
Dr Amortization Expense         $50,000
    Cr Accumulated Amortization       $50,000

**Or, some companies credit the intangible directly:**
Dr Amortization Expense         $50,000
    Cr Patent                          $50,000

sections.title: Legal Costs for Intangibles
sections.content:
**Costs to obtain/defend intangibles:**

**Registration/legal costs to OBTAIN:**
• CAPITALIZE as part of intangible cost
• Patent filing fees, trademark registration

**Legal costs to DEFEND (successful):**
• CAPITALIZE if intangible successfully defended
• Adds to carrying value; adjust amortization

**Legal costs to DEFEND (unsuccessful):**
• EXPENSE—intangible may be impaired too!

sections.title: Changes in Useful Life
sections.content:
**If estimated useful life changes:**

**Prospective treatment:**
• Revise remaining amortization period
• No restatement of prior periods
• Use current carrying value over new remaining life

**Example:**
Patent with $400,000 remaining value; originally 8 years remaining
Revised estimate: 5 years remaining
New annual amortization: $400,000 ÷ 5 = $80,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Renewal Costs
sections.content:
**Costs to RENEW an intangible:**

**If renewal is expected (indefinite life):**
• Expense renewal costs as incurred
• Intangible continues at existing carrying value

**If renewal extends useful life:**
• May capitalize if it genuinely extends benefits
• Rare—usually expense

**Trademark renewal fees:** Typically expensed because life was already indefinite

sections.title: Impairment of Definite-Life Intangibles
sections.content:
**Same two-step test as long-lived assets:**

**Step 1: Recoverability Test**
Carrying value > Undiscounted future cash flows?
• If yes → Asset is impaired, go to Step 2
• If no → No impairment

**Step 2: Measurement**
Impairment loss = Carrying value − Fair value

**Test when indicators present (not annually)**

sections.title: Impairment of Indefinite-Life Intangibles
sections.content:
**Different test (no recoverability step):**

**Compare fair value to carrying value:**
• FV < CV → Impairment loss = CV − FV
• FV ≥ CV → No impairment

**Test at least ANNUALLY (can test more frequently)**

**Optional qualitative assessment first:**
• Is it more likely than not that FV < CV?
• If no → Skip quantitative test
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Intangibles: Buy 'em, Don't DIY!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Intangibles can be a headache. But remember this golden rule: if you *bought* it (patent, customer list, whatever), you capitalize it! Someone else already valued it. Trying to capitalize internally developed intangibles? That's a recipe for disaster. The auditors will laugh you out of the room. Think R&D – expense it as you go. Save yourself the pain and stick to the easy rule: purchased = capitalize. Internally developed = expense (mostly!).

sections.title: Confusing Legal Costs with Intangible Value
sections.content: Students often incorrectly include legal fees *associated* with defending a patent (an acquired intangible) in the *amortizable base* of the patent. Those legal fees are expensed as incurred! The key is that the legal fees don't increase the *value* of the patent, they simply protect the existing value. Only the initial cost of acquiring the patent (or fair value in a business combination) is amortized. Keep those costs separate, or you'll overstate amortization expense.

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Situation: Customer Lists
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant, is reviewing the acquisition of a small marketing firm. The acquirer wants to amortize a large portion of the purchase price as the value of the acquired customer list. However, Sarah notes the acquired firm's customer retention rate is abysmal. The learning point? Even if a customer list is *identifiable*, its useful life (and therefore the amortization period) might be surprisingly short, potentially leading to a significant impairment charge down the road.

sections.title: Due Diligence: Spotting Hidden Intangibles
sections.content: In M&A due diligence, a crucial task is identifying all intangible assets. You'll encounter this when valuing a target company. Look beyond the obvious patents and trademarks. Are there valuable customer relationships, non-compete agreements, or unique software? Overlooking these can significantly undervalue the target, or worse, overvalue it if their lifespan is shorter than you think. Mastering this now translates to better deal analysis and more accurate financial statements for your clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Purchased intangibles: Capitalize; Internally developed: Generally expense
Definite life: Amortize over useful life (shorter of legal or economic)
Indefinite life: No amortization; test for impairment at least annually
Legal costs to defend: Capitalize if successful; expense if unsuccessful
Changes in useful life: Apply prospectively (no restatement)
Impairment: Two-step for definite life; FV vs. CV for indefinite life

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During the current year, Watson Corp. incurred legal costs of $50,000 to successfully defend a patent purchased three years ago for $150,000. The remaining legal life of the patent is 17 years. How should Watson account for these legal costs?
options: Expense the $50,000.
Capitalize the $50,000 and amortize it over 17 years.
Capitalize the $50,000 and amortize it over the remaining useful life of the patent.
Expense $2,941 and capitalize the remainder.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Legal costs incurred to successfully defend a patent should be capitalized and amortized over the remaining useful life of the patent. This is because the successful defense extends the period over which the patent will generate revenue. The remaining useful life would be less than 17 years if the patent was originally amortized over a period shorter than its legal life.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the benefit period of the legal costs. Does it extend the life of the asset?

reveal.question: What is the 'separability criterion' for recognizing an intangible asset separately from goodwill in a business combination?
reveal.answer: The 'separability criterion' states that an intangible asset should be recognized separately from goodwill if it can be separated or divided from the acquired entity and sold, transferred, licensed, rented, or exchanged, regardless of whether the acquirer intends to do so.

rows.label: Useful Life
values: No foreseeable limit on cash-generating period

title: Goodwill Impairment Testing
description: Apply the simplified goodwill impairment test under ASC 350

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Goodwill represents the premium paid in acquisitions—often billions of dollars for large companies. Unlike other assets, goodwill is NOT amortized under GAAP; instead, it's tested for impairment annually. When impairment occurs, it can devastate reported earnings. This is a frequently tested advanced topic!

sections.title: What is Goodwill?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Goodwill = Purchase Price − Fair Value of Identifiable Net Assets

**Arises only from business combinations (acquisitions)**

**Represents:**
• Synergies expected from the combination
• Value of assembled workforce
• Future cash flows beyond identifiable assets
• Premium paid for control

**Cannot recognize internally generated goodwill!**

sections.title: What is a Reporting Unit?
sections.content:
**Goodwill is tested at the REPORTING UNIT level**

**Reporting unit:** An operating segment or one level below

**Characteristics:**
• Has discrete financial information available
• Segment management regularly reviews results
• Represents the level at which goodwill is monitored

**Example:** A company with three operating segments may have each segment as a reporting unit, or further subdivided if discrete financial info exists

sections.title: Impairment Testing: When?
sections.content:
**Required testing:**

**1. ANNUAL test:**
• At least once per year
• Same time each year (consistency)
• Can be any date (doesn't have to be year-end)

**2. INTERIM test when indicators present:**
• Significant adverse change in business climate
• Decline in stock price (if public)
• Adverse legal or regulatory action
• Loss of key personnel
• More-likely-than-not that reporting unit will be sold

sections.title: The Simplified Impairment Test
sections.content:
**One-step approach (ASC 350 as amended):**

**Compare:** Fair Value of Reporting Unit vs. Carrying Amount (including goodwill)

**If FV < Carrying Amount:**
Impairment Loss = Carrying Amount − Fair Value

**But:** Loss limited to the amount of goodwill
(Can't impair other assets through this test)

**If FV ≥ Carrying Amount:**
No impairment—goodwill is fine!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Goodwill Impairment
sections.content:
**"Fair Value Floor"**

**Step 1:** Is FV of reporting unit < Carrying value?
• NO → Stop, no impairment
• YES → Impairment exists

**Step 2:** Impairment = CV − FV (but max = goodwill balance)

**Think of it as:** "The reporting unit can't be worth less than its fair value. If carrying value exceeds FV, goodwill takes the hit."

sections.title: Impairment Example
sections.content:
**Reporting Unit X:**
• Carrying amount (including $80,000 goodwill): $500,000
• Fair value of reporting unit: $450,000

**Step 1: Is there impairment?**
FV ($450,000) < CV ($500,000) → YES

**Step 2: Measure impairment**
Potential loss: $500,000 − $450,000 = $50,000
Goodwill balance: $80,000
Impairment loss: $50,000 (less than goodwill, so record full amount)

**Entry:**
Dr Goodwill Impairment Loss     $50,000
    Cr Goodwill                       $50,000

**New goodwill balance: $30,000**

sections.title: Impairment Limited to Goodwill
sections.content:
**Reporting Unit Y:**
• Carrying amount: $300,000 (including $40,000 goodwill)
• Fair value: $200,000

**Calculated impairment:**
$300,000 − $200,000 = $100,000

**But goodwill is only $40,000!**

**Record only $40,000 impairment (limited to goodwill)**

Dr Goodwill Impairment Loss     $40,000
    Cr Goodwill                       $40,000

**The remaining $60,000 excess is NOT recorded.** Test other long-lived assets separately if indicators exist.

sections.title: Optional Qualitative Assessment
sections.content:
**"Step Zero" — Qualitative Assessment (optional):**

Before performing quantitative test, assess whether it's **more likely than not** (>50%) that FV < CV.

**Consider factors:**
• Macroeconomic conditions
• Industry and market conditions
• Cost factors (labor, materials)
• Overall financial performance
• Entity-specific events
• Stock price (if publicly traded)

**If "not more likely than not" → Skip quantitative test**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: No Reversal!
sections.content:
**Once goodwill is impaired, it's GONE!**

• Even if fair value later increases
• Even if business conditions improve
• You CANNOT write goodwill back up

**IFRS difference:** Same rule—no reversal for goodwill impairment under IFRS either!

**This differs from long-lived assets held for sale, where impairment can be reversed.**

sections.title: Private Company Alternative
sections.content:
**ASC 350-20-35: Accounting alternative for private companies**

**Option 1: Amortize goodwill**
• Straight-line over 10 years (or less if shorter life)
• Reduces annual impairment testing burden

**Option 2: Simplified impairment**
• Test at entity level (not reporting unit)
• Triggering events only (not annual)

**Election is irrevocable; must apply to all goodwill**

sections.title: Determining Fair Value of Reporting Unit
sections.content:
**Common valuation approaches:**

**1. Income Approach:**
• Discounted cash flow (DCF) model
• Most common method

**2. Market Approach:**
• Comparable company multiples
• Comparable transactions

**3. Asset Approach:**
• Fair value of net assets
• Less common for going concerns

**Often use multiple methods and weight results**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Goodwill Impairment: It's All About the Reporting Unit
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Goodwill impairment testing isn't exactly riveting stuff. But trust me, getting this right is crucial. The biggest headache? Figuring out the reporting unit. Think of it like this: it's not the *entire* company, it's the smallest level you can get reliable financial data for. If you're testing goodwill at a level that's too high, you'll miss the localized impairment issues. So, drill down, find that reporting unit, and make sure you have discrete financial information. It'll save you a lot of explaining later!

sections.title: Confusing Fair Value of Reporting Unit with Net Assets
sections.content: Students often confuse the fair value of the reporting unit with the fair value of its *identifiable* net assets. Remember, the fair value of the reporting unit is what a willing buyer would pay for the *entire* unit, including goodwill. The fair value of identifiable net assets *excludes* goodwill. If the fair value of the reporting unit is *less* than its carrying amount, you have a potential impairment. This is the first step of the simplified test. Don't skip it! Use reasonable valuation techniques to determine fair value; don't just rely on book values.

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Situation: Reporting Unit Woes
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant at TechCorp, is responsible for goodwill impairment testing. TechCorp has two operating segments, but Sarah realizes that one segment's financial data is highly integrated with the corporate office. She decides that the *entire* segment cannot be considered a reporting unit. Instead, she identifies two distinct divisions within that segment, each with its own discrete financials and management. By testing at the division level, Sarah can more accurately identify potential goodwill impairments that might be masked at a higher level.

sections.title: Goodwill Impairment: Due Diligence in M&A
sections.content: In practice, understanding goodwill impairment is vital during mergers and acquisitions (M&A). During due diligence, you'll be scrutinizing the target company's goodwill and its assigned reporting units. You need to assess the reasonableness of their impairment testing process. Are the reporting units properly defined? Are the fair value estimations supported by solid evidence? A poorly defined reporting unit or aggressive fair value assumptions could hide significant future impairment charges, impacting the acquirer's financial statements down the line. This knowledge can give you leverage in negotiating the purchase price.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Goodwill arises only from business combinations (can't internally generate)
Goodwill is NOT amortized; test for impairment at least annually
Test at reporting unit level: Compare FV to carrying amount
Impairment = Carrying amount − Fair value (limited to goodwill balance)
Once impaired, goodwill CANNOT be restored (no reversal under GAAP or IFRS)
Private companies may elect to amortize goodwill over ≤10 years

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Company A acquired Company B for $5,000,000. At the time of acquisition, Company B's identifiable net assets had a fair value of $4,000,000. Which of the following statements is correct regarding goodwill?
options: Goodwill of $1,000,000 is recognized and amortized over its useful life.
Goodwill of $1,000,000 is recognized and tested for impairment annually, or more frequently if indicators exist.
Goodwill of $9,000,000 is recognized and tested for impairment annually, or more frequently if indicators exist.
No goodwill is recognized because the purchase price was greater than the fair value of the identifiable net assets.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Goodwill is calculated as the purchase price less the fair value of identifiable net assets ($5,000,000 - $4,000,000 = $1,000,000). Goodwill is not amortized, but it is tested for impairment at least annually.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula for calculating goodwill and how it is subsequently treated.

reveal.question: Define a 'reporting unit' for goodwill impairment testing purposes.
reveal.answer: A reporting unit is an operating segment or one level below an operating segment. It has discrete financial information available, is regularly reviewed by segment management, and represents the level at which goodwill is monitored.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following events would most likely trigger an interim goodwill impairment test?
options: A slight increase in interest rates.
A significant adverse change in the business climate.
Routine maintenance on a major piece of equipment.
An increase in the market price of the company's stock.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A significant adverse change in the business climate is a common indicator that an interim goodwill impairment test should be performed. The other options are less likely to indicate a potential impairment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about events that could negatively impact the value of the reporting unit.

title: Accounts Payable & Accrued Liabilities
description: Recognize and measure current trade and accrued liabilities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Current liabilities affect working capital, the current ratio, and cash flow timing. Understanding proper cutoff for payables and when to accrue expenses is essential for accurate financial statements. These concepts underpin many exam questions on adjusting entries and liability recognition.

sections.title: What are Current Liabilities?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Obligations expected to be settled within:
• One year, OR
• The normal operating cycle (if longer)

**Characteristics:**
• Require current assets or creation of other current liabilities to settle
• Due within near-term

**Examples:**
• Accounts payable
• Accrued expenses
• Short-term notes payable
• Current portion of long-term debt
• Unearned revenue

sections.title: Accounts Payable
sections.content:
**Definition:** Obligations to pay for goods or services received on credit.

**Recognition:** When goods/services are received (regardless of invoice receipt)

**Measurement:** Face amount (no discounting for short-term)

**Key timing issue:** Proper CUTOFF at period-end
• Include goods received by period-end (even if invoice not received)
• Exclude goods not yet received (even if invoice received)

sections.title: Trade Payables Cutoff
sections.content:
**Year-end: December 31**

**Scenario A:** Goods shipped FOB Shipping Point Dec 28, arrived Jan 2
→ Include in payables and inventory (title passed Dec 28)

**Scenario B:** Goods shipped FOB Destination Dec 28, arrived Jan 2
→ Exclude from payables and inventory (title passes on arrival)

**Scenario C:** Invoice received Dec 30, goods arrive Jan 5
→ Exclude—no liability until goods received

**Key:** FOB terms determine when title transfers!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: FOB Terms
sections.content:
**FOB = Free On Board**

**FOB Shipping Point:**
• Title transfers when goods LEAVE seller's dock
• Buyer owns goods in transit
• Buyer records inventory/payable at shipment

**FOB Destination:**
• Title transfers when goods ARRIVE at buyer's dock
• Seller owns goods in transit
• Buyer records inventory/payable at arrival

**"Shipping point = Shipped, it's yours!"**

sections.title: Accrued Liabilities
sections.content:
**Definition:** Expenses incurred but not yet paid or invoiced.

**Recognition:** When expense is incurred (matching principle)

**Common accrued liabilities:**
• Wages/salaries payable
• Interest payable
• Utilities payable
• Property taxes payable
• Rent payable
• Professional fees payable

sections.title: Accrued Wages Example
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Weekly payroll: $70,000 (Mon-Fri)
• Pay date: Friday
• Year-end: Wednesday, December 31

**Accrual calculation:**
3 days worked (Mon, Tue, Wed) × ($70,000 ÷ 5 days) = $42,000

**December 31 adjusting entry:**
Dr Wages Expense              $42,000
    Cr Wages Payable               $42,000

**January 2 (pay date):**
Dr Wages Payable              $42,000
Dr Wages Expense              $28,000
    Cr Cash                         $70,000

sections.title: Accrued Interest
sections.content:
**Recognize interest expense as time passes:**

**Formula:**
Interest = Principal × Rate × Time

**Example:**
$100,000 note at 6% annual rate; year-end 3 months after issuance

Interest = $100,000 × 6% × 3/12 = $1,500

**Entry:**
Dr Interest Expense           $1,500
    Cr Interest Payable            $1,500

sections.title: Property Taxes
sections.content:
**Accrue property taxes over the lien period:**

**Lien date:** Date tax liability attaches to property

**Methods:**

**1. Accrue monthly over fiscal year:**
Total tax ÷ 12 = Monthly expense

**2. Accrue over calendar year:**
Allocate based on months of ownership

**Key:** Match expense to period property is used!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Deposits & Prepayments
sections.content:
**Refundable deposits received are LIABILITIES!**

**Examples:**
• Customer deposits on orders
• Security deposits (renter)
• Bottle deposits

**Entry when received:**
Dr Cash                       $XXX
    Cr Customer Deposits (liability)  $XXX

**NOT revenue until earned!**

Don't confuse deposits received (liability) with deposits paid (asset).

sections.title: Dividends Payable
sections.content:
**Record liability when dividends are DECLARED:**

**Declaration date (liability created):**
Dr Retained Earnings         $XXX
    Cr Dividends Payable          $XXX

**Record date:**
No entry—just determines who gets dividend

**Payment date (liability settled):**
Dr Dividends Payable         $XXX
    Cr Cash                        $XXX

**Undeclared dividends: NO liability (even if expected)**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: AP: It's All About Timing, Baby!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Accounts Payable and Accrued Liabilities? Sounds snoozy, right? But trust me, nailing this is HUGE. Think of it like this: you get the coffee beans delivered, but haven't paid yet. That delivery truck is like a ticking clock. You *owe* that money, even if the bill hasn't shown up. Get good at spotting those 'unbilled deliveries' and 'services rendered' situations. The exam loves to trick you with dates, so pay attention and you will not get tripped up.

sections.title: AP vs. Accrued Expenses: The Great Divide
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking Accounts Payable and Accrued Expenses are interchangeable. They aren't! Accounts Payable is specifically for goods or services received on credit, usually with an invoice expected. Accrued expenses are for expenses incurred but not yet billed, like utilities or salaries. Students often miss this because both are current liabilities. The key is to remember: AP = Invoice likely coming. Accrued Expense = Invoice not yet received (or maybe never will be). Get this straight, and you'll ace those questions.

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Situation: Unrecorded Invoice!
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant at Tech Solutions, is reviewing month-end expenses. She finds a receiving report from December 30th showing a shipment of servers arrived, but no invoice has been recorded. The amount is $50,000. Sarah needs to accrue the $50,000 as Accounts Payable. Even though there's no invoice yet, the company received the goods and has an obligation to pay. Failing to record this would understate liabilities and expenses, impacting net income and balance sheet accuracy.

sections.title: AP in the Real World: Month-End Close
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter accounts payable and accrued liabilities every month during the month-end close process. You'll be reviewing vendor statements, matching receiving reports to invoices, and identifying any unrecorded liabilities. For example, you might notice that a consulting service was performed in December, but the invoice hasn't arrived by January 5th. You need to accrue that expense. Mastering this now will save you hours of reconciliation headaches and prevent inaccurate financial reporting later on. It's all about accuracy and completeness!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Current liabilities due within one year or operating cycle
Record A/P when goods received, regardless of invoice timing
FOB Shipping Point: title at shipment; FOB Destination: title at arrival
Accrue expenses when incurred, even if not yet billed or paid
Interest accrual: Principal × Rate × Time elapsed
Refundable deposits received are liabilities, not revenue

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the cutoff for accounts payable at year-end?
options: Goods should be included in accounts payable only if the invoice has been received by year-end.
Goods should be included in accounts payable if they are shipped by the supplier before year-end, regardless of when received.
Goods should be included in accounts payable if title to the goods has passed to the buyer before year-end, even if the goods are still in transit.
Goods should be included in accounts payable only if payment has been made before year-end.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is (C). The proper cutoff for accounts payable requires including goods for which title has transferred to the buyer before year-end. This is true even if the goods are still in transit. Options A and D are incorrect because the receipt of the invoice or payment is not the determining factor. Option B is incorrect because shipment alone is not sufficient; title needs to have transferred.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about when the company legally owns the goods.

reveal.question: What are the two criteria that define a current liability?
reveal.answer: A current liability is an obligation expected to be settled within one year, OR the normal operating cycle (if longer).

rows.label: Documentation
values: Typically supported by a purchase order and vendor invoice.
May not have an invoice; often based on estimates or allocations.

rows.label: Examples
values: Purchase of raw materials, office supplies, or services from a vendor.
Salaries payable, interest payable, utilities payable.

title: Asset Retirement Obligations
description: Account for legal obligations to dismantle or restore long-lived assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When a company buys an oil rig, builds a nuclear plant, or signs a lease requiring restoration, there's often a legal obligation to eventually dismantle or clean up. ARO accounting requires recognizing this future cost TODAY at present value. It's a complex topic that combines liability recognition, asset capitalization, and interest accretion.

sections.title: What is an ARO?
sections.content:
**Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO):**

A legal obligation to perform retirement activities when a long-lived asset is:
• Dismantled
• Removed
• Remediated (environmental cleanup)
• Restored to original condition

**Must be a LEGAL obligation:**
• Laws, regulations, contracts
• Promissory estoppel
• NOT just company policy or intent

content.definition: Legal requirement to plug wells and restore land
Decommissioning and waste disposal requirements
Land reclamation and environmental restoration
Lease requires removal at end of term
Environmental regulations require removal
Contract requires dismantling

sections.title: Initial Recognition
sections.content:
**When ARO is incurred:**

1. **Record liability** at fair value (present value of future cost)
2. **Increase asset cost** by the same amount

**Fair value = Present Value of expected future cash flows**

**Entry:**
Dr Asset (capitalized ARO cost)    $XXX
    Cr Asset Retirement Obligation      $XXX

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: ARO Recording
sections.content:
**"Capitalize the liability"**

**Day 1:** Both sides of entry are equal (PV of retirement cost)

**Over time:**
• **Liability grows** (accretion expense) → Interest-like
• **Asset depreciates** → Depreciation expense

**At retirement:**
• Liability = Actual retirement cost (approximately)
• Asset fully depreciated

sections.title: ARO Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Company purchases oil well on Jan 1, Year 1:**
• Equipment cost: $1,000,000
• Estimated retirement cost in 10 years: $150,000
• Credit-adjusted risk-free rate: 6%

**PV of ARO:**
$150,000 × (1/1.06)^10 = $150,000 × 0.5584 = $83,760

**Initial Entry:**
Dr Oil Well Equipment         $1,083,760
    Cr Cash                        $1,000,000
    Cr Asset Retirement Obligation     $83,760

**The $83,760 ARO cost is capitalized as part of the asset!**

sections.title: Subsequent Measurement: Accretion
sections.content:
**Each period, the liability grows (accretion):**

**Accretion Expense = Beginning ARO Balance × Original Rate**

**Entry:**
Dr Accretion Expense          $XXX
    Cr Asset Retirement Obligation     $XXX

**Think of it as:** "Interest" on the liability

**Year 1 from example:**
$83,760 × 6% = $5,026 accretion expense

sections.title: Subsequent Measurement: Depreciation
sections.content:
**The capitalized ARO cost is depreciated:**

**Depreciate with the related asset** (same method and life)

**From example:**
Total asset cost: $1,083,760
Useful life: 10 years
Annual depreciation: $108,376

**Entry:**
Dr Depreciation Expense       $108,376
    Cr Accumulated Depreciation      $108,376

sections.title: Settlement of ARO
sections.content:
**When retirement activities are performed:**

**If actual cost = liability:**
Dr Asset Retirement Obligation   $150,000
    Cr Cash                           $150,000

**If actual cost ≠ liability (common):**

**Actual < Liability → Gain**
Dr ARO                        $150,000
    Cr Cash                        $140,000
    Cr Gain on ARO Settlement       $10,000

**Actual > Liability → Loss**
Dr ARO                        $150,000
Dr Loss on ARO Settlement      $15,000
    Cr Cash                        $165,000

sections.title: Changes in Estimates
sections.content:
**When estimated costs or timing change:**

**Upward revision (cost increase):**
• Use CURRENT credit-adjusted risk-free rate
• Increase liability and asset

**Downward revision (cost decrease):**
• Use ORIGINAL rate when layer was recorded
• Decrease liability and asset (but not below zero)

**Changes in timing:**
• Treated as change in estimate
• Adjust PV calculations accordingly

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Rate Selection
sections.content:
**Which rate to use?**

**Original ARO and accretion:** Original credit-adjusted risk-free rate

**Upward revisions:** Current rate at time of revision

**Downward revisions:** Original rate for that layer

**Why the difference?** Upward revisions are like new obligations; downward revisions are removing old obligations.

sections.title: Conditional AROs
sections.content:
**What if timing or method is uncertain?**

**Conditional ARO:** Obligation exists, but timing/method uncertain

**Recognition:**
• Still recognize at fair value if reasonable estimate possible
• Use expected value (probability-weighted) approach
• Uncertainty reflected in fair value, not recognition

**Example:** Asbestos removal—obligation exists, but timing depends on renovation plans
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: ARO: It's All About Future Costs!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. AROs aren't the sexiest topic, but they're crucial. Think of it like this: You're buying a house with a leaky roof. Sure, the house looks great now, but you know you'll have to fix that roof eventually. AROs are the accounting equivalent of that leaky roof, but for companies. We're talking about future dismantling, cleanup, and restoration costs. Get a handle on present value, and you're golden. Ignore it, and you'll be scrambling during the audit. Nobody wants that!

sections.title: Confusing Legal vs. Moral Obligations
sections.content: A very common mistake is thinking any future cleanup counts as an ARO. It DOESN'T. It MUST be a LEGAL obligation. Just because a company *should* clean up a contaminated site doesn't automatically create an ARO. There has to be a law, regulation, contract, or promissory estoppel forcing them to do it. If it's purely a moral or 'best practice' issue, it's not an ARO, and you'd account for it differently, likely as an expense when incurred. Always check the legal basis!

sections.title: Sarah's Oil Rig Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a newly promoted financial analyst at OilCo, is reviewing the financials for a new offshore oil rig. The contract states OilCo is legally required to dismantle the rig and restore the seabed to its original condition at the end of its useful life. The estimated dismantling cost is $5 million in 20 years. Sarah needs to calculate the present value of that $5 million liability to record the ARO properly and capitalize it as part of the asset's cost. Ignoring this legal obligation would materially misstate OilCo's financials.

sections.title: AROs: A Real-World Audit Hotspot
sections.content: In practice, you'll often encounter AROs when auditing companies in the extractive industries (mining, oil & gas), utilities (nuclear power plants), or any industry with significant environmental impact. Auditors scrutinize the assumptions used to calculate the present value of the ARO, such as the discount rate and the estimated future costs. A small change in these assumptions can have a material impact on the financial statements. Therefore, be prepared to defend your estimates with solid documentation and industry benchmarks.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ARO = legal obligation to retire, dismantle, or restore a long-lived asset
Initial: Record liability at PV; capitalize same amount to asset
Accretion: Liability grows each period (like interest expense)
Depreciation: Capitalized ARO cost depreciated with related asset
Settlement gains/losses when actual cost differs from liability
Rate selection: Original rate for accretion; current rate for upward revisions

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Mining Co. is legally required to restore land to its original condition after mining operations cease. The estimated cost of restoration is $5,000,000, expected to be paid in 10 years. The current risk-adjusted discount rate is 6%. At what amount should XYZ Mining Co. initially recognize the Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) and the corresponding increase to the asset cost?
options: Dr. Asset (capitalized ARO cost) $5,000,000; Cr. Asset Retirement Obligation $5,000,000
Dr. Asset (capitalized ARO cost) $2,791,974; Cr. Asset Retirement Obligation $2,791,974
Dr. Asset (capitalized ARO cost) $5,300,000; Cr. Asset Retirement Obligation $5,300,000
No entry is required until the restoration work begins.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The ARO must be recognized at its fair value, which is the present value of the future cost. The present value of $5,000,000 discounted at 6% for 10 years is $2,791,974 ($5,000,000 / (1.06)^10 = $2,791,974).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to discount the future cost back to its present value using the appropriate discount rate.

reveal.question: What are the four main categories of retirement activities that would trigger an Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO)?
reveal.answer: The four main categories are: Dismantling, Removing, Remediating (environmental cleanup), and Restoring to original condition. The obligation MUST be a LEGAL one.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would NOT create a legal obligation that requires recognition of an Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO)?
options: A company signs a contract agreeing to dismantle equipment at the end of its useful life.
A company operates in a jurisdiction with environmental regulations requiring land restoration after mining.
A company publicly announces its intention to restore a site to its original condition, creating a reasonable expectation that it will do so (promissory estoppel).
A company has a long-standing internal policy of voluntarily cleaning up any environmental damage caused by its operations, but there are no external legal requirements.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An ARO must be a LEGAL obligation. While a company's internal policy might be a good business practice, it does not create a legal obligation requiring ARO recognition unless it has been formalized such that promissory estoppel applies.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the obligation is enforceable by law or regulation.

title: Notes Payable & Interest
description: Account for short-term and long-term notes payable with interest calculations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Notes payable represent formal debt obligations with specified interest rates and terms. Understanding how to record notes, calculate interest expense, and handle zero-interest or below-market notes is essential for proper liability measurement and income statement presentation.

sections.title: Notes Payable Overview
sections.content:
**Definition:** Written promise to pay a specified amount with interest at a stated date.

**Key features:**
• Formal written agreement
• Specified principal amount
• Stated or implied interest rate
• Defined maturity date

**Classification:**
• **Current:** Due within one year
• **Non-current:** Due beyond one year

sections.title: Interest-Bearing Notes
sections.content:
**Note with STATED interest rate:**

**Issuance (borrowing cash):**
Dr Cash                       $XXX
    Cr Notes Payable               $XXX

**Interest accrual (period-end):**
Dr Interest Expense           $XXX
    Cr Interest Payable            $XXX

**Payment at maturity:**
Dr Notes Payable              $XXX
Dr Interest Payable           $XXX
    Cr Cash                        $XXX

sections.title: Interest Calculation
sections.content:
**Simple Interest Formula:**
Interest = Principal × Rate × Time

**Time conventions:**
• **Exact days:** Actual days ÷ 365 (or 360)
• **Months:** Number of months ÷ 12

**Example:**
$100,000 note at 6% for 3 months:
Interest = $100,000 × 6% × 3/12 = $1,500

sections.title: Note Issued for Cash Example
sections.content:
**Oct 1: Borrow $50,000, 8%, 6-month note**

**Oct 1 - Issuance:**
Dr Cash                       $50,000
    Cr Notes Payable               $50,000

**Dec 31 - Accrue interest (3 months):**
Interest = $50,000 × 8% × 3/12 = $1,000
Dr Interest Expense            $1,000
    Cr Interest Payable             $1,000

**April 1 - Maturity (pay principal + 6 months interest):**
Total interest = $50,000 × 8% × 6/12 = $2,000
Dr Notes Payable              $50,000
Dr Interest Payable            $1,000
Dr Interest Expense            $1,000
    Cr Cash                        $52,000

sections.title: Non-Interest-Bearing Notes
sections.content:
**"Zero-interest" note—interest is implicit:**

**Face value includes hidden interest!**

**Issued at present value:**
Note Payable recorded at **PV** (not face value)

**Discount = Face Value − Present Value**

**Over note term:**
Amortize discount to interest expense

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Zero-Interest Notes
sections.content:
**"Nothing is free!"**

A "zero-interest" note doesn't mean no interest—the interest is EMBEDDED in the face amount.

**You receive LESS than face value**
(or pay less for an asset than stated price)

**The difference IS interest, recognized over time.**

sections.title: Non-Interest-Bearing Note Example
sections.content:
**Borrow cash; sign $10,000 non-interest note due in 2 years**
**Market rate: 6%**

**Present value:**
$10,000 × (1/1.06)² = $10,000 × 0.8900 = $8,900

**Issuance:**
Dr Cash                        $8,900
Dr Discount on N/P             $1,100
    Cr Notes Payable               $10,000

**Year 1 Interest:**
$8,900 × 6% = $534
Dr Interest Expense              $534
    Cr Discount on N/P                $534

**Carrying value end of Year 1: $8,900 + $534 = $9,434**

sections.title: Note Issued for Non-Cash Consideration
sections.content:
**If note issued to acquire asset:**

**Determine appropriate value:**
1. Fair value of asset received, OR
2. Fair value of note (PV at market rate)

**Use more clearly determinable value**

**Any difference between note face and recorded value:**
→ Discount or premium on note payable

sections.title: Below-Market Note Example
sections.content:
**Purchase equipment with $50,000, 2%, 3-year note**
**Equipment FMV: $45,000; Market rate: 8%**

**Record at equipment FMV:**
Dr Equipment                  $45,000
Dr Discount on N/P             $5,000
    Cr Notes Payable               $50,000

**Imputed interest:**
The $5,000 discount represents additional interest to be recognized over 3 years.

**Effective interest rate will be HIGHER than stated 2%.**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Imputing Interest
sections.content:
**When to impute interest (ASC 835-30):**

Required for notes with:
• No stated rate, OR
• Unreasonable stated rate

**Use:**
• Rate borrower could obtain elsewhere, OR
• Rate that discounts note to FV of consideration

**Exceptions (no imputation):**
• Normal trade payables (< 1 year)
• Security deposits
• Transactions with related parties (disclose)

sections.title: Current Portion of Long-Term Debt
sections.content:
**Each period, reclassify portion due within one year:**

**Example:** 5-year note with $20,000 annual principal payments

**At each year-end:**
Dr Notes Payable (long-term)   $20,000
    Cr Current Portion of L-T Debt   $20,000

**Balance sheet shows:**
• Current Liabilities: $20,000
• Long-term Liabilities: Remaining balance
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Notes Payable: It's All About the Benjamins!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Notes payable aren't rocket science, but they *are* about money. Think of it like this: you're borrowing cash, and the lender wants a return. The interest rate is their cut. Don't overthink the journal entries. Cash goes up, Notes Payable goes up. At year-end? Interest expense. It's like paying rent on money. Nail this down, and you'll thank yourself later. Trust me, getting the basics right makes the harder stuff way less scary. Now, go get those Benjamins... I mean, points!

sections.title: Confusing Stated vs. Effective Interest
sections.content: Students often confuse stated interest with effective interest. A note might *state* 8% interest, but if the market rate is different, the *effective* interest rate is adjusted when the note is issued at a discount or premium. The stated rate only determines the cash interest payments. The effective rate is what truly reflects the cost of borrowing. Remember, amortization of the discount or premium affects the interest expense recognized each period, bringing the effective rate into play. Always consider market rates and the present value calculations!

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Note Situation
sections.content: Sarah, an accounting intern, was tasked with reconciling the bank statement. She noticed a $50,000 short-term note payable issued three months ago with a stated interest rate of 6%. Sarah forgot to accrue for interest expense at the end of the last month. Because she missed this, the current month's income statement is understated, and the balance sheet is incorrect. Always remember to accrue interest expense at period-end, even for short-term notes! This ensures accurate financial reporting.

sections.title: Notes Payable: A Daily Grind?
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter notes payable when a company needs short-term or long-term financing. This could be for operational expenses, expansion, or acquisitions. You'll frequently be tasked with tracking the principal balance, calculating interest payments, and properly classifying the note as current or non-current on the balance sheet. Accuracy is crucial, as these liabilities directly impact a company's financial health and debt covenants. Understanding the intricacies of notes payable is essential for sound financial analysis and reporting.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Interest-bearing notes: Record at face; accrue interest as time passes
Non-interest-bearing notes: Record at PV; amortize discount to interest
Interest formula: Principal × Rate × Time
Note for non-cash: Use fair value of asset or fair value of note
Impute interest when stated rate is unreasonable or absent
Reclassify current portion of long-term debt each period

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: On October 1, Year 1, a company issued a $500,000 note payable with a 5% annual interest rate, due on September 30, Year 2. How should this note payable be classified on the company's December 31, Year 1 balance sheet?
options: Entirely as a current liability.
Entirely as a non-current liability.
As a current liability for $500,000 plus accrued interest, and a non-current liability for the remaining balance.
As a current liability for $500,000.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since the note is due within one year of the balance sheet date (December 31, Year 1), it is classified entirely as a current liability. The accrued interest would also be classified as a current liability but the question focuses on the note itself.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the definition of a current liability.

reveal.question: What is the formula to calculate simple interest?
reveal.answer: Interest = Principal × Rate × Time

knowledgeCheck.question: A company borrows $200,000 on July 1, Year 1, issuing an interest-bearing note with an annual interest rate of 8%. The note matures on December 31, Year 1. Assuming the company uses the months convention for calculating interest, what amount of interest expense should be recognized for Year 1?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The interest expense is calculated as follows: $200,000 (Principal) × 8% (Rate) × 6/12 (Time). This equals $8,000. The time is 6/12 because the note was outstanding for 6 months (July 1 to December 31).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to account for the portion of the year the note is outstanding.

title: Bonds Payable: Issuance, Premium, Discount
description: Account for bond issuance at par, premium, and discount with effective interest method

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Bonds are a major source of corporate financing, and bond accounting is HEAVILY tested on FAR. You must master the effective interest method, understand why bonds sell at premiums or discounts, and know how to calculate interest expense vs. cash paid. This is a must-know topic!

sections.title: Bond Basics
sections.content:
**Key terms:**

• **Face (Par) Value:** Principal amount due at maturity
• **Stated (Coupon) Rate:** Interest rate printed on bond; determines CASH paid
• **Market (Effective) Rate:** Current market rate; determines PRICE
• **Maturity Date:** When principal is repaid

**Bond pricing rule:**
• Stated Rate > Market Rate → **Premium** (sell above par)
• Stated Rate < Market Rate → **Discount** (sell below par)
• Stated Rate = Market Rate → **At Par**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Premium vs. Discount
sections.content:
**"Investors pay MORE for MORE interest"**

**Premium:** Stated rate > Market rate
• Bond pays more than market—investors pay extra!
• Price > Face value

**Discount:** Stated rate < Market rate
• Bond pays less than market—investors pay less
• Price < Face value

**Think of investors comparing: Would I rather have this bond or a market-rate bond?**

sections.title: Bond Price Calculation
sections.content:
**Bond price = PV of future cash flows at MARKET rate:**

**Price = PV of Interest Payments + PV of Principal**

PV of Interest = Payment × PV of Annuity factor
PV of Principal = Face × PV of Single Sum factor

**Use MARKET rate to discount!**

sections.title: Issuance at Par
sections.content:
**Issue $100,000, 5-year bonds at par (stated = market = 6%):**

**Price:** $100,000 (no premium or discount)

**Issuance Entry:**
Dr Cash                      $100,000
    Cr Bonds Payable              $100,000

**Interest payment (annually):**
Cash = $100,000 × 6% = $6,000

Dr Interest Expense           $6,000
    Cr Cash                        $6,000

sections.title: Issuance at Discount
sections.content:
**Issue $100,000, 5-year bonds; Stated 5%, Market 6%:**

**Price (given or calculated):** $95,788
**Discount:** $100,000 − $95,788 = $4,212

**Issuance Entry:**
Dr Cash                       $95,788
Dr Discount on Bonds Payable   $4,212
    Cr Bonds Payable              $100,000

**Or (net presentation):**
Dr Cash                       $95,788
    Cr Bonds Payable               $95,788

**Carrying value at issuance: $95,788**

sections.title: Issuance at Premium
sections.content:
**Issue $100,000, 5-year bonds; Stated 7%, Market 6%:**

**Price (given or calculated):** $104,212
**Premium:** $104,212 − $100,000 = $4,212

**Issuance Entry:**
Dr Cash                      $104,212
    Cr Bonds Payable              $100,000
    Cr Premium on Bonds Payable     $4,212

**Or (net presentation):**
Dr Cash                      $104,212
    Cr Bonds Payable              $104,212

**Carrying value at issuance: $104,212**

sections.title: Effective Interest Method
sections.content:
**Required method under GAAP:**

**Interest Expense = Carrying Value × Market Rate**

**Cash Paid = Face Value × Stated Rate**

**Amortization = Difference**

• Discount: Expense > Cash → Amortize discount (CV increases)
• Premium: Expense < Cash → Amortize premium (CV decreases)

**Carrying value approaches face value at maturity!**

sections.title: Effective Interest: Discount Example
sections.content:
**$100,000 bonds issued at $95,788 (5% stated, 6% market):**

**Year 1:**
Interest Expense: $95,788 × 6% = $5,747
Cash Paid: $100,000 × 5% = $5,000
Discount Amortization: $747

Dr Interest Expense           $5,747
    Cr Discount on Bonds             $747
    Cr Cash                        $5,000

**New carrying value: $95,788 + $747 = $96,535**

**Year 2:**
Interest Expense: $96,535 × 6% = $5,792
...

sections.title: Effective Interest: Premium Example
sections.content:
**$100,000 bonds issued at $104,212 (7% stated, 6% market):**

**Year 1:**
Interest Expense: $104,212 × 6% = $6,253
Cash Paid: $100,000 × 7% = $7,000
Premium Amortization: $747

Dr Interest Expense           $6,253
Dr Premium on Bonds             $747
    Cr Cash                        $7,000

**New carrying value: $104,212 − $747 = $103,465**

**Year 2:**
Interest Expense: $103,465 × 6% = $6,208
...

sections.title: Bonds Issued Between Interest Dates
sections.content:
**If bonds issued between payment dates:**

**Buyer pays:**
• Bond price, PLUS
• Accrued interest from last payment date

**Why?** Buyer will receive full interest payment on next date, but didn't own bond for entire period.

**Entry:**
Dr Cash (price + accrued)     $XXX
    Cr Bonds Payable               $XXX
    Cr Interest Payable (accrued)  $XXX

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Interest Expense vs. Cash
sections.content:
**Don't confuse these!**

**Cash paid** = Face × Stated Rate (always the same each period)

**Interest expense** = Carrying Value × Market Rate (changes each period)

**Income statement:** Interest Expense
**Cash flow statement:** Cash paid for interest

**They are NOT equal unless issued at par!**

sections.title: Bond Issuance Costs
sections.content:
**Legal, underwriting, printing costs:**

**Treatment (ASC 835-30):**
• Present as direct DEDUCTION from bond liability
• Like additional discount
• Amortize over bond term (effective interest method)

**Example:**
Issue $100,000 bond at par with $3,000 issuance costs:

**Net carrying value: $100,000 − $3,000 = $97,000**

Amortize $3,000 to interest expense over bond term.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Bond Pricing: It's All About the Market, Baby!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk bonds. Forget the fancy formulas for a sec. Think of it like buying a used car. The sticker price (stated rate) might be cool, but what's the *market* saying? Is that '67 Mustang really worth $50k in *this* economy? Same with bonds. The market rate tells you what investors *actually* want. If the stated rate is less than the market, you gotta discount that bad boy to make it attractive! Get it? Good. Now go ace this exam.

sections.title: Stated vs. Market Rate: The Great Confusion
sections.content: Students often confuse the stated rate with the market rate, leading to incorrect bond pricing. The stated rate determines the *cash interest payments* the bondholder receives. The market rate, on the other hand, is used to *discount* those cash flows (and the principal) to their present value. Think of it this way: the stated rate is what the company *promises* to pay, while the market rate is what investors *demand* to be paid. Use the market rate for the PV calculations, always!

sections.title: Sarah's Bond Dilemma: Premium or Discount?
sections.content: Sarah, an intern at a large corporation, is reviewing a bond issuance. The bond has a face value of $1,000, a stated rate of 6%, and matures in 10 years. However, the market rate for similar bonds is 5%. Because the stated rate is higher than the market rate, Sarah knows the bond will be issued at a premium. This means investors are willing to pay more than the face value to get that juicy 6% interest payment when the market only offers 5%.

sections.title: Bond Amortization: Every Month, Every Entry
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter bond amortization schedules monthly when companies issue bonds. You'll be responsible for calculating the interest expense using the effective interest method, which involves amortizing the premium or discount over the life of the bond. This impacts the income statement (interest expense) and the balance sheet (carrying value of the bond). Mastering this now will save you countless hours of recalculating schedules and explaining variances to your manager later on.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Premium: Stated rate > Market rate; Discount: Stated rate < Market rate
Bond price = PV of interest payments + PV of principal (at market rate)
Effective interest method: Expense = Carrying Value × Market Rate
Cash paid: Face Value × Stated Rate (constant each period)
Discount: CV increases over time; Premium: CV decreases over time
Issuance costs: Deduct from liability, amortize over term

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A company issues bonds with a face value of $500,000, a stated interest rate of 6%, and a maturity date of 5 years. The market rate of interest for similar bonds is 8%. What is the present value of the principal component of the bond issuance price?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The present value of the principal is calculated as Face Value * PV Factor. In this case, $500,000 * PV factor for 5 periods at 8% which is 0.68058.  $500,000 * 0.68058 = $340,292.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to use the MARKET rate to discount the principal amount. The PV Factor is for a SINGLE sum, not an annuity.

reveal.question: What rate is used to determine the CASH interest payments made to bondholders?
reveal.answer: The STATED (or coupon) rate is used to calculate the cash interest payments. The stated rate is multiplied by the face value of the bond to determine the periodic interest payment amount.

rows.label: Market Rate vs. Stated Rate
values: Market rate is LESS than stated rate
Market rate is GREATER than stated rate

rows.label: Bond Price
values: Bond sells for MORE than face value
Bond sells for LESS than face value

rows.label: Carrying Value over Time
values: Carrying value decreases over time to face value
Carrying value increases over time to face value

title: Debt Modifications & Extinguishments
description: Account for early debt retirement, troubled debt restructuring, and modifications

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Companies often retire debt early, modify loan terms, or restructure troubled debt. Each scenario has different accounting rules. The exam tests your ability to calculate gains/losses on extinguishment, determine if a modification is a new debt or continuation, and handle troubled debt restructuring.

sections.title: Debt Extinguishment Overview
sections.content:
**When is debt extinguished?**

• Debt is PAID (principal and interest)
• Debtor is legally RELEASED from obligation
• Defeasance (in-substance, limited)

**Gain/Loss = Carrying Value − Reacquisition Price**

**Carrying value:** Face ± unamortized premium/discount − issuance costs

**Reacquisition price:** Cash paid or FV of consideration given

sections.title: Early Retirement for Cash
sections.content:
**Bonds retired before maturity:**

• Face value: $100,000
• Unamortized discount: $3,000
• Unamortized issuance costs: $1,500
• Cash paid to retire: $98,000

**Carrying value:** $100,000 − $3,000 − $1,500 = $95,500
**Reacquisition price:** $98,000
**Loss on extinguishment:** $98,000 − $95,500 = $2,500

**Entry:**
Dr Bonds Payable              $100,000
Dr Loss on Extinguishment       $2,500
    Cr Discount on Bonds            $3,000
    Cr Debt Issuance Costs          $1,500
    Cr Cash                         $98,000

sections.title: Gain on Extinguishment
sections.content:
**Retiring bonds at discount:**

• Face value: $500,000
• Carrying value: $485,000 (unamortized discount $15,000)
• Call price: $470,000

**Gain:** $485,000 − $470,000 = $15,000

**Entry:**
Dr Bonds Payable              $500,000
    Cr Discount on Bonds           $15,000
    Cr Cash                        $470,000
    Cr Gain on Extinguishment       $15,000

**Gain or loss goes on INCOME STATEMENT (usually below operating income)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: G/L on Extinguishment
sections.content:
**"Pay More = Loss; Pay Less = Gain"**

If you pay MORE than carrying value → **LOSS**
(You overpaid to get rid of debt)

If you pay LESS than carrying value → **GAIN**
(You got a discount to eliminate debt)

**Formula:**
Carrying Value − Cash Paid = Gain (+) or Loss (−)

sections.title: Debt Modification: TDR or Not?
sections.content:
**First, determine if this is Troubled Debt Restructuring (TDR):**

**TDR exists when:**
1. Debtor is experiencing financial difficulties, AND
2. Creditor grants a concession it wouldn't otherwise consider

**If NOT TDR:**
Analyze as debt modification or extinguishment based on 10% test.

**If TDR:**
Special rules apply (covered below).

sections.title: The 10% Test for Modifications
sections.content:
**For non-TDR modifications:**

**Calculate PV of cash flows under new terms vs. old terms (using original rate):**

**If difference > 10%:**
• Treat as EXTINGUISHMENT of old debt
• Record new debt at fair value
• Recognize gain/loss immediately

**If difference ≤ 10%:**
• Treat as MODIFICATION (not extinguishment)
• No gain/loss
• Adjust effective rate prospectively
• Expense new fees

sections.title: Modification Example: < 10%
sections.content:
**Old debt: $1,000,000 at 8%, 5 years remaining**
**New terms: $1,000,000 at 7%, 5 years (PV difference < 10%)**

**Accounting (modification):**
• Continue carrying old debt
• Calculate new effective rate to equate PV of new cash flows with carrying value
• Recognize interest expense using new effective rate
• Expense any fees paid to creditor

**No gain/loss recognized!**

sections.title: Modification Example: > 10%
sections.content:
**Old debt: $1,000,000 at 8%, carrying value $980,000**
**New terms: $800,000 at 10% (PV difference > 10%)**
**New debt FV: $800,000**

**Accounting (extinguishment):**
• Derecognize old debt at carrying value
• Record new debt at fair value
• Gain = $980,000 − $800,000 = $180,000

**Entry:**
Dr Bonds Payable (old)        $1,000,000
    Cr Discount (old)              $20,000
    Cr Bonds Payable (new)        $800,000
    Cr Gain on Extinguishment     $180,000

sections.title: Troubled Debt Restructuring (TDR)
sections.content:
**TDR occurs when creditor grants concession due to debtor's financial difficulty.**

**Common concessions:**
• Reduction in principal amount
• Reduction in interest rate
• Extension of maturity date
• Transfer of assets or equity to creditor

**Two types of TDR:**
1. Settlement (transfer assets/equity)
2. Continuation with modified terms

sections.title: TDR: Settlement with Assets
sections.content:
**Debtor transfers assets to settle debt:**

**Debtor recognizes TWO gains/losses:**

1. **Asset disposition G/L:**
FV of asset − Book value of asset

2. **Debt restructuring gain:**
Carrying value of debt − FV of asset transferred

**Example:**
Debt: $500,000; Land (cost $300,000, FV $400,000)

Gain on land: $400,000 − $300,000 = $100,000
Gain on restructuring: $500,000 − $400,000 = $100,000

sections.title: TDR: Continuation with Modified Terms
sections.content:
**If total future cash flows ≥ carrying amount:**
• No gain recognized at restructuring
• New effective rate calculated
• Apply over remaining term

**If total future cash flows < carrying amount:**
• Recognize gain = Carrying amount − PV of new cash flows
• New carrying amount = Total future cash flows
• All cash payments reduce principal (no interest expense)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: TDR vs. Standard Modification
sections.content:
**Don't confuse TDR with normal modifications!**

**TDR requires BOTH:**
• Debtor in financial difficulty
• Creditor grants concession

**If either element is missing:**
• Use standard 10% test for modifications
• Different accounting treatment

**TDR rules are more favorable to debtor (may defer gain recognition)**

sections.title: In-Substance Defeasance
sections.content:
**Placing assets in trust to service debt:**

**True defeasance (legal release):**
• Debt is extinguished
• Recognize gain/loss

**In-substance defeasance (no legal release):**
• Under current GAAP, debt is generally NOT extinguished
• Still report liability
• Exception: Very limited circumstances

**Key:** Without legal release, you're still liable!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Debt Extinguishment: It's all about the paperwork!
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Debt modifications and extinguishments can feel like a giant word puzzle. But here's the real deal: it often boils down to the legal release. I've seen companies sweat bullets over minor details, but if the creditor hasn't *officially* released you, you're still on the hook! Think of it like breaking up with your gym membership – until you cancel in writing, you're still paying! So, pay attention to the paperwork, especially the fine print. Trust me, your future self will thank you when dealing with debt restructuring.

sections.title: Confusing Debt Modification with Extinguishment
sections.content: Students often confuse debt modification with debt extinguishment, especially when the interest rate or payment terms change significantly. A modification is NOT an extinguishment unless the debt is legally discharged or replaced with something substantially different. Think of it this way: if you renegotiate your car loan, you still have a car loan, just with new terms. However, if you sell the car and use the proceeds to pay off the loan, *that's* an extinguishment. Focus on whether the original debt obligation is truly gone, or merely altered.

sections.title: Sarah's Bond Buyback Blunder
sections.content: Sarah, a bright-eyed staff accountant, recorded a $50,000 loss on a bond buyback. The company repurchased bonds with a face value of $1,000,000 for $980,000. However, the bonds had $30,000 of unamortized discount and $10,000 of issuance costs. Sarah forgot to factor in the discount and issuance costs when calculating the carrying value. Remember: Carrying Value = Face +/- unamortized premium/discount - issuance costs. In this case, 1,000,000 - 30,000 - 10,000 = 960,000. Gain = 980,000 - 960,000 = $20,000.

sections.title: Debt Extinguishments: M&A and Restructuring
sections.content: You'll frequently encounter debt extinguishment scenarios during mergers and acquisitions (M&A) or corporate restructurings. For example, a company acquiring another might assume its debt and then refinance it at a lower rate, effectively extinguishing the old debt. Accurately calculating the gain or loss on extinguishment is crucial for financial statement accuracy and proper disclosures. You need to understand the details of the debt agreement, including any call provisions or prepayment penalties, to correctly account for the transaction. So, this is a key skill to develop.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Extinguishment G/L = Carrying Value − Reacquisition Price
Gain/loss on extinguishment reported in income statement
10% test: > 10% PV difference = extinguishment; ≤ 10% = modification
TDR requires debtor difficulty AND creditor concession
TDR settlement: Two gains possible (asset disposition + restructuring)
Without legal release, debt is NOT extinguished

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios definitively results in the extinguishment of debt for accounting purposes?
options: The debtor purchases U.S. Treasury securities and irrevocably places them in an escrow account to service the debt until maturity.
The debtor issues new debt with a lower interest rate than the existing debt and uses the proceeds to pay off the old debt.
The debtor's board of directors approves a plan to refinance the debt within the next year.
The debtor is legally released from being the primary obligor under the debt, whether judicially or by the creditor.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A legal release from being the primary obligor, judicially or by the creditor, definitively extinguishes the debt. While using treasury securities is a form of defeasance, its recognition is limited. Refinancing and plans to refinance do not extinguish debt.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the fundamental requirement for debt extinguishment: the debtor is no longer obligated.

sections.title: Gain or Loss on Debt Extinguishment

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the gain or loss on debt extinguishment?
reveal.answer: Gain/Loss = Carrying Value − Reacquisition Price.  Remember to consider the components of Carrying Value: Face Value ± unamortized premium/discount − issuance costs.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company extinguishes bonds with a face value of $500,000. The unamortized discount is $20,000, and unamortized issuance costs are $5,000. The reacquisition price is $480,000. What is the gain or loss on extinguishment?
options: Gain of $5,000
Loss of $5,000
Gain of $15,000
Loss of $15,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Carrying Value = $500,000 (Face Value) - $20,000 (Unamortized Discount) - $5,000 (Unamortized Issuance Costs) = $475,000. Gain/Loss = $475,000 (Carrying Value) - $480,000 (Reacquisition Price) = -$5,000. The question is asking about the *gain* or *loss* - the absolute value of the result is $5,000, but since the value is negative, this means the company had a $5,000 loss. The correct answer is loss of $5,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Carefully calculate the carrying value before determining the gain or loss.

title: Stock Issuances: Common & Preferred
description: Account for stock issuances for cash and noncash consideration

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Equity transactions don't affect the income statement (except for compensation), but they're critical to understanding the balance sheet. You must know how to record stock issuances, distinguish par from no-par stock, and properly allocate proceeds when stock is issued with other securities.

sections.title: Components of Stockholders Equity
sections.content:
**Stockholders' Equity consists of:**

**Contributed Capital:**
• Common stock (par or stated value)
• Preferred stock (par or stated value)
• Additional Paid-In Capital (APIC)
• Treasury stock (contra)

**Earned Capital:**
• Retained earnings

**Other:**
• Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (AOCI)

sections.title: Par vs. No-Par Stock
sections.content:
**Par Value Stock:**
• Arbitrary minimum legal value per share
• Record at par → Common/Preferred Stock
• Excess over par → Additional Paid-In Capital

**No-Par Stock:**
• No minimum legal value
• May have stated value (treated like par)
• If no stated value, entire proceeds → Common Stock

**Note:** Par value has little economic significance today

sections.title: Issuance for Cash: Par Value Stock
sections.content:
**Issue 10,000 shares, $1 par, at $25 per share:**

**Total proceeds:** 10,000 × $25 = $250,000
**Par value:** 10,000 × $1 = $10,000
**Excess (APIC):** $250,000 − $10,000 = $240,000

**Entry:**
Dr Cash                       $250,000
    Cr Common Stock                $10,000
    Cr APIC - Common              $240,000

sections.title: Issuance for Cash: No-Par Stock
sections.content:
**Issue 10,000 shares, no par, at $25 per share:**

**Option 1 (no stated value):**
Dr Cash                       $250,000
    Cr Common Stock               $250,000

**Option 2 (stated value of $5):**
Dr Cash                       $250,000
    Cr Common Stock (stated)       $50,000
    Cr APIC - Common              $200,000

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Stock Issuance Entry
sections.content:
**"Cash in, Equity out"**

**Always ask:**
1. What did we receive? → Debit (Cash or asset)
2. What's the par/stated value? → Credit to Stock account
3. What's the excess? → Credit to APIC

**Par value × Shares = Stock account**
**Everything else = APIC**

sections.title: Issuance for Noncash Consideration
sections.content:
**Stock issued for services, property, etc.:**

**Record at:**
• Fair value of stock issued, OR
• Fair value of consideration received
• **Use whichever is more clearly determinable**

**Entry (stock for land):**
Dr Land (at FV)               $XXX,XXX
    Cr Common Stock                $X,XXX
    Cr APIC - Common              $XX,XXX

content.definition: Unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before common dividends
Skipped dividends are lost forever
Shares in additional dividends beyond stated rate
Can be exchanged for common stock at holder's option
Issuer can repurchase at specified price
Holder can require repurchase (may be liability!)

sections.title: Preferred Stock Issuance
sections.content:
**Issue 5,000 shares of $100 par preferred at $105:**

**Entry:**
Dr Cash                       $525,000
    Cr Preferred Stock            $500,000
    Cr APIC - Preferred            $25,000

**Note:** Par value is often higher for preferred stock and may reflect liquidation preference.

sections.title: Lump-Sum Stock Issuance
sections.content:
**When common and preferred sold together for single price:**

**Allocation methods:**

**1. Proportional method (both FVs known):**
Allocate based on relative fair values

**2. Incremental method (only one FV known):**
Allocate known FV first, residual to other security

**Use proportional if both FVs determinable!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Redeemable Preferred Stock
sections.content:
**If preferred stock is MANDATORILY redeemable:**
• Classify as LIABILITY (not equity!)
• Dividends are interest expense

**SEC Rule:** Redeemable preferred at holder's option is reported OUTSIDE of stockholders' equity ("mezzanine")

**Key:** Redemption feature at holder's option means company may have to pay cash—that's a liability characteristic!

sections.title: Stock Issuance Costs
sections.content:
**Direct costs of issuing stock (underwriter fees, legal, etc.):**

**Treatment:** Reduce APIC (not expensed!)

**Entry:**
Dr APIC - Common              $XXX
    Cr Cash                        $XXX

**Rationale:** These are costs of a capital transaction, not operating expenses.

**Note:** Different from debt issuance costs (reduce liability, amortize)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Par Value: More Like Barely-There Value!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, par value is kinda like that one friend who's always 'technically' there but never really contributes. In the real world, its impact is minimal. Companies set it low, like a penny or a dollar, just to satisfy legal requirements. The real meat of the deal is in that Additional Paid-In Capital (APIC). So, don't get too hung up on the par value itself. Focus on understanding APIC, because that's where the money actually flows when a company issues stock!

sections.title: APIC Confusion: Where Does the Money Go?
sections.content: A common mistake is failing to correctly allocate the proceeds from a stock issuance between the stock account (Common or Preferred) and APIC. For example, if a company issues 1,000 shares of $1 par value common stock for $15 per share, students sometimes only credit the Common Stock account for $1,000 (1,000 shares * $1 par). The key is to remember that the difference between the issue price ($15,000) and the par value ($1,000) goes directly into APIC ($14,000). Always account for the full issuance price!

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Snafu: Non-Cash Contribution
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant, is struggling with a stock issuance where the company received a building in exchange for stock. The building's appraised value is $500,000, but its book value is only $300,000. The company issued 10,000 shares of $1 par value stock. The learning point: Sarah must record the transaction at the fair value of the building ($500,000), debiting the building account for $500,000, crediting Common Stock for $10,000, and crediting APIC for $490,000, regardless of the building's book value. Fair value rules!

sections.title: Stock Issuances in the Real World: IPOs & More
sections.content: You'll encounter stock issuance accounting constantly, especially during IPOs (Initial Public Offerings) or follow-on offerings. Companies raise capital by selling shares, and you need to accurately record the transactions. Think about valuing the non-cash consideration like services or property received in exchange for stock - this is crucial during audits. Being proficient in stock issuance accounting is essential for ensuring financial statement accuracy and compliance with accounting standards when a company decides to raise capital.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Par value × shares = Stock account; excess = APIC
No-par stock: All proceeds to stock (or stated value + APIC if stated value)
Noncash issuance: Use more clearly determinable fair value
Lump-sum: Proportional if both FVs known; incremental if only one known
Mandatorily redeemable preferred = LIABILITY, not equity
Stock issuance costs reduce APIC, not expensed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corporation issued 1,000 shares of $5 par value common stock for $15 per share. Which of the following is the correct journal entry to record this transaction?
options: Debit Cash $5,000, Credit Common Stock $5,000
Debit Cash $15,000, Credit Common Stock $15,000
Debit Cash $15,000, Credit Common Stock $5,000, Credit Additional Paid-In Capital $10,000
Debit Cash $5,000, Debit Additional Paid-In Capital $10,000, Credit Common Stock $15,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is C. Cash is debited for the total proceeds ($15 x 1,000 = $15,000). Common Stock is credited for the par value ($5 x 1,000 = $5,000). The excess ($15,000 - $5,000 = $10,000) is credited to Additional Paid-In Capital.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the definition of par value and how proceeds exceeding par are treated.

reveal.question: What are the three main categories of Stockholders' Equity?
reveal.answer: The three main categories of Stockholders' Equity are Contributed Capital, Earned Capital, and Other (Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income).

sections.title: Par Value vs. No-Par Value Stock

rows.label: Legal Minimum Value
values: Yes, an arbitrary minimum legal value per share
No, there is no minimum legal value per share

rows.label: Accounting Treatment
values: Recorded at par value; excess is Additional Paid-In Capital
Entire proceeds are recorded as Common Stock (unless a stated value exists)

rows.label: Stated Value
values: May have a stated value, which is treated like par value

title: Treasury Stock: Cost & Par Value Methods
description: Account for stock repurchases and reissuances under both methods

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When a company buys back its own stock, it becomes treasury stock—a contra-equity account. Understanding the two methods (cost and par value) and how to account for reissuance at different prices is essential. This is a frequently tested equity topic!

sections.title: What is Treasury Stock?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A corporation's own stock that has been issued and subsequently reacquired.

**Characteristics:**
• NOT an asset (can't own yourself!)
• Contra-equity account (reduces total equity)
• No voting rights
• No dividends
• Reduces shares outstanding (not shares authorized or issued)

**Reasons to repurchase:**
• Return cash to shareholders
• Support stock price
• Acquire shares for employee plans
• Reduce shares outstanding for EPS improvement

sections.title: Cost Method: Acquisition
sections.content:
**Repurchase 1,000 shares at $30 per share:**

**Entry:**
Dr Treasury Stock             $30,000
    Cr Cash                        $30,000

**Balance sheet:** Treasury stock shown as contra-equity at $30,000

**Simple!** Just record at what you paid.

sections.title: Cost Method: Reissuance Above Cost
sections.content:
**Reissue 500 treasury shares (cost $30) at $35:**

**Proceeds:** 500 × $35 = $17,500
**Cost:** 500 × $30 = $15,000
**Excess:** $2,500

**Entry:**
Dr Cash                       $17,500
    Cr Treasury Stock             $15,000
    Cr APIC - Treasury Stock       $2,500

**The excess goes to APIC (never gain on equity transactions!)**

sections.title: Cost Method: Reissuance Below Cost
sections.content:
**Reissue 500 treasury shares (cost $30) at $25:**

**Proceeds:** 500 × $25 = $12,500
**Cost:** 500 × $30 = $15,000
**Deficiency:** $2,500

**Entry:**
Dr Cash                       $12,500
Dr APIC - Treasury Stock       $2,500*
    Cr Treasury Stock             $15,000

*If no APIC from treasury exists, debit Retained Earnings

**Never report loss on equity transactions!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cost Method Reissuance
sections.content:
**"APIC absorbs, RE takes the hit"**

**Reissue ABOVE cost:**
→ Credit APIC - Treasury Stock (excess)

**Reissue BELOW cost:**
→ First reduce APIC - Treasury Stock (if any)
→ Then reduce Retained Earnings (if needed)

**Never recognize gain or loss in income!**

sections.title: Cost Method: Retirement
sections.content:
**When treasury stock is formally retired:**

**Entry removes:**
• Treasury stock at cost
• Original par value issued
• Pro rata APIC from original issuance

**If cost > original issue price:**
• Debit APIC, then Retained Earnings

**If cost < original issue price:**
• Credit APIC - Treasury Stock (or retirement)

sections.title: Par Value Method: Acquisition
sections.content:
