**Repurchase 1,000 shares ($1 par, originally issued at $20) at $30:**

**Entry:**
Dr Treasury Stock ($1 par)     $1,000
Dr APIC - Common              $19,000
Dr Retained Earnings          $10,000*
    Cr Cash                        $30,000

*Excess of cost over original issue price

**Par value method "undoes" the original issuance**

sections.title: Par Value Method: Reissuance
sections.content:
**Under par value method, treasury stock is at par, so:**

**Reissuance treated like NEW issuance!**

**Reissue at $35 (1,000 shares, $1 par):**
Dr Cash                       $35,000
    Cr Common Stock                $1,000
    Cr APIC - Common              $34,000

**Par value method: Reissuance = New issuance entry**

sections.title: Comparison: Methods Side by Side
sections.content:
**Original issue: 1,000 shares, $1 par, at $20**
**Repurchase at $30:**

**Cost Method:**
Dr Treasury Stock             $30,000
    Cr Cash                        $30,000

**Par Value Method:**
Dr Treasury Stock              $1,000
Dr APIC - Common              $19,000
Dr Retained Earnings          $10,000
    Cr Cash                        $30,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: No Income Effect!
sections.content:
**Treasury stock transactions NEVER affect income!**

**There is NO:**
• Gain on sale of treasury stock
• Loss on sale of treasury stock

**All effects are within equity:**
• APIC - Treasury Stock
• Retained Earnings (if APIC exhausted)

**Why?** You can't profit from trading with yourself!

sections.title: Donated Treasury Stock
sections.content:
**If shareholders donate stock back:**

**No cost was paid, so:**
• Treasury stock recorded at fair value
• Credit goes to APIC - Donated Capital

**Entry:**
Dr Treasury Stock (at FV)      $XXX
    Cr APIC - Donated Capital        $XXX

**When reissued, apply normal cost method rules with FV as "cost"**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Treasury Stock: My Biggest Headache (Solved!)
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest. Treasury stock accounting? Not exactly a party. I remember my first time, I was so confused by the cost and par value methods. I kept asking myself, 'Why are we buying back our own shares anyway?' Turns out, it's often about boosting earnings per share or preventing a hostile takeover. Master these methods now, especially the cost method – it's the popular kid. Trust me, understanding the journal entries for repurchase and reissuance will save you from a late-night accounting crisis later. You'll thank me (and yourself!).

sections.title: The Dividend Dilemma: Treasury Stock Trap!
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking treasury stock receives dividends. Remember, it's NOT outstanding stock. So, dividends are NOT paid on treasury shares. Students often miscalculate EPS because they incorrectly subtract the number of treasury shares from the *issued* shares instead of the *outstanding* shares. To avoid this, always think about who's actually holding the shares. Only shares held by external investors (outstanding shares) are entitled to dividends. Treasury shares are essentially 'retired' until reissued.

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Snafu: A Reissuance Riddle
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, is recording the reissuance of treasury stock. Her company repurchased 1,000 shares at $20 and reissued them at $25. Sarah debited cash for $25,000 and credited Treasury Stock for $20,000. Where should she credit the $5,000 difference? The key takeaway is that under the cost method, the difference is credited to Paid-In Capital from Treasury Stock Transactions (if it's a gain) or debited to Retained Earnings (if it's a loss, after exhausting any prior Paid-In Capital from Treasury Stock Transactions).

sections.title: Treasury Stock: A Board Meeting Must-Know
sections.content: In practice, understanding treasury stock is crucial for analyzing a company's financial health. You'll see it when preparing financial statement analysis for potential investors or during a due diligence process for a merger or acquisition. The board often uses treasury stock to manage earnings per share and stock options. Knowing how these repurchases affect shareholder equity and key ratios is essential for providing informed advice and making sound business decisions. This knowledge is not just academic—it's a key part of being a valuable financial professional.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Treasury stock is contra-equity, not an asset
Cost method: Record at purchase price; most common
Reissuance above cost: Credit APIC - Treasury Stock
Reissuance below cost: Debit APIC - TS first, then Retained Earnings
Par value method: Record at par, remove original APIC; reissue like new issuance
NEVER recognize gain or loss on treasury transactions—all within equity

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding treasury stock?
options: Treasury stock is an asset of the corporation.
Treasury stock increases the number of shares outstanding.
Treasury stock receives dividend payments.
Treasury stock reduces total equity.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Treasury stock represents a company's own shares that have been repurchased. Because a company cannot own itself, it is not an asset. It also reduces the number of shares outstanding and is not eligible for dividends. Because it reduces equity without affecting assets or liabilities, it is a contra-equity account.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the fundamental accounting equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. What happens to equity when a company buys back its own shares?

reveal.question: Is treasury stock considered an asset on the balance sheet?
reveal.answer: No, treasury stock is NOT an asset. It is a contra-equity account, meaning it reduces shareholders' equity.

reveal.question: Does treasury stock have voting rights?
reveal.answer: No, treasury stock does not have voting rights. Because the shares are held by the company itself, they cannot be voted.

title: Dividends: Cash, Property, Stock, Liquidating
description: Account for all types of dividends and their effects on equity

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Dividends are distributions to shareholders and reduce equity. Each type of dividend has different accounting—cash dividends reduce retained earnings and create a liability, property dividends require fair value remeasurement, and stock dividends move amounts within equity without changing total equity. Know the differences!

sections.title: Dividend Key Dates
sections.content:
**Three important dates:**

**1. Declaration Date:**
• Board declares dividend
• Liability created (for cash/property)
• Debit Retained Earnings

**2. Record Date:**
• Determines who receives dividend
• No journal entry!

**3. Payment Date:**
• Dividend distributed
• Liability settled

sections.title: Cash Dividends
sections.content:
**Board declares $1.00 per share dividend; 100,000 shares outstanding:**

**Declaration Date:**
Dr Retained Earnings         $100,000
    Cr Dividends Payable          $100,000

**Record Date:**
No entry

**Payment Date:**
Dr Dividends Payable         $100,000
    Cr Cash                       $100,000

sections.title: Property Dividends
sections.content:
**Dividend paid in assets other than cash (inventory, investments, etc.):**

**Step 1: Revalue property to FAIR VALUE**
Recognize gain or loss at declaration date!

**Step 2: Record dividend at fair value**
Debit Retained Earnings, Credit Liability

**Step 3: Distribute property**
Settle liability

sections.title: Property Dividend Example
sections.content:
**Declare dividend of investment securities:**
• Book value: $80,000
• Fair value: $100,000

**Declaration Date:**
Step 1 - Revalue:
Dr Investment                 $20,000
    Cr Gain on Investment          $20,000

Step 2 - Declare:
Dr Retained Earnings         $100,000
    Cr Property Dividends Payable $100,000

**Payment Date:**
Dr Property Dividends Payable $100,000
    Cr Investment                 $100,000

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Property Dividend Steps
sections.content:
**"Fair Value First, Then Declare"**

1. **Remeasure** property to FV (recognize G/L)
2. **Declare** dividend at FV (RE ↓, Liability ↑)
3. **Pay** with property (Liability ↓, Asset ↓)

**The gain/loss on revaluation DOES hit income!**
(Unlike treasury stock transactions)

sections.title: Stock Dividends: Overview
sections.content:
**Distribution of additional shares to existing shareholders:**

**Key characteristics:**
• No assets distributed
• Total equity UNCHANGED
• Transfers from Retained Earnings to Contributed Capital
• Shareholders' proportional ownership unchanged

**Two categories:**
• Small stock dividend (< 20-25%): Fair value
• Large stock dividend (≥ 20-25%): Par value

sections.title: Small Stock Dividend (< 20-25%)
sections.content:
**Declare 10% stock dividend; 100,000 shares outstanding:**
**Par: $1; FMV: $30**

**New shares:** 100,000 × 10% = 10,000 shares

**Entry at declaration:**
Dr Retained Earnings (10,000 × $30)  $300,000
    Cr Common Stock Distributable        $10,000
    Cr APIC - Common                     $290,000

**At distribution:**
Dr Common Stock Distributable  $10,000
    Cr Common Stock                $10,000

sections.title: Large Stock Dividend (≥ 20-25%)
sections.content:
**Declare 50% stock dividend; 100,000 shares outstanding:**
**Par: $1; FMV: $30**

**New shares:** 100,000 × 50% = 50,000 shares

**Entry at declaration (use PAR, not FMV):**
Dr Retained Earnings (50,000 × $1)  $50,000
    Cr Common Stock Distributable       $50,000

**At distribution:**
Dr Common Stock Distributable  $50,000
    Cr Common Stock                $50,000

**No APIC for large stock dividends!**

sections.title: Stock Splits
sections.content:
**Increase shares by reducing par value:**

**2-for-1 split:**
• Shares double
• Par value halves
• Total equity unchanged

**Entry:** Usually NO journal entry (memo only)

Before: 100,000 shares × $2 par = $200,000
After: 200,000 shares × $1 par = $200,000

**Reverse split:** Opposite effect (e.g., 1-for-5)

sections.title: Liquidating Dividends
sections.content:
**Return of CAPITAL (not earnings):**

**Characteristics:**
• Reduces contributed capital, not retained earnings
• Occurs when:
  - Company winding down
  - Dividends exceed retained earnings
  - Intentional return of capital

**Entry:**
Dr APIC - Common (or other capital) $XXX
    Cr Dividends Payable               $XXX

**Disclose liquidating nature to shareholders!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cumulative Preferred Dividends
sections.content:
**Dividends in arrears on cumulative preferred:**

• NOT a liability until declared!
• Do NOT record until board declares
• Disclose in notes to financial statements

**But:** Must be paid before common dividends

**Example:** 2 years of dividends in arrears = footnote disclosure only (until declared)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Dividend Dates: Don't Be Late!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Those dividend dates (declaration, record, payment) might seem simple, but messing them up on the exam is a surefire way to lose points. Think of it like this: declaration is the announcement, record is the guest list for the dividend party, and payment is when the checks go out. Don't show up to the payment date without being on the record date list! Get those dates drilled into your head, and you'll be golden.

sections.title: Fair Value Confusion with Property Dividends
sections.content: A common mistake students make is forgetting to revalue property dividends to fair value *before* recording the dividend. They might accidentally use the book value, which is incorrect. Remember, the company is essentially selling the asset at fair value, distributing the proceeds as a dividend. This means you *must* recognize any gain or loss on the disposal of the asset (difference between book value and fair value) at the declaration date. This gain or loss impacts net income and ultimately retained earnings.

sections.title: Sarah's Stumbling Block: Property Dividend Woes
sections.content: Sarah, an eager CPA candidate, was reviewing a company's financial statements where a piece of land with a book value of $500,000 was declared as a property dividend when its fair value was $700,000. Sarah initially forgot to record the $200,000 gain on the revaluation of the land. Always remember to recognize the gain or loss before recording the dividend itself. Neglecting this step will understate net income and retained earnings.

sections.title: Dividends: More Than Just Cash Payments
sections.content: In practice, you'll often see companies distributing assets other than cash as dividends, especially when they have excess inventory or investments they want to get rid of. For example, a real estate company might distribute land as a dividend, or a tech company might distribute shares of another company it owns. Understanding how to account for these property dividends is crucial for accurately assessing a company's financial health and its ability to return value to shareholders.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Declaration creates liability; record date = no entry; payment settles liability
Property dividends: Remeasure to FV (recognize G/L), then declare at FV
Small stock dividend (< 20-25%): Use fair value; Large (≥ 20-25%): Use par
Stock dividends transfer RE to contributed capital; total equity unchanged
Stock splits: No entry usually; par value changes; total equity unchanged
Cumulative dividends in arrears: Disclose but no liability until declared

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: On December 1, Year 1, the board of directors of ABC Corp. declared a cash dividend of $1.00 per share payable on January 15, Year 2, to shareholders of record on December 31, Year 1. How would this dividend affect ABC Corp.'s balance sheet on December 31, Year 1?
options: Increase assets and decrease liabilities.
Decrease assets and increase liabilities.
No effect on total assets or total liabilities.
Decrease retained earnings and increase liabilities.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: On the declaration date (December 1), Retained Earnings is debited and a liability (Dividends Payable) is credited. By December 31, the liability still exists, and Retained Earnings has been reduced. Therefore, the correct answer is a decrease in retained earnings and an increase in liabilities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider when the liability is created and when it is settled.

reveal.question: What is the significance of the record date in the dividend process?
reveal.answer: The record date determines which shareholders are eligible to receive the declared dividend. No journal entry is recorded on the record date.

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. declares a property dividend of available-for-sale securities with a book value of $50,000 and a fair value of $80,000. The securities are distributed shortly after the declaration date. What is the impact on XYZ Corp.'s income statement?
options: A gain of $30,000.
A loss of $30,000.
No impact on the income statement.
A gain of $80,000.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When a property dividend is declared, the asset is revalued to fair value, and any gain or loss is recognized. In this case, the gain is $80,000 (fair value) - $50,000 (book value) = $30,000. This gain is recognized on the income statement.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to revalue the property to fair value before recording the dividend.

title: Stock Compensation: Options, RSUs, ESPPs
description: Account for share-based payments to employees under ASC 718

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Stock compensation is how companies pay employees with equity instead of cash. Under ASC 718, the fair value of equity awards must be recognized as compensation expense over the service period. This is complex and heavily tested—you need to understand options, RSUs, and the accounting for modifications and forfeitures.

sections.title: Stock Compensation Basics
sections.content:
**ASC 718 core principle:**
Recognize compensation expense based on **grant-date fair value** of equity instruments over the **requisite service period**.

**Key concepts:**
• **Grant date:** Date award is approved and terms set
• **Service period:** Period employee must work to earn award (usually vesting period)
• **Fair value:** Measured at grant date; not remeasured (for equity awards)

content.definition: Right to buy stock at fixed price (exercise price) after vesting
Actual shares granted but subject to forfeiture until vested
Promise to deliver shares upon vesting (no shares until vested)
Right to receive cash or stock equal to appreciation
Employee Stock Purchase Plan—buy stock at discount

sections.title: Stock Options: Accounting
sections.content:
**At grant date:**
• Determine fair value (usually Black-Scholes or binomial model)
• No entry on grant date

**Over service period:**
• Recognize compensation expense ratably
• Credit APIC - Stock Options

**At exercise:**
• Cash received (exercise price × shares)
• Remove APIC - Stock Options
• Issue stock (credit Common Stock + APIC)

sections.title: Stock Option Example
sections.content:
**Grant: 1,000 options, exercise price $50, FV $10 each**
**Vesting: 4 years (cliff)**

**Total compensation:** 1,000 × $10 = $10,000
**Annual expense:** $10,000 ÷ 4 = $2,500

**Each year (Years 1-4):**
Dr Compensation Expense        $2,500
    Cr APIC - Stock Options        $2,500

**At exercise (all 1,000 options; par $1):**
Dr Cash (1,000 × $50)         $50,000
Dr APIC - Stock Options       $10,000
    Cr Common Stock                $1,000
    Cr APIC - Common              $59,000

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Option Accounting
sections.content:
**"Grant = Value it; Vest = Expense it; Exercise = Cash it"**

**Grant date:** Measure fair value (no entry)
**Vesting period:** Recognize expense (Dr Expense, Cr APIC)
**Exercise:** Cash in, APIC out, issue stock

**Key:** Fair value is FIXED at grant date—don't remeasure!

sections.title: Restricted Stock Units (RSUs)
sections.content:
**RSUs = Promise to deliver shares when vested**

**Key difference from options:**
• No exercise price (employee pays nothing)
• Fair value = stock price at grant date
• Full value delivered at vesting

**Accounting similar to options:**
• Expense over service period
• At vesting, issue shares (debit APIC, credit Stock + APIC)

sections.title: RSU Example
sections.content:
**Grant: 1,000 RSUs, stock price $30, 3-year vesting**

**Total compensation:** 1,000 × $30 = $30,000
**Annual expense:** $30,000 ÷ 3 = $10,000

**Each year:**
Dr Compensation Expense       $10,000
    Cr APIC - RSUs                $10,000

**At vesting (shares issued; par $1):**
Dr APIC - RSUs                $30,000
    Cr Common Stock                $1,000
    Cr APIC - Common              $29,000

sections.title: Forfeitures
sections.content:
**When employee leaves before vesting:**

**ASC 718 options:**

**Option 1: Estimate forfeitures at grant**
• Reduce compensation expense by expected forfeitures
• True-up when actual forfeitures known

**Option 2: Recognize forfeitures when they occur**
• Simpler—recognize full expense, then reverse if forfeited

**Either method is acceptable (policy election)**

sections.title: Forfeiture Example
sections.content:
**100 options granted, $10 FV, 3-year vest**
**Year 2: Employee leaves (not vested)**

**Year 1 entry:**
Dr Compensation Expense        $333
    Cr APIC - Stock Options         $333

**Year 2 - Forfeiture reversal:**
Dr APIC - Stock Options        $333
    Cr Compensation Expense         $333

**Net effect: No compensation recognized (award not earned)**

sections.title: Modifications
sections.content:
**If terms of award are modified:**

**Calculate incremental value:**
FV of modified award − FV of original award (both at modification date)

**Treatment:**
• Continue recognizing original compensation
• PLUS recognize incremental value
• Over remaining service period

**If modification is improbable to vest → equity to liability**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Performance Conditions
sections.content:
**Two types of performance conditions:**

**Service condition (e.g., stay 4 years):**
• Included in determining service period
• Expense recognized if probable

**Market condition (e.g., stock price reaches $100):**
• Included in FAIR VALUE at grant
• Recognized regardless of whether condition is met

**Key:** Market conditions affect FV measurement; service conditions affect whether expense is recognized.

sections.title: ESPP (Employee Stock Purchase Plans)
sections.content:
**Plan allowing employees to buy stock at discount:**

**Compensatory if:**
• Discount > 5%, OR
• Not available to all employees, OR
• Other favorable terms

**Compensatory plans:**
• Recognize compensation expense
• Like other stock compensation

**Non-compensatory plans (meet safe harbor):**
• No compensation expense
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Stock Options: The 'Free Money' Illusion
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Stock options sound like free money, right? But accounting for them is where the party stops. Remember ASC 718's core principle: grant-date fair value over the service period. Companies use Black-Scholes or binomial models to determine this fair value, but trust me, understanding the inputs and how they affect the final expense is crucial. Don't just blindly accept the model output! Know what drives the numbers, or you'll be chasing your tail trying to reconcile differences later on. It's not free money till the expense is correctly accounted for!

sections.title: Forgetting the Service Period Cliff
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming the service period always equals the vesting period. It doesn't! If an employee needs to work for *at least* three years to get *any* options, but the options vest ratably after that point, the service period is still three years. Many students calculate expense over a shorter period, leading to understated expenses in early years and overstated expenses later. Always carefully read the stock option plan to determine the true service period, including any cliff vesting requirements.

sections.title: RSUs and John's Unexpected Departure
sections.content: John, a marketing manager, received RSUs vesting over four years. After two years, John unexpectedly quits. The company needs to determine the accounting treatment for these unvested RSUs. The learning point here is that since John didn't complete the service period for the remaining RSUs, the company must reverse any compensation expense previously recognized related to those unvested RSUs. It's not just about the initial grant; tracking forfeitures is equally important.

sections.title: Annual Audit and Stock Comp Disclosures
sections.content: In a typical annual audit, you'll review the company's stock compensation expense calculation and related disclosures. Auditors will scrutinize the assumptions used in the valuation model (Black-Scholes, etc.) and test the accuracy of the expense recognition schedule. You'll also need to ensure the company complies with disclosure requirements, including the number of options outstanding, weighted-average exercise price, and the assumptions used in the fair value calculation. Mastering this area now will make those audit walkthroughs smoother and less stressful.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Measure fair value at GRANT DATE; expense over SERVICE PERIOD
Stock options: FV determined by option pricing model (Black-Scholes)
RSUs: FV = stock price at grant (no exercise price)
At exercise/vesting: Cash (if any) + APIC - Options → Stock + APIC
Forfeitures: Either estimate upfront or recognize when they occur
Market conditions affect FV; service conditions affect recognition

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: On January 1, Year 1, ABC Company grants 1,000 stock options to each of its 5 executives. Each option allows the executive to purchase one share of ABC Company stock at an exercise price of $50 per share. The fair value of each option on the grant date is $15. The options vest ratably over a 3-year service period. What journal entry, if any, should ABC Company record on January 1, Year 1 related to the stock option grant?
options: Debit Compensation Expense $75,000, Credit APIC - Stock Options $75,000
Debit Deferred Compensation $75,000, Credit Stock Options $75,000
No journal entry is required.
Debit Cash $75,000, Credit APIC - Stock Options $75,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under ASC 718, no journal entry is recorded on the grant date of stock options. The fair value is determined, but the expense is recognized over the service period.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about when the company actually recognizes the compensation expense.

reveal.question: What is the definition of the 'Grant Date' under ASC 718?
reveal.answer: The grant date is the date the award is approved and the terms of the stock compensation are definitively set. This is the date the fair value of the award is determined.

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Company grants stock options to its employees with a total fair value of $600,000. The options vest over a 4-year service period. Assuming no forfeitures, what amount of compensation expense should XYZ Company recognize in Year 2?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Compensation expense is recognized ratably over the service period. Therefore, the expense recognized each year is $600,000 / 4 years = $150,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the core principle of ASC 718: recognize expense over the service period.

title: ASC 606: Five-Step Revenue Model
description: Master the comprehensive framework for recognizing revenue from contracts with customers under ASC 606

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: ASC 606 is one of the MOST HEAVILY TESTED topics on FAR! This single standard governs virtually all revenue recognition (except leases, insurance, and financial instruments). The five-step model is the foundation—master this and you'll handle any revenue question the exam throws at you.

sections.title: The Five Steps: The Complete Framework
sections.content:
**ASC 606 Core Principle:** Recognize revenue to depict the transfer of promised goods or services to customers in an amount that reflects the consideration the entity expects to receive.

**The Five Steps (memorize this order!):**

**Step 1:** Identify the contract(s) with a customer
**Step 2:** Identify the performance obligations in the contract
**Step 3:** Determine the transaction price
**Step 4:** Allocate the transaction price to performance obligations
**Step 5:** Recognize revenue when (or as) performance obligations are satisfied

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Five Steps
sections.content:
**"I I D A R"** or **"I Identify Dollars And Recognize"**

1. **I**dentify the contract
2. **I**dentify performance obligations
3. **D**etermine transaction price
4. **A**llocate price to obligations
5. **R**ecognize revenue when satisfied

**Think: "Before I can recognize revenue, I need to know what contract, what promises, how much money, how to split it, and when I've delivered."

sections.title: Step 1: Identify the Contract
sections.content:
**A contract exists when ALL five criteria are met:**

1. **Approval and commitment** – Parties have approved and are committed
2. **Rights identified** – Each party's rights regarding goods/services can be identified
3. **Payment terms identified** – Payment terms can be identified
4. **Commercial substance** – The contract has commercial substance
5. **Collectibility probable** – Collection of consideration is probable

**If criteria NOT met:** Don't recognize revenue until met OR cash received (and nonrefundable)

sections.title: Contract Criteria Mnemonic
sections.content:
**"A R P C C"** – **A**pproval, **R**ights, **P**ayment terms, **C**ommercial substance, **C**ollectibility

**Or remember:** "Are Really Poor Contracts Collectible?" (Probably not!)

sections.title: Step 2: Identify Performance Obligations
sections.content:
**Performance obligation:** A promise to transfer a distinct good or service (or bundle).

**A good or service is DISTINCT if BOTH:**

1. **Capable of being distinct** – Customer can benefit from it alone or with readily available resources
2. **Distinct within the contract** – Promise is separately identifiable from other promises

**Series guidance:** Multiple distinct goods/services that are substantially the same with the same pattern of transfer = single performance obligation (e.g., monthly cleaning services)

sections.title: Distinct vs. Not Distinct
rows: Customer can use phone with other carriers
If highly interrelated = 1 PO; if standard install = 2 POs
Combined output—customer wants building, not separate parts
Shipping available from others; separately identifiable
Assurance warranty = not separate; service warranty = separate PO

sections.title: Step 3: Determine Transaction Price
sections.content:
**Transaction price:** Amount of consideration expected in exchange for goods/services (excludes amounts collected for third parties like sales tax).

**Components to consider:**

• **Fixed consideration** – Stated contract price
• **Variable consideration** – Discounts, rebates, refunds, bonuses, penalties
• **Constraining variable consideration** – Only include if highly probable no significant reversal
• **Significant financing component** – Adjust if > 1 year between payment and transfer
• **Noncash consideration** – Measure at fair value
• **Consideration payable to customer** – Reduce transaction price (unless for distinct goods/services)

sections.title: Step 4: Allocate Transaction Price
sections.content:
**Allocation basis:** Stand-alone selling prices (SSP)

**Determine SSP using (in order of preference):**
1. **Observable price** – Price when sold separately
2. **Adjusted market assessment** – What market would pay
3. **Expected cost plus margin** – Your costs + reasonable margin
4. **Residual approach** – Only when SSP highly variable/uncertain

**Allocation formula:**

Revenue for PO = (SSP of PO ÷ Total SSP of all POs) × Transaction Price

sections.title: Allocation Example
sections.content:
**TechCo sells a bundle for $900:**
• Software license (SSP $600)
• 1-year support (SSP $400)
• Total SSP: $1,000

**Allocation:**

**Software:** $600 ÷ $1,000 × $900 = **$540**
**Support:** $400 ÷ $1,000 × $900 = **$360**

**Revenue recognition:**
• $540 when software delivered (point in time)
• $360 ratably over 12 months (over time)

sections.title: Step 5: Recognize Revenue
sections.content:
**Revenue is recognized when control transfers to customer:**

**Over time** – if ANY of these criteria met:
1. Customer simultaneously receives and consumes benefits
2. Entity's performance creates/enhances asset customer controls
3. Entity's performance creates asset with no alternative use AND entity has enforceable right to payment for performance to date

**Point in time** – if none of the above criteria met

**Point-in-time indicators:**
• Entity has present right to payment
• Customer has legal title
• Physical possession transferred
• Significant risks and rewards transferred
• Customer has accepted the asset

sections.title: Over Time vs Point in Time
rows: No alternative use + right to payment
Control of IP at grant date

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Contract Modifications
sections.content:
**Contract modifications can be:**

1. **Separate contract** – If adds distinct goods/services at SSP → account for separately

2. **Termination + new contract** – If remaining goods/services distinct but not at SSP → cumulative catch-up NOT allowed; prospective treatment

3. **Part of original contract** – If remaining goods NOT distinct → cumulative catch-up adjustment

**Exam loves to test modification accounting!**

sections.title: Bill-and-Hold Arrangements
sections.content:
**Bill-and-hold:** Customer is billed but seller retains physical possession.

**Revenue recognized when ALL criteria met:**
1. Reason for arrangement is substantive (customer requested)
2. Product separately identified as belonging to customer
3. Product currently ready for transfer
4. Entity cannot use product or direct it to another customer

**If criteria met:** Recognize revenue; also consider separate storage service PO

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**TechBuild Corp signs a $500,000 contract:**
• Custom software development ($300,000 SSP)
• Hardware ($150,000 SSP)
• 2-year maintenance ($100,000 SSP)
• Total SSP: $550,000

**Step 1:** Contract criteria met ✓
**Step 2:** 3 performance obligations identified
**Step 3:** Transaction price = $500,000
**Step 4:** Allocate:
• Software: $300K/$550K × $500K = $272,727
• Hardware: $150K/$550K × $500K = $136,364
• Maintenance: $100K/$550K × $500K = $90,909

**Step 5:** Recognize:
• Software: Over time (no alternative use, right to payment) - % complete
• Hardware: Point in time (delivery)
• Maintenance: Over time (ratably over 24 months)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: ASC 606: It's All About the Benjamins (Eventually)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, ASC 606 can feel like wading through molasses. But think of it this way: it's how companies get to count their money! Seriously. The five-step model might seem tedious, but it dictates *when* you can recognize revenue. Nail this, and you'll understand how companies like Netflix or Amazon actually book their income. Mess it up, and you're looking at a restatement faster than you can say 'material misstatement.' So, buckle up, and let's get those Benjamins recognized... eventually!

sections.title: Contract Criteria Confusion: It's NOT Just a Verbal Agreement!
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming *any* agreement is a contract under ASC 606 Step 1. Many students think a handshake deal or a preliminary discussion counts. Wrong! Remember, ALL five criteria must be met. For example, if the parties haven't actually approved and committed to the agreement (criterion 1), or the rights haven't been clearly identified (criterion 2), you don't have a contract for revenue recognition purposes. This means you CANNOT start the revenue recognition process. Always check the fine print and look for formal acceptance.

sections.title: Sarah's Software Snafu: When Does the Clock Start?
sections.content: Sarah, a junior accountant, is reviewing a contract for custom software development. The client and Sarah's company have exchanged emails outlining the project, but haven't signed a formal agreement. Sarah wants to start recognizing revenue as her team begins coding. The problem? No signed contract means Step 1 of ASC 606 isn't met. Sarah needs a fully executed contract before she can even think about recognizing a dime of revenue. Otherwise, she's jumping the gun!

sections.title: ASC 606: Your Daily Grind in Audit
sections.content: In audit, you'll be using the five-step model constantly to verify revenue recognition. Think about auditing a SaaS company: You'll need to examine their contracts to ensure they're recognizing subscription revenue appropriately over the service period. Or, imagine auditing a construction company. You'll have to analyze long-term construction contracts to confirm revenue is recognized as performance obligations are satisfied. Mastering Step 1, identifying the contract, is the foundation. Without a valid contract, the rest of the revenue recognition analysis is meaningless. Get it right, and you'll save yourself (and your team) a lot of headaches.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Five steps: Identify contract → Identify POs → Determine price → Allocate → Recognize
Contract requires: Approval, rights, payment terms, commercial substance, collectibility probable
Distinct = capable of being distinct + distinct within contract
Transaction price includes variable consideration (if highly probable no reversal)
Allocate based on relative stand-alone selling prices
Over time if: simultaneous receive/consume, asset enhanced, or no alternative use + right to payment
Point in time: when control transfers (title, possession, risk/rewards, acceptance)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion that must be met for a contract to exist under ASC 606, Step 1?
options: The parties to the contract have approved the contract and are committed to performing their respective obligations.
Each party's rights regarding the goods or services to be transferred can be identified.
The payment terms for the goods or services to be transferred can be identified.
The contract has a guaranteed profit margin for the seller.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A guaranteed profit margin is not a requirement for a contract to exist under ASC 606. The five criteria are: approval and commitment, rights identified, payment terms identified, commercial substance, and probable collectibility.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the five criteria that must be met for a contract to exist.

reveal.question: List the five steps of the revenue recognition model under ASC 606 in the correct order.
reveal.answer:
1. Identify the contract(s) with a customer
2. Identify the performance obligations in the contract
3. Determine the transaction price
4. Allocate the transaction price to the performance obligations
5. Recognize revenue when (or as) the entity satisfies a performance obligation

knowledgeCheck.question: Under ASC 606, the core principle of revenue recognition is that an entity should recognize revenue when?
options: When the customer pays for the goods or services.
When the contract is signed.
To depict the transfer of promised goods or services to customers in an amount that reflects the consideration the entity expects to be entitled to.
When the goods are delivered to the customer's location.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The core principle of ASC 606 states that revenue should be recognized to depict the transfer of promised goods or services to customers in an amount that reflects the consideration the entity expects to be entitled to.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what the entity is truly earning, not just when cash changes hands.

title: Identifying Performance Obligations
description: Deep dive into determining when promises are distinct performance obligations requiring separate revenue recognition

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Step 2 of ASC 606 is where many candidates struggle. Correctly identifying performance obligations determines HOW MUCH revenue to recognize and WHEN. Get this wrong, and your entire revenue calculation is off. The exam loves to test whether goods/services are distinct!

sections.title: What is a Performance Obligation?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A promise in a contract to transfer to the customer either:

1. A good or service (or bundle of goods/services) that is **distinct**, OR
2. A **series** of distinct goods/services that are substantially the same and have the same pattern of transfer

**Key insight:** One contract can have one or many performance obligations. Each PO is a separate unit of account for revenue recognition.

sections.title: The Two-Part Distinct Test
sections.content:
**A good or service is distinct if BOTH criteria are met:**

**Criterion 1: Capable of Being Distinct**
Customer can benefit from the good or service either:
• On its own, OR
• Together with other resources readily available to the customer

**Criterion 2: Distinct Within the Context of the Contract**
The promise to transfer is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract.

**Both must be YES for a separate performance obligation!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Two-Part Test
sections.content:
**"Can they USE it? Can you SEPARATE it?"**

**Part 1 (Customer view):** Can the customer get value from this alone or easily combine it?
**Part 2 (Contract view):** Is this promise separate from other promises, or are they interrelated?

**Think of it as:**
• Part 1 = Is it useful?
• Part 2 = Is it separate?

sections.title: Criterion 1: Capable of Being Distinct
sections.content:
**Factors indicating capable of being distinct:**

• Entity regularly sells the item separately
• Customer could resell the item
• Item has stand-alone functionality
• Item can be used with readily available resources

**Examples of readily available resources:**
• Items sold separately by the entity or others
• Resources customer already has

**NOT capable of being distinct:**
• Highly specialized component with no use alone
• Item that requires other goods only available from entity

sections.title: Criterion 2: Distinct Within the Contract
sections.content:
**Factors indicating NOT separately identifiable (NOT distinct within contract):**

1. **Significant integration** – Entity uses goods/services as inputs to produce combined output
2. **Significant modification/customization** – One item significantly modifies/customizes another
3. **Highly interdependent** – Each item significantly affected by the other(s)

**If any of these exist → goods/services should be combined into single PO**

sections.title: Decision Framework
rows: Can customer benefit alone or with readily available resources?
Does entity significantly integrate the items?

sections.title: Common Scenarios: Distinct
sections.content:
**✓ DISTINCT (Separate POs):**

**1. Phone + Wireless Service**
Phone can work with other carriers → capable of distinct
Service works without their phone → capable of distinct
Not integrated/interdependent → distinct within contract
**Result: 2 POs**

**2. Equipment + Extended Warranty**
Equipment works without warranty → capable of distinct
Warranty is a service-type warranty → separately identifiable
**Result: 2 POs**

**3. Software License + Training**
Software functional without training → capable of distinct
Training doesn't customize software → distinct within contract
**Result: 2 POs**

sections.title: Common Scenarios: Not Distinct
sections.content:
**✗ NOT DISTINCT (Combine into 1 PO):**

**1. Construction Contract (Materials + Labor)**
Customer wants a building, not separate materials
Significant integration service provided
**Result: 1 PO (combined)**

**2. Custom Software + Installation + Testing**
Software highly customized for customer
Installation/testing integral to customization
**Result: 1 PO (combined)**

**3. Consulting + Proprietary Tools**
Tools only work with entity's consulting
Consulting requires the tools
**Result: 1 PO (combined)**

sections.title: Series of Distinct Goods/Services
sections.content:
**Series guidance:** Multiple distinct goods/services that meet BOTH:

1. **Substantially the same** – Each distinct good/service is substantially similar
2. **Same pattern of transfer** – Same method to measure progress (single measure)

**If BOTH met → Account as SINGLE performance obligation**

**Examples:**
• Monthly cleaning services (same service repeated)
• Daily processing transactions
• Monthly IT support

**Why?** Simplifies accounting—don't need separate allocation for each instance

sections.title: Warranty Example
sections.content:
**AutoCo sells car for $30,000 with:**
• 3-year/36,000-mile warranty (standard, cannot be purchased separately)
• Optional 5-year/60,000-mile extended warranty available for $1,500

**Analysis:**

**Standard warranty:** Assurance-type → NOT a separate PO
• Covers defects existing at sale
• Customer cannot purchase separately
• Accrue warranty liability at sale

**Extended warranty (if purchased):** Service-type → SEPARATE PO
• Provides service beyond assurance
• Available for separate purchase
• Allocate portion of price, recognize over period

sections.title: Options for Additional Goods/Services
sections.content:
**Customer options (like renewal options, discounts):**

**Separate PO if option provides MATERIAL RIGHT:**
• Right customer would not receive without entering contract
• Significant discount beyond typical discounts

**Examples of material rights:**
• "Buy 10, get 1 free" (if incremental)
• Significant renewal discount not available to new customers
• Free maintenance for first year (if normally charged)

**Accounting:** Allocate transaction price to the material right; recognize when option exercised or expires

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Shipping and Handling
sections.content:
**Shipping/handling activities:**

**Policy election available:**
1. **Separate PO** – Recognize shipping revenue when shipped
2. **Fulfillment cost** – Not a separate PO; expense as incurred

**Key point:** If shipping occurs AFTER control transfers to customer, shipping is a SEPARATE PO (entity is providing service on customer's goods).

**Exam tip:** Read carefully whether control transfers before or after shipping!

sections.title: Principal vs Agent Considerations
sections.content:
**When another party is involved in providing goods/services:**

**Principal:** Controls goods/services BEFORE transfer to customer
• Recognize revenue GROSS (full amount)

**Agent:** Arranges for another party to provide goods/services
• Recognize revenue NET (commission only)

**Control indicators for PRINCIPAL:**
• Primary responsibility for fulfillment
• Inventory risk (before/after transfer, during shipping, returns)
• Discretion in establishing price

**This affects WHAT your performance obligation is!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Distinct? More Like 'Distinction-Impossible'
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. 'Distinct' sounds simple, but ASC 606 throws curveballs. I've seen contracts where companies try to bundle services to avoid recognizing revenue early. Think about it: a software company offers 'free' installation. Is that really free? Or is it essential to the software's functionality? If the customer can't use the software *without* the installation, it's likely NOT a distinct performance obligation. Don't let companies pull a fast one on you! Dig deep to see if the customer truly benefits from each element separately.

sections.title: Confusing 'Capable' with 'Actually' Distinct
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming that because a customer *could* theoretically benefit from a good or service separately, it automatically *is* a distinct performance obligation. Just because a customer *could* use a component on its own doesn't mean that's how it's intended or that the customer is likely to do so. Focus on the contract's specifics and the customer's intentions. If the goods/services are highly interdependent and integrated, it's probably one performance obligation, even if technically separable.

sections.title: Sarah's Software Snafu: One Obligation or Two?
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, is reviewing a contract for software and related support services. The customer can purchase the software independently, but the support is essential for the software to function properly. The contract states the customer can benefit from the software on its own. However, Sarah knows that without the support, the software is unusable. The key takeaway? Even if the contract *says* it's distinct, consider the practical reality. In Sarah's case, likely one performance obligation.

sections.title: Real-World Revenue Recognition: It's Everywhere!
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter this constantly when auditing software companies, construction companies, and even retailers offering extended warranties. Think about a construction project: is the foundation a distinct performance obligation from the framing? Or are they so interdependent that they're essentially one? Mastering the concept of distinct performance obligations is critical for correctly recognizing revenue and ensuring financial statements accurately reflect a company's performance. Get this right, and you'll be a revenue recognition rockstar!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Distinct requires BOTH: capable of being distinct AND distinct within the contract
Capable = customer can benefit alone or with readily available resources
Distinct within contract = NOT significantly integrated, modified, or interdependent
Series of substantially same items with same transfer pattern = 1 PO
Assurance warranty = NOT separate PO; Service warranty = separate PO
Customer option is separate PO only if it provides a MATERIAL RIGHT
Principal (gross) vs Agent (net) affects how you identify your PO

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: SoftwareCo enters into a contract with a customer to provide a software license, installation services, and ongoing technical support for a period of three years. Installation services are routinely performed by other companies in the market and do not significantly customize the software. The technical support is also sold separately by SoftwareCo. How many performance obligations exist in this contract under ASC 606?
options: One performance obligation (the entire contract)
Two performance obligations (software license and combined installation/support)
Two performance obligations (combined software license and installation, and technical support)
Three performance obligations (software license, installation, and technical support)
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is three performance obligations. The software license, installation services, and technical support are all distinct. Installation is distinct because the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and it is distinct within the context of the contract because it is not highly interrelated or interdependent with the other promises. Technical support is also sold separately, indicating distinctness.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether each element in the contract meets both criteria of the distinct test.

reveal.question: What are the two criteria that must be met for a good or service to be considered 'distinct'?
reveal.answer:
Criterion 1: The customer can benefit from the good or service either on its own or together with other resources that are readily available to the customer.
Criterion 2: The promise to transfer the good or service is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract.

sections.title: Standalone Selling Price vs. Readily Available Resources

rows.label: Definition
values: The price at which an entity would sell a promised good or service separately to a customer.
Resources that the customer has (already obtained from the entity or from other sources) that would allow the customer to benefit from the related goods or services

rows.label: Relevance to Distinctness
values: Evidence that a good or service is distinct because it is sold separately.
Indicates that a customer can benefit from a good or service on its own or together with those resources, meeting part of the distinctness criteria.

rows.label: Example
values: Hardware needed to run the software, purchased from a third party.

title: Variable Consideration & Constraints
description: Master the estimation and constraint of variable consideration including discounts, rebates, refunds, and performance bonuses

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Most real-world contracts include variable elements—discounts, bonuses, penalties, rebates, refunds. Step 3 of ASC 606 requires estimating these AND applying a constraint. Overestimate and you'll have revenue reversals; underestimate and you miss legitimate revenue. The exam tests your ability to estimate AND constrain properly!

sections.title: What is Variable Consideration?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Consideration that can vary due to discounts, rebates, refunds, credits, price concessions, incentives, performance bonuses, penalties, or similar items.

**Also variable if contingent on:**
• Future event occurrence
• Resolution of uncertainty

**Key principle:** Estimate variable consideration and include in transaction price, but ONLY to the extent it's highly probable that a significant reversal won't occur.

sections.title: Types of Variable Consideration
rows: Mail-in rebate if customer submits form

sections.title: Two Estimation Methods
sections.content:
**Choose method that better predicts amount entitled to:**

**1. Expected Value Method**
• Sum of probability-weighted amounts
• Best when: Large number of contracts with similar characteristics
• Example: Returns, volume discounts with many customers

**2. Most Likely Amount Method**
• Single most likely outcome
• Best when: Only two possible outcomes (binary)
• Example: Performance bonus (earned or not earned)

sections.title: Expected Value Example
sections.content:
**SoftCo sells 1,000 software licenses at $100 each with return rights.**

Historical return data:
• 95% probability: 2% returns
• 5% probability: 10% returns

**Expected Value Calculation:**

(95% × 2% × $100,000) + (5% × 10% × $100,000)
= $1,900 + $500 = **$2,400 expected returns**

**Transaction price:** $100,000 - $2,400 = **$97,600**

**Entries:**
Dr Accounts Receivable         $100,000
    Cr Revenue                      $97,600
    Cr Refund Liability              $2,400

Dr Inventory - Returns (at cost)  $1,680*
    Cr Cost of Goods Sold            $1,680

*Asset for expected returns (at cost)

sections.title: Most Likely Amount Example
sections.content:
**BuildCo has construction contract for $1,000,000:**
• $100,000 bonus if completed by December 31
• Management assesses 80% probability of on-time completion

**Most Likely Amount Method:**

**Most likely outcome:** Bonus will be earned (80%)
**Amount:** $100,000

**But wait! Apply the constraint...**

Is it highly probable that including the $100,000 won't result in a significant reversal?

**Consider:**
• 20% chance of NOT earning bonus
• $100,000 is significant (10% of contract)

**Conclusion:** May need to constrain (not include) until more certain

sections.title: The Constraint: Highly Probable Test
sections.content:
**Variable consideration constraint:**

Include variable consideration in transaction price ONLY to the extent that it is **highly probable** that a **significant reversal** of cumulative revenue won't occur when uncertainty is resolved.

**Factors increasing risk of reversal (constrain more):**
• Amount highly susceptible to factors outside entity's influence
• Uncertainty not expected to resolve for long time
• Limited experience with similar contracts
• Practice of offering price concessions or changing payment terms
• Contract has large number/broad range of possible outcomes

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Constraint Factors
sections.content:
**"S-U-L-L-C" = Susceptible, Uncertainty, Limited experience, Large range, Concession history**

**The more factors present, the MORE you constrain!**

Think: "Is this a SULLC situation?" (sulky situation = be conservative!)

sections.title: Applying the Constraint
sections.content:
**Scenario: PharmaCo licenses drug to GenericCo**
• $5 million upfront payment
• $2 per unit royalty on sales

**Upfront payment:** Not variable → include $5M

**Royalties:** Sales-based royalty on license of IP

**Special rule!** Sales-based royalties on licenses of IP are recognized when:
• Later of: Subsequent sale occurs OR performance obligation satisfied

**This is an EXCEPTION to normal variable consideration rules!**

No estimation/constraint needed—recognize as sales occur.

sections.title: Sales-Based Royalty Exception
sections.content:
**⚠️ CRITICAL EXCEPTION:**

**For licenses of intellectual property:**
Sales-based or usage-based royalties are recognized when (or as) the later of:

1. Subsequent sale or usage occurs, OR
2. Performance obligation (to which royalty relates) is satisfied

**Why?** Too much uncertainty to estimate at contract inception.

**This exception ONLY applies to:**
• Royalties on licenses of IP
• When royalty relates predominantly to IP license

**Does NOT apply to:** Sales bonuses on product sales (use normal estimation)

sections.title: Right of Return
sections.content:
**When customer has right of return, recognize:**

1. **Revenue** – Reduced for expected returns (estimated)
2. **Refund liability** – Obligation to refund customer (CR)
3. **Asset for right to recover** – Expected returned inventory, at COST less expected recovery costs (DR)
4. **Reduce COGS** – For cost of goods expected to be returned

**Reassess at each reporting date:**
• Update estimate of expected returns
• Adjust revenue, refund liability, and asset accordingly

sections.title: Right of Return Entries
sections.content:
**RetailCo sells $500,000 of goods (cost $300,000). Historical returns = 3%.**

**At sale:**

Dr Accounts Receivable         $500,000
    Cr Revenue                     $485,000
    Cr Refund Liability             $15,000

Dr Cost of Goods Sold          $291,000
Dr Asset - Right to Recover      $9,000*
    Cr Inventory                   $300,000

*$300,000 × 3% = $9,000 expected return cost

**When return occurs (assume $10,000 returned, cost $6,000):**

Dr Refund Liability             $10,000
    Cr Cash                         $10,000

Dr Inventory                     $6,000
    Cr Asset - Right to Recover      $6,000

sections.title: Significant Financing Component
sections.content:
**Adjust transaction price if significant financing exists:**

**Indicators of significant financing:**
• Difference between promised and cash selling price
• Length of time between transfer and payment
• Prevailing interest rates

**Practical expedient:** No adjustment needed if:
• ≤ 1 year between transfer and payment

**If significant financing exists:**
• Adjust transaction price to reflect time value of money
• Recognize interest expense (customer pays later) or interest revenue (customer pays earlier)

sections.title: Financing Component Example
sections.content:
**MachineCo sells equipment for $500,000, payment due in 3 years.**
**Cash price would be $400,000. Implicit rate = 7.72%**

**At sale:**
Dr Receivable                  $500,000
    Cr Revenue                     $400,000
    Cr Discount on Receivable      $100,000

**Year 1 interest:**
$400,000 × 7.72% = $30,880

Dr Discount on Receivable       $30,880
    Cr Interest Revenue             $30,880

**The $100,000 difference is interest, not revenue!**

sections.title: Noncash Consideration
sections.content:
**If customer pays with noncash consideration:**

**Measurement:** Fair value of noncash consideration

**If FV not reasonably estimable:** Use stand-alone selling price of goods/services promised

**Variable noncash consideration:** Apply same constraint rules

**Example:** Customer pays with shares of stock
• Measure at FV of stock received
• If stock value varies based on future events, apply constraint

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Consideration Payable to Customer
sections.content:
**If entity pays customer (cash, credits, vouchers):**

**Treatment depends on purpose:**

1. **Payment for distinct good/service from customer:**
   → Account as purchase (separate transaction)

2. **Not for distinct good/service:**
   → Reduce transaction price

**Common example:** Slotting fees paid to retailers
• If for distinct service (shelf placement) → expense
• If just to get business → reduce revenue

**Exam tip:** Payments to customers usually REDUCE revenue!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Variable Consideration: Not as Scary as it Sounds!
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, 'variable consideration' sounds intimidating. But really, it's just accounting for the 'what ifs' in revenue. Think of it like this: you promise a client a $10,000 bonus if they hit a certain sales target. That's variable consideration! You need to estimate it, but conservatively. Don't get caught overstating revenue! I’ve seen companies get burned for aggressive estimates, so err on the side of caution. When in doubt, constraint is your friend.

sections.title: Forgetting the Constraint! (Big Revenue No-No)
sections.content: Students often estimate variable consideration, but then completely forget to apply the constraint! You can't just recognize the whole potential bonus if there's a significant chance you won't get it. The constraint means you only recognize revenue to the extent that it is *probable* that a significant reversal will *not* occur in the future. So, even if the expected value is $50,000, if there's a 30% chance of not getting *any* of it, you're probably going to have to constrain that revenue.

sections.title: Sarah's Sales Bonus Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a sales manager, promised a key client a 5% discount on their next order if they reached $1 million in purchases this quarter. They're currently at $900,000 with two weeks left. Should Sarah's company recognize the revenue *before* the million-dollar threshold is met, assuming they will meet it? No! They need to consider the probability of them actually reaching the million. If it's not probable, they can't recognize the revenue at the discounted price *yet*.

sections.title: Bonus Time! Variable Consideration in Action
sections.content: In practice, you'll see variable consideration all the time, especially when dealing with performance bonuses or rebates. Think about a software company offering a volume discount to a large customer. You'll need to track their purchases, estimate if they'll hit the volume threshold, and adjust your revenue recognition accordingly. Mastering this now means you can confidently analyze contracts, ensure accurate financial reporting, and avoid potential audit issues down the road. It really is essential knowledge.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Variable consideration includes discounts, rebates, refunds, bonuses, penalties
Estimate using expected value (probability-weighted) or most likely amount
Apply constraint: include only if highly probable no significant reversal
Sales-based royalties on IP licenses: recognize when later of sale/usage or PO satisfied
Right of return: reduce revenue, record refund liability AND asset for recovery right
Significant financing (> 1 year): adjust for time value of money
Consideration payable to customer: usually reduces transaction price

title: Contract Costs: Obtain & Fulfill
description: Learn when to capitalize costs to obtain and fulfill contracts and how to amortize them

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: ASC 606 doesn't just cover revenue—it also addresses contract costs! Knowing when to capitalize vs expense costs to obtain and fulfill contracts can significantly impact both the balance sheet AND income statement timing. The exam tests whether you can properly apply the asset recognition criteria.

sections.title: Two Types of Contract Costs
sections.content:
**ASC 606 addresses two categories:**

**1. Incremental Costs to OBTAIN a Contract**
• Costs incurred to obtain contract that wouldn't have been incurred otherwise
• Example: Sales commissions

**2. Costs to FULFILL a Contract**
• Costs directly related to contract that generate/enhance resources used to satisfy POs
• Example: Setup costs, mobilization costs

**Different rules apply to each!**

sections.title: Costs to Obtain a Contract
sections.content:
**Capitalize if BOTH criteria met:**

1. **Incremental** – Would not have been incurred if contract not obtained
2. **Expected to be recovered** – Through revenue from contract

**If met → Capitalize as asset, amortize over contract period (including renewals)**

**Practical expedient available:**
If amortization period would be ≤ 1 year → may expense as incurred

**What's incremental?**
✓ Sales commissions (paid only if sale made)
✗ Salaries (paid regardless of contract)
✗ Travel to bid on contract (incurred before winning)

sections.title: Obtaining Cost Example
sections.content:
**SaaS Corp wins a 3-year software contract:**
• Annual fee: $100,000
• Sales commission: $15,000 (5% of 3-year value)
• Expected renewals: Yes, at same commission rate

**Analysis:**
• Commission is incremental (only paid if contract won) ✓
• Expected to be recovered through contract ✓
• Amortization period > 1 year ✓

**Capitalize $15,000 as contract asset**

**Amortization:**
If expected customer life = 5 years (including renewals):
$15,000 ÷ 5 years = **$3,000 per year**

**Entry at contract inception:**
Dr Contract Asset - Commissions  $15,000
    Cr Cash/Liability                $15,000

**Annual amortization:**
Dr Amortization Expense          $3,000
    Cr Contract Asset                 $3,000

sections.title: Costs to Fulfill a Contract
sections.content:
**Capitalize ONLY if ALL three criteria met:**

1. **Relate directly to contract** – Identified contract (existing or anticipated specific)
2. **Generate or enhance resources** – Used to satisfy performance obligations
3. **Expected to be recovered** – Through contract revenue

**Plus: NOT within scope of other GAAP!**

If another standard applies (inventory, PP&E, intangibles), use that standard instead.

**Fulfill costs must pass ALL THREE tests + other GAAP check!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Fulfill Cost Criteria
sections.content:
**"D-E-R + Check"**

**D**irectly relates to contract
**E**nhances resources to satisfy POs
**R**ecoverable through revenue

**+ Check:** Not covered by other GAAP (inventory, PP&E, etc.)

All four must be YES to capitalize!

sections.title: Fulfill Cost Examples
rows: May be inventory or separate standard

sections.title: Fulfill Cost Example
sections.content:
**ConstructCo wins a bridge construction contract:**

**Costs incurred before construction:**
• Equipment mobilization to site: $50,000
• Engineering/design for this bridge: $80,000
• General bidding costs: $10,000
• Insurance (covers all projects): $5,000

**Analysis:**

✓ **Capitalize:** Mobilization ($50K), Engineering ($80K)
• Directly relate to contract
• Enhance resources to perform
• Recoverable through contract price

✗ **Expense:** Bidding costs ($10K), General insurance ($5K)
• Not directly related to specific contract
• Would be incurred regardless

**Contract Asset = $130,000**
Amortize as performance obligation satisfied (% complete)

sections.title: Amortization of Contract Costs
sections.content:
**Amortization method:** Systematic basis consistent with transfer of goods/services

**For costs to obtain:**
• Over period of contract AND expected renewals (if commission paid on renewal)
• Consider customer expected life if significant renewals

**For costs to fulfill:**
• Over period goods/services provided
• Often matches revenue recognition pattern (e.g., percentage of completion)

**Update estimates if facts change!**

sections.title: Impairment of Contract Assets
sections.content:
**Test for impairment if indicators exist:**

**Carrying amount > Remaining consideration expected LESS direct costs to transfer**

**Remaining consideration includes:**
• Contract price adjustments (variable consideration updates)
• Expected renewals/extensions

**Less costs not yet recognized:**
• Remaining fulfillment costs
• Direct costs of transfer

**If impaired:** Recognize loss immediately, reduce carrying amount

sections.title: Impairment Example
sections.content:
**ServiceCo's contract cost asset:**
• Carrying amount: $100,000
• Remaining contract revenue: $150,000
• Expected direct costs to complete: $120,000

**Recoverable amount:**
$150,000 - $120,000 = **$30,000**

**Impairment test:**
Carrying amount ($100,000) > Recoverable ($30,000)

**Impairment loss = $70,000**

**Entry:**
Dr Impairment Loss              $70,000
    Cr Contract Asset               $70,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Pre-Contract Costs
sections.content:
**Costs incurred BEFORE contract is obtained:**

**Generally EXPENSE immediately!**

**Exception for fulfill costs:**
Can capitalize costs for "anticipated" contract if:
• Specific contract is anticipated
• Costs generate resources for that contract
• Costs are recoverable

**But costs to obtain contract?**
→ Must wait until contract is obtained to capitalize
→ Costs before winning (proposal, bidding) = expense!

**Key insight:** You can't capitalize sales commissions on contracts you haven't won!

sections.title: Contract Asset vs Receivable
sections.content:
**Important distinction:**

**Contract Asset:**
• Right to consideration conditional on something other than passage of time
• Example: Recognized revenue before billing right exists

**Receivable (Trade Receivable):**
• Unconditional right to consideration
• Only passage of time required before payment

**Contract cost assets (this lesson) are SEPARATE from contract assets!**

**Presentation:** Contract cost assets may be combined with contract assets, but should be disclosed separately

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
content: **Costs capitalized:** Disclose judgments about what costs to capitalize
**Amortization method:** Describe how costs are amortized
**Asset balances:** Contract assets vs receivables vs cost assets
**Impairment losses:** Amount and circumstances
**Closing balance by category:** Costs to obtain vs costs to fulfill

sections.content: <!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Commission Conundrums: My Real-World Revenue Rant
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Contract costs can feel like a giant headache, especially when it comes to commissions. You see a sales rep close a deal, and BAM, there's a commission. But can you just expense it? Nope! Gotta decide if it's incremental AND recoverable. I've seen firms try to expense everything upfront, claiming it's 'immaterial.' Don't fall for that trap! Auditors *will* ask for backup. So, document everything. Seriously, everything. Your future self will thank you when you’re not scrambling to justify your accounting treatment.

sections.title: Confusing Fulfillment Costs with SG&A
sections.content: A common mistake students make is confusing costs to *fulfill* a contract with regular Selling, General, and Administrative (SG&A) expenses. SG&A is period cost, fulfillment costs can be capitalized. The key difference is the direct relationship. Fulfillment costs are *directly* tied to fulfilling *that specific* contract and create a resource that will be used to fulfill that contract. If the cost would be incurred *regardless* of the contract, it's likely SG&A. Always ask: would we incur this cost if we didn't win this specific contract? If not, dig deeper into fulfillment capitalization.

sections.title: Sarah's Software Setup: Capitalize or Expense?
sections.content: Sarah, a controller at Tech Solutions Inc., just signed a lucrative software implementation contract. They had to customize the software, at a cost of $50,000, *specifically* for this client. The software customization is only usable for this contract. The learning point: This customization is an incremental cost to fulfill a contract, and since it generates a resource (the customized software) that is expected to be recovered through the contract revenue, it should be capitalized and amortized over the life of the contract.

sections.title: Contract Cost Audits: Get Ready to Justify!
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter these contract costs all the time, especially in industries like construction, software, and telecommunications. Auditors will scrutinize your capitalization policies for both obtaining and fulfilling contracts. Be prepared to provide detailed support for your incremental cost assessments and recovery expectations. For example, sales commission agreements must be clearly defined and consistently applied. Failure to do so can lead to significant restatements and auditor headaches. So, understand ASC 606 inside and out – it’s a career saver!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Costs to obtain: Capitalize if incremental AND expected to be recovered
Practical expedient: Expense obtain costs if amortization ≤ 1 year
Costs to fulfill: Must be directly related, enhance resources, AND recoverable
Fulfill costs: Check if other GAAP applies first (inventory, PP&E)
Amortize over period of goods/services transfer (including expected renewals)
Test for impairment: Carrying amount vs remaining consideration less direct costs
Pre-contract obtain costs: Expense; fulfill costs: may capitalize if specific anticipated contract

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Incremental Costs to Obtain a Contract - Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company incurred $50,000 in sales commissions to secure a contract with a new customer. These commissions are incremental and are expected to be recovered. ABC also spent $10,000 on legal fees related to the contract negotiation, regardless of whether the contract was secured. What amount should ABC capitalize as an asset related to the contract?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Only the incremental costs to obtain the contract that would not have been incurred otherwise should be capitalized. The $50,000 in sales commissions meets this criteria, while the $10,000 in legal fees does not because it would have been incurred anyway.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to focus on the 'incremental' aspect of the costs.

reveal.question: What two criteria must be met for incremental costs to obtain a contract to be capitalized?
reveal.answer: 1. The costs are incremental (would not have been incurred if the contract was not obtained). 2. The costs are expected to be recovered.

rows.label: Definition
values: Costs incurred to secure a contract that would not have been incurred otherwise.
Costs directly related to a contract that generate or enhance resources used to satisfy the contract.

rows.label: Example
values: Sales commissions paid to secure the contract.
Direct labor and materials used to produce goods for the contract.

rows.label: Capitalization
values: Capitalized if incremental and expected to be recovered.
Capitalized if they meet specific criteria (directly related, generate/enhance resources, expected to be recovered). More complex criteria apply.

title: ASC 842: Lease Classification
description: Master the criteria for classifying leases as finance or operating under the new lease standard

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: ASC 842 changed everything! Now ALL leases (except short-term) go on the balance sheet. But classification as finance or operating still affects the income statement pattern and lessor accounting. The classification criteria are HEAVILY TESTED—memorize them!

sections.title: The Big Change Under ASC 842
sections.content:
**What changed from ASC 840?**

**Old (ASC 840):**
• Operating leases = Off balance sheet
• Capital leases = On balance sheet

**New (ASC 842):**
• **ALL leases = On balance sheet** (with exceptions)
• Record: Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset AND Lease Liability

**Exceptions (still off balance sheet):**
• Short-term leases (≤ 12 months, no purchase option reasonably certain)
• Leases of low-value assets (practical expedient for lessees)

**Classification still matters for:** Income statement pattern and lessor accounting

sections.title: What is a Lease?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A contract that conveys the right to control the use of identified property, plant, or equipment for a period of time in exchange for consideration.

**Control exists if customer has BOTH:**

1. **Right to obtain substantially all economic benefits** from use of the asset
2. **Right to direct the use** of the asset throughout the period

**Not a lease if:**
• Supplier has substantive substitution rights
• Customer doesn't control decisions about how/for what purpose asset is used

sections.title: The Five Classification Criteria
sections.content:
**A lease is classified as FINANCE (lessee) / SALES-TYPE (lessor) if ANY ONE of these is met:**

**1. Transfer of ownership** at end of lease term

**2. Purchase option** that lessee is reasonably certain to exercise

**3. Lease term** is for major part of remaining economic life (≥ 75% rule of thumb)

**4. Present value** of lease payments ≥ substantially all of fair value (≥ 90% rule of thumb)

**5. Specialized asset** with no alternative use to lessor at end of term

**If NONE met → Operating lease**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Classification Criteria
sections.content:
**"T-O-L-E-S"** (Think: TOLES like you're "TOLLING" ownership)

**T**ransfer of ownership
**O**ption to purchase (reasonably certain)
**L**ease term ≥ major part of economic life (75%)
**E**conomic (PV ≥ 90% of FV)
**S**pecialized asset (no alternative use)

**ANY one = Finance/Sales-Type lease!**

sections.title: Lease Term Determination
sections.content:
**Lease term includes:**

1. **Noncancelable period** – Required lease period
2. **Renewal options** – If reasonably certain to exercise
3. **Termination options** – Periods beyond termination option if reasonably certain NOT to exercise
4. **Lessor renewal/termination options** – Periods controlled by lessor

**Reasonably certain factors:**
• Economic incentives (significant leasehold improvements)
• Termination penalties
• Importance of asset to operations
• Past practices
• Cost/disruption to lease alternative

sections.title: Lease Payments Included
sections.content:
**Lease payments for PV test include:**

✓ Fixed payments (less lease incentives receivable)
✓ Variable payments based on index or rate
✓ Purchase option price (if reasonably certain to exercise)
✓ Termination penalty (if reasonably certain to terminate)
✓ Residual value guarantee:
   • Lessee: Amount expected to owe
   • Lessor: Full guaranteed amount

**Exclude:**
✗ Variable payments NOT based on index/rate (expense as incurred)
✗ Payments for non-lease components (unless combined)

sections.title: Discount Rate
sections.content:
**For PV calculation:**

**Lessee:**
• Rate implicit in the lease (if readily determinable)
• If not → Lessee's incremental borrowing rate

**Lessor:**
• Always uses rate implicit in the lease

**Rate implicit in lease:** Rate that causes PV of (lease payments + unguaranteed residual value) = Fair value of asset + Initial direct costs

**Incremental borrowing rate:** Rate lessee would pay to borrow funds necessary to purchase similar asset over similar term

sections.title: Classification Example
sections.content:
**LesseCo leases equipment:**
• Fair value: $100,000
• Lease term: 4 years
• Economic life: 5 years
• Annual payments: $24,000 (end of year)
• No purchase option, no transfer
• Discount rate: 6%

**Apply TOLES criteria:**

❌ **T**ransfer? No
❌ **O**ption? No
✅ **L**ease term? 4/5 = 80% ≥ 75% **YES!**
✅ **E**conomic? PV = $24,000 × 3.4651 = $83,162
   $83,162 / $100,000 = 83% < 90% **No**
❌ **S**pecialized? No

**Classification: FINANCE LEASE**
(Criterion 3 met - lease term ≥ 75% of economic life)

sections.title: Lessor Classification
sections.content:
**Lessor has THREE lease types:**

**1. Sales-Type Lease:**
• Meets ANY of TOLES criteria
• Recognize selling profit/loss at commencement
• Derecognize asset; record lease receivable

**2. Direct Financing Lease:**
• Meets ANY of TOLES criteria AND
• PV of payments + residual = Fair value (no selling profit)
• AND collectibility is probable

**3. Operating Lease:**
• Meets NONE of TOLES criteria
• Keep asset on books
• Recognize rent income straight-line

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: 75% and 90% Are Guidelines!
sections.content:
**Important nuance:**

**75% and 90% are NOT bright lines in ASC 842!**
