• In period of change
• Through income tax expense (continuing operations)
• Unless related to prior period discontinued operations

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Statute of Limitations
sections.content:
**When statute expires:**

**If position was NOT recognized:**
→ Recognize full benefit in period statute expires
→ Tax benefit (reduces expense)

**If position was PARTIALLY recognized:**
→ Recognize remaining benefit
→ Also release any accrued interest/penalties

**Entry example (statute expires, $40,000 unrecognized):**
Dr Liability for UTPs            $8,400
Dr Accrued Interest               $X,XXX
    Cr Income Tax Benefit            $8,400+
    Cr Interest Expense (or Tax Exp) $X,XXX

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
content: **Tabular reconciliation** of unrecognized tax benefits (beginning to ending)
**Amounts that would affect ETR** if recognized
**Significant positions** for which resolution is reasonably possible in next 12 months
**Description** of years that remain subject to examination
**Interest and penalties** policy and amounts recognized
**Change in unrecognized benefits** reasonably possible in next 12 months

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**Year 1: TechCo takes aggressive R&D credit position**
• Credit claimed: $500,000
• MLT to sustain: 60% (passes Step 1)
• Measurement: 70% cumulative probability at $350,000
• Benefit recognized: $350,000

**Year 1 Entry:**
Dr Income Tax Receivable        $350,000
Dr Income Tax Expense           $150,000
    Cr Income Tax Expense - Credit   $500,000*

*Or record $150,000 UTP liability

**Year 3: Settled with IRS at $400,000**
Dr Income Tax Expense (Benefit) $50,000 CR
    Cr Liability for UTP            $50,000*

*Recognize additional $50,000 benefit
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: FIN 48: The Tax Man Cometh (Maybe)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. FIN 48, or ASC 740-10 if you want to sound fancy, is all about CYA – Cover Your Assets. The IRS isn't always a friendly neighbor, and companies take tax positions that might be a little...optimistic. Think of it like this: You're claiming a deduction for that 'home office' that's conveniently located next to your gaming rig. Is it *really* just for work? Probably not. But is it more likely than not the IRS will call you on it? That's the question FIN 48 helps you answer. And if they do, how much will it cost you? Prepare for the worst, hope for the best!

sections.title: More Likely Than What, Exactly?
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking 'more likely than not' means 'pretty sure.' It doesn't! It means *over 50%* probability that the tax position will be sustained *on its technical merits* if the IRS audits it. Students often get hung up on the 'likelihood of being audited' itself, which is irrelevant to Step 1. The audit is ASSUMED. Focus solely on the technical justification. If you're hovering around 49%, you can't recognize the tax benefit, even if you're 99% sure you won't get audited. Technical merits, technical merits, technical merits!

sections.title: R&D Credit Rumble
sections.content: Tech startup 'Innovate Inc.' claimed a substantial R&D tax credit. Their CFO, Sarah, believes the credit is valid but knows the supporting documentation is…light. She estimates a 60% chance the IRS would allow the credit if challenged, based on similar cases. However, after a closer look at the documentation requirements, she realizes that while the overall project qualified for the credit, they lack sufficient records to prove the specific expenses. This significantly reduces the probability of successfully defending the credit. Even though the underlying activity is R&D, the lack of documentation prevents recognition of the benefit.

sections.title: Auditing Uncertain Tax Positions
sections.content: As an auditor, you'll encounter FIN 48 when reviewing a client's tax accruals. Management will have already assessed their uncertain tax positions, and your job is to challenge their assumptions. This involves scrutinizing their support for the 'more likely than not' determination, examining relevant tax law, and potentially consulting with tax specialists. Look for inconsistencies between the company's tax position and industry norms, or aggressive interpretations of complex tax regulations. A robust understanding of FIN 48 is crucial for identifying potential material misstatements in the financial statements.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Two-step process: Recognition (MLT to sustain?) then Measurement (how much?)
Step 1: >50% likely to sustain on technical merits assuming full examination
Step 2: Largest amount with >50% cumulative probability
Assume examination occurs with full knowledge (no detection risk consideration)
Interest/penalties: Policy choice for classification (tax expense vs other)
Reassess each period; statute expiration = recognize full benefit
Disclose tabular reconciliation and amounts that would affect effective rate

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Uncertain Tax Position Recognition: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following factors should *NOT* be considered when determining whether it is more-likely-than-not that a tax position will be sustained?
options: The technical merits of the position
The assumption that the tax authority will examine the position with full knowledge
The likelihood that the tax authority will detect the issue during an audit
Applicable tax laws and regulations
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The more-likely-than-not threshold for recognizing a tax benefit under FIN 48 focuses solely on the technical merits of the tax position, assuming the tax authority will examine the position with full knowledge. Detection risk (the likelihood of audit) and negotiation/settlement possibilities are specifically excluded from consideration.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that the recognition step focuses on the inherent strength of the tax position itself.

reveal.question: List the two-step process for evaluating uncertain tax positions under FIN 48.
reveal.answer:
Step 1: Recognition - Determine if it is more-likely-than-not (>50%) that the position will be sustained based on its technical merits.
Step 2: Measurement - If the recognition threshold is met, determine the largest amount of benefit that has a greater than 50% probability of being realized upon settlement.

rows.label: Primary Question
values: Is it more-likely-than-not (>50%) that the position will be sustained?
What is the largest amount of benefit that has a greater than 50% cumulative probability of being realized?

rows.label: Focus
values: Technical merits of the position, assuming examination and full knowledge by the tax authority.
Determining the amount of the benefit to recognize based on cumulative probabilities.

rows.label: Outcome if Threshold Not Met
values: N/A (Measurement only occurs if Recognition threshold is met)

title: Defined Benefit Pensions: Expense Components
description: Master the five components of pension expense and the calculation of the pension liability/asset

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Defined benefit pension accounting is one of the MOST COMPLEX topics on FAR! Companies promise retirement benefits and must account for this multi-decade obligation. The five-component expense calculation and funded status reporting are heavily tested. Master this and you've conquered a major FAR challenge!

sections.title: Key Pension Terms
sections.content:
**Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO):**
• Present value of ALL benefits earned to date
• Uses projected future salary levels
• This is the LIABILITY measure

**Plan Assets:**
• Fair value of assets set aside to pay benefits
• Held in separate trust

**Funded Status:**
• Plan Assets - PBO
• If positive = Net pension asset (overfunded)
• If negative = Net pension liability (underfunded)

sections.title: The Five Components of Pension Expense
sections.content:
**"S-I-R-A-G" (Some Investments Really Are Great)**

**S** = Service Cost (+)
**I** = Interest Cost (+)
**R** = Return on Plan Assets (-)
**A** = Amortization of Prior Service Cost (+/-)
**G** = Gain/Loss Amortization (+/-)

**Pension Expense = S + I - R ± A ± G**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SIRAG
sections.content:
**"S-I-R-A-G"**

**S**ervice cost → Expense (employees working this year)
**I**nterest cost → Expense (time value on PBO)
**R**eturn on assets → REDUCES expense (earnings on investments)
**A**mortization of PSC → Expense (plan changes from past)
**G**ain/loss amortization → ± (smoothing mechanism)

**Note:** Expected return reduces expense; actual return affects OCI

sections.title: Component 1: Service Cost
sections.content:
**Definition:** Present value of benefits earned by employees THIS PERIOD

**Characteristics:**
• Provided by actuary
• Based on current period's work
• Uses projected salaries (future pay increases)
• Always INCREASES pension expense

**Income statement:** Only component in OPERATING income

**All other components go below operating income (or in OCI)**

sections.title: Component 2: Interest Cost
sections.content:
**Definition:** Increase in PBO due to passage of time

**Formula:**
**Interest Cost = Beginning PBO × Discount Rate**

**Characteristics:**
• PBO is a present value → grows with interest
• Discount rate = high-quality corporate bond rate
• Always INCREASES pension expense

**Example:**
Beginning PBO: $1,000,000
Discount rate: 5%
Interest cost = $1,000,000 × 5% = **$50,000**

sections.title: Component 3: Return on Plan Assets
sections.content:
**Two types of return:**

**Expected Return (affects pension expense):**
• Beginning plan assets × Expected rate of return
• REDUCES pension expense
• Smoothed, predictable amount

**Actual Return (affects OCI):**
• What plan assets actually earned
• Difference from expected = gain/loss → OCI

**Formula:**
**Expected Return = Beginning Plan Assets × Expected Rate**

sections.title: Return Example
sections.content:
**Beginning plan assets: $800,000**
**Expected return: 7%**
**Actual return: $70,000 (8.75%)**

**Expected return:** $800,000 × 7% = **$56,000** (reduces expense)

**Actual vs Expected:**
• Actual: $70,000
• Expected: $56,000
• Gain: $14,000 → Goes to OCI (accumulated OCI)

**Effect on expense:** Only $56,000 reduces expense
**Effect on OCI:** $14,000 gain increases AOCI

sections.title: Component 4: Prior Service Cost Amortization
sections.content:
**Prior Service Cost (PSC):**
• Arises when plan is amended to increase (or decrease) benefits
• Retroactive benefits for past service

**Accounting:**
• Initially: Record in OCI (not expense)
• Subsequently: Amortize to expense over future service period

**Amortization method:**
• Straight-line over average remaining service period of active employees
• OR years to full eligibility (if most participants are inactive)

sections.title: Component 5: Gain/Loss Amortization
sections.content:
**Gains and losses arise from:**
• Actual return ≠ Expected return
• PBO actuarial changes (assumption changes)

**Accounting:**
• Initially: Record in OCI (not expense)
• Subsequently: Amortize to expense using CORRIDOR approach

**Corridor rule:**
Amortize only if accumulated gain/loss exceeds:
**Greater of: 10% of PBO or 10% of Plan Assets**

Amount over corridor ÷ Average remaining service years = Amortization

sections.title: Corridor Amortization Example
sections.content:
**Accumulated net loss in AOCI: $150,000**
**Beginning PBO: $1,000,000**
**Beginning Plan Assets: $800,000**
**Average remaining service: 10 years**

**Corridor = Greater of:**
• 10% × $1,000,000 = $100,000
• 10% × $800,000 = $80,000
**Corridor = $100,000**

**Excess over corridor:**
$150,000 - $100,000 = $50,000

**Amortization:**
$50,000 ÷ 10 years = **$5,000** added to pension expense

sections.title: Comprehensive Pension Expense Example
sections.content:
**Year 1 Pension Data:**
• Service cost: $80,000
• Beginning PBO: $1,000,000; Discount rate: 5%
• Beginning plan assets: $800,000; Expected return: 7%
• PSC amortization: $12,000
• G/L amortization (loss): $5,000

**Pension Expense Calculation:**

| Component | Amount |
|-----------|--------|
| Service cost | $80,000 |
| Interest cost ($1M × 5%) | $50,000 |
| Expected return ($800K × 7%) | ($56,000) |
| PSC amortization | $12,000 |
| G/L amortization | $5,000 |
| **Total Pension Expense** | **$91,000** |

sections.title: Balance Sheet Presentation
sections.content:
**Report FUNDED STATUS on balance sheet:**

**Funded Status = Fair Value of Plan Assets - PBO**

**If overfunded (assets > PBO):**
→ Noncurrent asset (Prepaid pension cost)

**If underfunded (PBO > assets):**
→ Liability (current portion + noncurrent portion)

**Amounts in AOCI:**
• Unrecognized prior service cost
• Unrecognized net gain or loss
• Reported net of tax in shareholders' equity

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Contributions vs Expense
sections.content:
**Contributions and expense are DIFFERENT!**

**Cash contribution:**
• Increases plan assets
• Reduces pension liability (improves funded status)
• NOT equal to pension expense

**Pension expense:**
• Calculated using SIRAG components
• Affects income statement
• May be more or less than contribution

**Common exam trick:** Don't confuse contribution amount with expense amount!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Pension Expense: It's All About the Funding Gap
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Pensions can feel like a black box. But at its core, it's about making sure there's enough money to pay retirees. The 'S-I-R-A-G' formula helps break down the movement in that funding gap. Think of service cost as the new promise you're making each year, and interest cost as the cost of waiting to pay. Return on plan assets? That's your offset! Amortization and gains/losses? Those are adjustments to smooth out the ride. Nail this, and you'll be golden.

sections.title: Confusing PBO with Accumulated Benefit Obligation
sections.content: Students often confuse the Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO) with the Accumulated Benefit Obligation (ABO). Both are present values of future benefits, but the PBO uses *projected* future salary levels, while the ABO uses current salary levels. This is crucial! For example, if a company's salaries are expected to increase significantly, the PBO will be much larger than the ABO. Remember, PBO = future salary projections, ABO = current salary. Failing to distinguish between the two will result in miscalculated pension liabilities.

sections.title: Sarah's Pension Plan Headache
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant at GlobalTech, is reviewing the company's pension disclosures. She notices a significant difference between the expected return on plan assets (7%) and the actual return (2%). The difference is material. Sarah needs to understand how this unexpected loss on plan assets will impact pension expense and the funded status. Remember, a lower actual return than expected will increase pension expense and decrease the funded status, potentially requiring additional contributions to the plan.

sections.title: Auditing Pension Expense: Where the Rubber Meets the Road
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter defined benefit pension plans when auditing larger companies. You'll need to understand the actuary's report, which provides the PBO, service cost, and other key components. Then, you'll trace these amounts to the financial statements and ensure they're properly disclosed. Pay close attention to the assumptions used, such as discount rates and expected return on plan assets, as these can significantly impact the reported numbers. A small change in these assumptions can have a huge effect on the financials!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Pension expense = Service + Interest - Expected Return ± Amortizations (SIRAG)
Service cost only component in operating income; others below or in OCI
Expected return (not actual) reduces expense; difference to OCI
Prior service cost: OCI initially, then amortize over service period
Gains/losses: OCI initially, then corridor amortization (10% rule)
Balance sheet shows funded status (Plan Assets - PBO)
Contributions affect funded status, NOT pension expense

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following components of pension expense would DECREASE the total pension expense?
options: Service Cost
Interest Cost
Return on Plan Assets
Amortization of Prior Service Cost (resulting from plan amendment increasing benefits)
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The return on plan assets reduces pension expense. Service cost, interest cost, and amortization of prior service cost (when benefits are increased) all increase pension expense.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the 'S-I-R-A-G' mnemonic. Which component has a negative sign?

reveal.question: What does the acronym 'S-I-R-A-G' stand for in the context of pension expense?
reveal.answer: S = Service Cost; I = Interest Cost; R = Return on Plan Assets; A = Amortization of Prior Service Cost; G = Gain/Loss Amortization

rows.label: Impact on Funded Status
values: Increases funded status (Plan Assets are added).

title: OPEB: Other Post-Employment Benefits
description: Understand accounting for retiree health care and other post-employment benefits beyond pensions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many companies promise retirees health care, life insurance, and other benefits beyond pensions. These 'OPEB' obligations can be enormous—sometimes exceeding pension obligations! The accounting mirrors pensions but has key differences. Understanding OPEB is essential for a complete picture of employee benefit accounting.

sections.title: What is OPEB?
sections.content:
**Other Post-Employment Benefits include:**

• **Health care benefits** (most significant)
• Life insurance for retirees
• Legal services
• Tuition assistance
• Other welfare benefits provided after employment ends

**NOT OPEB:**
• Pensions (separate standard)
• Termination benefits (different accounting)
• Stock compensation (different standard)

sections.title: The APBO Calculation
sections.content:
**Accumulated Postretirement Benefit Obligation (APBO):**

**Definition:** Present value of expected future benefits attributed to employee service rendered to date.

**Key inputs:**
• Per capita claims cost (expected cost per retiree)
• Health care cost trend rate (expected increase in costs)
• Discount rate (high-quality corporate bonds)
• Mortality assumptions
• Expected retirement age and coverage period

**The APBO is the LIABILITY measure (like PBO for pensions)**

sections.title: Health Care Cost Trend Rate
sections.content:
**Unique to OPEB accounting!**

**Definition:** Expected annual increase in per capita health care costs

**Typically:**
• Starts higher (e.g., 7-8%)
• Grades down to ultimate rate (e.g., 4-5%)
• Reaches ultimate rate over several years

**Example:**
Year 1: 7%
Year 2: 6.5%
Year 3: 6%
...
Ultimate: 4.5%

**Impact:** Higher trend rate = Higher APBO = Higher expense

sections.title: OPEB Expense Components
sections.content:
**Same SIRAG components as pensions:**

**S** = Service cost (benefits earned this period)
**I** = Interest cost (APBO × discount rate)
**R** = Expected return on plan assets (if any)
**A** = Amortization of prior service cost
**G** = Gain/loss amortization (corridor method)

**Formula:**
**OPEB Expense = S + I - R ± A ± G**

**Key difference:** R is often zero (unfunded plans)

sections.title: OPEB Example
sections.content:
**Year 1 OPEB Data:**
• Service cost: $50,000
• Beginning APBO: $500,000; Discount rate: 5%
• Plan assets: $0 (unfunded)
• PSC amortization: $8,000
• No G/L amortization needed

**OPEB Expense Calculation:**

| Component | Amount |
|-----------|--------|
| Service cost | $50,000 |
| Interest cost ($500K × 5%) | $25,000 |
| Expected return | $0 |
| PSC amortization | $8,000 |
| G/L amortization | $0 |
| **Total OPEB Expense** | **$83,000** |

sections.title: Unfunded Plans
sections.content:
**Most OPEB plans are unfunded or underfunded:**

**Why?**
• No tax deduction for prefunding (unlike pensions)
• Health care costs harder to predict
• Companies prefer to pay-as-you-go

**Accounting impact:**
• Full APBO appears as liability
• No plan asset return to offset expense
• Greater balance sheet impact
• Higher reported expense

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: OPEB vs Pension
sections.content:
**"OPEB = Often Pays Everything in Benefits (no assets!)"**

**Key differences to remember:**
• **APBO** not PBO (Accumulated vs Projected)
• **Health care trend rate** not salary growth
• **Usually unfunded** = No asset return offset
• **Medicare eligibility** affects obligation

**Same SIRAG formula, different inputs!**

sections.title: Attribution Period
sections.content:
**When to attribute benefits:**

**Pensions:** From hire date to expected retirement

**OPEB:** From hire date OR date when service begins to earn OPEB, whichever is LATER

**Full eligibility date:** Date when employee has earned full benefits

**No attribution after full eligibility!**

**Example:**
If plan requires 20 years service:
• Hire age 30, retire age 65
• Attribution: Years 30-50 (20 years to full eligibility)
• No cost attributed from age 50-65

sections.title: Plan Amendments
sections.content:
**When OPEB plan is amended:**

**Increases in benefits:**
• Prior service cost (PSC) recognized in OCI
• Amortize to expense over remaining service period

**Decreases in benefits (plan curtailment):**
• Negative PSC (prior service credit)
• May result in immediate gain recognition

**Common trend:** Companies reducing OPEB promises
→ Watch for curtailment gains in practice

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
content: **Obligation and funded status:** APBO, plan assets, net liability
**Components of OPEB cost:** Each SIRAG component
**Assumptions:** Discount rate, health care cost trend rate, expected return
**Sensitivity analysis:** Effect of 1% change in health care trend rate
**Expected future benefit payments:** Next 5 years + 5-year aggregate
**Expected contributions** in upcoming year

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Medicare Coordination
sections.content:
**Medicare affects OPEB obligations!**

**At age 65, Medicare becomes primary:**
• Employer plan becomes secondary
• Employer cost decreases significantly

**Measurement must consider:**
• Medicare eligibility age
• Expected Medicare coverage
• Coordination of benefits provisions

**Result:** APBO often drops at Medicare age in projections
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: OPEB: The Sneaky Retirement Cost
sections.content: Alright, listen up. Pensions get all the glory, but OPEB? That's the sneaky cost companies often underestimate. Think about it: retiree healthcare is EXPENSIVE. We're talking about covering medical bills for folks who are, statistically, going to need more care! I've seen companies get blindsided by the APBO because they only focused on the pension liability. Don't be that company. Understand the inputs, especially per capita claims cost, because that's where the real risk hides. Ignoring OPEB is like ignoring the fine print – it'll bite you in the audit.

sections.title: Confusing APBO with Pension Obligations
sections.content: Students often lump APBO and pension obligations together, thinking they're both just 'retirement stuff.' Big mistake! While both are future obligations, they're accounted for very differently. Pensions are usually based on formulas tied to salary and years of service, while OPEB, particularly healthcare, is driven by unpredictable factors like medical inflation and retiree health. The key difference: OPEB relies heavily on actuarial assumptions about future healthcare costs, which can fluctuate wildly. Don't just memorize; understand the drivers behind each liability to avoid misclassifying them.

sections.title: Sarah's OPEB Surprise
sections.content: Sarah, a financial analyst at MedCorp, was tasked with projecting the company's future liabilities. She diligently calculated the pension obligations but glossed over the OPEB, assuming it was insignificant. However, MedCorp had a generous retiree healthcare plan. The actuarial valuation revealed a massive APBO, dwarfing the initial pension estimate. Lesson learned: Always give OPEB the attention it deserves, especially if the company offers substantial retiree health benefits. Even seemingly small benefits can add up to a significant liability.

sections.title: Auditing OPEB: Digging Into Assumptions
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter OPEB when auditing a company's financial statements. A key audit area is the reasonableness of the actuarial assumptions used to calculate the APBO. This means scrutinizing assumptions about healthcare cost trend rates, discount rates, and mortality rates. For instance, if a company is using a healthcare cost trend rate significantly lower than the industry average, that's a red flag. You'll need to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence to support (or challenge) these assumptions, often involving consultation with a specialist. Master this, and you'll save yourself from potential misstatements and headaches.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: OPEB = Health care and other post-employment benefits (not pensions)
Uses APBO (Accumulated) instead of PBO
Health care cost trend rate replaces salary growth assumption
Most OPEB plans unfunded → full liability, no asset return offset
Same SIRAG expense formula as pensions
Attribution stops at full eligibility date (not retirement)
Medicare coordination reduces employer obligation at age 65

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following benefits would be classified as an Other Post-Employment Benefit (OPEB) and not a pension benefit?
options: A defined benefit plan promising a fixed monthly payment upon retirement.
A defined contribution plan where the employer matches employee contributions.
Healthcare coverage provided to retirees.
A lump-sum payment made to employees upon termination of employment.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Healthcare coverage for retirees is specifically included in OPEB. Pensions are accounted for under separate standards. Termination benefits are also accounted for separately.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the *type* of benefit provided *after* employment ends.

reveal.question: What does APBO stand for, and what is its fundamental definition?
reveal.answer: APBO stands for Accumulated Postretirement Benefit Obligation. It is the present value of expected future benefits attributed to employee service rendered to date.

sections.title: Health Care Cost Trend Rate Impact

knowledgeCheck.question: An increase in the health care cost trend rate will have what effect on the Accumulated Postretirement Benefit Obligation (APBO)?
options: Decrease the APBO.
Increase the APBO.
Have no effect on the APBO.
Only affect the service cost component.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An increase in the health care cost trend rate means that future healthcare costs are expected to be higher, thereby increasing the present value of the expected future benefits, and thus the APBO.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how a higher expected cost in the future affects a present value calculation.

rows.label: Timing
values: Provided at or near the time of termination.

rows.label: Examples
values: Healthcare, life insurance, tuition assistance for retirees.

rows.label: Accounting Standard
values: Separate accounting standards (contingency or compensation expense).

title: Contingencies & Commitments
description: Master the recognition and disclosure criteria for loss contingencies, gain contingencies, and commitments

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Companies face uncertainties—lawsuits, warranties, environmental liabilities, guarantees. ASC 450 tells us when to accrue a loss, when to just disclose, and when to stay silent. Getting the probability assessment right is critical for proper financial reporting and a frequent exam topic!

sections.title: What is a Contingency?
sections.content:
**Definition:** An existing condition, situation, or set of circumstances involving uncertainty that will be resolved when one or more future events occur or fail to occur.

**Two types:**
• **Loss contingencies** – Potential liabilities
• **Gain contingencies** – Potential assets

**Key:** The outcome depends on uncertain future events

sections.title: Loss Contingency Framework
sections.content:
**Two criteria for accrual (BOTH required):**

**1. Probability of loss:**
• **Probable** – Likely to occur
• **Reasonably possible** – More than remote but less than probable
• **Remote** – Slight chance of occurrence

**2. Amount of loss:**
• **Reasonably estimable** – Can determine amount
• **Not reasonably estimable** – Cannot determine

**Accrue loss if:** Probable AND Reasonably Estimable

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: PE for Accrual
sections.content:
**"P-E" = Probable AND Estimable**

Both required to accrue!

**"P" without "E"** = Disclose (probable but can't estimate)
**"E" without "P"** = Disclose (can estimate but only reasonably possible)
**Neither P nor E** = Usually nothing (remote)

**Think:** "Physical Education is required" → PE (Probable + Estimable) is required to accrue!

sections.title: Accrual Entry
sections.content:
**LawsuitCo is being sued for patent infringement:**
• Probability of loss: Probable
• Estimated amount: $500,000

**Entry:**
Dr Loss from Litigation         $500,000
    Cr Estimated Liability           $500,000

**Financial statement impact:**
• Income statement: Loss of $500,000
• Balance sheet: Liability of $500,000

sections.title: Range of Estimates
sections.content:
**When loss is probable but only a range can be estimated:**

**If NO amount in range is better estimate:**
• Accrue the MINIMUM of the range
• Disclose the range in notes

**If a specific amount IS the best estimate:**
• Accrue that amount (even if not minimum)
• Disclose range if material

**Example:**
Range: $200,000 to $800,000
No amount more likely than another
**Accrue: $200,000** (minimum)

sections.title: Common Loss Contingencies
content: **Litigation** – Lawsuits, claims, assessments
**Warranties** – Product defect obligations
**Environmental** – Cleanup, remediation costs
**Guarantees** – Debt guarantees, indemnifications
**Self-insurance** – Uninsured portions of claims
**Collectibility** – Uncollectible receivables
**Expropriation** – Asset seizure by government

sections.title: Warranty Example
sections.content:
**ProductCo sells 10,000 widgets with 1-year warranty:**
• Sales price: $100 each
• Historical warranty cost: 2% of sales
• Sales this year: $1,000,000

**Analysis:**
• Probability: Probable (warranties will be claimed)
• Estimate: $1,000,000 × 2% = $20,000

**Entry at sale:**
Dr Warranty Expense             $20,000
    Cr Estimated Warranty Liability  $20,000

**When claims paid:**
Dr Estimated Warranty Liability  $X,XXX
    Cr Cash/Inventory               $X,XXX

sections.title: Gain Contingencies
sections.content:
**Asymmetric treatment!**

**Gain contingencies are NEVER accrued before realization!**

**Why?** Conservatism—don't count chickens before they hatch

**Treatment:**
• May disclose if probable and amount can be estimated
• Do NOT mislead as to likelihood of realization
• Recognize gain only when realized (contingency resolved)

**Example:** Lawsuit where company is plaintiff
→ Don't accrue until judgment collected

sections.title: Subsequent Events
sections.content:
**Events after balance sheet date but before issuance:**

**Type I (Recognized):**
• Provide additional evidence about conditions at balance sheet date
• ADJUST financial statements
• Example: Settlement of lawsuit that was pending at year-end

**Type II (Disclosed):**
• Conditions arose AFTER balance sheet date
• DISCLOSE but don't adjust
• Example: Major casualty loss after year-end

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
sections.content:
**For loss contingencies disclosed but not accrued:**

**Required disclosures:**
• Nature of the contingency
• Estimate of possible loss (or range)
• Statement that estimate cannot be made (if applicable)

**For accrued losses:**
• Nature of contingency
• Amount accrued (if not obvious from financial statements)
• Possible additional loss beyond amount accrued

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Remote Guarantees
sections.content:
**Exception to "no disclosure for remote":**

**Guarantees of indebtedness of others:**
• Disclose even if remote!
• FIN 45 requires disclosure of:
  - Nature of guarantee
  - Maximum potential payment
  - Carrying amount of liability
  - Recourse provisions

**Why?** Users need to know about guarantee exposure regardless of probability

sections.title: Commitments
sections.content:
**Different from contingencies!**

**Commitments:** Obligations to do something in the future

**Examples:**
• Purchase commitments (contracts to buy inventory)
• Operating lease commitments (now on balance sheet under ASC 842)
• Construction commitments
• Employment contracts

**Accounting:**
• Generally disclosed only (no accrual until goods/services received)
• Exception: Loss on firm purchase commitment → accrue if material

sections.title: Purchase Commitment Loss
sections.content:
**CommitCo contracted to buy 1,000 units at $50 each:**
• Contract is firm and noncancelable
• Market price at year-end: $35 per unit
• Loss: ($50 - $35) × 1,000 = $15,000

**Entry:**
Dr Loss on Purchase Commitment   $15,000
    Cr Estimated Liability           $15,000

**When goods received:**
Dr Inventory                     $35,000
Dr Estimated Liability           $15,000
    Cr Cash                          $50,000
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Contingencies: The Crystal Ball of Accounting
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk contingencies. It's like trying to predict the future, but with GAAP rules! Loss contingencies? Think lawsuits. Gain contingencies? Unicorns. The key is understanding 'probable' vs. 'reasonably possible.' If your client says, 'Yeah, we're probably gonna lose that lawsuit,' start accruing! But if they say, 'Eh, maybe we'll win, maybe we won't,' then disclosure is your best friend. Don't get caught sleeping on these – auditors LOVE digging into these.

sections.title: Confusing Disclosure with Accrual
sections.content: Students often confuse disclosing a loss contingency with actually accruing it. Remember, accrual requires BOTH 'probable' AND 'estimable.' A common mistake is accruing a loss when it's probable but you can't reasonably estimate the amount. In that case, you DISCLOSE the contingency in the footnotes. Accrual hits the income statement and balance sheet; disclosure just informs the reader. If you can't put a number on it, it's disclosure only!

sections.title: ABC Corp's Patent Infringement Lawsuit
sections.content: ABC Corp is being sued for patent infringement. Their lawyers believe there's a 40% chance they'll lose, and potential damages are estimated between $500,000 and $1,000,000. Even though there's a range, and the loss isn't 'probable,' it's 'reasonably possible.' Therefore, ABC Corp must disclose the lawsuit in the footnotes to their financial statements, including the range of potential loss. No accrual is required because the loss is not probable.

sections.title: Negotiating Commitments in Contracts
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter commitments when reviewing contracts. Think purchase agreements, construction contracts, or lease agreements. These are future obligations. While not always recorded as liabilities upfront, understanding them is crucial. For example, a non-cancelable purchase commitment for raw materials at a fixed price should be disclosed if the market price has significantly dropped below the commitment price. This is because the commitment could result in a future loss. Being able to identify and assess these commitments is essential for accurate financial reporting and forecasting.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Accrue loss contingency if BOTH probable AND reasonably estimable
Reasonably possible → Disclose only
Remote → Generally no disclosure (except guarantees)
Range with no best estimate → Accrue minimum
Gain contingencies → NEVER accrue before realization
Guarantees must be disclosed even if remote
Purchase commitment losses → Accrue if material

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company is involved in a lawsuit resulting from a product defect. Legal counsel advises that it is probable that ABC will lose the suit and estimates the loss will be between $500,000 and $800,000. No single amount within that range is more probable than any other. What amount, if any, should ABC accrue as a loss contingency?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When a range of loss is estimated and no amount within the range is more probable than any other, the minimum amount in the range should be accrued. In this case, the minimum is $500,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider both the probability and estimability criteria.

reveal.question: Define a contingency.
reveal.answer: An existing condition, situation, or set of circumstances involving uncertainty that will be resolved when one or more future events occur or fail to occur.

title: Earnings Per Share: Basic & Diluted
description: Master the calculation of basic and diluted EPS including the treatment of stock options, convertibles, and contingent shares

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Earnings Per Share is one of the MOST SCRUTINIZED metrics by investors and analysts! ASC 260 requires public companies to report both basic and diluted EPS. The calculations involve weighted averages, treasury stock method, if-converted method, and antidilution tests. EPS calculations are a FAR exam staple!

sections.title: Basic EPS Formula
sections.content:
**Basic Earnings Per Share:**

**Numerator:** Net Income - Preferred Dividends
**Denominator:** Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding

$$\text{Basic EPS} = \frac{\text{Net Income} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{Weighted Avg Common Shares}}$$

**Key points:**
• Subtract preferred dividends (whether declared or not for cumulative)
• Use weighted average shares (not year-end shares)
• Stock dividends/splits retroactively adjust ALL prior periods

sections.title: Weighted Average Shares
sections.content:
**ShareCo share activity:**
• January 1: 100,000 shares outstanding
• April 1: Issue 20,000 shares
• October 1: Repurchase 12,000 shares (treasury)

**Weighted Average Calculation:**

| Period | Shares | Months | Weighted |
|--------|--------|--------|----------|
| Jan-Mar | 100,000 | 3/12 | 25,000 |
| Apr-Sep | 120,000 | 6/12 | 60,000 |
| Oct-Dec | 108,000 | 3/12 | 27,000 |
| **Total** | | | **112,000** |

sections.title: Stock Dividends and Splits
sections.content:
**Critical rule:** Treat as if occurred at BEGINNING of earliest period presented!

**Stock dividend/split:**
• Retroactively adjust ALL weighted average calculations
• Adjust prior period EPS for comparability
• No weighting for timing during year

**Example:**
10% stock dividend on September 1
→ Multiply ALL prior shares by 1.10
→ Adjust prior year EPS by dividing by 1.10

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Stock Splits
sections.content:
**"Splits go BACK!"**

Stock dividends and splits are treated as if they happened at the BEGINNING of the EARLIEST period.

**Why?** For comparability—you want apples to apples across periods.

**Easy adjustment:** If 2-for-1 split occurs mid-year:
• Multiply ALL prior shares by 2
• Don't weight the split for timing

sections.title: Diluted EPS: The Concept
sections.content:
**Diluted EPS shows:** What EPS would be if all dilutive securities were converted/exercised.

**Potentially dilutive securities:**
• Stock options and warrants
• Convertible bonds
• Convertible preferred stock
• Contingently issuable shares

**Diluted EPS formula:**

$$\text{Diluted EPS} = \frac{\text{Adjusted Net Income}}{\text{Adjusted Weighted Avg Shares}}$$

**Include ONLY if dilutive (reduces EPS)!**

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method: Options/Warrants
sections.content:
**For stock options and warrants:**

**Assume:**
1. Options exercised at beginning of year (or grant date if later)
2. Company receives exercise proceeds
3. Proceeds used to repurchase shares at AVERAGE market price
4. Net INCREMENTAL shares added to denominator

**Formula:**
$$\text{Incremental Shares} = \text{Shares from Options} - \text{Shares Repurchased}$$

**Only dilutive if:** Exercise price < Average market price (options "in the money")

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method Example
sections.content:
**Options outstanding: 10,000**
**Exercise price: $20**
**Average market price: $50**

**Step 1: Shares from exercise**
10,000 options = 10,000 shares

**Step 2: Proceeds received**
10,000 × $20 = $200,000

**Step 3: Treasury shares repurchased**
$200,000 ÷ $50 = 4,000 shares

**Step 4: Incremental shares**
10,000 - 4,000 = **6,000 incremental shares**

**Add 6,000 to denominator for diluted EPS**

sections.title: If-Converted Method: Convertible Bonds
sections.content:
**For convertible bonds:**

**Assume converted at beginning of year (or issue date if later):**

**Numerator adjustment:**
+ Interest expense avoided (after tax)

**Denominator adjustment:**
+ Shares that would be issued upon conversion

**Formula:**
$$\text{Interest Saved (net)} = \text{Interest Expense} \times (1 - \text{Tax Rate})$$

sections.title: Convertible Bond Example
sections.content:
**Convertible bonds:**
• Face value: $1,000,000
• Interest rate: 6%
• Conversion: 50 shares per $1,000 bond
• Tax rate: 21%

**Numerator adjustment:**
Interest expense: $1,000,000 × 6% = $60,000
After-tax: $60,000 × (1 - 0.21) = **$47,400 added back**

**Denominator adjustment:**
Bonds: $1,000,000 ÷ $1,000 = 1,000 bonds
Shares: 1,000 × 50 = **50,000 shares added**

**Dilutive test:** Does adding $47,400 / 50,000 reduce EPS?

sections.title: If-Converted: Convertible Preferred
sections.content:
**For convertible preferred stock:**

**Assume converted at beginning of year:**

**Numerator adjustment:**
+ Preferred dividends NOT subtracted (if converted, no dividend paid)

**Denominator adjustment:**
+ Shares issued upon conversion

**Key difference from bonds:** NO tax effect (dividends not tax-deductible)

sections.title: Antidilution Rule
sections.content:
**Critical rule:** Include security ONLY if DILUTIVE!

**Dilutive means:** Reduces EPS (or increases loss per share)

**Test each security:**
• Options: Exercise price < Average market price
• Convertibles: Incremental EPS < Basic EPS

**Incremental EPS for convertibles:**
$$\frac{\text{Numerator Effect}}{\text{Denominator Effect}}$$

**If incremental EPS > Basic EPS → ANTIDILUTIVE → Exclude!**

sections.title: Antidilution Example
sections.content:
**Basic EPS = $2.00**

**Convertible Bond A:**
• Interest saved (net): $30,000
• Shares from conversion: 20,000
• Incremental EPS: $30,000 ÷ 20,000 = **$1.50** ← DILUTIVE ✓

**Convertible Bond B:**
• Interest saved (net): $50,000
• Shares from conversion: 10,000
• Incremental EPS: $50,000 ÷ 10,000 = **$5.00** ← ANTIDILUTIVE ✗

**Include Bond A, EXCLUDE Bond B from diluted EPS**

sections.title: Order of Entry
sections.content:
**When multiple dilutive securities exist:**

**Rank by incremental EPS (lowest to highest):**
1. Include most dilutive security first
2. Recalculate EPS
3. Test next security against new EPS
4. Stop if next security is antidilutive

**This ensures maximum dilution is reported**

**Note:** Options/warrants have zero numerator effect, so their incremental EPS = 0 → Always enter first!

sections.title: Contingently Issuable Shares
sections.content:
**Shares contingent on future events:**

**For Basic EPS:**
• Include if ALL contingencies are met by year-end

**For Diluted EPS:**
• Include if contingencies would be met assuming year-end is end of contingency period

**Example contingencies:**
• Earnings targets (include if met at year-end level)
• Time-based (include if time has passed)
• Price-based (include if price met at year-end)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Loss Periods
sections.content:
**If company has a NET LOSS:**

**Basic EPS:** Use the loss (loss per share)

**Diluted EPS:** Usually equals Basic EPS!

**Why?** Adding shares to denominator when numerator is negative makes loss per share SMALLER (less negative) = ANTIDILUTIVE!

**Exception:** Convertible preferred would reduce the loss (don't subtract preferred dividend) → MAY be dilutive

**Rule:** In loss periods, most securities are excluded from diluted EPS

sections.title: EPS Presentation
sections.content:
**Required disclosure (face of income statement):**

**For each period presented:**
• Basic EPS from continuing operations
• Diluted EPS from continuing operations
• Basic EPS from net income
• Diluted EPS from net income

**If discontinued operations:**
• May present EPS from discontinued operations on face OR in notes

**Per share amounts for:**
• Each class of common stock
• Continuing operations and net income

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**Year-end data:**
• Net income: $500,000
• Preferred dividends: $50,000
• Weighted avg shares: 100,000
• Options: 10,000 (exercise $30, market $50)
• Convertible bonds: Interest $40,000, tax 21%, conversion 15,000 shares

**Basic EPS:**
($500,000 - $50,000) ÷ 100,000 = **$4.50**

**Treasury stock (options):**
Incremental: 10,000 - (10,000 × $30 ÷ $50) = 4,000

**If-converted (bonds):**
Numerator add: $40,000 × 0.79 = $31,600

**Diluted EPS:**
($450,000 + $31,600) ÷ (100,000 + 4,000 + 15,000)
= $481,600 ÷ 119,000 = **$4.05**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: EPS: Not Just a Formula, It's a Story
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. EPS can feel like alphabet soup. But think of it this way: it's Wall Street's favorite yardstick for a company's profit per share. You *need* to nail this. I’ve seen seasoned analysts trip up on the diluted EPS calc, especially with those tricky convertible bonds. So, memorize the formulas, sure. But more importantly, understand *why* we're doing what we're doing. It's about painting an accurate picture of what shareholders are *really* earning. Don't just crunch numbers; tell the story!

sections.title: Forgetting the Retroactive Stock Split Adjustment
sections.content: Students often forget to retroactively adjust for stock splits and stock dividends when calculating weighted average shares outstanding for *all* periods presented. This is a HUGE error! Imagine you have 100,000 shares outstanding and then a 2-for-1 split. You need to restate the prior period as if you had 200,000 shares outstanding. Why? Because the prior period EPS is now directly comparable to the current period. Not doing this makes trend analysis completely useless and will cost you points on the exam.

sections.title: Sarah's Convertible Debenture Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a financial analyst at TechCorp, is calculating diluted EPS. The company issued convertible debentures mid-year. Sarah struggles to determine how many additional shares to include in the denominator. Remember, use the *if-converted* method. Assume the debentures were converted at the *beginning* of the year (or date of issuance, if later). Increase the denominator by the number of shares issuable upon conversion and adjust the numerator for the after-tax interest expense saved. Sarah needs to get this right; the CEO is watching!

sections.title: EPS: Key Metric for Investment Decisions
sections.content: Every day, financial analysts use EPS to value companies and make investment recommendations. You'll see EPS figures prominently featured in earnings releases and financial news. Understanding how EPS is calculated, and more importantly, its limitations, is critical for making informed decisions. For example, a company might manipulate earnings to artificially inflate EPS. That's why you need to understand the underlying assumptions and be able to critically evaluate the reported numbers. It's not just a test, it's a real-world skill.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) ÷ Weighted Avg Shares
Stock dividends/splits: Retroactively adjust ALL periods
Treasury stock method for options: Shares issued - Shares repurchased at avg price
If-converted method: Add back interest (net of tax) or preferred dividends
Antidilution rule: Include only if reduces EPS; exclude if increases
Loss periods: Most securities are antidilutive
Order: Rank securities by incremental EPS; include most dilutive first

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Nelson Corp. reported net income of $500,000 for the year ended December 31, 2023. Preferred dividends of $50,000 were declared and paid during the year. Nelson had 100,000 shares of common stock outstanding on January 1, 2023, and issued an additional 20,000 shares on July 1, 2023. What is Nelson's basic earnings per share for 2023?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Basic EPS is calculated as (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding.  Weighted average shares are (100,000 * 1) + (20,000 * 0.5) = 110,000.  Therefore, EPS = ($500,000 - $50,000) / 110,000 = $4.17 (rounded).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to weight the shares issued during the year based on the portion of the year they were outstanding.

reveal.question: How are stock dividends and stock splits treated in a Basic EPS calculation?
reveal.answer: Stock dividends and stock splits are treated retroactively. This means they are considered to have occurred at the beginning of the *earliest* period presented. ALL weighted average calculations and prior period EPS figures must be adjusted for comparability. No weighting is used for the timing of the stock dividend/split during the year.

rows.label: Shares Outstanding
values: Increased number of shares (original * (1 + dividend rate)) or (original * split factor)

rows.label: EPS
values: Lower (assuming constant Net Income) - adjusted retroactively for all periods presented

title: Statement of Cash Flows
description: Master the preparation and classification of cash flows using both direct and indirect methods

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Statement of Cash Flows reveals the ACTUAL cash movements that the accrual-based income statement hides! Understanding operating, investing, and financing classifications—plus the indirect method adjustments—is critical. Cash flow questions appear on EVERY FAR exam!

sections.title: Three Categories of Cash Flows
rows: Cash from customers, interest received, dividends received
Cash to suppliers, employees, interest paid, taxes paid
Sale of PP&E, sale of investments, collection of loans made
Purchase of PP&E, purchase of investments, loans made
Repurchase of stock, repayment of debt, dividends paid

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: OIF
sections.content:
**"O-I-F: Operations pay for Investments through Financing"**

**Operating:** Day-to-day business (income statement related)
**Investing:** Long-term assets (buy/sell PP&E, investments)
**Financing:** Capital structure (debt and equity changes)

**Interest paid:** Operating (even though debt is financing!)
**Dividends paid:** Financing (distribution to owners)
**Dividends received:** Operating (investment income)

sections.title: Direct vs Indirect Method
sections.content:
**Two methods for presenting OPERATING activities:**

**Direct Method:**
• Shows actual cash receipts and payments
• Cash from customers, cash to suppliers, etc.
• More informative but rarely used
• If used, must provide reconciliation schedule

**Indirect Method:**
• Starts with net income
• Adjusts for non-cash items and working capital changes
• Most commonly used in practice
• FASB prefers direct; most companies use indirect

sections.title: Indirect Method Framework
sections.content:
**Start:** Net Income

**Adjust for non-cash expenses:**
+ Depreciation and amortization
+ Deferred taxes (increase)
+ Stock compensation expense
+ Losses (on asset sales, impairments)
- Gains (on asset sales)

**Adjust for working capital changes:**
+ Decrease in current assets (except cash)
- Increase in current assets
+ Increase in current liabilities
- Decrease in current liabilities

**= Cash from Operating Activities**

sections.title: Indirect Method Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Net income: $100,000
• Depreciation: $20,000
• Gain on sale of equipment: $5,000
• Increase in A/R: $15,000
• Decrease in inventory: $8,000
• Increase in A/P: $12,000

**Cash from Operating Activities:**

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Net income | $100,000 |
| + Depreciation | $20,000 |
| - Gain on sale | ($5,000) |
| - Increase in A/R | ($15,000) |
| + Decrease in inventory | $8,000 |
| + Increase in A/P | $12,000 |
| **Operating Cash Flow** | **$120,000** |

sections.title: Direct Method: Operating Section
sections.content:
**Direct method shows actual cash flows:**

**Cash received from customers:**
Sales + Decrease in A/R (or - Increase in A/R)

**Cash paid to suppliers:**
COGS + Increase in Inventory + Decrease in A/P
(Adjusted for non-cash items)

**Cash paid for operating expenses:**
Operating Expenses + Increases in Prepaids + Decreases in Accruals
(Exclude depreciation and other non-cash)

**Cash paid for interest and taxes:**
As actually paid (different from expense if accruals changed)

sections.title: Investing Activities
sections.content:
**Cash flows from investing:**

**Inflows:**
• Sale of property, plant, equipment
• Sale of investments (debt or equity securities)
• Collection of principal on loans made to others
• Sale of intangible assets

**Outflows:**
• Purchase of PP&E
• Purchase of investments
• Loans made to others
• Capitalized software development costs

**Present separately** (don't net purchases and sales)

sections.title: Investing: Equipment Sale Example
sections.content:
**Sold equipment:**
• Original cost: $50,000
• Accumulated depreciation: $35,000
• Book value: $15,000
• Sale price: $20,000
• Gain: $5,000

**Cash flow presentation:**

**Investing activities:**
• Proceeds from sale of equipment: **$20,000**

**Operating activities (indirect):**
• Subtract gain: **($5,000)**

**The gain was included in net income, but it's not operating cash!**

sections.title: Financing Activities
sections.content:
**Cash flows from financing:**

**Inflows:**
• Issuance of common stock
• Issuance of preferred stock
• Borrowing (bonds, notes, loans)

**Outflows:**
• Repurchase of treasury stock
• Repayment of debt principal
• Dividends paid to shareholders
• Payment of debt issuance costs

**Note:** Interest paid is OPERATING (not financing!)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Interest and Dividends
sections.content:
**Classification of interest and dividends:**

**Interest PAID → Operating** (even on debt)
**Interest RECEIVED → Operating** (even on investments)
**Dividends RECEIVED → Operating** (investment income)
**Dividends PAID → Financing** (distribution to owners)

**Common mistake:** Classifying interest paid as financing because it relates to debt. **WRONG!** Interest expense is an operating cost.

sections.title: Non-Cash Transactions
sections.content:
**Significant non-cash investing/financing activities:**

**Disclosed separately (NOT in cash flow statement):**
• Conversion of debt to equity
• Acquisition of assets by assuming liabilities
• Exchange of noncash assets
• Capital lease transactions
• Stock dividends

**Why?** These are important but involve no cash

**Present in:** Separate schedule or notes to financial statements

sections.title: Free Cash Flow
sections.content:
**Free Cash Flow (not GAAP, but commonly used):**

$$\text{FCF} = \text{Operating Cash Flow} - \text{Capital Expenditures}$$

**Represents:** Cash available for debt repayment, dividends, acquisitions

**Variations:**
• FCF to Firm: Before interest
• FCF to Equity: After interest and debt payments

**Analysts love this metric!**

sections.title: T-Account Approach
sections.content:
**Useful technique for preparing cash flow statement:**

**Create T-account for Cash:**
• All debits = Cash inflows
• All credits = Cash outflows

**Analyze each balance sheet change:**
• What caused the change?
• Was cash involved?
• What category?

**Cross-check:** Net change in cash should match beginning to ending cash balance

sections.title: Comprehensive Example Setup
sections.content:
**Balance Sheet Changes:**
• Cash: +$40,000 (to verify)
• A/R: +$20,000
• Inventory: -$15,000
• Equipment: +$100,000 (purchased)
• Accum Depr: +$25,000
• A/P: +$10,000
• Notes Payable: +$50,000 (borrowed)
• Common Stock: +$30,000 (issued)
• Retained Earnings: +$60,000

**Income Statement:**
• Net income: $80,000
• Depreciation: $25,000
• Dividends paid: $20,000

sections.title: Comprehensive Example Solution
sections.content:
**Operating Activities (Indirect):**
Net income: $80,000
+ Depreciation: $25,000
- Increase in A/R: ($20,000)
+ Decrease in inventory: $15,000
+ Increase in A/P: $10,000
**Operating CF: $110,000**

**Investing Activities:**
Purchase of equipment: ($100,000)
**Investing CF: ($100,000)**

**Financing Activities:**
Proceeds from notes: $50,000
Issuance of stock: $30,000
Dividends paid: ($20,000)
**Financing CF: $60,000**

**Net Change in Cash:**
$110,000 - $100,000 + $60,000 = **$70,000** ✗

*(Recheck—should equal $40,000 change in cash)*
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Statement of Cash Flows: My Indirect Method Confession
sections.content: Okay, real talk? When I first started, the indirect method felt like voodoo magic. Net income plus or minus a bunch of stuff? It made no sense! The key is to remember you're *undoing* accrual accounting. Depreciation isn't a cash outflow, but it *reduced* net income. So, you add it back to get closer to actual cash. Think of it as translating from 'accountant speak' to 'cash is king' language. Once it clicks, you'll see it everywhere!

sections.title: Confusing Depreciation with Cash Flow: A Costly Error
sections.content: A common mistake is treating depreciation expense as a cash outflow. It's not! Depreciation is a non-cash expense that *reduces* net income. Therefore, in the indirect method, you *add it back* to net income in the operating activities section. Forgetting this will drastically understate your cash flow from operations. Remember, you're trying to reconcile net income to actual cash, so reverse the effects of non-cash items.

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Options Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, was preparing the statement of cash flows for her company. She forgot to adjust for stock compensation expense when using the indirect method. As a result, her cash flow from operations was understated. This oversight led to misleading financial reporting that concerned investors until the error was corrected. Always remember to adjust for stock compensation expense, as it's a non-cash expense impacting net income.

sections.title: Mastering Cash Flows: Daily Life in Corporate Accounting
sections.content: In a typical month-end close, you'll be reviewing journal entries and ensuring all non-cash expenses like depreciation, amortization, and deferred taxes are properly adjusted for on the statement of cash flows. Analysts regularly use the statement of cash flows to assess a company's liquidity and solvency. Understanding the indirect method is crucial for accurately interpreting financial statements and providing valuable insights to management and investors. Knowing this inside and out will save you hours of reconciliation headaches.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three categories: Operating (day-to-day), Investing (long-term assets), Financing (capital)
Indirect method: Net income ± non-cash items ± working capital changes
Add back non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization, losses)
Subtract gains on asset sales (cash is in investing, not operating)
Interest paid = Operating; Dividends paid = Financing
Non-cash transactions: Disclose separately, not in cash flow statement
Verify: Net change in cash = Operating + Investing + Financing

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A company using the indirect method to prepare its statement of cash flows reported net income of $500,000. During the year, depreciation expense was $50,000, accounts receivable decreased by $20,000, and accounts payable increased by $30,000. What is the net cash flow from operating activities?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is $600,000. To arrive at this answer, you start with net income of $500,000. Then, add back depreciation expense ($50,000) because it is a non-cash expense. A decrease in accounts receivable ($20,000) represents cash collected, and an increase in accounts payable ($30,000) represents cash not yet paid out, so both are added. The calculation is $500,000 + $50,000 + $20,000 + $30,000 = $600,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to add back non-cash expenses and consider how changes in working capital accounts impact cash flow.

reveal.question: What is the starting point for calculating cash flows from operating activities using the indirect method?
reveal.answer: Net Income

rows.label: Presentation of Operating Activities
values: Shows actual cash inflows and outflows
Adjusts net income for non-cash items and changes in working capital

rows.label: Information Provided
values: More detailed information about cash receipts and payments
Less detailed; reconciles net income to cash flow

rows.label: Reconciliation Schedule
values: Required if using the direct method

title: Government Accounting: Measurement Focus & Basis
description: Understand the unique measurement focuses and bases of accounting used in government financial reporting

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Government accounting is fundamentally different from business accounting! Governments use different measurement focuses and bases of accounting depending on which financial statements you're looking at. Understanding these concepts is the KEY to mastering governmental accounting on FAR.

sections.title: Why Government Accounting is Different
sections.content:
**Governments exist to provide services, not make profits!**

**Key differences from business:**
• No profit motive → Focus on accountability
• Tax revenues are involuntary
• Legal requirements (budgets) are paramount
• Multiple reporting perspectives needed
• Dual reporting: Government-wide AND Fund statements

sections.title: Two Key Concepts
sections.content:
**1. Measurement Focus:**
What resources are being measured?
• Economic resources (ALL assets and liabilities)
• Current financial resources (available for spending)

**2. Basis of Accounting:**
When are transactions recognized?
• Accrual basis (like business accounting)
• Modified accrual basis (unique to governmental)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Focus + Basis Combinations
sections.content:
**"ECO for the Wide view; MOD-CUR for the Funds"**

**Government-wide statements:**
ECOnomic resources + Accrual = Full Accrual

**Governmental fund statements:**
CURrent financial resources + MODified accrual

**Proprietary fund statements:**
ECOnomic resources + Accrual (like business!)

sections.title: Modified Accrual: Revenue Recognition
sections.content:
**Revenues recognized when BOTH:**

**1. Measurable:** Amount can be determined
**2. Available:** Collectible within current period or soon enough to pay current liabilities

**"Available" definition:** Typically 60 days after year-end (or government's policy period)

**Example:** Property taxes levied in December, collected in January → Revenue this year (if within 60 days)

sections.title: Modified Accrual: Expenditures
sections.content:
**Expenditures recognized when fund liability incurred:**

**Except for:**
• Debt service (principal & interest): When LEGALLY DUE
• Compensated absences: When PAID (or matured)
• Claims and judgments: When PAID
• Pensions: When contribution due

**Key insight:** No depreciation in governmental funds! (Capital outlays are expenditures)

sections.title: The Dual Perspective
sections.content:
**Governments present TWO sets of financial statements:**

**1. Government-Wide Statements:**
• Economic resources, full accrual
• Big picture view
• Similar to business accounting

**2. Fund Financial Statements:**
• Governmental funds: Modified accrual
• Proprietary funds: Full accrual
• Fiduciary funds: Full accrual
• Detailed accountability view

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Fixed Assets in Governmental Funds
sections.content:
**In GOVERNMENTAL FUND statements:**
• Capital outlays are EXPENDITURES (not capitalized!)
• No fixed assets on fund balance sheet
• No depreciation expense

**In GOVERNMENT-WIDE statements:**
• Capital outlays ARE capitalized
• Fixed assets appear on Statement of Net Position
• Depreciation IS recorded

**Same transaction, different treatment!**

sections.title: Reconciliation Required
sections.content:
**Because of different measurement focuses:**

Reconciliations needed between:
• Governmental fund balance sheet → Government-wide Statement of Net Position
• Governmental fund statement of revenues, expenditures → Government-wide Statement of Activities

**Common reconciling items:**
• Capital assets and depreciation
• Long-term debt
• Accrual adjustments for revenues
• Internal service fund balances
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Government Accounting: Not Your Grandpa's GAAP
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Government accounting? It's weird. You're used to thinking about profit, but governments are all about services. Forget about maximizing shareholder value; it's about accountability to the taxpayers. The measurement focus and basis of accounting? They're designed to show how the government is using your tax dollars. So, ditch the for-profit mindset for a bit, and embrace the world of funds and budgetary compliance. It's a whole different ballgame, but once you get it, you'll be golden.

sections.title: Confusing Economic Resources with Current Financial
sections.content: Students often confuse economic resources measurement focus (government-wide) with the current financial resources measurement focus (governmental funds). Why? Because they forget the scope! Economic resources looks at ALL assets and liabilities, providing a long-term view. Current financial resources focuses ONLY on assets available for spending in the near term and related liabilities. Think: government-wide looks at the forest, while governmental funds look at the trees. Don't mix them up, or you'll misinterpret the government's financial health.

sections.title: Sarah's Budget Blues: A Measurement Mix-Up
sections.content: Sarah, a newly appointed budget analyst for the City of Springfield, was reviewing the city's financial statements. She noticed a discrepancy between the city's government-wide statements and its general fund statement. The government-wide statement showed significant capital assets, while the general fund statement focused on cash and near-cash assets. Sarah realized the difference stemmed from the different measurement focuses: economic resources (government-wide) versus current financial resources (general fund). Understanding this distinction allowed her to properly reconcile the statements.

sections.title: Budget Season: Where This Really Matters
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter measurement focus and basis of accounting every budget season. When preparing the budget, you need to understand the available current financial resources to allocate funds effectively. Then, when reviewing the government-wide financial statements, you'll analyze the long-term economic resources and obligations, such as infrastructure assets and debt. Knowing the difference allows you to analyze both the short-term and long-term financial health of the government, and make informed decisions about resource allocation and future planning.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Measurement focus: Economic resources (all) vs Current financial resources (near-term)
Basis: Full accrual (when earned/incurred) vs Modified accrual (measurable & available)
Government-wide: Economic resources + Full accrual
Governmental funds: Current financial resources + Modified accrual
Modified accrual revenue: Measurable AND available (typically 60 days)
No depreciation or capital assets in governmental fund statements
Reconciliations bridge fund statements to government-wide statements

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Governmental vs. Commercial Accounting - Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes a fundamental difference between governmental accounting and for-profit accounting?
options: Governmental entities focus on demonstrating accountability and stewardship of public resources, while for-profit entities focus on maximizing profits for shareholders.
Governmental entities primarily use the accrual basis of accounting, while for-profit entities primarily use the modified accrual basis of accounting.
Governmental entities do not prepare budgetary comparisons, while for-profit entities are required to prepare budgetary comparisons.
Governmental entities report on an entity-wide basis only, while for-profit entities report on a fund basis only.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is A. Governmental accounting emphasizes accountability and stewardship due to the nature of public resources. For-profit accounting prioritizes profit maximization.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core purpose of each type of entity.

reveal.question: What resources are measured under the 'current financial resources' measurement focus?
reveal.answer: Only those resources that are available for spending in the near term. This typically includes cash, receivables expected to be collected soon, and other liquid assets.

rows.label: Revenue Recognition
values: Recognized when earned, regardless of when cash is received.
Recognized when measurable and available (generally collected within 60 days of year-end).

rows.label: Expense Recognition
values: Recognized when incurred, regardless of when cash is paid.
Generally recognized when the liability is incurred, with the exception of principal and interest on long-term debt, and compensated absences, which are generally recognized when due.

rows.label: Long-Term Assets/Liabilities
values: Generally NOT reported in governmental fund financial statements.

title: Fund Accounting: Types & Purposes
description: Learn the eleven fund types used in government accounting and their purposes

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Governments use FUNDS to track resources for specific purposes. There are 11 fund types organized into 3 categories. Knowing which fund to use for different activities—and the accounting rules for each—is essential for governmental accounting questions on FAR!

sections.title: What is a Fund?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A fiscal and accounting entity with a self-balancing set of accounts.

**Purpose:** Track resources that must be used for specific purposes.

**Why funds?** Governments must demonstrate compliance with legal requirements—funds help track restricted resources separately.

**Three categories:**
1. Governmental Funds (5 types)
2. Proprietary Funds (2 types)
3. Fiduciary Funds (4 types)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Fund Categories
sections.content:
**"GoPro-Fi" = Governmental, Proprietary, Fiduciary**

**Governmental (5):** Government's basic services
**G**eneral, **S**pecial Revenue, **D**ebt Service, **C**apital Projects, **P**ermanent
→ "**G**reat **S**tates **D**on't **C**reate **P**roblems"

**Proprietary (2):** Business-type activities
**E**nterprise, **I**nternal Service
→ "**E**arning **I**ncome"

**Fiduciary (4):** Held for others
**P**ension Trust, **I**nvestment Trust, **P**rivate-Purpose Trust, **C**ustodial
→ "**PIP-C**"

sections.title: Governmental Funds (5)
rows: All activities not in other funds
Specific revenue sources for specific purposes
Principal must remain intact; earnings expendable

sections.title: Measurement Focus by Fund Type
sections.content:
**Governmental Funds (all 5):**
• Current financial resources
• Modified accrual basis
• Focus on available resources

**Proprietary Funds (both):**
• Economic resources
• Full accrual basis
• Like business accounting

**Fiduciary Funds (all 4):**
• Economic resources
• Full accrual basis
• Track resources held for others

sections.title: General Fund Specifics
sections.content:
**The "catch-all" fund:**
• Used for all activities NOT accounted for in another fund
• Every government MUST have a General Fund
• Only ONE General Fund allowed
• Largest fund for most governments
• Primary operating fund

**Typical revenues:** Property taxes, sales taxes, fines, licenses
**Typical expenditures:** Police, fire, general government, parks

sections.title: Enterprise Fund Criteria
sections.content:
**Enterprise fund REQUIRED when:**

1. **Debt backed solely by fees:** Revenue bonds secured by fees only

2. **Legal requirement:** Laws require cost recovery through fees

3. **Pricing policy:** Fees designed to recover costs (including depreciation)

**May use for:** Any activity that could be operated as a business

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Internal Service Funds
sections.content:
**Internal Service Fund classification:**

**On government-wide statements:**
• Usually included with GOVERNMENTAL activities (not business-type)
• Why? They primarily serve governmental functions

**Exception:** If enterprise funds are primary customers → include with business-type activities

**Exam tip:** Internal Service = Governmental activities on government-wide (usually)

sections.title: Fiduciary Funds: Key Points
sections.content:
**Fiduciary funds are NOT included in government-wide statements!**

**Why?** Resources belong to OTHERS, not the government.

**Reported separately:**
• Statement of Fiduciary Net Position
• Statement of Changes in Fiduciary Net Position

**Key change (GASB 84):** Custodial funds replaced "Agency funds" and now report a net position and changes.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Fund Accounting: Government's Hidden Wallet
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Fund accounting sounds like something cooked up by accountants to make your life miserable. But think of it like this: imagine your grandma gave you $500 specifically for a new TV. You wouldn't spend it on pizza, right? Governments have tons of 'grandma's' – except they're called taxpayers and grantors, and the 'TV' is things like schools, roads, and public safety. Fund accounting just makes sure they spend the money where they promised to. Don't overthink it; it's about accountability!

sections.title: Confusing Governmental Funds with Proprietary Funds
sections.content: A common mistake is mixing up governmental funds with proprietary funds. Governmental funds (like the General Fund or Special Revenue Funds) focus on short-term financial resources and modified accrual accounting. Proprietary funds (like Enterprise Funds) operate more like businesses, using full accrual accounting and focusing on economic resources. The key difference? Governmental funds are about 'spendable' resources and legal compliance, while proprietary funds are about ongoing operations and profitability. Remembering this core distinction will save you major headaches on the exam.

sections.title: Sarah's Special Revenue Fund Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a new government accountant, is reviewing the Special Revenue Fund for a city's parks and recreation program. She notices that some of the money designated for park maintenance was used to purchase new playground equipment. While both are related to parks, the grant specifically stated 'maintenance only'. Sarah needs to reclassify the expenditure and potentially find another funding source to cover the playground equipment purchase to maintain compliance with the grant requirements. This illustrates the importance of understanding fund restrictions.

sections.title: Auditing Governmental Funds: A Real-World View
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter fund accounting when auditing state and local governments. You'll be verifying that expenditures are made in accordance with budgetary and legal requirements. For example, you might be testing whether a Special Revenue Fund for transportation is actually being used for transportation-related projects, not for unrelated city expenses. A thorough understanding of the different fund types and their permitted uses is crucial for detecting fraud and ensuring compliance. Failing to understand fund restrictions is a major audit risk!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 11 fund types in 3 categories: Governmental (5), Proprietary (2), Fiduciary (4)
General Fund: Catch-all for activities not in other funds; every government has one
Governmental funds: Current financial resources, modified accrual
Proprietary funds: Economic resources, full accrual (like businesses)
Enterprise funds: Serve external users; required if debt secured solely by fees
Internal Service funds: Serve other government departments; usually reported as governmental
Fiduciary funds: Held for others; NOT on government-wide statements

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes the primary purpose of fund accounting in governmental entities?
options: To maximize the overall profitability of governmental operations.
To demonstrate compliance with legal restrictions and track resources designated for specific purposes.
To simplify the financial reporting process by consolidating all governmental activities into a single set of accounts.
To minimize the need for external audits by streamlining internal financial controls.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Fund accounting's core function is to segregate and track resources with specific restrictions, ensuring legal compliance. Profitability is not the primary goal of governmental entities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about why governments use funds in the first place.

reveal.question: What is the fundamental reason why governments utilize fund accounting?
reveal.answer: Governments use fund accounting to demonstrate compliance with legal requirements and track resources that are legally restricted for specific purposes.

knowledgeCheck.question: What does it mean for a fund to have a 'self-balancing' set of accounts?
options: The fund's revenue always equals its expenses.
The fund's assets always equal its liabilities plus fund balance.
The fund is not subject to external audits.
The fund operates independently of the overall government.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A self-balancing set of accounts means that the accounting equation (Assets = Liabilities + Fund Balance) is always in balance within the fund. This is a fundamental principle of accounting.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the basic accounting equation.

title: Budgetary Accounting & Encumbrances
