description: Master the budgetary entries and encumbrance accounting unique to governmental funds

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Unlike businesses, governments are LEGALLY REQUIRED to adopt budgets—and they're legally bound by them! Budgetary accounting integrates the budget into the accounting system, and encumbrances help prevent overspending. These concepts are unique to government accounting and frequently tested!

sections.title: Why Budget Integration?
sections.content:
**Budgets are LEGALLY BINDING for governments:**

• Appropriations = Legal spending authority
• Cannot exceed appropriations (most jurisdictions)
• Budget vs actual comparison required
• Budgetary accounts help track compliance

**Budget integration:** Recording the budget in the accounting system to track compliance throughout the year

sections.title: Budgetary Accounts
sections.content:
**Three budgetary accounts:**

**1. Estimated Revenues (Debit):**
• Expected inflows for the year
• Debit = increases the account

**2. Appropriations (Credit):**
• Legal spending authority
• Credit = increases the account

**3. Budgetary Fund Balance:**
• Plug to balance the entry
• Debit if expected deficit; Credit if expected surplus

sections.title: Budget Entry at Year Start
sections.content:
**Adopted budget:**
• Estimated revenues: $5,000,000
• Appropriations: $4,800,000
• Expected surplus: $200,000

**Entry:**
Dr Estimated Revenues           $5,000,000
    Cr Appropriations               $4,800,000
    Cr Budgetary Fund Balance         $200,000

**This entry sets up control accounts for the year.**

sections.title: Closing Entry at Year End
sections.content:
**Close budgetary accounts (reverse the opening entry):**

Dr Appropriations               $4,800,000
Dr Budgetary Fund Balance         $200,000
    Cr Estimated Revenues           $5,000,000

**Budgetary accounts are temporary—closed each year.**
**The "real" revenues and expenditures remain in Fund Balance.**

sections.title: What are Encumbrances?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Commitments for future expenditures (e.g., purchase orders)

**Purpose:** Reserve a portion of appropriations for committed spending

**Encumbrances are NOT expenditures!**
• They're commitments, not actual liabilities
• Deducted from available appropriations
• Prevent overspending

**Example:** Issue PO for $10,000 of supplies → Encumber $10,000

sections.title: Encumbrance Entries
sections.content:
**1. Issue Purchase Order for $10,000:**

Dr Encumbrances                 $10,000
    Cr Budgetary Fund Balance - 
       Reserved for Encumbrances    $10,000

**2. Receive goods (actual cost $9,800):**

Reverse encumbrance:
Dr Budgetary Fund Balance - 
   Reserved for Encumbrances    $10,000
    Cr Encumbrances                 $10,000

Record actual expenditure:
Dr Expenditures                  $9,800
    Cr Vouchers Payable              $9,800

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Encumbrance Process
sections.content:
**"E-R-E" = Encumber, Reverse, Expenditure**

**Step 1: ENCUMBER** when PO issued
(Dr Encumbrances, Cr Reserve)

**Step 2: REVERSE** encumbrance when goods received
(Reverse the entry—always reverse at ORIGINAL amount)

**Step 3: EXPENDITURE** at actual cost
(Dr Expenditures, Cr Vouchers Payable)

**Key:** Reverse at ORIGINAL encumbrance amount, NOT actual cost!

sections.title: Outstanding Encumbrances at Year-End
sections.content:
**If POs are outstanding at year-end:**

**Two approaches:**

**1. Lapsing appropriations:**
• Close encumbrances to Fund Balance
• Re-encumber next year (if policy allows)

**2. Non-lapsing appropriations:**
• Encumbrances may remain open
• Report as committed or assigned fund balance

**Disclosure:** Outstanding encumbrances should be disclosed

sections.title: Budgetary Comparison Schedule
sections.content:
**Required supplementary information (RSI):**

**Budget-to-actual comparison showing:**
• Original budget
• Final amended budget
• Actual results (budgetary basis)
• Variance

**Required for:**
• General Fund
• Major special revenue funds with legally adopted budgets

**May present on same basis as fund statements or budgetary basis with reconciliation**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: GAAP vs Budgetary Basis
sections.content:
**Budgetary basis may differ from GAAP!**

**Common differences:**
• Encumbrances treated as expenditures for budget
• Different revenue recognition timing
• Different fund structure

**If different:**
• Budget comparison on budgetary basis
• Reconciliation to GAAP required (or explanation of differences)

**Exam tip:** Watch for questions asking for "budgetary basis" results!

sections.title: Available Appropriations
sections.content:
**Calculating available spending authority:**

**Available Appropriations =**
Total Appropriations
- Expenditures to date
- Outstanding Encumbrances

**Example:**
Appropriations: $1,000,000
Expenditures: $600,000
Encumbrances: $150,000
**Available: $250,000**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Government Budgets: More Than Just Numbers!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Budgetary accounting might seem dry, but trust me, it's where the rubber meets the road in government. Think of appropriations as the government's credit card limit. They literally CANNOT spend more than that, or heads roll! You'll see this constantly in news about government shutdowns or budget crises. This isn't just about balancing the books; it's about legal compliance and public trust. So, nail this down, future CPAs!

sections.title: Mixing Up Estimated Revenues & Actual Revenues
sections.content: Students often confuse Estimated Revenues with the actual revenue the government collects. Estimated Revenues is a budgetary account, an *anticipation* of inflows recorded at the beginning of the year. Actual revenues are the real dollars coming in throughout the year. They are NOT the same! Think of it like this: Estimated Revenue is your New Year's resolution to earn $100,000 this year. Actual revenue is how much you *actually* earn. Don't debit Estimated Revenues when cash comes in; that's a debit to cash and a credit to Revenues.

sections.title: Sarah's Encumbrance Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a new staff accountant for the City of Sunnyvale, received an invoice for $5,000 for new playground equipment. She immediately debited Expenditures and credited Accounts Payable. Big mistake! Sunnyvale had already encumbered $5,000 when they placed the purchase order. Sarah should have reversed the encumbrance first (debit Encumbrances Outstanding, credit Budgetary Fund Balance - Reserve for Encumbrances) *before* recording the actual expenditure. Always remember the encumbrance process!

sections.title: Spotting Budget Variances in Real Life
sections.content: In governmental auditing, you'll be constantly comparing budgeted amounts to actual amounts. Did the city's actual road repair costs exceed the original appropriation? Why? Was it unforeseen weather, or poor planning? These variances can trigger further investigation and potentially highlight inefficiencies or even fraud. Understanding budgetary accounting allows you to quickly identify red flags and perform meaningful analytics, not just crunch numbers.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Budgets are legally binding; budget integration tracks compliance
Budgetary accounts: Estimated Revenues (Dr), Appropriations (Cr)
Opening entry records budget; closing entry reverses it
Encumbrances = commitments (POs); NOT expenditures
Encumbrance process: Encumber → Reverse at original → Record actual expenditure
Outstanding encumbrances at year-end may lapse or carry forward
Budget-to-actual comparison required for General and major special revenue funds

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A government entity anticipates revenues of $5,000,000 and has legally authorized appropriations of $4,800,000. What is the journal entry to record the budget?
options: Debit Estimated Revenues $5,000,000; Credit Appropriations $4,800,000; Credit Budgetary Fund Balance $200,000
Debit Estimated Revenues $5,000,000; Credit Appropriations $4,800,000; Debit Budgetary Fund Balance $200,000
Credit Estimated Revenues $5,000,000; Debit Appropriations $4,800,000; Debit Budgetary Fund Balance $200,000
Debit Estimated Revenues $4,800,000; Credit Appropriations $5,000,000; Debit Budgetary Fund Balance $200,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The journal entry to record the budget requires a debit to Estimated Revenues, a credit to Appropriations, and a credit to Budgetary Fund Balance to balance the entry. The budgetary fund balance is credited because expected revenues are higher than appropriations.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the normal balances of Estimated Revenues and Appropriations. Think about how to make the journal entry balance.

reveal.question: What budgetary account is used to represent legally authorized spending limits?
reveal.answer: Appropriations. Appropriations represent the legal spending authority granted to a governmental entity.

title: Government-Wide: Statement of Net Position
description: Understand the format and content of the government-wide Statement of Net Position

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The government-wide statements show the 'big picture' of a government's finances. The Statement of Net Position is like a corporate balance sheet—it shows ALL assets, liabilities, and net position using full accrual accounting. Understanding its format and components is essential for FAR!

sections.title: Government-Wide vs Fund Statements
sections.content:
**Government-wide statements:**
• Full accrual, economic resources
• Consolidate governmental and business-type activities
• Show long-term assets and liabilities
• Present net position (not fund balance)

**Fund statements:**
• Modified accrual for governmental funds
• Each fund reported separately
• Current financial resources focus
• Present fund balance

sections.title: Statement Format
sections.content:
**Three columns:**

**1. Governmental Activities**
• General government functions
• Funded primarily by taxes
• Includes General Fund activities

**2. Business-Type Activities**
• Enterprise funds
• Fee-based services
• Self-supporting operations

**3. Total Primary Government**
• Combined columns

sections.title: Asset Categories
sections.content:
**Presented in order of liquidity:**

**Current Assets:**
• Cash and investments
• Receivables
• Inventories
• Prepaid items

**Noncurrent Assets:**
• Restricted assets
• Capital assets (net of depreciation)
• Other long-term assets

**Note:** Capital assets ONLY appear on government-wide (not governmental fund statements)

sections.title: Liability Categories
sections.content:
**Also in liquidity order:**

**Current Liabilities:**
• Accounts payable
• Accrued liabilities
• Current portion of long-term debt

**Noncurrent Liabilities:**
• Bonds payable
• Net pension liability
• Net OPEB liability
• Compensated absences
• Other long-term obligations

**Note:** Long-term debt ONLY on government-wide (not governmental fund statements)

sections.title: Deferred Outflows/Inflows
sections.content:
**Reported separately from assets/liabilities:**

**Deferred Outflows of Resources:**
• Consumption of net position applicable to future periods
• Example: Pension contributions after measurement date
• Shown AFTER assets

**Deferred Inflows of Resources:**
• Acquisition of net position applicable to future periods
• Example: Property taxes levied for next year
• Shown AFTER liabilities

sections.title: Net Position Categories
sections.content:
**Three components (not fund balance!):**

**1. Net Investment in Capital Assets:**
Capital assets - Related debt - Unspent debt proceeds

**2. Restricted:**
• Externally imposed restrictions (grants, donors)
• Constitutionally or legally imposed
• Must be spent for specific purposes

**3. Unrestricted:**
• Available for any purpose
• May be designated, but not restricted

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Net Position
sections.content:
**"NIC-RU" = Net Investment in Capital assets, Restricted, Unrestricted**

**Net Investment in Capital Assets:**
"What we OWN free and clear" (capital assets minus related debt)

**Restricted:**
"What we CAN'T spend freely" (earmarked by others)

**Unrestricted:**
"What we CAN use for anything" (flexible resources)

sections.title: Internal Service Fund Treatment
sections.content:
**Where are Internal Service Funds reported?**

**Default:** Include with GOVERNMENTAL activities
• Internal Service funds primarily serve governmental functions
• Consolidate and eliminate internal transactions

**Exception:** If Enterprise funds are predominant participants → include with business-type activities

**Key:** Assets/liabilities distributed between governmental and business-type based on services provided

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Fiduciary Funds
sections.content:
**Fiduciary funds are NOT on government-wide statements!**

**Why?**
• Resources belong to OTHERS
• Government is trustee, not owner
• Should not be mixed with government's own resources

**Reported separately:**
• Statement of Fiduciary Net Position
• Statement of Changes in Fiduciary Net Position

sections.title: Component Units
sections.content:
**Legally separate entities included based on relationship:**

**Discrete Presentation:**
• Separate column(s) on government-wide statements
• Used when entity is legally separate but accountable

**Blended:**
• Mixed with primary government data
• Used when very closely related (essentially same as primary)

**Criteria:** Financial benefit/burden, appointment authority, fiscal dependence
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Government-Wide: It's All About the Big Picture
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Fund accounting can feel like you're stuck in the weeds, tracking every nickel and dime. Government-wide statements? That's zooming out to 30,000 feet. Think of it as consolidating all those individual fund reports into a single, comprehensive view of the government's financial health. It's the difference between seeing the individual trees and understanding the entire forest. If you only focus on the fund level, you'll miss the forest for the trees. The Statement of Net Position is where it all comes together.

sections.title: Net Position vs. Fund Balance: A Critical Distinction
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking net position on the government-wide Statement of Net Position is the same as fund balance in the fund statements. They are NOT interchangeable! Fund balance uses the modified accrual basis and focuses on current financial resources. Net position, on the other hand, uses the full accrual basis and economic resources measurement focus. It includes long-term assets and liabilities, unlike fund balance. Always remember that net position is the government-wide equivalent of equity for a for-profit company; it's the residual interest in the government's assets after deducting liabilities.

sections.title: City of Anytown's Unfunded Pension Liability
sections.content: Sarah, a new financial analyst for the City of Anytown, is reviewing the draft government-wide Statement of Net Position. She notices a massive unfunded pension liability. While this liability might not be fully reflected in the individual fund statements due to the modified accrual basis, it MUST be included in the government-wide statement. This highlights the importance of the full accrual basis for accurately portraying the government's long-term obligations and financial position.

sections.title: Auditing Government-Wide Statements: A Must-Know
sections.content: As an auditor, you'll frequently encounter government-wide statements. Understanding how governmental activities and business-type activities are presented is crucial for assessing the overall financial health of the government. For example, a city might have a profitable water utility (business-type) offsetting deficits in its general government functions. This is why the Statement of Net Position is invaluable in providing a comprehensive, consolidated view, allowing stakeholders to make informed decisions.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Government-wide uses full accrual, economic resources focus
Two activity columns: Governmental and Business-type
Capital assets and long-term debt appear here (not in governmental fund statements)
Deferred outflows/inflows reported separately
Net Position: Net Investment in Capital Assets + Restricted + Unrestricted
Internal Service funds usually included with governmental activities
Fiduciary funds NOT included in government-wide statements

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is reported in the Governmental Activities column of the government-wide Statement of Net Position?
options: Activities of the General Fund, funded primarily by taxes.
Activities of enterprise funds, which are fee-based services.
Fiduciary fund activities, which represent resources held for others.
Component units that are legally separate from the primary government.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Governmental Activities column includes general government functions funded primarily by taxes, encompassing the activities of the General Fund. Enterprise funds are reported in the Business-Type Activities column. Fiduciary funds are not included in the government-wide statements. Component units may be blended or discretely presented, but the question refers to the *column* in which the primary government's activities are reported.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which activities are funded primarily through taxes versus user fees.

reveal.question: What are the three columns presented on the government-wide Statement of Net Position?
reveal.answer: 1. Governmental Activities, 2. Business-Type Activities, 3. Total Primary Government

sections.title: Government-Wide vs. Fund Statements: Key Differences

rows.label: Reporting of Long-Term Assets and Liabilities

title: Government-Wide: Statement of Activities
description: Master the unique net cost format of the government-wide Statement of Activities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Statement of Activities shows the cost of government services—and how they're funded. Its unique 'net cost' format shows which programs pay for themselves and which require tax support. Understanding this format is crucial for government-wide reporting questions!

sections.title: Unique Format: Net Cost
sections.content:
**Unlike a business income statement:**

The Statement of Activities shows:
• Expenses by FUNCTION (not nature)
• Program revenues directly related to each function
• NET (expense) or revenue for each function
• General revenues that support all functions

**Purpose:** Show the net cost of each function to taxpayers

sections.title: Statement Structure
sections.content:
**Format:**

**Functions/Programs** | Expenses | Program Revenues | Net (Expense) Revenue
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Governmental Activities:
  General government
  Public safety
  Education
  etc.

Business-Type Activities:
  Water utility
  Airport
  etc.

**Total Primary Government**

**General Revenues:**
  Taxes, Investment earnings, etc.

**Change in Net Position**

sections.title: Program Revenues
sections.content:
**Revenues directly related to a specific function:**

**1. Charges for Services:**
• Fees charged to those who use the service
• Example: Building permits, recreation fees, utility charges

**2. Operating Grants and Contributions:**
• Restricted for operating purposes
• Example: Federal grant for job training

**3. Capital Grants and Contributions:**
• Restricted for capital purposes
• Example: State grant for highway construction

sections.title: General Revenues
sections.content:
**Revenues NOT tied to specific programs:**

• **Property taxes**
• **Sales taxes**
• **Unrestricted grants**
• **Investment earnings (unless program restricted)**
• **Gain on sale of capital assets**

**Reported at bottom of statement:**
General revenues support ALL functions, so they're not allocated to specific programs.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Program vs General Revenues
sections.content:
**"Program revenues have a PROGRAM destination"**

**Ask:** Is this revenue DIRECTLY tied to a specific function?

**YES → Program Revenue**
Examples: Parking fees (parking function), water bills (water utility)

**NO → General Revenue**
Examples: Property tax (supports everything), unrestricted grants

sections.title: Net Cost Example
sections.content:
**Public Safety Function:**

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Expenses | ($5,000,000) |
| Charges for services (fees) | $500,000 |
| Operating grants | $800,000 |
| Capital grants | $200,000 |
| **Net (Expense)** | **($3,500,000)** |

**Interpretation:** Public safety costs taxpayers $3.5M (general revenues must cover this)

sections.title: Change in Net Position
sections.content:
**Bottom of statement shows:**

Net (Expense) from all functions
+ General Revenues
± Special items
± Extraordinary items
± Transfers
**= Change in Net Position**

**This flows to Statement of Net Position** (similar to how net income flows to retained earnings)

sections.title: Special and Extraordinary Items
sections.content:
**Special Items:**
• Unusual OR infrequent
• Within management's control
• Report separately

**Extraordinary Items:**
• Both unusual AND infrequent
• Outside management's control
• Report separately

**Note:** GASB still uses extraordinary item concept (unlike FASB which eliminated it)

sections.title: Interfund Transfers
sections.content:
**Transfers between governmental and business-type:**

**Reported separately on Statement of Activities**

**Governmental activities:** Transfers in (out)
**Business-type activities:** Transfers in (out)
*These should offset to zero for total government*

**Internal Service fund activity:** Eliminated in consolidation (usually)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Expense vs Expenditure
sections.content:
**Government-wide uses EXPENSES (full accrual):**
• Includes depreciation
• Accrues pension costs
• Accrues compensated absences

**Fund statements use EXPENDITURES (modified accrual):**
• Capital outlay, not depreciation
• Different timing for long-term items

**Exam tip:** "Expense" = government-wide; "Expenditure" = fund statements

sections.title: Required Reconciliation
sections.content:
**Must reconcile fund statements to government-wide:**

**From governmental fund statements to government-wide:**

• Add capital asset acquisitions (expenditure → capitalized)
• Deduct depreciation expense
• Add debt proceeds (other financing source → liability)
• Deduct debt repayments (expenditure → reduce liability)
• Adjust for accrual items (pensions, compensated absences)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Statement of Activities: It's All About the Functions!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. The Statement of Activities isn't your typical income statement. Forget about cost of goods sold! We're talking *functions*. Think 'Public Safety,' 'Highways and Streets,' 'Education.' The government wants to know how much each *function* costs and how much direct revenue it brings in. Don't get tripped up trying to allocate indirect costs perfectly. The goal is to show the *net* expense or revenue of each function. Nail this, and you're golden.

sections.title: Mixing Program Revenues with General Revenues
sections.content: A common mistake is treating *all* government revenue the same. Program revenues are ONLY those directly tied to a specific function. Think charges for services, operating grants, and capital grants related to that function. General revenues, like property taxes or unrestricted grants, support *all* functions and come *after* the 'Net (Expense) Revenue' line. Don't lump them together! Incorrectly classifying revenue throws off the net cost calculation for each function, leading to a wrong answer on the exam and in real life.

sections.title: Sarah's Budget Blues: Public Safety Spending
sections.content: Sarah, a budget analyst for the City of Anytown, is reviewing the Statement of Activities draft. The Public Safety function shows total expenses of $10 million. They received $2 million in federal grants earmarked for police training and $500,000 in fines directly related to public safety violations. The rest is covered by general tax revenue. The learning point? Only the $2 million grant and $500,000 in fines are considered program revenues for the Public Safety function, resulting in a net expense of $7.5 million.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Prioritizing Budget Allocations
sections.content: In practice, understanding the Statement of Activities is crucial for informed budget allocations. If the 'Highways and Streets' function consistently shows a large net expense, despite fuel taxes and tolls, the government might need to explore alternative funding sources, reduce spending, or increase user fees. This analysis helps governments make data-driven decisions about resource allocation, ensuring taxpayer dollars are used effectively and efficiently. It's not just an exam topic; it's about responsible governance.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Net cost format: Expenses minus Program Revenues = Net (Expense)
Program revenues: Charges for services, operating grants, capital grants
General revenues: Taxes, unrestricted grants, investment earnings
Net cost shows how much general revenues must cover
Change in Net Position = Net (Expense) + General Revenues ± Other items
Transfers between governmental and business-type shown separately
Must reconcile from fund statements to government-wide

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is reported as a program revenue on the statement of activities?
options: Property taxes levied to support general government operations.
A federal grant specifically restricted to fund a city's after-school education program.
Investment income earned on a city's general fund.
Sales taxes used to finance infrastructure projects across the city.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Program revenues are directly related to a specific function. A federal grant restricted to a specific program (after-school education) qualifies as program revenue. The other options represent general revenues.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what revenues are tied DIRECTLY to a particular function.

reveal.question: What are the three main columns in the Statement of Activities format?
reveal.answer:
1. Functions/Programs
2. Expenses
3. Program Revenues
4. Net (Expense) Revenue

sections.title: Program Revenues: Charges for Services vs. Operating Grants and Contributions

rows.label: Source
values: Fees charged to those who directly use the service.
Funding from another entity (e.g., federal government) specifically for operating purposes.

rows.label: Example
values: A grant from the state to support a local library's operating budget.

rows.label: Restriction
values: Restricted for a specific operating purpose.

title: Governmental Fund Financial Statements
description: Master the balance sheet and operating statement formats for governmental funds

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Governmental fund financial statements show accountability for current financial resources—the money available to spend. Understanding the unique formats and terminology (fund balance, expenditures, other financing sources) is essential for FAR governmental questions!

sections.title: Governmental Fund Statements Overview
sections.content:
**Two required statements:**

**1. Balance Sheet**
• Assets = Liabilities + Deferred Inflows + Fund Balance
• Current financial resources only
• NO capital assets or long-term debt

**2. Statement of Revenues, Expenditures, and Changes in Fund Balance**
• Modified accrual basis
• Expenditures (not expenses)
• Other financing sources/uses

sections.title: Balance Sheet Components
sections.content:
**Assets (Current Financial Resources):**
• Cash and investments
• Receivables (net of allowance)
• Due from other funds
• Inventory and prepaids (if significant)

**Liabilities:**
• Accounts payable
• Accrued liabilities (current only!)
• Due to other funds
• Deferred revenue (unavailable)

**Deferred Inflows of Resources:**
• Unavailable revenue
• Amounts that don't meet "available" criteria

sections.title: Fund Balance Classifications
sections.content:
**Five categories (GASB 54):**

**1. Nonspendable:** Can't be spent
• Inventory, prepaid items, long-term receivables
• Principal of permanent funds

**2. Restricted:** Externally imposed constraints
• Grants, donors, laws

**3. Committed:** Self-imposed by highest authority
• Formal action required to remove

**4. Assigned:** Intended for specific purpose
• Less formal than committed

**5. Unassigned:** Available for any purpose
• Only in General Fund (usually)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Fund Balance Categories
sections.content:
**"Never Run Constantly Around Unprepared"**

**N**onspendable - Can't spend it
**R**estricted - Others say how to use
**C**ommitted - Council formally decided
**A**ssigned - Management intends to use
**U**nassigned - Free to spend anywhere

**Constraint strength:** Nonspendable → Restricted → Committed → Assigned → Unassigned (strongest to weakest)

sections.title: Statement of Revenues, Expenditures, and Changes in Fund Balance
sections.content:
**Format:**

**Revenues:**
  Property taxes
  Sales taxes
  Intergovernmental
  Charges for services
  Fines and forfeitures
  Investment earnings
  **Total Revenues**

**Expenditures:**
  Current (by function)
  Capital outlay
  Debt service - principal
  Debt service - interest
  **Total Expenditures**

**Excess (Deficiency)**

**Other Financing Sources (Uses)**

**Net Change in Fund Balance**

sections.title: Expenditure Categories
sections.content:
**Current expenditures by function:**
• General government
• Public safety
• Public works
• Health and welfare
• Culture and recreation
• Education

**Capital outlay:**
• Purchases of capital assets
• Not capitalized in fund statements!

**Debt service:**
• Principal payments
• Interest payments
• When legally due

sections.title: Other Financing Sources/Uses
sections.content:
**Sources (inflows not from operations):**
• Proceeds from debt issuance
• Transfers in from other funds
• Proceeds from capital asset sales
• Premium on bonds issued

**Uses (outflows not expenditures):**
• Transfers out to other funds
• Discount on bonds issued
• Payment to refunded bond escrow

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Bond Proceeds
sections.content:
**Bond proceeds treatment:**

**In Governmental Fund Statements:**
• Other Financing Source (NOT a liability)
• Full face value recorded
• Premium = additional OFS
• Discount = reduction of OFS

**In Government-Wide Statements:**
• Bonds payable (liability)
• Premium/discount amortized

**Exam tip:** OFS in fund statements; Liability on government-wide!

sections.title: Major Fund Reporting
sections.content:
**Major funds reported separately:**

**Always major:**
• General Fund

**Major if meets 10% AND 5% tests:**
• 10% of total governmental funds for that element
• AND 5% of governmental + enterprise combined

**Elements tested:** Assets, liabilities, revenues, expenditures

**Non-major funds:** Aggregated in "Other Governmental Funds" column

sections.title: Reconciliation to Government-Wide
sections.content:
**Reconciliation required from fund balance to net position:**

**Common adjustments:**

**Add:** Capital assets
**Subtract:** Depreciation (accumulated)
**Add:** Long-term receivables
**Subtract:** Long-term debt
**Subtract:** Accrued interest payable
**Adjust:** Internal service fund net position
**Adjust:** Deferred inflows/outflows differences

sections.title: Sample Reconciliation Items
sections.content:
**Fund Balance (governmental funds):** $5,000,000

**Adjustments:**
+ Capital assets                  $50,000,000
- Accumulated depreciation       (15,000,000)
+ Deferred charges (bond issue)      200,000
- Bonds payable                  (25,000,000)
- Accrued interest                  (300,000)
- Compensated absences            (1,500,000)
+ Internal service fund net         1,100,000

**Net Position (governmental activities):** $14,500,000
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Governmental Funds: It's All About the Flow!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Governmental funds can feel like drinking from a firehose. But remember, it's all about tracking the short-term flow of financial resources. Think of it like this: the government gets cash (revenues), spends cash (expenditures), and that changes the fund balance. No fancy buildings or long-term debt on these statements! It's like balancing your checkbook, but with more rules and a slightly higher chance of getting yelled at if you mess up. Keep it current, keep it cash-focused, and you'll be golden.

sections.title: Confusing Expenditures with Expenses!
sections.content: A big mistake is treating 'expenditures' like 'expenses' from commercial accounting. They're NOT the same! Expenditures are decreases in net financial resources, generally when a government spends cash. Expenses, on the other hand, use the accrual basis. So, paying $50,000 for police salaries is an expenditure. The accrual-based expense might be different if some salaries are accrued. Don't let the similar-sounding words fool you! Think 'Expenditures = Cash Out' in governmental funds.

sections.title: Sarah's Budget Blues
sections.content: Sarah, a newly appointed budget analyst for the City of Anytown, notices a large purchase of a fire truck listed as an expense on the General Fund Statement. She knows this is incorrect. Because fire trucks are capital assets, the purchase should be recorded as an expenditure on the Statement of Revenues, Expenditures, and Changes in Fund Balance. Remember, capital assets aren't reported on the governmental fund balance sheet, only the government-wide financial statements.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Budget Monitoring
sections.content: You'll use this knowledge constantly when monitoring a governmental entity's budget. Let's say you're an auditor reviewing the City of Springfield's financial statements. You'll need to trace the reported revenues and expenditures back to the approved budget to ensure compliance. Understanding the format and content of the governmental fund statements is crucial for verifying that the city is spending its funds appropriately and within the approved budgetary limits. This is vital for accountability and transparency.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Governmental fund balance sheet: No capital assets or long-term debt
Fund balance categories: Nonspendable, Restricted, Committed, Assigned, Unassigned
Expenditures include capital outlays and debt service (not expenses!)
Other financing sources: Bond proceeds, transfers in
General Fund always major; others if meet 10%/5% tests
Reconciliation bridges fund balance to government-wide net position

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Governmental Fund Balance Sheet: Comprehension Check

knowledgeCheck.question: The City of Anytown's Governmental Funds Balance Sheet includes a 'Due from the General Fund' balance in its Special Revenue Fund. How should this 'Due from' balance be classified on the Special Revenue Fund's Balance Sheet?
options: A current asset.
A capital asset.
A long-term receivable.
A deferred outflow of resources.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The 'Due from the General Fund' represents a short-term claim on the General Fund's resources, and because the governmental funds balance sheet only includes current financial resources, it is classified as a current asset.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what types of assets are reported on a governmental fund balance sheet.

reveal.question: List the five categories of fund balance classifications under GASB 54.
reveal.answer:
1. Nonspendable
2. Restricted
3. Committed
4. Assigned
5. Unassigned

rows.label: Capital Assets
values: Purchases are expenditures in the period incurred.
Capital assets are capitalized and depreciated over their useful lives.

rows.label: Long-Term Debt
values: Principal and interest payments are expenditures when due.
Long-term debt is reported as a liability.

title: Proprietary Fund Financial Statements
description: Learn the business-like statements for enterprise and internal service funds

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Proprietary funds operate like businesses—they use full accrual accounting and prepare statements similar to commercial entities. Knowing the differences between enterprise and internal service funds, and their unique cash flow statement format, is essential for FAR!

sections.title: Proprietary Funds Overview
sections.content:
**Two types:**

**Enterprise Funds:**
• Serve external customers
• User fees intended to cover costs
• Examples: Water utility, airport, transit

**Internal Service Funds:**
• Serve other government departments
• Costs recovered through charges
• Examples: Motor pool, IT services, printing

**Both use:** Economic resources measurement focus, full accrual basis

sections.title: Required Financial Statements
sections.content:
**Three required statements:**

**1. Statement of Net Position**
• Similar to balance sheet
• Classified format (current/noncurrent)
• Net position categories

**2. Statement of Revenues, Expenses, and Changes in Net Position**
• Operating vs nonoperating
• Like income statement

**3. Statement of Cash Flows**
• Direct method required!
• Four categories (unique to government)

sections.title: Statement of Net Position
sections.content:
**Assets:**
• Current assets
• Noncurrent assets
• Capital assets (net)

**Liabilities:**
• Current liabilities
• Noncurrent liabilities (bonds, etc.)

**Net Position:**
• Net investment in capital assets
• Restricted
• Unrestricted

sections.title: Operating vs Nonoperating
sections.content:
**Operating Revenues:**
• Charges for services
• Fees directly related to principal operations

**Operating Expenses:**
• Salaries, supplies, depreciation
• Costs of providing services

**Nonoperating:**
• Interest revenue and expense
• Grants and contributions (usually)
• Gains/losses on asset disposal

**The distinction matters for performance analysis!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: GASB Cash Flow Categories
sections.content:
**"Only Nerds Count Finance"**

**O**perating activities
**N**oncapital financing activities
**C**apital and related financing activities
**F**inancing from investing activities

**Key difference from FASB:**
• GASB: 4 categories (splits financing)
• FASB: 3 categories (operating, investing, financing)

sections.title: Cash Flow Categories Explained
rows: Capital asset purchases, bond proceeds, bond payments

sections.title: Direct Method Required
sections.content:
**GASB requires direct method for operating activities:**

**Cash flows from operating activities:**
  Cash received from customers      $5,000,000
  Cash paid to suppliers           (2,000,000)
  Cash paid to employees           (1,500,000)
  **Net cash from operating**       $1,500,000

**Reconciliation required:**
Reconcile operating income to cash from operations (indirect method reconciliation in notes or on face)

sections.title: Interest Classification
sections.content:
**Under GASB (different from FASB!):**

**Interest received:**
• Investing activities

**Interest paid:**
• If related to capital debt → Capital financing
• If related to operating debt → Noncapital financing

**FASB difference:** Under FASB, interest paid/received is usually operating

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Internal Service Fund Reporting
sections.content:
**Internal Service Funds:**

**In fund financial statements:**
• Reported with proprietary funds
• Separate column

**On government-wide statements:**
• Usually combined with GOVERNMENTAL activities
• Why? They primarily serve governmental functions

**Exception:** If enterprise funds are predominant users → include with business-type activities

sections.title: Enterprise Fund Requirements
sections.content:
**Enterprise fund REQUIRED when:**

**1. Debt secured solely by fees:**
• Revenue bonds backed only by service fees

**2. Legal requirement:**
• Law requires cost recovery through fees

**3. Pricing policy:**
• Fees designed to recover costs including depreciation

**May use for:** Any activity that COULD be a business

sections.title: Major Enterprise Fund Test
sections.content:
**Same 10%/5% test as governmental funds:**

**Major if:**
• 10% of total enterprise funds for an element
• AND 5% of total governmental + enterprise

**Or:** Management may designate as major

**Non-major:** Aggregated in "Other Enterprise Funds"
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Fund Financials: Not as Scary as They Look!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Governmental accounting can feel like a foreign language. But proprietary funds? They're your friends! Think of them as businesses run *by* the government. Enterprise funds are like the city water company, and internal service funds are like the government's own IT department. They use accrual accounting, just like the for-profit world. Master these statements, and you'll be golden on the FAR exam. Don't overthink it!

sections.title: Net Position: Restricted vs. Unrestricted Confusion
sections.content: A common mistake is confusing restricted and unrestricted net position in the Statement of Net Position. Students often assume all revenue generated by an enterprise fund is 'unrestricted.' However, grants or bond covenants might impose restrictions on how the funds can be used. For example, revenue from a new airport terminal expansion project funded by a federal grant might be restricted to only airport improvements. Always carefully analyze the source and purpose of the funds before classifying net position.

sections.title: City Motor Pool Overcharge: A Case of ISF Gone Wrong
sections.content: The city's motor pool, an internal service fund, charged the police department $5,000 for a routine oil change on a patrol car. The police chief, suspicious of the high cost, investigated and discovered the motor pool was padding its expenses to cover inefficiencies. This highlights the importance of proper cost accounting and oversight in internal service funds to ensure charges to other departments are fair and reasonable. Otherwise, it defeats the purpose of the ISF.

sections.title: Auditing Proprietary Funds: Real-World Scenarios
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter proprietary fund financial statements when auditing local governments. You'll need to verify that enterprise funds are properly accounting for user fees and that internal service funds are fairly allocating costs to other departments. Look for things like unusual fluctuations in revenue or expenses, unexpected deficits in internal service funds, and ensure proper disclosures are made regarding restrictions on net position. Understanding these statements is critical for a clean audit opinion.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Proprietary funds: Enterprise (external) and Internal Service (internal)
Use economic resources focus and full accrual basis
Three statements: Net Position, Revenues/Expenses, Cash Flows
Cash flows: 4 categories (Operating, Noncapital financing, Capital financing, Investing)
Direct method required for cash flow operating section
Internal Service funds usually reported with governmental on government-wide
Interest paid → financing activities (not operating as in FASB)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following activities would most appropriately be accounted for in an Enterprise Fund?
options: A city's central motor pool that provides vehicles to various city departments and charges them a fee.
A state's department of information technology that provides computer support to other state agencies and bills them for services rendered.
A municipal water utility that provides water services to residents and businesses outside the city limits and bills them for usage.
A county's printing department that provides printing services to county agencies and bills them based on the number of copies printed.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Enterprise Funds account for services provided to external customers for a fee. A municipal water utility serving residents and businesses outside the city is the best example of this. Internal Service Funds, on the other hand, serve other government departments.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who is receiving the service: external customers or internal government departments?

sections.title: Statement of Revenues, Expenses, and Changes in Net Position: Operating vs. Nonoperating

reveal.question: List three examples of items that would typically be classified as *nonoperating* revenues or expenses in the Statement of Revenues, Expenses, and Changes in Net Position for a proprietary fund.
reveal.answer:
Examples of nonoperating revenues and expenses include:
1.  Interest Expense on debt related to capital assets.
2.  Grants received from other governmental entities.
3.  Investment Income (e.g., interest or dividends earned on investments).

sections.title: Proprietary Funds: Key Statement Similarities to Commercial Accounting

rows.label: Statement of Revenues, Expenses, and Changes in Net Position
values: Statement of Revenues, Expenses, and Changes in Net Position

title: Fiduciary Fund Financial Statements
description: Understand fiduciary fund reporting for resources held in trust for others

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Fiduciary funds hold resources for others—pension beneficiaries, external investment pool participants, and individuals/organizations outside the government. They're NOT on government-wide statements because the government doesn't own these resources!

sections.title: Fiduciary Fund Types
sections.content:
**Four types (GASB 84):**

**1. Pension (and OPEB) Trust Funds:**
• Employee retirement benefits
• Other post-employment benefits

**2. Investment Trust Funds:**
• External investment pool participants
• Non-government entities in the pool

**3. Private-Purpose Trust Funds:**
• Trust benefits go to external parties
• Principal and/or income for others

**4. Custodial Funds:**
• Resources held temporarily
• Replaced old "Agency Funds"

sections.title: Required Statements
sections.content:
**Two required statements:**

**1. Statement of Fiduciary Net Position**
• Assets
• Liabilities
• Net Position (restricted for trust purpose)

**2. Statement of Changes in Fiduciary Net Position**
• Additions
• Deductions
• Change in net position

**Uses:** Economic resources measurement focus, full accrual basis

sections.title: Statement of Fiduciary Net Position
sections.content:
**Assets:**
• Cash and investments (at fair value)
• Receivables (contributions, interest)
• Capital assets (if any)

**Liabilities:**
• Amounts due to beneficiaries
• Other payables

**Net Position:**
• Restricted for pensions
• Restricted for other purposes
• Held in trust for participants

sections.title: Statement of Changes in Fiduciary Net Position
sections.content:
**Additions:**
• Contributions (employer, employee)
• Investment income (interest, dividends)
• Net increase in fair value of investments

**Deductions:**
• Benefit payments
• Administrative expenses
• Refunds to members
• Net decrease in fair value

**Net Increase (Decrease) in Net Position**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Fiduciary Funds
sections.content:
**"PIP-C" = Pension, Investment, Private-Purpose, Custodial**

**Pension:** Employee retirement $$
**Investment:** Pool participants' $$
**Private-Purpose:** Outsiders' trust $$
**Custodial:** Temporarily held $$

**Key:** All for OTHERS, not the government!
**Remember:** NOT on government-wide statements!

sections.title: Custodial Funds (GASB 84 Change)
sections.content:
**Replaced Agency Funds:**

**Old Agency Funds:**
• Assets = Liabilities (no net position)
• No operating statement

**New Custodial Funds:**
• Report net position
• Report additions and deductions
• Operating statement required

**Examples:**
• Tax collection for other governments
• Special assessments collected
• Pass-through grants

sections.title: Investment Reporting
sections.content:
**Investments reported at FAIR VALUE:**

**Includes:**
• Stocks, bonds, real estate
• Alternative investments

**Changes in fair value:**
• Reported in Statement of Changes
• As investment income (or deduction if negative)

**Note:** This differs from governmental funds where investments may be at amortized cost

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Not on Government-Wide
sections.content:
**Fiduciary funds are EXCLUDED from government-wide statements!**

**Why?**
• Resources belong to OTHERS
• Government is trustee/custodian
• Would overstate government's resources

**Where reported:**
• Separate fiduciary fund statements only
• In "Fund Financial Statements" section of CAFR

**Exam tip:** If a question asks about government-wide → exclude fiduciary!

sections.title: Pension Trust Fund Details
sections.content:
**Most common fiduciary fund:**

**Key components:**
• Net position = Plan assets available for benefits
• NOT the same as net pension liability

**Related reporting:**
• Government-wide shows net pension liability
• Pension fund shows assets held
• RSI: Schedules of employer contributions, investment returns

**Note:** Net pension liability is separate from pension trust fund reporting

sections.title: Fiduciary Activities Definition
sections.content:
**When to use fiduciary funds (GASB 84):**

**The government controls assets AND:**

**1. Assets from government-mandated programs:**
• Not entirely from government resources
• Benefits go to external parties or other governments

**2. Assets from voluntary agreements:**
• Government is not a beneficiary
• Assets are for benefit of others

**If government is beneficiary → NOT fiduciary**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Fiduciary Funds: Not Just Another Government Gig
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, fiduciary funds sound dry. But they're crucial! Think of it this way: governments often hold assets *for* someone else. Pensions, investments, private trusts – it's not *their* money. Mess this reporting up, and you're potentially shortchanging retirees, investment pool participants, or even private beneficiaries. Remember, you're not just crunching numbers; you're safeguarding other people's futures. So, pay attention to those fiduciary fund statements!

sections.title: Mixing Governmental & Fiduciary Fund Statements
sections.content: Students often mistakenly include fiduciary fund activities directly within the government-wide financial statements. This is wrong! Fiduciary funds are excluded from the government-wide financial statements because the resources held are *not* the government's own. They're held in trust for others. The key difference is economic benefit: governmental funds benefit the government, while fiduciary funds benefit external parties. Avoid this by remembering: fiduciary funds get their *own* set of statements, distinct from the government's consolidated view.

sections.title: Sarah's Pension Fund Panic
sections.content: Sarah, a new auditor, is reviewing the Statement of Changes in Fiduciary Net Position for a city's pension trust fund. She notices a huge drop in net position but can't find a corresponding expense in the city's governmental funds. The learning point: Pension trust fund changes are *not* reported as expenses in the governmental funds. They're reported solely within the fiduciary fund statements. Sarah needs to investigate the components of the change in net position within the fiduciary fund itself.

sections.title: Spotting Fiduciary Funds in the Wild
sections.content: In practice, you'll most often encounter fiduciary funds when auditing or preparing financial statements for state or local governments. You'll see them listed as separate columns or sections in the Comprehensive Annual Financial Report (CAFR). Knowing how to correctly classify and report these funds is critical for ensuring accurate and transparent financial reporting. Failing to do so can result in misstatements, potentially impacting bond ratings and public trust. Remember, these funds represent resources held *for* others, making accurate reporting paramount.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four fiduciary types: Pension Trust, Investment Trust, Private-Purpose Trust, Custodial
Resources held for OTHERS, not the government
NOT included in government-wide statements
Two statements: Fiduciary Net Position + Changes in Fiduciary Net Position
Custodial funds replaced Agency funds (now have net position and changes)
Investments at fair value; changes reported as investment income
Economic resources focus, full accrual basis

title: Government Fund Accounting: General and Special Revenue
description: Master the two primary governmental fund types

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The General Fund and Special Revenue Funds account for most of a government's day-to-day operations! Understanding how these funds work—including budgetary entries, revenue recognition, and expenditure recording—is essential for FAR governmental questions!

sections.title: General Fund Overview
sections.content:
**The General Fund is the "main operating fund":**

• Accounts for all resources NOT required to be in another fund
• Most significant fund for most governments
• Only ONE General Fund per government
• Records day-to-day operations

**Common revenues:** Property taxes, sales taxes, fees, fines

sections.title: General Fund Characteristics
sections.content:
**Measurement focus:** Current financial resources
**Basis of accounting:** Modified accrual

**What appears on balance sheet:**
• Current assets (cash, receivables, inventory)
• Current liabilities (payables)
• Fund balance classifications

**What does NOT appear:**
• Capital assets (no depreciation)
• Long-term debt

sections.title: Budgetary Accounting
sections.content:
**Governments must adopt legal budgets:**

**Recording the budget (beginning of year):**
```
Dr: Estimated Revenues         $5,000,000
Dr: Budgetary Fund Balance       200,000
   Cr: Appropriations           $4,800,000
   Cr: Estimated Other Uses       400,000
```

**Reverse at year-end when closing!**

**Purpose:** Legal control over spending

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Budgetary Entries
sections.content:
**"DEAR COCA" for Budget Entry:**

**D**ebit: **E**stimated **R**evenues (expected income)
**C**redit: **A**ppropriations (spending authority)

**Balance goes to Budgetary Fund Balance**

**Surplus budget:** Credit Budgetary FB
**Deficit budget:** Debit Budgetary FB

sections.title: Special Revenue Funds
sections.content:
**Account for resources restricted to specific purposes:**

**Examples:**
• Gas tax revenue → Road maintenance fund
• Hotel tax → Tourism fund
• Grant revenue → Grant fund

**Same accounting as General Fund:**
• Current financial resources
• Modified accrual basis

**When required:** When legally mandated OR resource provider requires

sections.title: Revenue Recognition - Modified Accrual
sections.content:
**Revenue recognized when:**

**1. Measurable** - Amount can be reasonably estimated
**2. Available** - Collected within availability period

**Availability period:** Typically 60 days after year-end

**Example:**
Property tax billed Dec 1, due Jan 15:
• If collected by Feb 28 → Revenue this year
• If collected March 15 → Deferred inflow this year

sections.title: Expenditure Recognition
sections.content:
**Expenditures recorded when fund liability incurred:**

**Current expenditures:** When goods/services received
**Capital outlays:** When asset acquired (not capitalized!)

**Special timing rules:**
• Debt principal/interest → When legally due
• Compensated absences → When paid (or matured)
• Claims/judgments → When paid

**No depreciation expense in governmental funds!**

sections.title: Encumbrance Accounting
sections.content:
**Record purchase orders before goods received:**

**When PO issued:**
```
Dr: Encumbrances            $10,000
   Cr: Budgetary Fund Balance  $10,000
```

**When goods received:**
```
Dr: Budgetary Fund Balance  $10,000
   Cr: Encumbrances            $10,000

Dr: Expenditures            $10,200
   Cr: Vouchers Payable        $10,200
```

**Prevents overspending appropriations!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Outstanding Encumbrances
sections.content:
**At year-end, outstanding encumbrances:**

• Close to Fund Balance - Committed or Assigned
• NOT reported as liabilities (goods not received)
• Represent future commitments

**Next year:** May re-encumber if policy permits

**On balance sheet:** Shown in Fund Balance classifications
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: General Fund: Think of it as the Government's Wallet
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Government fund accounting can feel like alphabet soup. But the General Fund? It's the government's wallet. Everything that doesn't *have* to go somewhere else ends up here. Think property taxes, sales taxes – the bread and butter. If you're auditing a city, you'll spend a HUGE chunk of time in the General Fund. So, nail this down, or you'll be drowning in journal entries faster than you can say 'modified accrual'!

sections.title: Forgetting the Modified Accrual Magic Trick
sections.content: Students often apply full accrual accounting to the General Fund. Big mistake! Remember, it's *modified* accrual. Revenues are recognized when measurable and available (generally, collectible within 60 days of year-end). Expenses are recognized when the liability is incurred, with some exceptions for debt service. Don't treat it like a for-profit business; you'll end up with inaccurate financial statements and a very confused audit manager.

sections.title: Sarah's Special Revenue Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a new government accountant, recorded a $500,000 grant earmarked for a specific city park improvement project in the General Fund because the cash was initially deposited there. This is incorrect. Since the grant is legally restricted for a specific purpose (the park), it should be accounted for in a Special Revenue Fund, not the General Fund, to ensure proper tracking and accountability of restricted resources.

sections.title: Real-World: Budget vs. Actual Analysis
sections.content: In your audit work, you'll frequently compare the General Fund's budgeted revenues and expenditures with actual results. This analysis helps assess the government's financial performance and compliance with budgetary requirements. For example, if property tax revenue is significantly below budget, you'll need to investigate the reasons, which could range from economic downturn to assessment errors. Understanding the General Fund is critical for this task.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: General Fund: Main operating fund, only one per government
Special Revenue: Restricted revenue sources, similar accounting
Modified accrual: Revenue when measurable AND available
Budget entry: DR Est. Revenue, CR Appropriations
Encumbrances: Record POs to control spending
Capital outlays: Expenditures, not capitalized
Outstanding encumbrances: Close to Fund Balance

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following activities would most likely be accounted for in a government's General Fund?
options: A city's water and sewer operations, funded by user fees.
A state's lottery operations, where proceeds are legally restricted for education.
A county's routine road maintenance, funded primarily by property taxes.
A special assessment levied to finance the construction of sidewalks in a specific neighborhood.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The General Fund accounts for all resources not required to be accounted for in another fund. Routine road maintenance funded by property taxes is a typical function of the General Fund. The other options involve specific revenue sources or activities that would likely be accounted for in other funds (e.g., enterprise fund for water/sewer, special revenue fund for lottery proceeds or special assessments).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the 'default' fund for government activities.

sections.title: Measurement Focus and Basis of Accounting

reveal.question: What are the measurement focus and basis of accounting used in the General Fund?
reveal.answer: The General Fund uses the current financial resources measurement focus and the modified accrual basis of accounting.

knowledgeCheck.question: A city approves a budget with estimated revenues of $8,000,000, appropriations of $7,500,000, and estimated other financing uses of $600,000. What is the budgetary fund balance entry?
options: Debit Budgetary Fund Balance $100,000
Credit Budgetary Fund Balance $100,000
Debit Budgetary Fund Balance $1,100,000
Credit Budgetary Fund Balance $1,100,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The budgetary fund balance is the difference between estimated revenues and appropriations, adjusted for other financing sources and uses. In this case: $8,000,000 (Estimated Revenues) - $7,500,000 (Appropriations) - $600,000 (Estimated Other Uses) = -$100,000. Since the result is negative, we need a debit to Budgetary Fund Balance to make the budgetary entry balance.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that estimated revenues and budgetary fund balance are debits, while appropriations and estimated other uses are credits.

title: Capital Projects and Debt Service Funds
description: Master funds for capital improvements and debt repayment

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Capital Projects Funds account for major construction and acquisitions, while Debt Service Funds handle debt repayment. Understanding how these funds interact with long-term assets and liabilities is critical for FAR governmental questions!

sections.title: Capital Projects Fund Overview
sections.content:
**Account for resources used to acquire capital assets:**

**Common uses:**
• Building construction
• Major equipment purchases
• Infrastructure projects

**Funding sources:**
• Bond proceeds
• Grants
• Transfers from General Fund

**Same measurement focus/basis as General Fund!**

sections.title: Bond Proceeds in Capital Projects Fund
sections.content:
**Recording bond issuance:**

**At par:**
```
Dr: Cash                    $1,000,000
   Cr: Other Financing Sources $1,000,000
```

**Premium goes to Debt Service Fund:**
```
Dr: Cash                    $1,050,000
   Cr: OFS - Bond Proceeds     $1,000,000
   Cr: OFS - Premium             $50,000
```

**No long-term debt recorded in Capital Projects Fund!**

sections.title: Construction Expenditures
sections.content:
**Construction costs are EXPENDITURES (not assets):**

**Progress billings:**
```
Dr: Expenditures           $500,000
   Cr: Contracts Payable      $500,000
```

**Retention:**
```
Dr: Expenditures           $50,000
   Cr: Contracts Payable - Retention $50,000
```

**Asset appears in Government-wide statements only!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Where Things Go
sections.content:
**"Capital Projects = Cash In, Cash Out"**

**In the Capital Projects Fund:**
• Bond proceeds (Other Financing Source)
• Expenditures (cash going out)
• NO capital assets
• NO long-term debt

**In Government-Wide Statements:**
• Capital assets (construction in progress)
• Long-term debt (bonds payable)

**Two views of same transaction!**

sections.title: Debt Service Fund Overview
sections.content:
**Account for resources used to pay long-term debt:**

**Purpose:**
• Accumulate resources for debt payments
• Pay principal and interest when due

**Revenue sources:**
• Transfers from General Fund
• Property taxes dedicated to debt
• Bond premium transferred from Capital Projects

**Expenditures recognized when LEGALLY DUE!**

sections.title: Debt Service Expenditures
sections.content:
**Principal and interest recognized when legally due:**

**NOT when accrued!**

**Example:** $1M bond, interest due Jan 1 and July 1

Fiscal year-end June 30:
• Interest for Jan-June: NOT recorded as expenditure
• Interest due July 1: Expenditure in NEXT year

**Exception:** If resources available and payment imminent, accrue

sections.title: Fund Balance Accumulation
sections.content:
**Debt Service Funds accumulate resources:**

**Over time:**
• Resources transferred in
• Held until debt payments due
• Expenditure only when legally due

**May result in significant fund balance**

**Classification:** Restricted for debt service

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Principal vs Interest
sections.content:
**Both principal AND interest:**

• Recorded as EXPENDITURES when legally due
• NO distinction on fund statements
• Both reduce fund balance equally

**In Government-Wide:**
• Principal reduces bonds payable
• Interest is expense

**Different treatment between the two views!**

sections.title: Reconciliation Items
sections.content:
**Converting to Government-Wide:**

**Add back:**
• Capital asset purchases (recorded as expenditure)
• Principal payments (reduced debt, not expense)

**Remove:**
• Bond proceeds (it's a liability, not revenue)

**Add:**
• Depreciation expense
• Accrued interest (not paid yet)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Fund Accounting: Not as Scary as it Sounds!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, fund accounting can seem like a bureaucratic monster. But Capital Projects and Debt Service Funds? They're actually pretty straightforward. Think of it like this: you're building a new school (Capital Projects) and need to pay off the loan you took out to do it (Debt Service). The key is remembering they use the same modified accrual basis as the General Fund. Don't overthink it! If you understand the general fund, these aren't a huge leap. I know, I know, easier said than done. But you got this!

sections.title: Premium Placement Predicament
sections.content: A common mistake is crediting 'Other Financing Sources' for the entire bond issuance amount, even when there's a premium. Remember, the premium *doesn't* stay in the Capital Projects Fund. It's destined for the Debt Service Fund to help pay down the debt later. So, if you issue bonds for $1,050,000, with a $50,000 premium, only $1,000,000 goes to 'Other Financing Sources' in the Capital Projects Fund. The $50,000 premium is recorded in the Debt Service Fund. Failing to do this throws off your fund balances and misrepresents where the money's going.

sections.title: Sarah's Spreadsheet Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a government accountant, was reconciling the Capital Projects Fund for a new city hall. She noticed a $10,000 discrepancy between the expected bond proceeds and the actual cash received. After digging, she realized the underwriter had taken a small cut for issuance costs, which hadn't been properly accounted for. The lesson? Remember to consider *all* costs associated with bond issuance, not just the par value and premium, when tracking funding sources. These costs are generally expensed.

sections.title: Bond Issuance Audits: Your New Best Friend
sections.content: In practice, you'll often encounter these funds during bond issuance audits. You'll be tracing the bond proceeds from the underwriter to the Capital Projects Fund, verifying the proper allocation of premiums to the Debt Service Fund, and ensuring compliance with bond covenants. Mastering the journal entries for bond issuance, especially the treatment of premiums and discounts, is crucial for these audits. Knowing where the money *should* be allows you to quickly identify potential irregularities or misstatements.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital Projects: Major capital asset acquisitions
Bond proceeds = Other Financing Sources (not revenue)
Construction costs = Expenditures (not capital assets)
Debt Service: Accumulates resources for debt payment
Principal/interest = Expenditure when LEGALLY DUE
No long-term debt in governmental fund balance sheets
Reconcile to Government-Wide for complete picture

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A city issued bonds at $1,050,000 to finance a new library. The bonds were issued at a premium. What is the correct journal entry in the Capital Projects Fund to record the bond issuance?
options: Dr: Cash $1,000,000; Cr: Other Financing Sources $1,000,000
Dr: Cash $1,050,000; Cr: Other Financing Sources $1,050,000
Dr: Cash $1,050,000; Cr: Other Financing Sources $1,000,000; Cr: Other Financing Sources - Premium $50,000
Dr: Cash $1,000,000; Dr: Due From Debt Service Fund $50,000; Cr: Other Financing Sources $1,050,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Capital Projects Fund receives the entire cash amount ($1,050,000). It credits Other Financing Sources for the face value of the bonds ($1,000,000) and recognizes the premium as a separate Other Financing Source ($50,000).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember where the premium goes after the bond is issued.

reveal.question: In a Capital Projects Fund, are construction costs recorded as expenditures or assets?
reveal.answer: Construction costs are recorded as EXPENDITURES in the Capital Projects Fund. The asset being constructed will be recorded in the government-wide financial statements.

title: NFP Statement of Financial Position
description: Master the unique balance sheet format for not-for-profit organizations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Not-for-profit organizations—like charities, hospitals, and universities—use different financial statement formats than businesses. Understanding net asset classifications and the unique terminology is essential for NFP questions on FAR!

sections.title: NFP vs Business Accounting
sections.content:
**Key differences:**

**Businesses:**
• Owners' equity (stockholders, partners)
• Focused on profit
• Retained earnings

**Not-for-profits:**
• No owners → Net assets (not equity)
• Mission-focused
• Net assets classifications

**NFP follows:** ASC 958 (FASB standards for NFPs)

sections.title: Required Financial Statements
sections.content:
**Four required statements:**

**1. Statement of Financial Position** (balance sheet)
**2. Statement of Activities** (income statement)
**3. Statement of Cash Flows**
**4. Statement of Functional Expenses** (required for some NFPs)

**Accrual basis of accounting throughout!**

sections.title: Statement of Financial Position Format
sections.content:
**Assets:**
• Current assets
• Long-term investments
• Property and equipment (net)
• Other assets

**Liabilities:**
• Current liabilities
• Long-term debt

**Net Assets:**
• Without donor restrictions
• With donor restrictions

sections.title: Net Asset Classifications (ASU 2016-14)
sections.content:
**Only TWO categories now:**

**1. Net Assets WITHOUT Donor Restrictions:**
• Available for general use
• Board may designate for specific purposes
• Designated ≠ Restricted

**2. Net Assets WITH Donor Restrictions:**
• Purpose restrictions (use for specific activities)
• Time restrictions (use in future periods)
• Perpetual restrictions (endowments - principal)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Net Asset Categories
sections.content:
**"With or Without" (like the U2 song!)**

**WITHOUT donor restrictions:**
• Board can use however needed
• "We can live WITH or WITHOUT limitations"

**WITH donor restrictions:**
• Donor says how/when to use
• Purpose: "Use for scholarships"
• Time: "Use after 2025"
• Perpetual: "Never spend the principal"

**Old terms (no longer used):** Unrestricted, Temporarily Restricted, Permanently Restricted

sections.title: Board Designations
sections.content:
**Board can designate net assets for specific uses:**

**Example:** Board designates $500,000 for building fund

**Classification:** Still WITHOUT donor restrictions!

**Why?** Board can change its mind; donors cannot.

**Disclosure:** May disclose board-designated amounts within "without donor restrictions" or in notes

sections.title: Investment Classification
sections.content:
**Investments reported at fair value:**

**Exceptions (may use other methods):**
• Equity method investments
• Certain alternative investments

**Gains/losses on investments:**
• Generally reported as without donor restrictions
• Unless gains are restricted by donor or law

**Endowment investments:**
• Original gift = with donor restrictions (perpetual)
• Accumulated gains = depends on law/donor stipulations

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Restrictions vs Designations
sections.content:
**Donor RESTRICTIONS → With donor restrictions**
• Externally imposed by donor
• Cannot be changed by NFP

**Board DESIGNATIONS → Without donor restrictions**
• Internally imposed by board
• Board can change anytime

**Exam tip:** If the BOARD decided, it's NOT restricted!

**Only DONORS (or law) can create restrictions.**

sections.title: Liquidity Disclosure
sections.content:
**Required liquidity information (ASU 2016-14):**

**Qualitative:** How NFP manages liquid resources

**Quantitative:** Financial assets available within one year to meet cash needs for general expenditures

**Reductions for:**
• Assets with donor restrictions
• Assets with board designations
• Assets not available for general use

sections.title: Sample Statement Format
sections.content:
**SAMPLE NFP STATEMENT OF FINANCIAL POSITION**

**ASSETS**
Cash and equivalents                $500,000
Accounts receivable                   75,000
Contributions receivable             300,000
Investments                        2,000,000
Property and equipment, net        1,500,000
**Total Assets**                  $4,375,000

**LIABILITIES**
Accounts payable                    $125,000
Notes payable                        400,000
**Total Liabilities**               $525,000

**NET ASSETS**
Without donor restrictions        $1,850,000
With donor restrictions            2,000,000
**Total Net Assets**              $3,850,000

**Total Liabilities and Net Assets** $4,375,000
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Net Assets: It's Not Equity, Folks!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Coming from corporate accounting, the idea of 'net assets' instead of 'equity' can feel weird. You're used to shareholders, retained earnings, the whole shebang. But NFPs don't have owners! So, think of net assets as the organization's 'stuff' after you subtract what it owes. It’s mission-focused, not profit-driven. Don't call it equity on the exam, or the examiners will know you're a corporate spy! Seriously, nail this distinction.

sections.title: Confusing Net Asset Classifications
sections.content: Students often struggle differentiating between net assets *with* donor restrictions and net assets *without* donor restrictions. The mistake lies in assuming all donor-imposed stipulations are permanent. A restriction is temporary if it can be fulfilled either by passage of time or by actions of the organization (e.g., spending money on a specific program). A permanent restriction means the principal must remain in perpetuity, and only the income can be used. Read the restriction language *carefully*! It's all about control.

sections.title: Sarah's Tricky Endowment
sections.content: Sarah, a new NFP accountant, is reviewing a $1 million endowment. The donor stipulated that the income be used for cancer research, but the principal must remain untouched. Sarah incorrectly classified the entire $1 million as net assets *without* donor restrictions. The endowment principal is subject to a permanent restriction. This misclassification would significantly misrepresent the NFP's financial position and how it can use its assets.

sections.title: Statement of Financial Position in the Wild
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter the NFP Statement of Financial Position when auditing or preparing financial statements for charities, foundations, and other non-profits. Understanding the net asset classifications is crucial for assessing the organization's financial health and its ability to fulfill its mission. For example, a large portion of net assets with permanent restrictions might indicate limited flexibility in addressing immediate needs. This affects your audit approach and the insights you provide to stakeholders.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NFPs use 'Net Assets' instead of equity or fund balance
Two net asset categories: Without donor restrictions, With donor restrictions
Board designations are still 'without donor restrictions'
Only donors (or law) can create true restrictions
With donor restrictions includes purpose, time, and perpetual restrictions
Liquidity disclosure required showing available resources
Investments generally at fair value

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following financial statements is unique to not-for-profit organizations and is NOT required for for-profit entities?
options: Statement of Cash Flows
Statement of Functional Expenses
Statement of Activities
Statement of Financial Position
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Statement of Functional Expenses is a required statement for certain NFPs that details expenses by program and supporting activities. This statement is unique to the NFP sector and helps donors understand how the organization uses its resources. The other statements are also used by for-profit entities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the statement that provides detail on how expenses are allocated within the organization.

reveal.question: Name the two classifications of net assets used in a NFP Statement of Financial Position.
reveal.answer: The two classifications are: Net Assets Without Donor Restrictions and Net Assets With Donor Restrictions.

title: NFP Statement of Activities
description: Learn how to report revenues, expenses, and changes in net assets for NFPs

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Statement of Activities is the NFP's 'income statement'—it shows how net assets changed during the year. Understanding revenue recognition, expense reporting, and the release of restrictions is essential for NFP questions on FAR!

sections.title: Statement of Activities Overview
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Show changes in net assets during the period

**Format options:**
• Single column with totals
• Multi-column by net asset class

**Key components:**
• Revenues and gains
• Expenses (by function or nature)
• Releases from restrictions
• Change in net assets

sections.title: Revenue Categories
sections.content:
**Common NFP revenues:**

**Contributions:**
• Cash donations
• Promises to give (pledges)
• Contributed services (if criteria met)
• In-kind contributions

**Earned Revenues:**
• Program service fees
• Membership dues
• Sales to public

**Other:**
• Investment return
• Special events (net)

sections.title: Revenue Classification
sections.content:
**Classify based on donor restrictions:**

**Without donor restrictions:**
• Unrestricted gifts
• Earned revenues
• Board-designated gifts

**With donor restrictions:**
• Purpose-restricted gifts
• Time-restricted gifts
• Gifts to endowment
