**Note:** Classification is based on DONOR INTENT at time of gift

sections.title: Release from Restrictions
sections.content:
**When restrictions are satisfied:**

**Reclassification entry:**
Decrease: Net assets WITH donor restrictions
Increase: Net assets WITHOUT donor restrictions

**Satisfaction occurs when:**
• Purpose accomplished (scholarships awarded)
• Time elapsed (time restriction met)
• Both purpose AND time met

**Perpetual restrictions:** NEVER released (endowment principal)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Release Process
sections.content:
**"Restricted IN, Released OUT"**

**Step 1:** Receive restricted gift → WITH restrictions (increase)
**Step 2:** Use as intended → Release to WITHOUT (decrease WITH, increase WITHOUT)

**Example:**
• 2024: Receive $50,000 for scholarships → WITH restrictions ↑
• 2025: Award scholarships → Release $50,000
  - WITH restrictions ↓
  - WITHOUT restrictions ↑

**Net effect:** Revenue recognized when received; released when used

sections.title: Expense Reporting
sections.content:
**All expenses reduce net assets WITHOUT donor restrictions!**

**Why?** Donors restrict REVENUES, not expenses. When money is spent, restrictions are released first.

**Expense classification:**
• By function (program, management, fundraising)
• By nature (salaries, supplies, depreciation)
• Or both (matrix format)

sections.title: Statement of Functional Expenses
sections.content:
**Required for:**
• Voluntary health and welfare organizations
• Other NFPs may present voluntarily

**Format:** Matrix showing:
• Columns: Functional categories (program, M&G, fundraising)
• Rows: Natural expense classifications (salaries, supplies, etc.)

**Alternative:** Present functional expenses in notes

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Investment Gains on Endowments
sections.content:
**Where do endowment investment gains go?**

**It depends on donor stipulation and applicable law!**

**If donor specifies gains must be reinvested:**
→ WITH donor restrictions

**If donor is silent:** Follow state law (UPMIFA)
→ Usually WITH donor restrictions until appropriated
→ Becomes WITHOUT when board appropriates for spending

**Original gift:** Always WITH restrictions (perpetual)

sections.title: Special Events
sections.content:
**Reporting special event revenues:**

**Gross method (preferred):**
• Gross revenues in revenue section
• Direct costs in expense section

**Net method (allowed):**
• Net revenue in revenue section
• Disclose gross amounts

**Exchange portion:** (e.g., dinner value)
• Report as exchange transaction

**Contribution portion:** (excess over value)
• Report as contribution

sections.title: Sample Statement Format
sections.content:
**STATEMENT OF ACTIVITIES**

|                              | Without | With | Total |
|------------------------------|---------|------|-------|
| **Revenues:**               |         |      |       |
| Contributions                | $400    | $300 | $700  |
| Program service fees         | 200     | -    | 200   |
| Investment return            | 50      | 25   | 75    |
| Net assets released          | 250     | (250)| -     |
| **Total Revenues**           | **900** |**75**|**975**|
| **Expenses:**                |         |      |       |
| Program services             | 500     | -    | 500   |
| Management & general         | 100     | -    | 100   |
| Fundraising                  | 75      | -    | 75    |
| **Total Expenses**           | **675** | -    |**675**|
| **Change in Net Assets**     | **225** |**75**|**300**|

*(amounts in thousands)*
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Statement of Activities: It's All About the Story
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest. The Statement of Activities might sound boring, but it's the NFP's way of telling its financial story. Forget just crunching numbers; think about what the organization *actually does*. Did they get a huge donation to build a new wing? Did membership dues plummet because of, say, a global pandemic? The statement needs to clearly show how net assets changed, broken down by those pesky restrictions. Master this, and you'll be able to tell if they're thriving or just surviving. No one wants to be part of a poorly managed NFP!

sections.title: Confusing Releases from Restriction with Revenue
sections.content: Students often mistakenly classify releases from restrictions as revenue. Remember, the *initial* contribution is revenue. The release is simply moving assets from the 'restricted' pot to the 'unrestricted' pot when the restriction is met (like time passing or a program being completed). For example, if a $100,000 donation was restricted for a specific program and $25,000 of the program expenses were incurred this year, only $25,000 would be released from restriction, *not* recorded as new revenue. Treat releases as a reclassification, not new income.

sections.title: Sarah's In-Kind Contribution Conundrum
sections.content: Sarah, a new staff accountant at 'Helping Hands' NFP, received a donation of office furniture. The donor claimed it was worth $5,000. Sarah isn't sure if she can record this as revenue. If the furniture is used in the organization's operations AND can be measured reliably, it's an in-kind contribution. Sarah needs to obtain documentation (like an appraisal or invoice) to support the $5,000 valuation and record it as both a contribution and an expense (office supplies or depreciation) in the Statement of Activities.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Grants and Pledges
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter complex revenue streams like government grants and multi-year pledges. Government grants often have very specific spending requirements and reporting deadlines. Pledges, or promises to give, require careful assessment for collectibility. You'll need to determine if a pledge is unconditional (record as revenue now) or conditional (record when conditions are met). Imagine a $500,000 pledge contingent on the NFP raising matching funds; you can't record that as revenue until the matching funds are secured. Understanding these nuances is crucial for accurate financial reporting and maintaining donor trust.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Statement of Activities shows changes in both net asset classes
Revenues classified based on donor restrictions at time of gift
Release from restrictions when purpose/time requirement met
ALL expenses reported as without donor restrictions
Functional expenses: Program services, Management & General, Fundraising
Endowment gains: Follow donor stipulations and state law (UPMIFA)
Special events: Separate exchange portion from contribution portion

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A not-for-profit organization received a $100,000 donation restricted to funding a specific research project. The donor explicitly stated that the funds should be used within the next two years. How should this donation be classified on the Statement of Activities?
options: Without donor restrictions
With donor restrictions - Purpose-restricted
With donor restrictions - Time-restricted
With donor restrictions - Both Purpose- and Time-restricted
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The donation is restricted because the donor has specified both the purpose (research project) and the time period (within the next two years) for which the funds can be used. Therefore, it is classified as both purpose- and time-restricted.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what elements of the donation create limitations on its use.

reveal.question: What is the primary purpose of the Statement of Activities for a not-for-profit organization?
reveal.answer: To report the changes in net assets (without donor restrictions, with donor restrictions) during a specific period.

rows.label: Examples
values: Unrestricted cash gift, Pledge for building fund

title: NFP Statement of Cash Flows
description: Understand the unique cash flow reporting requirements for not-for-profit organizations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: NFPs must present a Statement of Cash Flows just like businesses—but with some unique classifications. Knowing where restricted contributions and donor-restricted endowment gifts go is frequently tested on FAR!

sections.title: Cash Flow Basics for NFPs
sections.content:
**Same three categories as businesses:**

**1. Operating Activities**
**2. Investing Activities**
**3. Financing Activities**

**Method options:**
• Direct method (encouraged)
• Indirect method (allowed)

**Key difference:** Classification of restricted contributions

sections.title: Operating Activities
sections.content:
**Cash inflows:**
• Cash from service recipients
• Unrestricted contributions received
• Unrestricted dividends and interest
• Operating grants received

**Cash outflows:**
• Cash paid to employees and suppliers
• Cash paid for program services
• Interest paid

**Note:** Most contributions go here if unrestricted!

sections.title: Investing Activities
sections.content:
**Cash inflows:**
• Proceeds from sale of investments
• Proceeds from sale of fixed assets
• Collection of loans made

**Cash outflows:**
• Purchases of investments
• Purchases of property and equipment
• Loans made to others

**Similar to business investing activities**

sections.title: Financing Activities
sections.content:
**Cash inflows:**
• **Contributions restricted for long-term purposes**
• **Contributions to endowment**
• Proceeds from borrowings
• Interest/dividends restricted for reinvestment

**Cash outflows:**
• Repayment of debt
• Payments on capital leases

**Key NFP difference:** Donor-restricted long-term gifts are FINANCING!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Restricted Contribution Classification
sections.content:
**"If it builds the future, it's FINANCING"**

**Operating Activities:**
• Unrestricted contributions ✓
• Purpose-restricted for CURRENT operations ✓

**Financing Activities:**
• Contributions for LONG-TERM purposes ✓
• Endowment gifts ✓
• Building campaign contributions ✓

**Think:** Financing = Future focus; Operating = Operations now

sections.title: Interest and Dividends
sections.content:
**Generally Operating Activities (like businesses)**

**Exception:** If restricted for long-term purposes or endowment reinvestment:
→ Financing Activities

**Exam tip:** Most investment income is operating; only report as financing if there's a specific long-term restriction

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Reconciliation of Change in Net Assets
sections.content:
**If using indirect method:**

Start with: Change in Net Assets (NOT just operating!)

**Adjustments include:**
• Add back: Depreciation
• Deduct: Gains on investments
• Add: Losses on investments
• Adjust for changes in receivables, payables
• **Deduct: Contributions restricted for long-term/endowment**

**That last one is unique to NFPs!**

sections.title: Sample Indirect Method Reconciliation
sections.content:
**CASH FLOWS FROM OPERATING ACTIVITIES**

Change in net assets                        $300,000
Adjustments:
  Depreciation                               50,000
  Decrease in receivables                    20,000
  Increase in accounts payable               15,000
  Unrealized gain on investments            (30,000)
  **Contributions restricted for:**
    **Long-term purposes**                 **(75,000)**
    **Endowment**                          **(25,000)**

**Net cash from operating activities**      $255,000

**Note:** The restricted contributions are REMOVED from operating and reported in financing

sections.title: Noncash Transactions
sections.content:
**Disclose significant noncash activities:**

**Examples for NFPs:**
• Receipt of donated securities
• Receipt of donated equipment
• Receipt of donated services (if recognized)
• Conversion of debt to net assets

**Not on face of statement:** Disclosed in notes or supplementary schedule
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: NFP Cash Flows: It's All About Those Restrictions!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. NFP cash flow statements aren't rocket science, but those restricted contributions? They'll trip you up if you're not careful. Think of it this way: if someone gives an NFP $10,000 to build a playground, that money can't just be used to pay the executive director's bonus! The donor said 'playground,' so 'playground' it is. That restriction dictates how it's classified on the cash flow statement. Nail that down, and you're halfway there. Seriously, I've seen seasoned accountants fumble this. Don't be that person!

sections.title: Confusing Restricted & Unrestricted Contributions
sections.content: Students often misclassify temporarily restricted contributions as operating activities when they are initially received. The critical point is that temporarily restricted contributions, even if eventually used for operating purposes, are initially classified as financing activities. Why? Because the donor placed a restriction on the use of those funds. Only unrestricted contributions are included in operating activities. Avoid this by always considering the donor's intent and the nature of the restriction when classifying contributions.

sections.title: Sarah's Tricky Grant: How to Classify?
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant at a local food bank, received a $50,000 grant from a foundation. The grant stipulates that the funds must be used to purchase fresh produce within the next year. The money arrived in December. How should Sarah classify this cash inflow on the statement of cash flows? Since it's restricted for a specific purpose (produce) and a specific timeframe (one year), it's a financing activity until the restriction is met.

sections.title: Real-World Impact: Grants & Donor Reporting
sections.content: In the non-profit world, understanding cash flow classifications is crucial for grant applications and donor reporting. Foundations often want to see how an NFP manages its cash and whether it's using restricted funds appropriately. Misclassifying contributions can lead to inaccurate financial statements, potentially jeopardizing future funding. For example, a foundation might require a statement of cash flows using the direct method. Mastering this now ensures you can confidently handle these real-world scenarios and keep the funding flowing.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NFP cash flows use same three categories as businesses
Unrestricted and current-period restricted contributions → Operating
Long-term restricted and endowment contributions → Financing
Interest/dividends generally operating unless restricted for long-term
Indirect method starts with change in TOTAL net assets
Must remove financing-type contributions from operating reconciliation
Disclose significant noncash activities (donated securities, equipment)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Knowledge Check: NFP Cash Flow Classification

knowledgeCheck.question: A not-for-profit organization received a $100,000 contribution restricted by the donor for the acquisition of equipment. How would this contribution be classified on the statement of cash flows?
options: Operating Activities
Investing Activities
Financing Activities
Noncash Investing and Financing Activities
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Contributions restricted for long-term purposes, such as the acquisition of equipment, are classified as financing activities. These are considered similar to proceeds from borrowing, as they increase the organization's resources for a specific long-term purpose.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the nature of the restriction and how it impacts the organization's long-term assets.

reveal.question: Which method of presenting cash flows from operating activities is encouraged by accounting standards?
reveal.answer: The direct method is encouraged. It reports the actual cash inflows and outflows from operating activities, providing a more transparent view of the organization's cash generation.

sections.title: Comparison: NFP vs. For-Profit Investing Activities

rows.label: General Purpose
values: To support the organization's mission and programs, and generate income for future activities.

rows.label: Examples
values: Purchase/Sale of investments, Purchase/Sale of fixed assets, Loans made to others.
Purchase/Sale of investments, Purchase/Sale of fixed assets, Lending money and collecting on those loans.

rows.label: Overall Similarity
values: Highly similar to for-profit, focusing on long-term assets and investments.
Highly similar to NFP, focusing on long-term assets and investments.

title: Contributions, Pledges & Net Assets
description: Master contribution revenue recognition, pledge accounting, and net asset classification

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contributions are the lifeblood of many NFPs—and the accounting is tricky! Knowing when to recognize revenue from pledges, how to classify contributions, and when donated services qualify for recognition are all heavily tested on FAR!

sections.title: Contribution Definition
sections.content:
**Definition:** Unconditional transfer of assets (or settlement of liabilities) in a voluntary nonreciprocal transaction.

**Key elements:**
• Unconditional (no barriers to entitlement)
• Voluntary (not required by law)
• Nonreciprocal (donor gets nothing of value back)

**Recognition:** When unconditional promise received (not when cash received!)

sections.title: Conditional vs Unconditional
sections.content:
**Unconditional Contribution:**
• No barriers to overcome
• Donor intent is clear
• Recognize revenue immediately

**Conditional Contribution:**
• Barrier exists that must be overcome
• Right of return (or release) if not met
• DO NOT recognize until condition met

**Example barrier:** "$100,000 if you raise matching funds"
→ Conditional until matching requirement met

sections.title: Identifying Barriers (Conditions)
sections.content:
**Indicators of a barrier:**

**1. Measurable performance requirement:**
• Specific outputs required
• "Serve 500 meals" or "Complete building"

**2. Limiting stipulation:**
• Limits discretion in using funds
• More than just administrative tasks

**Plus:** Right of return (or release from obligation)

**Not barriers:** Administrative requirements, reporting deadlines

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Conditional vs Unconditional
sections.content:
**"BRR" = Barrier + Right of Return**

**Conditional gift requires BOTH:**
• **B**arrier to entitlement
• **R**ight of **R**eturn (or release)

**If BOTH present:** Don't recognize until barrier overcome
**If either missing:** Unconditional → Recognize now!

**Think:** "Brrrr, it's COLD!" = CONDitional = wait to recognize

sections.title: Promises to Give (Pledges)
sections.content:
**Unconditional pledge = Receivable + Revenue NOW**

**Recognition:**
• At fair value when pledge received
• Discount multi-year pledges to present value
• Recognize in appropriate net asset class

**Allowance:** Establish allowance for uncollectible pledges

**Conditional pledges:** Record when condition met

sections.title: Multi-Year Pledges
sections.content:
**Donor pledges $100,000 payable over 5 years ($20,000/year)**

**At pledge date (assume 5% discount rate):**

PV of $20,000 annuity for 5 years @ 5% = $86,590

**Entry:**
Dr Pledges Receivable - Current      $20,000
Dr Pledges Receivable - Noncurrent   $66,590
Dr Discount on Pledges               $13,410
    Cr Contribution Revenue - 
       With Restrictions              $86,590

**Each year:** Amortize discount as contribution revenue

sections.title: Classification by Restriction
sections.content:
**Donor restrictions determine classification:**

**Without donor restrictions:**
• No restrictions imposed by donor
• Available for general use

**With donor restrictions:**
• Purpose restrictions: Use for specific activities
• Time restrictions: Use in future periods
• Perpetual: Never spend principal (endowments)

**Multi-year pledges:** Inherently time-restricted (unless donor says otherwise)

sections.title: Contributed Services
sections.content:
**Recognize contributed services ONLY if they:**

**1. Create or enhance nonfinancial assets**
OR
**2. Require specialized skills AND would be purchased if not donated**

**Specialized skills:**
• Accounting, legal, medical
• Construction, teaching
• Other professional services

**NOT recognized:** General volunteer time, donated administrative help

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Volunteer Time
sections.content:
**Most volunteer time is NOT recognized!**

**Recognized:**
• CPA donates audit services ✓
• Doctor provides free clinic hours ✓
• Electrician wires new building ✓

**NOT recognized:**
• Volunteers answer phones ✗
• Board members attend meetings ✗
• Parents help at school events ✗

**Test:** Would you PAY for this AND does it require SPECIALIZED skills?

sections.title: Contributed Goods
sections.content:
**Recognize at fair value when received:**

**Types:**
• Donated inventory (food bank supplies)
• Donated equipment
• Donated securities
• Donated real estate

**Entry:**
Dr [Asset]                 Fair Value
    Cr Contribution Revenue     Fair Value

**Note:** May be restricted or unrestricted based on donor intent

sections.title: Collection Items
sections.content:
**Works of art, historical treasures, similar items:**

**May capitalize OR not capitalize if ALL three:**
1. Held for public exhibition/education/research
2. Protected, unencumbered, preserved
3. Proceeds from sale used to acquire other collection items

**If capitalized:** Recognize as revenue
**If not capitalized:** Must disclose policy
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Contribution Confusion: My Nonprofit Nightmare
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Contributions can be a HUGE pain, especially conditional ones. I remember auditing a small charity that received a $50,000 pledge to build a playground, contingent on raising matching funds. They immediately booked the $50k as revenue! Big no-no. We had to explain (nicely) that until they actually raised the matching funds, it was just a promise. Moral of the story: don't get ahead of yourself. Conditions matter. Think of it like this: no matching funds, no playground, no revenue!

sections.title: Unconditional vs. Conditional: The Fatal Flaw
sections.content: Students often mistake a stipulation for a barrier. A stipulation is a requirement of *how* the funds are used (e.g., 'for scholarships only'). A barrier is a condition that *must be overcome* before the recipient is entitled to the funds (e.g., 'only if you raise matching funds'). The key difference: a stipulation doesn't prevent recognition as revenue immediately, whereas a barrier does. So, read those pledge agreements carefully! Is it a 'must do' or a 'nice to do'?

sections.title: Sarah's Slippery Pledge: A Recognition Riddle
sections.content: Sarah, a new staff accountant at a local food bank, receives a pledge of $10,000 from a wealthy donor. The pledge agreement states the food bank must serve 5,000 families this year to receive the funds. This is a conditional contribution. Sarah should not recognize revenue until that barrier (serving 5,000 families) is overcome. Only then can the $10,000 be booked as contribution revenue.

sections.title: Pledges in the Real World: Budgeting & Forecasting
sections.content: In a typical month at a nonprofit, you'll encounter pledges when preparing budgets and forecasts. Understanding the difference between conditional and unconditional pledges is crucial for accurate financial planning. Unconditional pledges can be factored into expected revenue, while conditional pledges require careful monitoring of the fulfillment of the conditions. Ignoring this distinction can lead to overoptimistic budgets and potential cash flow problems down the line. Accurate pledge accounting is the backbone of reliable financial management for nonprofits.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Contributions recognized when unconditional (barrier + return right = conditional)
Pledges recognized at present value when unconditional promise received
Multi-year pledges are discounted; inherently time-restricted
Contributed services: Only if specialized skills AND would purchase OR enhance assets
Volunteer time generally NOT recognized
Contributed goods at fair value when received
Collection items may or may not be capitalized (NFP choice if criteria met)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A not-for-profit organization received a pledge of $50,000 designated for a new computer lab. The donor stipulated that the funds would only be released upon the organization successfully raising an additional $25,000 from other donors for the same purpose. How should the not-for-profit organization account for this pledge?
options: Recognize a contribution receivable of $50,000 immediately.
Recognize a conditional contribution of $50,000 immediately.
Disclose the pledge in the notes to the financial statements but do not recognize it until the condition is substantially met.
Recognize revenue of $25,000.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The pledge is a conditional contribution because it contains a barrier (raising an additional $25,000). Therefore, the organization should disclose the pledge but not recognize it until the condition is substantially met. This aligns with the principles of conditional contribution recognition.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether there are any barriers to entitlement.

reveal.question: When should an unconditional contribution be recognized as revenue?
reveal.answer: An unconditional contribution should be recognized as revenue immediately when received or pledged, assuming it meets the definition of a contribution (unconditional, voluntary, and nonreciprocal).

rows.label: Example
values: Funds for a building project contingent upon matching funds being raised.

title: Split-Interest Agreements
description: Learn the accounting for charitable remainder trusts, gift annuities, and other split-interest agreements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Split-interest agreements are complex arrangements where donors make gifts that benefit both themselves (or others) AND the NFP. Understanding these arrangements—particularly charitable remainder trusts and gift annuities—is tested on FAR and trips up many candidates!

sections.title: What is a Split-Interest Agreement?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A contribution arrangement where the donor or third party receives benefits for a period of time, with the remainder going to the NFP.

**The "split":**
• Lead interest: Benefits during the agreement term
• Remainder interest: What's left for NFP at the end

**Common types:**
• Charitable remainder trusts
• Charitable lead trusts
• Gift annuities
• Pooled income funds

sections.title: Charitable Remainder Trust
sections.content:
**How it works:**
1. Donor transfers assets to trust
2. Trust pays income to donor (or beneficiary) for life/term
3. Remainder goes to NFP when trust ends

**Two types:**
• **Annuity trust:** Fixed annual payment
• **Unitrust:** Variable payment (% of trust value)

**Accounting depends on:** Whether NFP is trustee

sections.title: NFP as Trustee of Charitable Remainder Trust
sections.content:
**Initial recognition:**

Dr Trust Assets            Fair Value
    Cr Liability to Beneficiary   PV of payments
    Cr Contribution Revenue       Difference

**Liability = Present value of expected payments to beneficiary**

**Revenue = Net present value of remainder interest**

**Subsequent periods:**
• Adjust liability for actuarial changes
• Record investment gains/losses
• When trust ends → Eliminate liability, recognize remaining assets

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Split-Interest Accounting
sections.content:
**"Assets - Liability = Revenue"**

**What does NFP record?**

**Assets:** Fair value of what's in trust (if NFP is trustee)
**Liability:** PV of what NFP owes beneficiary
**Revenue:** The difference (NFP's remainder interest)

**Think of it as:** Total pie minus their slice = our slice

sections.title: Charitable Gift Annuity
sections.content:
**How it works:**
1. Donor transfers assets directly to NFP
2. NFP promises fixed annuity payments to donor for life
3. When donor dies, NFP keeps remaining assets

**Key difference from CRT:** NFP owns assets directly (not in separate trust)

**Entry:**
Dr Assets                  Fair Value
    Cr Annuity Payable         PV of payments
    Cr Contribution Revenue    Difference

sections.title: Charitable Lead Trust
sections.content:
**Opposite of Charitable Remainder Trust:**

1. Donor transfers assets to trust
2. Trust pays income to NFP for term of years
3. Remainder goes back to donor/heirs

**NFP accounting:**
• Recognize contribution receivable
• At present value of expected payments
• Revenue recognized over trust term as payments received

**Assets stay with trust, not NFP**

sections.title: Pooled Income Fund
sections.content:
**How it works:**
1. Multiple donors contribute to a common pool
2. Each donor receives pro-rata share of income for life
3. At death, donor's share goes to NFP

**Accounting:**
• Record assets contributed
• Record liability for income interest
• Contribution = Assets - Liability

**Similar to charitable remainder trust but pooled**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Beneficiary is Third Party
sections.content:
**What if remainder goes to ANOTHER NFP?**

**If another NFP is beneficiary:**
• Trustee NFP records liability to other NFP
• Beneficiary NFP records receivable (if unconditional)

**If NFP is neither trustee nor beneficiary:**
• No accounting recognition

**Key:** Who is trustee? Who is beneficiary? Who holds assets?

sections.title: Perpetual Trust Held by Third Party
sections.content:
**When bank or other party is trustee:**

**NFP is income beneficiary:**
• Recognize interest in trust at fair value
• Classified as "with donor restrictions" (perpetual)
• Recognize income distributions as revenue

**NFP is remainder beneficiary:**
• Recognize PV of expected remainder
• "With donor restrictions" until received
• Adjust annually for changes in value

sections.title: Sample Calculation
sections.content:
**Gift Annuity Example:**

Donor (age 75) transfers $100,000 to NFP
NFP agrees to pay $6,000 annually for donor's life
Expected life: 12 years; Discount rate: 5%

**PV of annuity:** $6,000 × 8.8633 (PV factor) = $53,180

**Entry:**
Dr Cash                     $100,000
    Cr Annuity Payable          $53,180
    Cr Contribution Revenue -
       With Restrictions        $46,820

**Each year:**
• Pay $6,000 (reduce liability)
• Adjust liability for actuarial changes
• Interest accrues on liability
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Split-Interest Agreements: Not as Scary as They Sound!
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, 'split-interest agreements' sounds like something a villain in a spy movie would use. But in the NFP world, it's just a fancy way of saying someone's donating assets but wants some benefit back first. Think of it like this: Grandma gives her vacation home to the Humane Society, but gets to use it every summer until she can't travel anymore. Understanding the lead interest and remainder interest is key! Get that right, and you're halfway there. Trust me, nailing this topic will make you feel like a financial superhero.

sections.title: Fixed vs. Variable: The Annuity Trust Trap!
sections.content: Students often stumble when differentiating between annuity trusts and unitrusts. The big mistake? Assuming that 'annuity' always means a fixed dollar amount. With an annuity trust, the payment *is* fixed. However, a unitrust pays a fixed *percentage* of the trust's assets, revalued annually. So, even though both involve regular payments, one is a set dollar amount, and the other fluctuates with the trust's performance. Remembering 'annuity = amount' can help you avoid this common pitfall.

sections.title: Sarah's Surprise: The Understated Gift!
sections.content: Sarah, a controller at a local charity, received a notification that Mr. Henderson established a charitable remainder annuity trust, naming their NFP as the beneficiary. He transferred $500,000 to the trust, which will pay him $30,000 a year for the next 10 years. The key here is that Sarah must calculate the present value of the remainder interest to determine the contribution revenue recognized upfront. It's not simply the $500,000; it's $500,000 less the present value of the annuity payments!

sections.title: Split-Interest Agreements: Real-World Impact
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter split-interest agreements when auditing or preparing financials for large not-for-profits, especially hospitals, universities, and community foundations. Donors often use these arrangements for estate planning and tax benefits. Accurately accounting for these agreements is crucial for presenting a fair view of the NFP's financial position and performance. Expect to review legal documents, actuarial valuations, and investment statements to ensure proper reporting. A solid understanding prevents audit adjustments and keeps your clients happy.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Split-interest: Benefits split between donor/beneficiary and NFP
Record assets at fair value; liability at PV of amounts owed
Contribution revenue = Assets received - Liability to beneficiary
Charitable remainder trust: Donor gets income, NFP gets remainder
Charitable lead trust: NFP gets income, donor gets remainder
Gift annuity: NFP owns assets, pays fixed annuity to donor
Classified as 'with donor restrictions' until trust terminates

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A not-for-profit organization (NFP) is the trustee of a charitable remainder annuity trust. A donor contributed assets with a fair value of $500,000. The present value of the expected annuity payments to the beneficiary is $300,000. At initial recognition, what amount should the NFP credit to Contribution Revenue?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Contribution Revenue is the difference between the fair value of the assets received and the present value of the liability to the beneficiary. In this case, $500,000 (Fair Value) - $300,000 (Liability) = $200,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the journal entry: Dr. Trust Assets, Cr. Liability to Beneficiary, Cr. Contribution Revenue.

reveal.question: What is the remainder interest in a split-interest agreement?
reveal.answer: The remainder interest is what is left for the not-for-profit organization at the end of the agreement's term, after the lead interest (benefits during the agreement term) has been satisfied.

rows.label: Payment to Beneficiary
values: Variable payment (% of trust value, revalued annually)

rows.label: Payment Fluctuation
values: Payment remains constant regardless of trust value.
Payment fluctuates with the trust's value.

title: NFP Functional Expense Reporting
description: Master expense allocation and the Statement of Functional Expenses

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Donors want to know how their money is spent! NFPs must report expenses by function (programs vs support) AND by nature (salaries, rent, etc.). The Statement of Functional Expenses shows this important information!

sections.title: Two Ways to Classify Expenses
sections.content:
**By Function (what the money does):**
• Program services
• Management and general
• Fundraising

**By Nature (what was purchased):**
• Salaries and benefits
• Rent and utilities
• Supplies
• Professional services
• Depreciation

sections.title: Statement of Functional Expenses
sections.content:
**Matrix format required:**

• Rows: Expenses by NATURE
• Columns: Expenses by FUNCTION
• Shows how each type of expense supports each activity

**Required for:**
• Voluntary health and welfare organizations (always)
• Other NFPs (encouraged, statement or notes)

**ASU 2016-14:** Enhanced disclosure requirements

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Functional Expense Matrix
sections.content:
**Think of a GRID:**

```
                  Programs  M&G  Fundraising
Salaries          $500K    $80K    $40K
Rent               $50K    $10K     $5K
Supplies           $20K     $3K     $2K
```

**Rows = NATURE (what bought)**
**Columns = FUNCTION (why bought)**
**Must allocate shared costs!**

sections.title: Expense Allocation
sections.content:
**Many costs must be allocated:**

**Direct costs:** Charge directly to function

**Shared costs:** Allocate using reasonable basis:
• Salaries → Time spent
• Rent → Square footage
• Utilities → Square footage
• Depreciation → Use or square footage

**Document allocation methodology!**

sections.title: Program Ratio
sections.content:
**Key metric donors watch:**

Program Ratio = Program Expenses / Total Expenses

**Higher = Better** (more going to mission)

**Typical expectations:**
• 75%+ considered good by many raters
• Below 50% raises concerns

**Watchdog groups like Charity Navigator use this!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Joint Costs
sections.content:
**Multi-purpose activities (e.g., educational mailing that also solicits):**

**Can allocate to BOTH program AND fundraising IF:**
1. Purpose criteria met (would do without solicitation)
2. Audience criteria met (selected for reasons other than ability to give)
3. Content criteria met (serves program/M&G function)

**ALL THREE criteria must be met to allocate to program!**
**Otherwise → 100% Fundraising**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: NFP Expense Allocation: It's All About the Story
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be honest. NFP functional expense reporting can feel like pulling teeth. But think of it this way: you're telling a story. You're showing donors *exactly* where their money went. Did it feed the hungry (program services)? Keep the lights on (management & general)? Or convince others to donate (fundraising)? If you can clearly connect the expense's *nature* (salaries, rent) to its *function*, you'll nail this. Don't just blindly allocate; understand the narrative!

sections.title: Confusing Management & General with Fundraising
sections.content: A common mistake is lumping all administrative costs into 'Management & General' without considering if some support fundraising efforts. For example, the CFO's salary is primarily M&G. But if the CFO spends 20% of their time reviewing fundraising contracts, that 20% should be allocated to fundraising. Why? Because failing to properly allocate inflates M&G, making the organization appear less efficient. Always ask: Does this activity directly solicit contributions?

sections.title: Sarah's Salary Allocation Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, the program director at a local animal shelter, spends 60% of her time directly caring for animals (program services), 20% managing volunteers (program services support), and 20% writing grant proposals (fundraising). The shelter's bookkeeper initially allocated her entire salary to program services. The problem? This misrepresents the organization's fundraising expenses and could mislead potential donors. A correct allocation is crucial for transparency and accurate financial reporting.

sections.title: Real-World Impact: Donor Trust and Funding
sections.content: In practice, accurate functional expense reporting is directly tied to donor trust and continued funding. Foundations and major donors meticulously review these allocations. If a non-profit consistently reports excessively high fundraising or administrative costs, it raises red flags. They might assume the organization is inefficient or mismanaging resources. Master the matrix format; it's your key to showcasing responsible stewardship and securing future funding. A well-presented Statement of Functional Expenses is your organization's resume.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Expenses reported by function AND by nature
Functions: Program, Management & General, Fundraising
Statement of Functional Expenses: Matrix format
Shared costs allocated using reasonable bases
Program ratio: Program expenses / Total expenses
Joint costs: Must meet all three criteria to allocate to program
Document allocation methodology

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A not-for-profit organization incurred $50,000 in salaries. After analyzing employee time sheets, it was determined that 60% of the salaries related to program services, 30% to management and general, and 10% to fundraising. How should the $50,000 in salaries be reported on the statement of functional expenses?
options: $30,000 Program Services, $15,000 Management and General, $5,000 Fundraising
$50,000 Program Services
$50,000 Management and General
$16,667 Program Services, $16,667 Management and General, $16,666 Fundraising
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The statement of functional expenses requires expenses to be allocated based on their function. Therefore, the salaries must be split according to the percentages provided: 60% to Program Services ($30,000), 30% to Management and General ($15,000), and 10% to Fundraising ($5,000).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the matrix format and how expenses are broken down by function.

reveal.question: True or False: All Not-for-Profit Organizations (NFPs) are required to present a statement of functional expenses.
reveal.answer: False. Only Voluntary Health and Welfare Organizations are required to present the statement of functional expenses. Other NFPs are encouraged to present the statement (either in the statement itself or in the notes).

rows.label: Definition
values: Categorizes expenses based on the activity or purpose the expense supports (e.g., Program Services, Management & General, Fundraising).
Categorizes expenses based on the type of cost incurred (e.g., Salaries, Rent, Depreciation).

rows.label: Example
values: Allocating salary expense between program services and fundraising.
Reporting total salary expense as a line item.

title: Healthcare Entity Accounting
description: Learn the unique accounting for hospitals and healthcare organizations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Healthcare entities—hospitals, clinics, and health systems—have unique accounting requirements, especially for patient service revenue and charity care! Understanding these special rules is essential for FAR healthcare questions!

sections.title: Types of Healthcare Entities
sections.content:
**Healthcare entities can be:**

• **For-profit:** Follow business GAAP
• **Government:** Follow GASB standards
• **Not-for-profit:** Follow FASB NFP standards

**Most FAR questions focus on NFP healthcare**

**Same functional expense reporting as other NFPs**

sections.title: Patient Service Revenue
sections.content:
**Revenue recognition (ASC 606):**

**Transaction price = Amount expect to collect**

• Start with gross charges
• Less: Contractual adjustments (insurance discounts)
• Less: Implicit price concessions (expected bad debts)
• = Net patient service revenue

**Report NET amount (single line)**

sections.title: Contractual Adjustments
sections.content:
**Differences between charges and insurance payments:**

**Example:**
Charges for procedure: $10,000
Insurance pays: $6,500
Contractual adjustment: $3,500

**Recorded as reduction of revenue, NOT expense**

**Third-party payers:**
• Medicare
• Medicaid
• Commercial insurance

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Revenue Components
sections.content:
**"GROSS minus REDUCTIONS = NET"**

**Gross patient service revenue**
- Contractual adjustments (insurance deals)
- Implicit price concessions (won't collect)
- Charity care (never expected to collect)
= **Net patient service revenue**

**Only report the NET!**

sections.title: Charity Care
sections.content:
**Services provided for free or reduced price:**

**ASU 2016-14 treatment:**
• NOT recorded as revenue
• NOT recorded as bad debt expense
• Disclosed in notes:
  - Policy for providing charity care
  - Quantified (e.g., at cost)

**Why not revenue?** Never expected to collect

sections.title: Healthcare Operating Statement
sections.content:
**Typical format:**

**Operating revenues:**
• Net patient service revenue
• Premium revenue (capitation)
• Other operating revenue

**Operating expenses:**
• By function (nursing, support, admin)

**Non-operating:**
• Investment income
• Contributions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Malpractice Claims
sections.content:
**Healthcare entities have significant malpractice exposure:**

**Record liability when:**
• Probable AND
• Reasonably estimable

**Uninsured claims:**
• Accrue expected losses
• Use actuarial estimates

**Insurance recoveries:**
• Asset only if probable
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: NFP Healthcare Accounting: It's All About the Benjamins (and the Discounts)
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. NFP healthcare accounting isn't exactly riveting reading. But listen, understanding those contractual adjustments and implicit price concessions is crucial. Think of it this way: a hospital bills $1,000, but insurance only pays $600 and they expect $50 in bad debt. The actual revenue they'll recognize? $350. This 'transaction price' is what matters. If you don't get this, you're toast. So nail it down!

sections.title: Confusing Gross Charges with Net Patient Revenue
sections.content: Students often mistakenly report gross charges as revenue for NFP healthcare entities. This is WRONG! Gross charges are just the starting point. You MUST deduct contractual adjustments (discounts to insurance companies) and implicit price concessions (estimated uncollectible amounts). For example, if gross charges are $500,000, contractual adjustments are $100,000, and implicit price concessions are $20,000, net patient revenue is $380,000, NOT $500,000. Always focus on the *amount the healthcare entity expects to collect*.

sections.title: Sarah's Revenue Recognition Headache
sections.content: Sarah, a new staff accountant at St. Jude's NFP Hospital, incorrectly recorded a $50,000 surgery at the gross charge amount. The insurance company had a pre-negotiated rate of $30,000, and the hospital anticipated $5,000 in uncollectible patient balances. Sarah learned a valuable lesson: Revenue recognition for NFP healthcare entities requires careful consideration of contractual adjustments and implicit price concessions, ultimately recognizing only the $25,000 expected to be collected.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Revenue Cycle Audits
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter NFP healthcare revenue recognition during revenue cycle audits. You'll be tracing patient bills through the system, verifying the accuracy of contractual adjustments based on insurance contracts, and evaluating the reasonableness of the allowance for uncollectible accounts. A small error in calculating net patient revenue can significantly impact the financial statements and potentially trigger a material misstatement. Mastering this area now will save you time and potential headaches later when you're in the field.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Healthcare can be for-profit, government, or NFP
Patient service revenue: Net amount expected to collect
Contractual adjustments: Reduce revenue (insurance deals)
Charity care: Not revenue, disclosed in notes
Bad debts: Implicit price concession, reduces revenue
Malpractice: Accrue when probable and estimable
Most FAR questions focus on NFP healthcare

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A not-for-profit hospital bills a patient $1,000 for services rendered. The hospital expects to collect $600 from the patient's insurance company and estimates implicit price concessions of $50 due to uncollectible amounts. What amount should the hospital report as net patient service revenue?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Net patient service revenue is calculated as gross charges less contractual adjustments and implicit price concessions. In this case, $1,000 (gross charges) - $400 (contractual adjustment, $1000-$600) - $50 (implicit price concessions) = $550.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Gross Charges - Contractual Adjustments - Implicit Price Concessions = Net Patient Service Revenue

reveal.question: How are contractual adjustments treated in the financial statements of a not-for-profit healthcare entity?
reveal.answer: Contractual adjustments are treated as a reduction of patient service revenue, NOT as an expense.

rows.label: Financial Reporting Focus
values: Accountability to Taxpayers and Public Services

title: Defined Benefit Pension Plans
description: Master pension accounting including the PBO, plan assets, and net pension cost

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Pension accounting is one of the most complex topics on FAR! Understanding the components of pension expense, balance sheet presentation, and OCI impacts is essential. Many candidates struggle here—master this and you'll stand out!

sections.title: Key Pension Terms
sections.content:
**Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO):**
• Present value of ALL benefits earned to date
• Uses projected future salary increases
• PRIMARY measure used in US GAAP

**Plan Assets:**
• Fair value of investments set aside for pension
• Held in trust, separate from company

**Funded Status = Plan Assets - PBO**

sections.title: Funded Status on Balance Sheet
sections.content:
**Net Pension Asset or Liability:**

• **Overfunded (Assets > PBO):** Report net asset
• **Underfunded (PBO > Assets):** Report net liability

**Single amount on balance sheet = Funded status**

**No more minimum liability or intangible asset!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Pension Expense Components
sections.content:
**"SIR-AGE"** for Pension Cost:

**S**ervice cost (+)
**I**nterest cost (+)
**R**eturn on assets (-)
**A**mortization of prior service cost (+/-)
**G**ains and losses amortization (+/-)
**E**xpected, not actual return!

**Service and Interest ADD; Return SUBTRACTS**

sections.title: Service Cost
sections.content:
**Benefits earned by employees THIS YEAR**

• Calculated by actuary
• Based on pension formula
• Uses current salary levels projected forward

**This is the ONLY component in operating income!**

**All other components = Non-operating**

sections.title: Interest Cost
sections.content:
**Interest on the growing PBO:**

**Formula:** Beginning PBO × Discount Rate

**Think of it as:**
• PBO is a liability growing over time
• Interest accrues at the discount rate

**Increases pension expense**

sections.title: Expected Return on Plan Assets
sections.content:
**Expected earnings from investments:**

**Formula:** Beginning Plan Assets × Expected Return Rate

**Note:** Use EXPECTED return, not actual!

**Difference:** Actual vs Expected → OCI (deferred)

**Reduces pension expense (negative component)**

sections.title: Prior Service Cost
sections.content:
**When plan is amended retroactively:**

1. Measure change in PBO from amendment
2. Record in OCI immediately
3. Amortize to expense over remaining service period

**Example:** Company increases pension formula for past years → Increases PBO → Prior service cost

sections.title: Gains and Losses (Corridor Method)
sections.content:
**Gains/Losses arise from:**
• Actuarial assumption changes
• Difference between expected and actual returns

**Accumulated in OCI until:**
• Exceed the "corridor" (10% of greater of PBO or Assets)
• Then amortize excess over average remaining service

**This is the CORRIDOR approach - smooths volatility**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Expected vs Actual Return
sections.content:
**In pension expense: Use EXPECTED return!**

**Example:**
• Expected return: $100,000
• Actual return: $80,000
• Difference: $20,000 loss

**Pension expense uses:** $100,000 expected
**The $20,000 loss goes to:** OCI (deferred)

**Never use actual return in pension expense directly!**

sections.title: Sample Calculation
sections.content:
**Pension Expense Calculation:**

• Service cost: $200,000
• Interest cost (PBO $2M × 5%): $100,000
• Expected return ($1.5M × 6%): ($90,000)
• Prior service amortization: $15,000
• Loss amortization: $10,000

**Total Pension Expense: $235,000**

**Journal Entry:**
Dr Pension Expense    $235,000
Dr OCI (various)      XXX
    Cr Pension Asset/Liability   XXX
    Cr Cash (contribution)       XXX
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Pension Plans: The Black Box of Accounting?
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, pensions can feel like a black box. You're probably thinking, 'Present value? Future salary increases? What?!' Don't freak out. The key is understanding the big picture. Think of it like this: The company promised future retirement benefits (PBO), and they've set aside some money to pay for it (Plan Assets). The difference? That's what we need to account for. So take a deep breath, we'll break it down. Trust me, it's not as scary as it looks.

sections.title: PBO vs. Accumulated Benefit Obligation (ABO)
sections.content: Students often confuse the Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO) with the Accumulated Benefit Obligation (ABO). Both represent the present value of benefits earned to date, BUT the PBO includes assumptions about future salary increases, while the ABO does not. This distinction is CRUCIAL. If a question mentions future salary increases, you're almost certainly dealing with the PBO. For example, if salaries are expected to increase by 3% annually, the calculated obligation will be higher under PBO than ABO. Ignoring this distinction leads to incorrect calculations of net pension cost and funded status.

sections.title: Sarah's Pension Puzzle at GlobalTech
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant at GlobalTech, notices the pension plan's Plan Assets are $8 million and the PBO is $10 million. She knows immediately that GlobalTech has an underfunded pension plan and needs to report a net pension liability of $2 million ($10 million - $8 million) on the balance sheet. This liability represents the shortfall between the company's obligations and the assets set aside to meet those obligations, a critical piece of information for investors and creditors.

sections.title: Pensions: More Than Just Numbers
sections.content: In practice, understanding defined benefit pension plans is vital for auditors and financial analysts. As an auditor, you'll assess the reasonableness of actuarial assumptions used to calculate the PBO, ensuring compliance with GAAP. As an analyst, you'll use the funded status and net pension cost to evaluate a company's financial health and future cash flow obligations. Companies with significantly underfunded pension plans might face future financial strain. Mastering this topic now will give you a major advantage in analyzing real-world financial statements.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Funded status = Plan assets - PBO (single B/S amount)
SIR-AGE: Service, Interest, Return, Amortizations
Service cost = ONLY component in operating income
Use EXPECTED return, not actual (difference to OCI)
Prior service cost: OCI, then amortize
Corridor: 10% of greater of PBO or assets
Overfunded = Asset; Underfunded = Liability

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corp. sponsors a defined benefit pension plan. At December 31, Year 1, the projected benefit obligation (PBO) was $5,000,000, and the fair value of plan assets was $4,000,000. At December 31, Year 2, the PBO was $5,700,000 and the fair value of plan assets was $4,900,000. What amount should ABC report on its December 31, Year 2 balance sheet related to its pension plan?
options: $800,000 liability
$1,000,000 liability
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The funded status is calculated as Plan Assets - PBO. In this case, $4,900,000 - $5,700,000 = -$800,000. Since the PBO exceeds the plan assets, there is an underfunded status, which is reported as a net pension liability of $800,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the funded status (Plan Assets - PBO) first to determine if you have an asset or a liability.

reveal.question: What is the primary measure used in US GAAP for defined benefit pension plans?
reveal.answer: The Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO). It represents the present value of all benefits earned to date, using projected future salary increases.

title: Basic Earnings Per Share (EPS)
description: Calculate and present basic EPS for simple capital structures

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: EPS is one of the most watched metrics by investors! Public companies must present EPS on the income statement. Understanding basic vs diluted EPS and the calculation nuances is heavily tested on FAR!

sections.title: Basic EPS Formula
sections.content:
**Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares**

**Numerator:** Income available to common shareholders
**Denominator:** Weighted average shares outstanding

**Must subtract preferred dividends!**

sections.title: Who Must Report EPS?
sections.content:
**Required for:**
• Public companies (SEC registrants)
• Companies planning to go public

**NOT required for:**
• Private companies
• Not-for-profits

**Presented ON the face of income statement**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Basic EPS
sections.content:
**"NIP over WAC"**

**N**et Income
**I**minus (less)
**P**referred dividends
_______________
**W**eighted
**A**verage
**C**ommon shares

**Top = what's for common; Bottom = how many common**

sections.title: Preferred Dividend Treatment
rows: Subtract FULL dividend (whether declared or not)

sections.title: Weighted Average Shares
sections.content:
**Calculate shares on time-weighted basis:**

**Example:**
• 100,000 shares for 6 months
• 150,000 shares for 6 months

**Weighted avg = (100,000 × 6/12) + (150,000 × 6/12)**
**= 50,000 + 75,000 = 125,000 shares**

**Use actual days or months for precision**

sections.title: Stock Dividends and Splits
sections.content:
**Treat as if occurred at BEGINNING of year!**

**Example:** 2-for-1 split on July 1
• January 1 shares: 100,000
• After split: 200,000

**Weighted average:** 200,000 for ENTIRE year

**Also RESTATE prior period EPS for comparability!**

sections.title: Treasury Stock
sections.content:
**Treasury shares reduce weighted average:**

**Example:**
• 1/1: 200,000 shares outstanding
• 4/1: Buy back 20,000 shares

**Weighted avg:**
(200,000 × 3/12) + (180,000 × 9/12)
= 50,000 + 135,000 = 185,000 shares

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Stock Dividends vs Repurchases
sections.content:
**Stock dividends/splits:** Apply RETROACTIVELY to entire year (and prior years)

**Stock repurchases:** Apply PROSPECTIVELY from purchase date

**Why different?**
• Splits/dividends don't change company value
• Repurchases are economic transactions

sections.title: EPS Presentation
sections.content:
**Income statement presentation:**

• EPS from continuing operations
• EPS from discontinued operations
• EPS for net income

**Both basic AND diluted EPS required**

**Show on face of income statement, not just notes**

sections.title: Sample Calculation
sections.content:
**Calculate Basic EPS:**

• Net income: $500,000
• Preferred dividends (cumulative): $50,000
• Common shares 1/1: 100,000
• Issued 4/1: 20,000 additional
• 2-for-1 split on 9/1

**Step 1: Adjust for split**
• 1/1 shares: 100,000 × 2 = 200,000
• 4/1 issue: 20,000 × 2 = 40,000

**Step 2: Weighted average**
(200,000 × 3/12) + (240,000 × 9/12) = 230,000

**Step 3: Calculate EPS**
($500,000 - $50,000) / 230,000 = **$1.96**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: EPS: Not Just Another Acronym!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, EPS sounds boring. But trust me, understanding Basic EPS is crucial. Think of it as the 'piece of the pie' each shareholder gets. Mess it up, and suddenly investors are questioning everything! Remember: (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Shares Outstanding. It's that simple. Don't overthink it. Get the numerator and denominator right, and you're golden. This isn't some theoretical exercise; real people make real decisions based on these numbers. So, nail it!

sections.title: Forgetting Preferred Dividends!
sections.content: A very common mistake is forgetting to subtract preferred dividends from net income in the numerator. Students often just use net income directly. However, Basic EPS represents earnings available *to common shareholders*. Preferred shareholders get paid first! So, think of it like this: net income is the whole pie, preferred dividends are the slice you give to your picky cousin, and what's left is for the common shareholders. Always, always subtract those preferred dividends – even if they're cumulative and *not* declared!

sections.title: Sarah's IPO Headache
sections.content: Sarah, CFO of a rapidly growing tech startup, is prepping for their IPO. The underwriters are scrutinizing every line of their financials, especially EPS. Sarah initially forgot to calculate and present Basic EPS because the company was previously private. The underwriters quickly pointed out this omission as a major red flag. Now, Sarah has to quickly revise the income statement and disclosures to comply with SEC requirements, delaying the IPO launch. Don't be like Sarah; know your EPS requirements!

sections.title: EPS in the Real World: Deal Breaker?
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently encounter EPS when analyzing companies for investment or acquisition. If you're working in investment banking or equity research, calculating and interpreting EPS is a daily task. A declining EPS trend, even if the company is profitable, can signal underlying problems or poor management decisions. For example, if a company's net income is increasing, but its weighted average shares outstanding are increasing faster due to stock options being exercised, the EPS could decrease, making the company less attractive to investors. It's a critical metric.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Avg Shares
Cumulative preferred: Deduct whether declared or not
Weighted average: Time-weight share changes
Stock splits/dividends: Apply retroactively to entire year
Treasury stock purchases: Apply from purchase date forward
Required for public companies, on face of income statement
Restate prior periods for splits/dividends

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. reported net income of $500,000 for the year. They paid preferred dividends of $50,000. The weighted average number of common shares outstanding was 100,000. What is XYZ Corp.'s basic earnings per share?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is $4.50. Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares = ($500,000 - $50,000) / 100,000 = $4.50.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to subtract preferred dividends from net income before dividing by the weighted average common shares outstanding.

reveal.question: Which type of company is required to present EPS on the face of the income statement?
reveal.answer: Public companies (SEC registrants) and companies planning to go public are required to present EPS on the face of the income statement.

rows.label: EPS Reporting Requirement
values: Required on the face of the income statement.

rows.label: SEC Registration
values: Generally required to register with the SEC.
Generally not required to register with the SEC.

title: Diluted Earnings Per Share
description: Calculate diluted EPS with convertibles, options, and other dilutive securities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Diluted EPS shows the 'worst case' scenario if all potentially dilutive securities converted. Understanding the if-converted and treasury stock methods is essential for FAR—these calculations appear frequently on the exam!

sections.title: What is Diluted EPS?
sections.content:
**Diluted EPS = Income available / (Weighted Avg + Potential Shares)**

**Shows impact of:**
• Convertible bonds
• Convertible preferred stock
• Stock options and warrants
• Contingent shares

**Always ≤ Basic EPS (cannot exceed basic)**

sections.title: Two Key Methods
rows: Add back interest (net of tax), add shares
Add back pref dividends, add shares

sections.title: If-Converted Method: Convertible Bonds
sections.content:
**Assume bonds converted at beginning of year:**

**Numerator adjustment:**
• Add back interest expense (net of tax)
• Interest wouldn't be paid if converted

**Denominator adjustment:**
• Add shares that would be issued

**Test:** Only include if DILUTIVE (reduces EPS)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: If-Converted
sections.content:
**"IF converted, what changes?"**

**Convertible BONDS:**
• No more interest expense = Income UP (add back)
• More shares = Shares UP

**Convertible PREFERRED:**
• No more preferred dividends = Income UP
• More common shares = Shares UP

**Both: Income goes UP, Shares go UP**

sections.title: If-Converted Method: Convertible Preferred
sections.content:
**Assume preferred converted at beginning of year:**

**Numerator adjustment:**
• Don't subtract preferred dividends
• (They wouldn't exist if converted)

**Denominator adjustment:**
• Add common shares from conversion

**No tax adjustment needed (dividends aren't deductible)**

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method: Options
sections.content:
**For options and warrants:**

1. Assume all options exercised
2. Company receives exercise price
3. Company buys back shares at average market price
4. NET new shares added to denominator

**Formula:**
Net shares = Options - (Options × Exercise Price / Avg Market Price)

**Or:** Options × (Market - Exercise) / Market

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method Example
sections.content:
**Options outstanding:**
• 10,000 options
• Exercise price: $20
• Average market price: $50

**Step 1:** Assume exercise: Receive $200,000 (10,000 × $20)

**Step 2:** Buy back shares: $200,000 / $50 = 4,000 shares

**Step 3:** Net new shares = 10,000 - 4,000 = **6,000 shares**

**Only these 6,000 added to denominator!**

sections.title: When Are Options Dilutive?
sections.content:
**Options are dilutive when:**
Exercise price < Average market price
(In-the-money options)

**Options are anti-dilutive when:**
Exercise price > Average market price
(Out-of-the-money options)

**Anti-dilutive securities are EXCLUDED!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Antidilutive Securities
sections.content:
**NEVER include antidilutive securities!**

**A security is antidilutive if:**
• Including it INCREASES EPS
• Convertible: Incremental EPS > Basic EPS
• Options: Exercise price > Market price

**Must test EACH security separately**

**Aggregate approach:** Order by most dilutive first

sections.title: Order of Inclusion (Aggregate Test)
sections.content:
**Steps for multiple dilutive securities:**

1. Calculate basic EPS
2. Rank securities by dilutive impact (most dilutive first)
3. Add one at a time
4. Stop when adding security would be antidilutive

**For convertibles:** Rank by incremental EPS
(Earnings adjustment / Share adjustment)

sections.title: Sample Comprehensive Calculation
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Net income: $1,000,000
• Preferred dividends: $100,000
• Basic shares: 200,000
• Convertible bonds: $500,000 face, 8% interest, converts to 20,000 shares
• Tax rate: 25%
• Stock options: 10,000 options at $30, market = $50

**Basic EPS:** ($1,000,000 - $100,000) / 200,000 = **$4.50**

**Convertible bond adjustment:**
• Interest add-back: $40,000 × (1-25%) = $30,000
• New shares: 20,000

**Options (Treasury Stock):**
• Net shares: 10,000 × ($50-$30)/$50 = 4,000

**Diluted EPS:**
($900,000 + $30,000) / (200,000 + 20,000 + 4,000)
= $930,000 / 224,000 = **$4.15**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Diluted EPS: The Reality Check
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Diluted EPS can feel like alphabet soup. But think of it this way: companies love to dangle potential shares to attract investors and employees. Options, convertibles... it's all part of the 'maybe-someday' pie. Diluted EPS forces them to show what earnings *could* look like if everyone cashed in. It's a dose of reality for investors, showing the potential earnings per share if all those promises actually came true. No more pie in the sky, just cold, hard numbers… or as cold as accounting gets!

sections.title: Forgetting the Tax Shield!
sections.content: A common mistake is forgetting to adjust the numerator for the tax impact of convertible bonds. When calculating diluted EPS, you add back the interest expense (net of tax) related to the convertible bonds. Students often remember to add back the interest expense but fail to multiply it by (1 - tax rate). This omission significantly overstates the diluted EPS. Remember, if the bonds were converted, the company wouldn't pay the interest, and therefore wouldn't get the tax deduction. Don't leave money on the table - get that tax shield right!

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Options Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a financial analyst at TechCorp, is calculating diluted EPS. The company has outstanding stock options. She calculates the incremental shares using the treasury stock method. However, she forgets to consider if the options are actually dilutive. If the average market price is *lower* than the exercise price, the options are anti-dilutive and should be ignored. Sarah needs to remember to only include options that *decrease* EPS; otherwise, she's misleading investors about the company's true potential performance.

sections.title: Investor Relations & Due Diligence
sections.content: In practice, you'll use diluted EPS extensively when analyzing companies for potential investments or during due diligence for mergers and acquisitions. Investors rely on it to understand the potential impact of convertible securities and options on future earnings. Ignoring diluted EPS can lead to overvaluing a company, as it doesn't fully account for potential share dilution. Always cross-reference diluted EPS with basic EPS and understand the underlying assumptions. It's a critical metric for making informed financial decisions and protecting your clients' interests.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Diluted EPS includes potentially dilutive securities
Convertibles: If-converted method (add back interest/dividends, add shares)
Options: Treasury stock method (only net new shares)
Options dilutive only when market > exercise price
NEVER include antidilutive securities
Test each security—stop when adding would increase EPS
Diluted EPS must be ≤ Basic EPS

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Crestwood Corp. has income available to common shareholders of $500,000 and 100,000 weighted average shares outstanding. Crestwood also has convertible bonds outstanding that, if converted, would increase the weighted average shares outstanding by 10,000 and decrease income available to common shareholders by $30,000 (net of tax). What is Crestwood's diluted EPS?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Diluted EPS is calculated as ($500,000 + $30,000) / (100,000 + 10,000) = $4.55. The bonds are dilutive because including them reduces EPS. Without the bonds, basic EPS is $5.00. With the bonds, EPS is $4.55, so they are dilutive.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to adjust both the numerator (income) and the denominator (shares outstanding) when considering convertible securities.

reveal.question: What is the fundamental rule for determining whether a potential common stock is included in the diluted EPS calculation?
reveal.answer: A potential common stock is included in the diluted EPS calculation only if it is dilutive. This means its inclusion would decrease EPS (or increase loss per share).

rows.label: Impact on Numerator
values: Interest expense (net of tax) is added back to net income.

rows.label: Impact on Denominator
values: Additional shares are added to weighted average shares outstanding.
Additional shares are added to weighted average shares outstanding (only if dilutive, using the treasury stock method).

rows.label: Dilutive Effect
values: Dilutive if the adjusted EPS (with conversion) is lower than basic EPS.
Dilutive only if the average market price of the stock exceeds the option/warrant's exercise price.

title: Income Tax Accounting: Deferred Taxes
description: Master deferred tax assets, liabilities, and temporary differences

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Deferred tax accounting bridges the gap between GAAP and tax reporting! Understanding temporary differences, DTAs, DTLs, and valuation allowances is crucial for FAR. This topic appears in almost every exam!

sections.title: Temporary Differences
sections.content:
**Differences that create future taxable or deductible amounts:**

**Creates DTL (future taxable):**
• Accelerated tax depreciation
• Installment sale income
• Prepaid expenses (deducted for tax, not book)

**Creates DTA (future deductible):**
• Bad debt expense (allowance method)
• Warranty expense
• Accrued expenses not yet deductible

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DTA vs DTL
sections.content:
**"PAID less now → DTL; PAID more now → DTA"**

**DTL:** Tax paid NOW less than book expense
• Will pay MORE tax in future
• It's a LIABILITY

**DTA:** Tax paid NOW more than book expense
• Will pay LESS tax in future
• It's an ASSET (future benefit)

sections.title: Calculating Deferred Taxes
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Identify temporary differences
**Step 2:** Determine if each creates DTA or DTL
**Step 3:** Multiply by ENACTED tax rate
**Step 4:** Assess need for valuation allowance on DTAs

**Use the tax rate expected when difference reverses!**

sections.title: Common Temporary Differences
rows: Book full income, Tax when collected

sections.title: Permanent Differences (No Deferred Tax)
sections.content:
**Never reverse - affect current tax only:**

• Municipal bond interest (book income, not taxable)
• Life insurance premiums on executives
• Fines and penalties (book expense, not deductible)
• 50% meals deduction limit
• Dividends received deduction

**These affect effective tax rate, not deferred taxes**

sections.title: Journal Entry Approach
sections.content:
**Annual deferred tax entry:**

Dr Income Tax Expense (plug)
   Cr Income Tax Payable (current tax due)
   Cr Deferred Tax Liability (increase in DTL)
or
Dr Deferred Tax Asset (increase in DTA)

**Expense = Current tax +/- Changes in deferred taxes**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Rate Changes
sections.content:
**When tax rates change:**

• Adjust ALL existing deferred taxes
• Use NEW enacted rate
• Adjustment goes through income tax expense

**Example:** DTL of $100,000 at 35%
Rate changes to 21%
New DTL = Temp diff × 21%
Difference = Benefit (reduce expense)

sections.title: Balance Sheet Classification
sections.content:
**ALL deferred taxes are NONCURRENT:**

• Deferred Tax Assets → Noncurrent asset
• Deferred Tax Liabilities → Noncurrent liability

**Net within same tax jurisdiction:**
• Net DTA and DTL for same entity/jurisdiction
• Different jurisdictions shown separately

sections.title: Sample Calculation
sections.content:
**Year 1:**
• Book income: $500,000
• Taxable income: $400,000
• Difference: $100,000 (depreciation timing)
• Tax rate: 21%

**Current tax:** $400,000 × 21% = $84,000
**DTL created:** $100,000 × 21% = $21,000

**Entry:**
Dr Income Tax Expense    $105,000
    Cr Income Tax Payable    $84,000
    Cr Deferred Tax Liability $21,000

**Effective rate:** $105,000 / $500,000 = 21%
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Deferred Taxes: The Tax Man Cometh (Eventually)
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Deferred taxes can feel like a punishment for being too good at accounting. You're booking revenue now, but the IRS is like, 'Hold up, we'll get ours later!' Accelerated depreciation is the classic example. You expense a ton early on, reducing taxable income *now*, but those deductions will be smaller later. That's a Deferred Tax Liability (DTL) building up, waiting to bite you in the future. Think of it as a tax time bomb, but at least you know when (roughly) it'll go off.

sections.title: Confusing DTA/DTL: The Timing Game
sections.content: Students often mix up Deferred Tax Assets (DTAs) and Deferred Tax Liabilities (DTLs) because they focus on the *current* impact, not the *future* implications. For example, they see prepaid expenses deducted on the tax return *now* and incorrectly assume it creates a DTA. Remember, prepaid expenses deducted now mean *lower* taxable income *later* when the book expense is recognized, creating a DTL. Always ask yourself: will this temporary difference result in more taxable income or less taxable income in the *future*? That's your DTL or DTA, respectively.

sections.title: Warranty Woes: A DTA Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a CPA preparing the tax provision for a manufacturing company, notices a significant warranty expense accrued for financial reporting purposes but not yet deductible for tax. The company's effective tax rate is 21%. This creates a Deferred Tax Asset (DTA). Sarah must carefully assess the likelihood that the company will generate sufficient future taxable income to realize the benefit of this DTA. If not, a valuation allowance must be established, reducing the DTA recognized.

sections.title: Deferred Taxes: Your Daily Grind
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter deferred taxes constantly. Think about preparing the income tax provision for a publicly traded company. You'll be digging through depreciation schedules, warranty reserves, and analyzing revenue recognition policies to identify those pesky temporary differences. Mastering the DTA/DTL creation process is crucial for accurate financial reporting and ensuring your clients aren't surprised by unexpected tax liabilities down the road. Plus, it looks great on your resume!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Temporary differences reverse; Permanent differences don't
DTL = Will pay MORE tax later (tax < book now)
DTA = Will pay LESS tax later (tax > book now)
Use enacted rate expected when difference reverses
Rate changes: Adjust deferred taxes through expense
All deferred taxes are noncurrent on balance sheet
Net within same jurisdiction, separate different jurisdictions

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company uses accelerated depreciation for tax purposes and straight-line depreciation for financial reporting purposes. This difference will result in which of the following?
options: A deferred tax asset.
A deferred tax liability.
No deferred tax effect.
Either a deferred tax asset or a deferred tax liability, depending on the enacted tax rate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Using accelerated depreciation for tax purposes results in higher depreciation expense and lower taxable income in the early years of an asset's life, compared to using straight-line depreciation for book purposes. This creates a taxable temporary difference because future taxable income will be higher when the asset's book value is lower than its tax basis. Therefore, it creates a deferred tax liability.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which method results in higher taxable income in the future.
