reveal.question: Name three examples of temporary differences that create a Deferred Tax Asset (DTA).
reveal.answer:
1. Bad debt expense (allowance method)
2. Warranty expense
3. Any expense that is recognized for book purposes before it is deductible for tax purposes.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a deferred tax asset (DTA) of $50,000. Management believes it is more likely than not that only $30,000 of the DTA will be realized in the future. What is the impact on the financial statements?
options: A valuation allowance of $20,000 will be established, decreasing net income.
A valuation allowance of $30,000 will be established, decreasing net income.
A deferred tax asset of $30,000 will be reported, and no valuation allowance is needed.
A valuation allowance of $20,000 will be established, decreasing the deferred tax asset and increasing net income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When it is more likely than not that some or all of a deferred tax asset will not be realized, a valuation allowance is established. In this case, the company expects only $30,000 of the $50,000 DTA to be realized, so a valuation allowance of $20,000 ($50,000 - $30,000) is needed. The establishment of a valuation allowance decreases the deferred tax asset and decreases net income because it increases income tax expense.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The valuation allowance reduces the carrying amount of the DTA to the amount that is expected to be realized.

rows.label: Common Cause
values: Warranty expense recognized for book purposes before tax purposes

rows.label: Balance Sheet Impact
values: Increases deferred tax asset (potentially offset by valuation allowance)

title: Deferred Tax Asset Valuation Allowance
description: Determine when a valuation allowance is needed for deferred tax assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: A DTA is only valuable if the company will have future taxable income to use it! The valuation allowance is one of the most judgment-intensive areas in accounting—and a favorite FAR exam topic!

sections.title: What is a Valuation Allowance?
sections.content:
**Contra-asset reducing DTA to realizable amount**

**Required when:**
"More likely than not" (>50%) that some portion of DTA will NOT be realized

**Like an allowance for doubtful accounts—but for deferred taxes**

sections.title: Assessing Realizability
sections.content:
**Consider all available evidence:**

**Positive evidence (supports no VA needed):**
• Existing contracts/backlogs
• Strong earnings history
• Appreciated assets
• Deferred tax liabilities that will reverse

**Negative evidence (supports VA needed):**
• Cumulative losses in recent years
• History of unused carryforwards expiring
• Unsettled circumstances creating uncertainty

sections.title: Sources of Future Taxable Income
content: **Reversal of existing DTLs** in same jurisdiction
**Future taxable income** (exclusive of reversing differences)
**Tax planning strategies** that would create taxable income
**Carryback to prior years** (if available)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: "More Likely Than Not"
sections.content:
**MLTN = More than 50% likely**

**Question to ask:**
"Is it more likely than not that we WON'T use this DTA?"

**If YES → Need valuation allowance**
**If NO → No valuation allowance needed**

**Weight of evidence determines answer**

sections.title: Journal Entry for Valuation Allowance
sections.content:
**To establish or increase VA:**

Dr Income Tax Expense
    Cr Valuation Allowance (contra to DTA)

**To decrease VA (improved outlook):**

Dr Valuation Allowance
    Cr Income Tax Expense (benefit)

**VA is presented as offset to DTA on balance sheet**

sections.title: Cumulative Loss Test
sections.content:
**Three-year cumulative loss is significant negative evidence:**

• Very difficult to overcome
• Must have strong positive evidence to avoid VA
• Objectively verifiable

**But not automatic!**
• Consider nature of losses
• Unusual or nonrecurring?
• Future profitability reasonably expected?

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Changing the VA
sections.content:
**VA is reassessed EVERY reporting period!**

**Company turns profitable:**
• Reassess need for VA
• May release (reduce) VA
• Benefit goes through income tax expense

**This can significantly impact earnings!**

**Disclosure of VA changes required**

sections.title: Example Scenario
sections.content:
**Company has:**
• DTA from NOL carryforward: $500,000
• Three years of losses (cumulative loss)
• Expect modest profits next 2 years
• DTL that will reverse: $200,000

**Analysis:**
• Negative evidence: Cumulative losses
• Positive evidence: DTL reversal
• Expected income insufficient

**Conclusion:** VA needed for portion not supported
VA = $500,000 - $200,000 = $300,000
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Valuation Allowance: The 'Rainy Day Fund' for DTAs
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, deferred tax assets can feel a little abstract. Think of the valuation allowance as a 'rainy day fund' for your DTAs. If you're not reasonably sure you'll actually *use* those future tax deductions, you gotta sock some away in this allowance. It's all about being conservative. Don't get caught up in wishful thinking about future profits. The IRS doesn't care about your optimism, they care about cold, hard facts. If the evidence suggests you won't realize the full benefit, book the allowance. Simple as that (in theory, anyway!).

sections.title: Forgetting to Consider ALL Evidence!
sections.content: A very common mistake is only focusing on one piece of evidence, usually the negative stuff like recent losses. Students see losses and immediately think 'Valuation Allowance!' But remember, you MUST consider ALL available evidence, both positive and negative. Maybe the company just landed a huge multi-year contract, or maybe they have appreciated assets they plan to sell. Failing to weigh *everything* leads to overstating (or understating) the allowance, which throws off your financial statements and can lead to audit adjustments. Be thorough!

sections.title: Sarah's Struggling Startup
sections.content: Sarah's tech startup, 'InnovateNow', had significant net operating losses in its first three years. Their CFO initially balked at recording a large valuation allowance against the DTA. However, Sarah pointed out InnovateNow's projections showed profitability starting next year due to a major contract with a Fortune 500 company. The key takeaway? Even with past losses, strong future prospects (supported by solid evidence) can justify a lower, or even zero, valuation allowance. Don't be blinded by the past!

sections.title: DTA Valuation: A Crucial Part of Due Diligence
sections.content: When performing due diligence for a potential acquisition, assessing the target's deferred tax assets and related valuation allowances is critical. Overstated DTAs can significantly inflate the perceived value of the company. You'll need to scrutinize their projections, assess the likelihood of future taxable income, and evaluate the appropriateness of their valuation allowance methodology. Think about it: if the DTA isn't really realizable, you're overpaying for the company. This makes understanding the 'more likely than not' threshold absolutely essential in M&A work.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: VA needed when MLTN (>50%) DTA won't be realized
Consider all positive and negative evidence
Three-year cumulative loss = significant negative evidence
Sources: DTL reversals, future income, tax strategies, carrybacks
VA recorded against income tax expense
Reassess VA every period—changes go through expense
VA is contra-asset that reduces DTA on balance sheet

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following would be considered negative evidence when determining the need for a valuation allowance against a deferred tax asset?
options: Existing sales contracts that will generate taxable income in the future.
A strong earnings history in the past three years.
Cumulative losses in the most recent two years.
Deferred tax liabilities that will reverse in the future, creating taxable income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Cumulative losses in recent years are a strong indicator that future taxable income may not be sufficient to realize the deferred tax asset, thus supporting the need for a valuation allowance. The other options are examples of positive evidence.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what would make it *less* likely you'd realize the DTA.

reveal.question: What is the threshold for determining if a valuation allowance is required for a deferred tax asset?
reveal.answer: A valuation allowance is required if it is 'more likely than not' (i.e., >50% probability) that some portion of the deferred tax asset will not be realized.

rows.label: Examples
values: Existing contracts/backlogs, strong earnings history, appreciated assets, reversing DTLs
Cumulative losses in recent years, history of expiring tax credits, future uncertainty

title: Revenue Recognition: Multiple Performance Obligations
description: Master allocation of transaction price to performance obligations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many contracts have multiple performance obligations! Understanding how to identify and allocate the transaction price is critical. This is one of the most complex areas of revenue recognition!

sections.title: Identifying Performance Obligations
sections.content:
**Two criteria (BOTH must be met):**

1. **Distinct:** Customer can benefit on its own or with readily available resources

2. **Separately identifiable:** Not highly interdependent or integrated with other promises

**If both met → Separate performance obligation**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Allocation Steps
sections.content:
**"STAR"** method:

**S**tandalone prices - Determine for each PO
**T**otal transaction price - Determine amount
**A**llocate based on relative SSP
**R**ecognize as each PO is satisfied

**Formula:**
Allocated = (SSP of PO / Total SSP) × Transaction Price

sections.title: Allocating Discounts
sections.content:
**General rule:** Allocate discount proportionally to all POs

**Exception - Allocate to specific POs if:**
• Entity regularly sells each PO separately, AND
• Observable evidence discount relates to only some POs, AND
• Items in bundle approximate standalone prices

**Variable consideration:** May allocate entirely to one PO if specifically relates

sections.title: Series of Distinct Goods/Services
sections.content:
**When treated as single PO:**

• Each distinct good/service is substantially the same, AND
• Same pattern of transfer to customer

**Examples:**
• Monthly cleaning services
• Daily transaction processing

**Simplifies accounting—single measure of progress**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Highly Interdependent
sections.content:
**NOT separately identifiable if:**

• Significant integration service
• One modifies or customizes another
• Highly interdependent

**Example:** Software + significant customization = ONE PO

**Don't default to assuming everything is separate!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Bundling Blues: Don't Get Tripped Up!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, listen up. This multiple performance obligation stuff? It's like the cable company trying to upsell you. They bundle internet, phone, and TV, but you only want the internet! ASC 606 wants to know if those services are truly distinct. Can the customer benefit from internet alone? If so, it's likely a separate performance obligation. Don't just blindly allocate the transaction price proportionally. Dig in, see if there's observable evidence to allocate that discount to just the TV package nobody watches. Trust me, the client will appreciate you saving them a few bucks (and maybe give you some good coffee).

sections.title: Distinct vs. Separately Identifiable: The Trap!
sections.content: Students often confuse "distinct" with "separately identifiable." Just because a customer *can* benefit from something on its own doesn't automatically make it a separate performance obligation. The key difference is interdependence. Imagine a software installation and a year of tech support. The software might be distinct, but if the tech support is *highly* integrated with the software's functionality (fixes critical bugs, etc.), it might not be separately identifiable. The customer's ability to use the software effectively hinges on the support. Don't just check the "distinct" box and move on; really consider the overall arrangement.

sections.title: Sarah's Software Snafu: The Discount Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant, is reviewing a contract where a company sells software, installation, and training for $1,000. Individually, the software sells for $700, installation for $200, and training for $200. Sarah initially allocates the $100 discount proportionally. However, she remembers the company regularly sells the software and installation separately, and there's clear evidence the $100 discount is ONLY for the software. Sarah correctly allocates the entire discount to the software, resulting in a more accurate revenue recognition.

sections.title: Master Bundles: Real-World Revenue Impact
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter multiple performance obligations frequently, especially with SaaS companies offering tiered service packages. Think about a company selling accounting software with different support levels. Getting this right is crucial because it directly impacts revenue recognition timing. Misallocating the transaction price can lead to premature or delayed revenue recognition, potentially misstating financial performance. That's why mastering ASC 606's guidance on allocation is vital for accurate and reliable financial reporting. A small error here can snowball into a material misstatement quickly.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Performance obligation: Distinct AND separately identifiable
Allocate transaction price based on relative SSP
SSP methods: Observable > Market > Cost-plus > Residual
Discounts generally allocated proportionally
Series of similar services may be single PO
Integration may combine promises into one PO
Recognize revenue as each PO satisfied

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Apex Software sells a software license and provides technical support for one year. The software license is functional on its own, and the technical support is frequently sold separately by Apex. Customers can use the software without the technical support. Which of the following is the correct number of performance obligations in this contract?
options: One performance obligation because the software license and technical support are highly interdependent.
One performance obligation because the customer benefits from both items simultaneously.
Two performance obligations: the software license and the technical support.
The number of performance obligations cannot be determined without knowing the total transaction price.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is two performance obligations. The software license is distinct because the customer can benefit from it on its own. The technical support is also distinct because it is frequently sold separately and the customer can use the software without it. Both criteria for identifying separate performance obligations are met: (1) distinct and (2) separately identifiable. Therefore, there are two performance obligations.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the customer could benefit from each component on its own and whether the components are highly interdependent.

reveal.question: List the three conditions that must be met to allocate a discount entirely to one or more, but not all, performance obligations in a contract.
reveal.answer:
The three conditions are:
1. The entity regularly sells each performance obligation (PO) separately.
2. There is observable evidence that the discount relates to only some of the POs.
3. The items in the bundle approximate standalone prices.

knowledgeCheck.question: Tech Solutions sells a package of cybersecurity software and 10 hours of consulting services for $5,000. Separately, the cybersecurity software sells for $4,000, and 10 hours of consulting services sell for $2,000. Tech Solutions regularly sells both the software and consulting services separately. Management has determined that the discount is attributable to the consulting services because they are trying to gain market share in that area. How should Tech Solutions allocate the transaction price?
options: Allocate $3,333 to the cybersecurity software and $1,667 to the consulting services using a proportional allocation.
Allocate $4,000 to the cybersecurity software and $1,000 to the consulting services, allocating the entire discount to the consulting services.
Allocate $3,000 to the cybersecurity software and $2,000 to the consulting services, allocating the entire discount to the cybersecurity software.
Allocate $5,000 to the cybersecurity software and $0 to the consulting services.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is to allocate $4,000 to the cybersecurity software and $1,000 to the consulting services. The exception for allocating a discount to specific performance obligations applies because: (1) Tech Solutions regularly sells both POs separately, (2) there is observable evidence the discount relates only to the consulting services, and (3) the items in the bundle approximate standalone prices. Therefore, the entire discount of $1,000 ($6,000 standalone prices - $5,000 transaction price) should be allocated to the consulting services.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the exception to proportional allocation applies.

title: Revenue Recognition: Contract Modifications
description: Understand accounting for changes to revenue contracts

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contracts change all the time! Understanding how to account for modifications—new contract, cumulative adjustment, or prospective—is essential. The answer depends on whether goods/services are distinct!

sections.title: What Is a Contract Modification?
sections.content:
**Change to:**
• Scope (adding goods/services)
• Price (changing transaction price)
• Both

**Must be approved by parties**

**Examples:**
• Change orders
• Add-on services
• Price adjustments
• Quantity changes

sections.title: Three Accounting Models
rows: New contract, no adjustment to old

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Modification Flowchart
sections.content:
**Ask two questions:**

**Q1:** Are added goods/services distinct?
→ NO = Part of existing contract (catch-up)
→ YES = Go to Q2

**Q2:** Is price at standalone?
→ YES = Separate contract
→ NO = Terminate and create new

sections.title: Separate Contract Treatment
sections.content:
**Treat as new contract when:**

• Additional goods/services are distinct, AND
• Price increases by standalone selling price

**Result:**
• Original contract unaffected
• New contract starts fresh
• No cumulative adjustment

**Think of it as a completely new sale**

sections.title: Cumulative Catch-Up
sections.content:
**When modification changes measure of progress:**

**Steps:**
1. Revise total transaction price
2. Revise total estimated costs (if applicable)
3. Calculate new % complete
4. Apply to revised revenue
5. Recognize difference as adjustment

**Catch-up in period of modification!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Termination Model
sections.content:
**Terminate + New is NOT intuitive:**

**When goods are distinct but NOT at standalone price:**
• Allocate remaining transaction price
• Include modification consideration
• Recognize previously recognized revenue as starting point

**Remaining POs treated prospectively**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Contract Mods: The Paperwork Headache is Real!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Contract modifications... they're a paperwork nightmare! You think you've got a nice, clean revenue recognition schedule, and BAM! A change order hits. Suddenly, you're digging through emails, trying to figure out if those extra services are *distinct* enough to be a whole new contract. My advice? Document, document, document! And if you're unsure, consult with someone who's seen a few of these rodeo rides. Save yourself the late-night stress!

sections.title: Lumping it All Together: The Fatal Flaw
sections.content: Students often incorrectly assume that ANY change to a contract automatically requires a cumulative catch-up adjustment. This is wrong! Remember the key test: are the added goods or services distinct AND does the price increase reflect their standalone selling price? If BOTH are true, it's a new contract. Students forget that distinctness is only HALF the battle. If the price hike doesn't match the standalone selling price, you're back to modifying the original contract, not starting fresh. Don't fall into the 'lump it all together' trap!

sections.title: Sarah's Software Snafu: The Price Matters
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant at Tech Solutions Inc., is dealing with a client who wants to add training to their existing software contract. The training, if sold separately, is valued at $5,000. Tech Solutions only charges the client $4,000 as part of the contract modification. Even though the training is a distinct service, because the price doesn't reflect its standalone selling price, Sarah knows it's a modification to the original contract, not a new one. Whew, dodged a bullet!

sections.title: Audit Trail Alert: Traceability is Key
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter contract modifications constantly, especially in industries like construction or software. Think about a construction project where the client adds a new wing to the building. You'll need to meticulously document the original contract, the modification agreement, and the justification for treating it as either a modification or a new contract. Auditors will scrutinize these decisions, so ensure a clear audit trail exists to support your accounting treatment. This means having documented evidence that any price changes reflect standalone selling prices.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Modification: Change to scope, price, or both
Separate contract: Distinct goods + Standalone price
Terminate + New: Distinct goods but NOT standalone
Part of existing: Goods NOT distinct
Cumulative catch-up: Adjust in period of modification
Ask: Distinct? Then: Standalone price?
Document modification accounting at time of change

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Company initially contracts to sell 100 units of Product A to ABC Corp. for $10,000. Subsequently, the parties agree to modify the contract, increasing the quantity to 150 units of Product A and increasing the total price to $15,000. XYZ Company regularly sells Product A for $100 per unit. Assuming the original units have not yet been transferred, how should XYZ Company account for the contract modification?
options: As a separate contract, recognizing revenue for the additional 50 units at $100 per unit when control transfers.
As a prospective modification, allocating the remaining transaction price to the remaining performance obligations.
As a cumulative catch-up adjustment, adjusting revenue to reflect the modified contract terms immediately.
As a retrospective modification, restating prior periods to reflect the modified contract terms.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The additional 50 units of Product A are distinct because XYZ Company regularly sells Product A separately. The price increase of $5,000 for the additional 50 units reflects the standalone selling price ($100 x 50 = $5,000). Therefore, the modification should be treated as a separate contract. A prospective modification would be appropriate if the new price did not reflect the standalone selling price. Cumulative catch-up and retrospective approaches are not applicable here since the modification qualifies as a separate contract.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the conditions for treating a contract modification as a separate contract under ASC 606.

reveal.question: List the TWO criteria that must be met for a contract modification to be treated as a separate contract.
reveal.answer: 1. The additional goods or services are distinct. 2. The price increases by an amount that reflects the standalone selling price of the additional goods or services.

rows.label: Revenue Recognition
values: Remaining transaction price allocated to remaining obligations

rows.label: Cumulative Adjustment
values: Potentially, if the price does not reflect standalone selling price.

rows.label: When to Use
values: Additional goods/services are distinct AND price increase reflects standalone selling price
Additional goods/services are NOT distinct OR price increase does NOT reflect standalone selling price

title: Inventory: Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value
description: Apply LCNRV testing and write-down accounting

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Inventory must not be overstated! Understanding the lower of cost or NRV test ensures inventory is carried at the right amount. GAAP simplified this with ASU 2015-11!

sections.title: LCNRV Rule (Post-ASU 2015-11)
sections.content:
**For most inventory:**

**Lower of:**
• Cost (FIFO, LIFO, weighted average), OR
• Net Realizable Value (NRV)

**NRV = Estimated selling price − Costs to complete − Costs to sell**

**If NRV < Cost → Write down**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: LCNRV Simplified
sections.content:
**"Two numbers—pick the lower"**

• Cost (what you paid)
• NRV (what you'll get, net)

**No more ceiling/floor!**

**Exception:** LIFO and retail method still use old LCM approach

sections.title: Application Level
sections.content:
**Apply test to:**

• Individual items (most common)
• Categories of similar items
• Total inventory (rare)

**Be consistent!**

**Testing at item level:**
• Most conservative
• Catches individual write-downs
• Required for significant items

sections.title: Write-Down and Recovery
sections.content:
**Write-down entry:**

Dr Loss on Inventory Write-down (or COGS)
    Cr Inventory

**Recovery:**
• Can reverse write-down if NRV increases
• Only up to original cost
• Not above original carrying amount

**New cost basis = Written-down amount**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: LIFO and Retail Method
sections.content:
**LCNRV does NOT apply to:**

• LIFO inventory
• Retail inventory method

**These STILL use old LCM:**
• Market = Replacement cost
• Subject to ceiling (NRV) and floor (NRV − profit)

**Read the question carefully!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: NRV: It's All About Market Reality
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. LCNRV isn't just some academic exercise. Think of it as a cold, hard look at your inventory's actual worth. Are those fancy widgets really going to sell for what you *thought* they would? Or are they gathering dust while newer, shinier widgets steal the spotlight? NRV is your wake-up call! It forces you to write down inventory that's lost its luster, preventing you from overstating your assets and misleading investors. Bottom line: don't be afraid to face the music and write those puppies down if the market demands it!

sections.title: Confusing 'Costs to Complete' and 'Costs to Sell'
sections.content: A common stumble is mixing up 'Costs to Complete' and 'Costs to Sell' when calculating NRV. 'Costs to Complete' are the *remaining* production costs to get the inventory ready for sale. 'Costs to Sell' are the expenses directly related to selling the inventory, like commissions, shipping, and advertising. For example, imagine partially finished goods needing $5 more in labor to finish (Cost to Complete) and $2 in shipping to the customer (Cost to Sell). Don't add the *total* cost to produce, only what remains to complete them. This mistake can significantly inflate your NRV and lead to inaccurate inventory valuation.

sections.title: Sarah's Spreadsheet Saga
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, is performing the LCNRV test on her company's inventory of designer handbags. She finds that a particular line, 'Vintage Vogue,' originally costing $150 each, now has an estimated selling price of $120, with $10 in selling costs. Applying the LCNRV rule, Sarah must write down the inventory value of each 'Vintage Vogue' bag by $40 ($150 cost - ($120 selling price - $10 selling costs)). This write-down reflects the diminished market value of the handbags and ensures accurate financial reporting. Ignoring this would overstate inventory and inflate profits.

sections.title: LCNRV in the Real World: Auditing Inventory
sections.content: As an auditor, you'll frequently test LCNRV, especially for industries with rapidly changing technology or fashion trends. Think electronics retailers or clothing boutiques. You'll review the client's NRV calculations, scrutinizing their estimated selling prices and costs to sell. Look for evidence supporting these estimates, such as recent sales data, market research reports, and sales contracts. Remember, management has a responsibility to accurately reflect the market value of their inventory. Your job is to provide reasonable assurance that they've done so in accordance with GAAP.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: LCNRV: Lower of Cost or NRV (simplified from LCM)
NRV = Selling price − Completion costs − Selling costs
If NRV < Cost → Write down inventory
Apply at item, category, or total level
Write-down: Dr Loss/COGS, Cr Inventory
Can reverse write-down, but not above original cost
LIFO and retail method still use old LCM

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Watkins Co. has the following information regarding one of its inventory items: Historical Cost = $50, Estimated Selling Price = $75, Estimated Costs to Complete = $10, Estimated Costs to Sell = $20. Under the LCNRV rule, what is the carrying value of this inventory item?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The carrying value is determined by the Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value (NRV). NRV = Estimated Selling Price - Costs to Complete - Costs to Sell = $75 - $10 - $20 = $45. Since $45 is less than the cost of $50, the inventory should be carried at $45.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate NRV first, then compare it to the cost.

reveal.question: What is the formula for Net Realizable Value (NRV)?
reveal.answer: NRV = Estimated Selling Price - Costs to Complete - Costs to Sell

knowledgeCheck.question: An inventory item originally cost $100. At year-end, its NRV is determined to be $80. Which of the following journal entries is correct to record the write-down, assuming the company uses the loss method?
options: Debit: Cost of Goods Sold $20, Credit: Inventory $20
Debit: Loss on Inventory Write-Down $80, Credit: Inventory $80
Debit: Loss on Inventory Write-Down $20, Credit: Cost of Goods Sold $20
Debit: Inventory $20, Credit: Loss on Inventory Write-Down $20
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When NRV is lower than cost, inventory must be written down. The write-down is $100 (Cost) - $80 (NRV) = $20. The journal entry debits Cost of Goods Sold (or Loss on Inventory Write-Down) and credits Inventory to reduce its value.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The write-down amount is the difference between cost and NRV.

title: Troubled Debt Restructuring (ASC 470-60)
description: Understand debtor and creditor accounting for restructured debt

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When borrowers face financial difficulty, debt often gets restructured! Understanding both debtor and creditor accounting is essential. ASU 2022-02 significantly changed these rules!

sections.title: What Is Troubled Debt?
sections.content:
**Creditor grants concession NOT otherwise granted:**

• Debtor experiencing financial difficulty
• Creditor agrees to modify terms
• Modification wouldn't be granted normally

**Examples:**
• Reduced interest rate
• Extended maturity
• Reduced principal
• Transfer of assets in settlement

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Settlement Gain
sections.content:
**Debtor gains when paying less:**

**Gain = Carrying value − Amount paid**

**If transferring asset:**
1. Asset sold at FV → G/L on sale
2. Debt settled at FV → G/L on settlement

**Two separate transactions!**

sections.title: Modification Accounting (Debtor)
sections.content:
**Post-ASU 2022-02:**

**Compare future cash flows to carrying amount:**

• If future payments < Carrying → Gain recognized
• If future payments ≥ Carrying → No immediate gain
  - Adjust effective interest rate
  - Prospective treatment

**No longer use old 10% test for distressed modifications**

sections.title: Creditor Accounting
sections.content:
**When restructuring occurs:**

• Measure loan at present value of future cash flows
• Use original effective interest rate
• Difference = Adjustment to allowance (or direct write-off)

**Consider impairment:**
• If collateral dependent: Fair value of collateral
• Expected credit loss model applies

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ASU 2022-02 Changes
sections.content:
**Major changes for creditors:**

**OLD (TDR specific):**
• Separate TDR designation
• Different impairment model

**NEW (ASU 2022-02):**
• Eliminated TDR designation for creditors
• Use CECL expected loss model
• Enhanced disclosure requirements

**Debtor accounting: Less change**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Troubled Debt: A Creditor's Headache
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Troubled debt restructuring is a fancy way of saying 'Uh oh, someone can't pay.' As an auditor, when you see this pop up, put on your skeptical hat. Creditors aren't in the business of giving away money for fun. They're doing this because the alternative (bankruptcy) is worse. So, dig deep. Are those 'future cash flows' *really* realistic? Or are they just wishful thinking to avoid recognizing a massive loss? Remember, everyone's incentivized to paint a rosy picture, especially the debtor.

sections.title: Forgetting the Time Value of Money
sections.content: A frequent error is forgetting to consider the time value of money when comparing future cash flows to the carrying amount. Students often simply sum the undiscounted future payments and compare that total to the carrying amount. This is wrong! You MUST discount those future cash flows using the *original* effective interest rate to their present value. If the present value of the future cash flows is less than the carrying amount, the creditor recognizes a loss. Failing to discount significantly overstates the recoverable amount and hides potential impairment.

sections.title: Sarah's Restructuring Conundrum
sections.content: Sarah, a loan officer at First National Bank, is reviewing a $5 million loan to Acme Corp. Acme is struggling, and Sarah is considering restructuring the loan by reducing the interest rate from 8% to 3% and extending the maturity by two years. Sarah needs to determine if this constitutes a troubled debt restructuring and, if so, how to account for it. The key question: is the concession granted because of Acme's financial difficulties, and would it be granted to a borrower in good standing? If yes, ASC 470-60 applies.

sections.title: Real-World Loan Workouts
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter troubled debt restructurings most often during economic downturns when companies face liquidity issues. For example, a struggling restaurant chain might renegotiate lease terms or loan agreements with its lenders. As an auditor, you will need to meticulously analyze the terms of the restructured debt, recalculate effective interest rates, and assess the reasonableness of management's estimates of future cash flows. Document everything! This is a high-risk area, and clear documentation is crucial to support your audit opinion.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: TDR: Creditor grants concession to distressed debtor
Settlement: Debtor recognizes gain for forgiveness
Asset transfer: Separate gain/loss on asset and debt
Modification: Compare future payments to carrying value
Creditor: Use CECL model, no separate TDR category
Present value calculations use original effective rate
ASU 2022-02 simplified creditor accounting

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would NOT be considered a troubled debt restructuring?
options: A creditor reduces the stated interest rate on a loan due to the debtor's financial difficulties.
A creditor extends the maturity date of a loan for a debtor who is struggling to make payments.
A creditor agrees to accept equipment with a fair value less than the carrying amount of the debt in full settlement of the debt, where the debtor is experiencing financial difficulty.
A creditor reduces the interest rate on a loan for all of its borrowers due to a general decline in market interest rates.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A troubled debt restructuring requires that the creditor grant a concession that it would not otherwise consider. A general reduction in interest rates for all borrowers doesn't meet this criterion. The other options represent concessions made due to the debtor's financial difficulties.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the concession is specifically related to the debtor's financial distress.

sections.title: Future Cash Flows vs. Carrying Amount

reveal.question: Under post-ASU 2022-02 troubled debt restructuring accounting, what action does a debtor take if the total future cash payments after restructuring are less than the carrying amount of the debt?
reveal.answer: The debtor recognizes a gain immediately for the difference between the carrying amount of the debt and the total future cash payments.

knowledgeCheck.question: After a troubled debt restructuring, if the total future cash payments are greater than or equal to the carrying amount of the debt, how does the debtor account for the modification?
options: Recognize an immediate gain for the difference.
Adjust the carrying amount of the debt to equal the total future cash payments.
Adjust the effective interest rate prospectively.
Recognize a loss immediately for the difference.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: If future payments are greater than or equal to the carrying amount, no immediate gain or loss is recognized. Instead, the debtor adjusts the effective interest rate prospectively to reflect the new terms of the debt.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how the time value of money will be accounted for in the future.

title: Software Development Costs
description: Understand capitalization of internal-use and for-sale software

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Software costs can be significant! Understanding when to capitalize vs expense is critical. Different rules apply to internal-use software vs software for sale!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Internal-Use Phases
sections.content:
**"PED" = Expense-Capitalize-Expense**

**P**reliminary → EXPENSE
**E**xploration, planning, evaluation

**D**evelopment → CAPITALIZE
Application development stage

**Post-implementation** → EXPENSE
Training, maintenance

sections.title: Internal-Use Software (ASC 350-40)
sections.content:
**Preliminary stage:** Expense
• Conceptual formulation
• Vendor evaluation
• Feasibility analysis

**Application development:** Capitalize
• Coding, testing
• Configuration
• Installation to hardware

**Post-implementation:** Expense
• Training, maintenance
• Data conversion (unless for new capabilities)

sections.title: Software for Sale (ASC 985-20)
sections.content:
**Before technological feasibility:** Expense all costs

**After technological feasibility:**
• Capitalize until ready for release
• Amortize over sales life

**Technological feasibility achieved when:**
• Detailed program design complete, OR
• Working model completed and confirmed

sections.title: Amortization
sections.content:
**Internal-use software:**
• Straight-line over useful life
• Generally 3-7 years

**Software for sale:**
• Greater of:
  - Straight-line over estimated life, OR
  - Revenue ratio (revenue/total expected revenue)

**Subject to impairment testing**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cloud Computing Costs
sections.content:
**Hosting arrangements (SaaS):**

**If NO software asset obtained:**
• Implementation costs = Service contract
• Capitalize and expense over contract term

**If software license obtained:**
• Treat as internal-use software
• Capitalize development costs

**Substance over form!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Software Dev Costs: The Wild West of Accounting
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Software development costs can feel like the Wild West of accounting. Everyone's got a different interpretation of when to capitalize and when to expense. The key is technological feasibility. Nail down when that happens! I've seen companies try to sneak pre-feasibility costs into capitalized assets and it NEVER ends well during an audit. Remember, auditors are looking for evidence. Solid documentation is your best friend. Think of it like this: if you can't show it, it didn't happen (or at least, you can't capitalize it!).

sections.title: Confusing 'Preliminary' and 'Application' Stages
sections.content: A common mistake is confusing costs in the 'preliminary project stage' with those in the 'application development stage'. Students often think that extensive planning and feasibility analysis should be capitalized because they're crucial to the project's success. However, GAAP specifically states that these costs are expensed. The key difference is whether the company is actively creating the software or just figuring out if it's worth creating. Avoid this by remembering: Preliminary = Planning = Expense. Application = Active Development = Capitalize (after technological feasibility).

sections.title: Sarah's Software Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a financial analyst at Tech Solutions Inc., was tasked with categorizing the costs of a new internal-use software project. She capitalized all the costs from the initial planning meetings, including vendor evaluations, arguing they were part of the 'software development' process. The auditors flagged it as an error, requiring Sarah to restate earnings and expense the preliminary costs. The lesson? Stick to the bright line between preliminary and application development stages. Document everything!

sections.title: Real-World Impact: Valuation and M&A
sections.content: You'll encounter software development cost capitalization rules frequently when working in valuation or M&A. A company's balance sheet can be significantly impacted by how it treats these costs. Over-capitalizing inflates assets and net income, potentially leading to a higher valuation (which is great for them but bad for you if you're buying!). Understanding these rules allows you to critically assess a company's financial statements and make informed decisions. For instance, if you see a massive jump in capitalized software costs without a corresponding increase in revenue, that's a red flag to investigate further.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Internal-use: Capitalize application development stage
For sale: Capitalize after technological feasibility
Preliminary and post-implementation: Always expense
Technological feasibility: Detailed design or working model
Amortize over useful life or revenue pattern
Cloud computing: Generally expense unless license obtained
Subject to impairment testing

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During 2024, JKL Company incurred the following costs related to the development of internal-use software: Preliminary project stage $50,000, Application development stage $150,000, Post-implementation stage $25,000. What amount of these costs should JKL capitalize?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Only the application development stage costs are capitalized for internal-use software. Preliminary and post-implementation stage costs are expensed. Therefore, the correct answer is $150,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember which stages are capitalized and which are expensed for internal-use software.

reveal.question: Under ASC 985-20, what are the two conditions that indicate technological feasibility has been achieved for software to be sold?
reveal.answer: Technological feasibility is achieved when either (1) a detailed program design is complete, or (2) a working model is completed and confirmed.

rows.label: Post-Implementation Costs
values: Expensed (except for new capability data conversion)
N/A - typically not applicable as post-release costs are marketing/support.

sections.title: Software for Sale: Expense vs. Capitalize

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company is developing software to be sold to customers. Before achieving technological feasibility, ABC incurred $75,000 in research and development costs. After achieving technological feasibility but before the software was ready for release, ABC incurred $50,000 in additional costs. How much should ABC expense related to this software development?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under ASC 985-20, all costs incurred before technological feasibility is achieved are expensed. In this case, the $75,000 incurred before technological feasibility should be expensed. The $50,000 is capitalized.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the timing of technological feasibility to determine expense versus capitalization.

title: Asset Retirement Obligations (ARO)
description: Account for legal obligations to retire long-lived assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many assets come with retirement obligations! Understanding how to recognize, measure, and update AROs is essential. Think oil wells, mines, nuclear plants—obligations to restore the environment!

sections.title: What Is an ARO?
sections.content:
**Legal obligation to retire long-lived asset:**

• Dismantling
• Removal
• Site restoration

**Arises from:**
• Existing laws/regulations
• Written or oral agreements
• Promises implied by past practice

**Must be legally enforceable**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: ARO Entry
sections.content:
**Initial recognition:**

Dr Asset (retirement cost)
    Cr ARO Liability (PV of obligation)

**Both increase at inception!**

**Over time:**
• Asset portion depreciated
• Liability accretes (interest)

sections.title: Subsequent Measurement
sections.content:
**Asset retirement cost:**
• Depreciate over asset life
• Systematic and rational method

**ARO Liability:**
• Accretion expense each period
• Uses credit-adjusted risk-free rate
• Accretion = Beginning liability × Rate

**Adjustments for estimate changes**

sections.title: Changes in Estimates
sections.content:
**When expected costs change:**

**Upward revision:**
• Increase liability at current rate
• Increase asset (new layer)
• Depreciate over remaining life

**Downward revision:**
• Decrease liability at original rate
• Decrease asset (not below zero)
• Debit expense if exceeds asset

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Settlement
sections.content:
**When obligation settled:**

**If settle for more than liability:**
Dr ARO Liability
Dr Loss on settlement
    Cr Cash

**If settle for less:**
Dr ARO Liability
    Cr Gain on settlement
    Cr Cash

**Timing differences can cause G/L**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: ARO: It's All About That Future Cleanup Bill!
sections.content: Okay, so Asset Retirement Obligations. Let's be real, it's basically about admitting you're going to make a mess and have to clean it up later. Think oil rigs, nuclear plants, even just a landfill. You can't just walk away! The key is recognizing the legal obligation *now*, and estimating what that cleanup is going to cost in the future. Discount it back to present value, book the liability, and start depreciating that asset retirement cost. Don't bury your head in the sand – that cleanup bill *will* come due!

sections.title: Forgetting the 'Legally Enforceable' Part
sections.content: Students often include costs in the ARO that aren't actually *legally* required. Just because a company *wants* to restore a site to an even better condition than it was before doesn't mean it's part of the ARO. The ARO liability only includes the costs the company is legally obligated to incur based on laws, contracts, or established practice. So, if a company voluntarily does more, that's a separate expense, not part of the ARO. Always double-check the source of the obligation!

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Situation: Accretion Expense
sections.content: Sarah, an accounting intern, is working on the ARO for a mining company. The initial ARO liability was calculated correctly, but she's unsure how to handle the accretion expense. She thinks it should be based on the current market interest rate. The correct approach is to use the credit-adjusted risk-free rate that was in effect when the ARO was initially recorded and apply that rate to the beginning liability balance each period. Using the market rate would be incorrect.

sections.title: ARO: Beyond the Textbook, Into Reality
sections.content: You'll frequently encounter AROs in industries like oil and gas, mining, and nuclear energy. When auditing these companies, a key procedure is verifying the accuracy of the estimated retirement costs and the discount rate used. Changes in environmental regulations or technological advancements can significantly impact these costs, requiring adjustments to the ARO liability. A seemingly small change in the discount rate can have a large impact on the overall liability, so pay close attention to the assumptions.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ARO: Legal obligation to retire long-lived asset
Initial: PV of expected costs → Liability AND Asset
Depreciate asset portion over useful life
Accrete liability using credit-adjusted rate
Upward revisions: New layer at current rate
Downward revisions: Reduce at original rate
Settlement difference → Gain or loss

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would MOST likely result in recognition of an asset retirement obligation (ARO)?
options: A company voluntarily decides to dismantle an old piece of equipment that is no longer in use, even though there is no legal requirement to do so.
A company signs a contract to purchase a new building, which will require routine maintenance over its useful life.
A mining company operates in a jurisdiction with laws requiring the restoration of the land after mining operations cease.
A company anticipates future costs associated with complying with new environmental regulations that are not yet in effect.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is (C). An ARO arises from existing laws, regulations, contracts, or promises. The mining company's legal obligation to restore the land after mining operations cease meets this criterion. (A) is incorrect because a voluntary decision does not create a legal obligation. (B) is incorrect because routine maintenance is not related to asset retirement. (D) is incorrect because the environmental regulations are not yet in effect, so there is no present obligation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which scenario creates a present legal obligation.

reveal.question: How is accretion expense calculated for an asset retirement obligation (ARO) in a given period?
reveal.answer: Accretion expense is calculated by multiplying the beginning liability balance of the ARO by the credit-adjusted risk-free interest rate. This effectively represents the increase in the present value of the ARO liability over time due to the passage of time.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company initially estimated its asset retirement obligation (ARO) to be $500,000. After two years, the estimated retirement cost is revised upward to $600,000. How should the company account for this change in estimate?
options: Amortize the $100,000 increase over the remaining life of the asset.
Immediately expense the $100,000 increase.
Adjust the ARO liability and the related asset retirement cost (ARC) for the $100,000 increase prospectively.
Restate prior periods to reflect the revised estimate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is (C). Changes in estimates are accounted for prospectively. The ARO liability and the related asset retirement cost (ARC) are adjusted for the $100,000 increase, and the revised depreciation expense is calculated based on the adjusted ARC. This adjustment is applied from the date of the change forward. Restatement is not required for changes in estimates.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how changes in estimates are generally handled under US GAAP.

title: Contingencies and Loss Accruals
description: Apply contingency recognition and disclosure rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contingencies can make or break financial statements! Understanding when to accrue, disclose, or ignore contingencies is essential. Litigation, warranties, guarantees—these are everywhere!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Probable = Accrue
sections.content:
**"Probable AND Estimable"**

To ACCRUE:
• **P**robable (likely to occur)
• **E**stimable (can measure)

**Both required!**

**Probable but NOT estimable:**
→ Disclose, don't accrue

sections.title: Measurement of Loss
sections.content:
**When amount is a range:**

**If best estimate within range exists:**
• Accrue best estimate

**If no best estimate:**
• Accrue MINIMUM of range
• Disclose full range

**Example:**
Range: $1M - $5M
No best estimate → Accrue $1M

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
sections.content:
**For accrued contingencies:**
• Nature of contingency
• Amount accrued

**For disclosed (not accrued):**
• Nature of contingency
• Estimate of possible loss (or state cannot estimate)

**May omit if prejudicial** (rare exception for litigation)

sections.title: Gain Contingencies
sections.content:
**Conservative treatment:**

• NEVER accrue gain contingencies
• May disclose if probable
• Avoid misleading implications

**Examples:**
• Pending lawsuits (plaintiff)
• Claims for insurance recovery
• Tax refund claims

**Recognize only when realized!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Warranty Costs
sections.content:
**Warranty = Always accrue!**

• Sale occurs → Probable warranty claims
• Past history → Estimable amount

**Accrue at time of sale:**
Dr Warranty Expense
    Cr Warranty Liability

**When claims paid:**
Dr Warranty Liability
    Cr Cash/Parts
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Loss Accruals: Don't Be A Hero!
sections.content: Okay, listen up. Contingencies and loss accruals aren't about being optimistic. I've seen too many accountants try to downplay potential losses, hoping they'll magically disappear. Newsflash: they rarely do! If there's a reasonable possibility a loss has been incurred and you can estimate it (even as a range), accrue something! Err on the side of caution. The SEC doesn't take kindly to underreporting liabilities. Remember, it's better to be conservative and adjust later than to face a restatement.

sections.title: Range Confusion: Minimum vs. Best Estimate
sections.content: A very common mistake is accruing the average of a range when no best estimate exists. Remember, you ONLY accrue the MINIMUM when you can't determine a best estimate within the range. For example, if a company faces a lawsuit with a potential loss between $2 million and $6 million, and there's no indication that any particular amount is more likely, you accrue $2 million, not $4 million. Disclose the full range of $2M - $6M. Failing to do so understates your liabilities and misrepresents the company's financial position.

sections.title: Sarah's Slip-Up: Failing to Accrue
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, is reviewing a contract dispute where the company could lose between $500,000 and $1 million. The legal team believes a loss is probable, but can't pinpoint a best estimate within that range. Sarah suggests disclosing the contingency in the footnotes but not accruing anything because 'it's just a range'. Oops! Sarah needs to accrue the $500,000 minimum and disclose the range in the footnotes, demonstrating proper application of contingency accounting.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Due Diligence
sections.content: You'll encounter contingencies frequently during due diligence for mergers and acquisitions. Identifying and properly evaluating potential loss contingencies is crucial in assessing the target company's true financial health. For example, undisclosed environmental liabilities or pending lawsuits can significantly impact the purchase price. Knowing how to analyze these contingencies—assessing probability and estimating potential losses—is a key skill for any accountant involved in M&A or corporate finance. Don't just accept management's assessment; dig into the supporting documentation.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Accrue if: Probable AND Estimable
Disclose if: Probable but not estimable, or Reasonably possible
Remote contingencies: Generally no action
Range with no best estimate: Accrue minimum
Gain contingencies: NEVER accrue, may disclose
Warranties: Always accrue (probable and estimable)
May omit disclosure if prejudicial (litigation)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During 2023, a company was named as a defendant in a lawsuit alleging product defects. The company's legal counsel believes there is a reasonable possibility that the company will lose the suit. Counsel estimates that the loss could be anywhere between $500,000 and $1,500,000. However, counsel believes that $900,000 is the best estimate within that range. What amount, if any, should the company accrue as a loss contingency at December 31, 2023?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since there is a reasonable possibility of loss and a best estimate exists within the range, the company should accrue the best estimate of $900,000. The lower bound of the range is not relevant when a best estimate is available.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the rule: if a best estimate exists within a range, that is the amount to accrue. If no best estimate, accrue the MINIMUM.

reveal.question: What two items must be disclosed for an *accrued* contingency?
reveal.answer: 1. The nature of the contingency. 2. The amount accrued. (Note: These disclosures provide transparency regarding the company's judgment and potential future impact.)

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company is involved in a legal dispute. The company's lawyers believe it is probable that ABC Company will lose the case. The lawyers estimate the potential loss to be between $2,000,000 and $4,000,000, but they cannot determine a best estimate within that range. What amount should ABC Company accrue and what amount (if any) should be disclosed?
options: Accrue $2,000,000 and disclose the range of $2,000,000 to $4,000,000.
Accrue $4,000,000 and disclose the range of $2,000,000 to $4,000,000.
Accrue $3,000,000 and disclose the range of $2,000,000 to $4,000,000.
Accrue $0 and disclose the range of $2,000,000 to $4,000,000.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When there is no best estimate within a range, the minimum amount of the range ($2,000,000) should be accrued, and the entire range ($2,000,000 to $4,000,000) should be disclosed.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember, if probable AND range with no best estimate, accrue the *minimum* and disclose the *full range*.

title: Equity Method Investments
description: Master accounting for significant influence investments

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Equity method applies to investments with significant influence! Understanding how to apply it—including basis differences and intercompany transactions—is critical for FAR!

sections.title: When to Use Equity Method
sections.content:
**Significant influence presumed at 20-50% ownership:**

**Indicators of significant influence:**
• Board representation
• Policy-making participation
• Material transactions
• Interchange of personnel
• Technical dependence

**Can rebut presumption either direction**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Investment Balance
sections.content:
**"CIDE"** changes investment:

**C**ost (initial investment)
**I**ncome (% of investee earnings)
**D**ividends (received)
**E**xcess amortization (basis differences)

**Investment = Cost + Income − Dividends − Excess**

sections.title: Basis Differences
sections.content:
**When cost ≠ % of book value:**

**Excess over book value:**
• Allocate to identifiable assets (FV > BV)
• Remainder = Goodwill

**Amortization:**
• Depreciable assets → Amortize over useful life
• Inventory → Expense when sold
• Goodwill → Not amortized (but test for impairment)

**Reduces equity income each period**

sections.title: Intercompany Transactions
sections.content:
**Eliminate investor's % of unrealized profit:**

**Downstream (investor → investee):**
• Eliminate % of profit from investor's income

**Upstream (investee → investor):**
• Eliminate % of profit from equity income

**Profit realized when asset sold to third party**

sections.title: Investment Below Zero
sections.content:
**When losses exceed investment:**

• Stop recognizing losses at zero
• Continue recognizing if:
  - Guaranteed investee debt
  - Made loans/advances
  - Committed to provide support

**Apply losses to other investments in investee first**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Change in Level of Influence
sections.content:
**If drop below 20%:**
• Stop equity method
• Carry investment at FV (usually)
• Use new basis going forward

**If acquire additional and exceed 50%:**
• Now consolidated
• Remeasure prior investment to FV
• Recognize gain/loss
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Equity Method: It's Like a Bad Relationship
sections.content: Okay, so the equity method. Think of it like being in a relationship where you have *significant influence* but not total control. You're not calling all the shots, but you're definitely weighing in. So, if you own 20-50% of a company, you're probably using this method. When the investee makes money, you make money. When they lose money, you lose money. Just like real life, right? And just like real life, you need to keep track of all the changes to your investment because it's going to impact your bottom line. Don't ignore it, or you'll regret it later!

sections.title: Forgetting the Amortization Adjustment!
sections.content: A super common mistake is forgetting to amortize the excess paid over book value allocated to depreciable assets. Students often remember the initial allocation but then completely blank on the annual depreciation expense. This means you're overstating your investment income each year! Remember, if you paid extra for a building that the investee owns, you need to depreciate that extra cost over the building's remaining useful life. It directly reduces your share of the investee's earnings, so don't let it slip your mind. Think 'Amortization Always Applies!'

sections.title: Sarah's Share of Sunny Corp's Profits
sections.content: Sarah's firm just acquired 30% of Sunny Corp. for $600,000. Sunny Corp's book value was $1.5 million. Sarah allocated $100,000 of the excess to undervalued equipment with a 5-year life. Many forget that each year, Sarah needs to reduce her share of Sunny Corp's income by $6,000 ($100,000 / 5 years) to reflect the amortization of that equipment. Ignoring this will overstate the investment income and the investment balance on the balance sheet. Don't be like Sarah's colleagues who missed this!

sections.title: Equity Method in M&A Due Diligence
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter the equity method most often during mergers and acquisitions due diligence. You're looking for unconsolidated subsidiaries or investments where your client has significant influence. Understanding how the equity method affects the reported financials of the target company is crucial for accurate valuation. You’ll need to scrutinize the investee’s financial statements and understand how the investor is accounting for its share of their earnings. Incorrect application of the equity method can significantly distort the financial picture, potentially leading to bad investment decisions. This is why knowing it well is so important.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Equity method: 20-50% ownership (significant influence)
Record % of income; dividends reduce investment
Basis differences: Allocate excess to assets, amortize
Goodwill: Not amortized, test for impairment
Intercompany: Eliminate % of unrealized profit
Losses limited to investment plus committed amounts
Change in influence: Stop/start method, remeasure

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Parker Corp. owns 30% of the voting stock of Sterling Co. Parker's management believes that despite the ownership percentage, they do NOT exert significant influence over Sterling. Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the accounting treatment of the investment?
options: Parker must use the equity method because ownership is between 20% and 50%.
Parker cannot use the equity method because they do not own more than 50% of Sterling.
Parker should use the fair value method unless they can demonstrate that they exert significant influence.
Parker should use the fair value method if they can demonstrate that they do NOT exert significant influence, despite the ownership percentage.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The 20-50% ownership is a presumption of significant influence, but it can be rebutted. If Parker can demonstrate that they do not exert significant influence, they should use the fair value method.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the word 'presumption'. What does that imply?

reveal.question: List three indicators that suggest an investor has 'significant influence' over an investee.
reveal.answer:
Acceptable answers include:
* Board representation
* Policy-making participation
* Material transactions between the investor and investee
* Interchange of managerial personnel
* Technological dependency

knowledgeCheck.question: An investor acquired a 40% interest in an investee for $800,000 when the investee's book value was $1,500,000. The fair value of the investee's equipment exceeded its book value by $200,000. The remaining excess is attributed to goodwill. The equipment has a remaining useful life of 5 years. What is the annual amortization related to the equipment that the investor must recognize?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The excess fair value over book value for equipment is $200,000. The investor's share is 40% * $200,000 = $80,000. This amount is amortized over the equipment's useful life of 5 years: $80,000 / 5 = $16,000 per year.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Don't forget to consider the investor's percentage ownership!

title: Consolidation: Noncontrolling Interests
description: Account for NCI in consolidated financial statements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When parent owns less than 100%, noncontrolling interest exists! Understanding how to present NCI in consolidated statements—and what gets allocated to it—is essential for FAR!

sections.title: What Is NCI?
sections.content:
**Noncontrolling interest:**

• Portion of subsidiary NOT owned by parent
• Equity component of consolidated F/S
• Presented within equity, separate from parent's equity

**Example:**
Parent owns 80% of Sub
NCI = 20% of Sub

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: NCI in Equity
sections.content:
**NCI is EQUITY, not liability!**

**Presented:**
• Within equity section
• Separate line from parent's equity

**Allocate to NCI:**
• % of subsidiary income/loss
• % of OCI
• Dividends to NCI shareholders

sections.title: Consolidated Income Statement
sections.content:
**Bottom of income statement:**

Net income: $X
  Net income attributable to NCI: $(Y)
  Net income attributable to Parent: $X-Y

**NCI gets their %:**
• Of subsidiary's net income
• After fair value adjustments
• Including excess amortization

sections.title: Changes in Ownership
sections.content:
**Parent buys more (stays in control):**
• Equity transaction (APIC adjustment)
• No gain/loss recognized
• NCI decreases

**Parent sells some (maintains control):**
• Equity transaction (APIC adjustment)
• No gain/loss recognized
• NCI increases

**Deconsolidation (loses control):**
• Gain/loss recognized in P&L

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Losses Exceeding NCI
sections.content:
**Under current GAAP:**

• Losses allocated to NCI even if goes negative
• NCI can have negative balance
• Parent absorbs all losses historically is OLD rule

**NCI shares in ALL income and losses!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: NCI: It's All About Fairness!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk NCI. Think of it like this: you're co-running a lemonade stand with your friend. You own 80% of the stand (Parent), and they own 20% (NCI). If the stand makes $100, your friend deserves their fair share, right? That's $20! You can't just pocket the whole thing and say 'finders keepers!' That's what the 'Net income attributable to NCI' line on the consolidated income statement is all about – giving credit where credit is due, even if you're the big boss.

sections.title: Forgetting Fair Value Adjustments!
sections.content: A common mistake is calculating the NCI's share of net income *before* considering fair value adjustments and excess amortization. Students often just take the subsidiary's reported net income and multiply it by the NCI percentage. WRONG! Remember, consolidation aims to present a fair picture of the *combined* entity. If the subsidiary's assets were written up to fair value during acquisition, and that leads to extra depreciation expense, the NCI's share of income is calculated *after* deducting that extra depreciation. Don't shortchange them!

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Situation with Subsidiary Income
sections.content: Sarah, a senior accountant, is consolidating Parent Co. and Sub Co. Parent owns 75% of Sub. Sub reported net income of $500,000. However, there was $50,000 of excess amortization related to the fair value write-up of Sub's equipment. Sarah needs to remember to subtract the $50,000 from Sub's net income *before* calculating the NCI's 25% share. The NCI gets 25% of ($500,000 - $50,000) = $112,500, not 25% of $500,000.

sections.title: NCI: More Than Just a Line Item
sections.content: In practice, understanding NCI is crucial for accurate financial analysis. Investors use consolidated financial statements to assess the overall performance and financial position of a group of companies. A miscalculation of NCI can significantly distort key metrics like earnings per share and return on equity. For instance, in a deal, if you are representing a potential buyer for a company with significant subsidiaries, you will want to carefully analyze the NCI calculations to fully understand the economic benefits of the acquisition. Getting this right matters for investment decisions!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NCI: Portion of subsidiary not owned by parent
Presented in equity, separate from parent's equity
Full goodwill: Measure NCI at fair value
Allocate % of income, loss, and OCI to NCI
Changes maintaining control: Equity transactions
Loss of control: Gain/loss recognized
Losses can make NCI balance negative

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Parent Co. owns 70% of Sub Co. Sub Co. reported net income of $500,000. Additionally, there was excess amortization of $50,000 related to the fair value adjustment of Sub Co.'s assets at the acquisition date. What amount of net income is attributable to the noncontrolling interest?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The noncontrolling interest (NCI) is 30% of Sub Co. NCI's share of net income is calculated as: 30% * ($500,000 (Net Income) - $50,000 (Excess Amortization)) = $135,000. Therefore, the correct answer is $135,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to subtract excess amortization from subsidiary net income before calculating the NCI's share.

reveal.question: Where is the Noncontrolling Interest (NCI) presented on the consolidated financial statements?
reveal.answer: The Noncontrolling Interest (NCI) is presented within the equity section of the consolidated balance sheet, separately from the parent's equity. It is also presented at the bottom of the consolidated income statement as 'Net income attributable to NCI'.

knowledgeCheck.question: When calculating the net income attributable to the noncontrolling interest, how are fair value adjustments of the subsidiary's assets treated?
options: Fair value adjustments are ignored when calculating NCI.
Only fair value increases are considered when calculating NCI.
Fair value adjustments, including excess amortization, are factored into the subsidiary's net income before calculating NCI's share.
Fair value adjustments are only relevant to the parent company's portion of net income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The NCI is entitled to their percentage of the subsidiary's net income after considering all fair value adjustments made at the acquisition date, including any related amortization. This ensures that the NCI reflects their proportionate share of the subsidiary's adjusted earnings.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how the consolidated financial statements reflect the economics of the acquisition.

title: Statement of Cash Flows: Operating Section
description: Master direct and indirect methods for operating activities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The operating section is the most complex part of the cash flow statement! Understanding both indirect and direct methods—and the adjustments needed—is essential for FAR!

sections.title: Two Methods
sections.content:
**Indirect method (most common):**
• Start with net income
• Adjust for non-cash items
• Adjust for working capital changes

**Direct method:**
• Show gross cash receipts/payments
• Requires supplemental reconciliation

**Same total CFO—different presentation**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Working Capital
sections.content:
**"IOLA"** for current assets:

**I**ncrease in asset = **O**ut (subtract)
**L**oss of asset = **A**dd

**Opposite for liabilities:**
• Increase in liability = ADD
• Decrease in liability = SUBTRACT

sections.title: Direct Method Categories
sections.content:
**Cash receipts:**
• Cash collected from customers
• Interest received
• Dividends received

**Cash payments:**
• Cash paid to suppliers
• Cash paid to employees
• Interest paid
• Income taxes paid

sections.title: Converting to Direct Method
sections.content:
**Cash from customers:**
Sales − ↑A/R (or + ↓A/R)

**Cash to suppliers:**
COGS + ↑Inventory − ↑A/P

**Cash for expenses:**
Expenses + ↑Prepaids − ↑Accrued liabilities

**Use T-accounts to trace!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Interest and Dividends
sections.content:
**Under U.S. GAAP:**

**Operating:**
• Interest paid
• Interest received
• Dividends received

**Financing:**
• Dividends PAID

**IFRS allows more flexibility in classification!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: The Indirect Method: Your New Best Friend
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. The indirect method is the bread and butter of Statement of Cash Flows. You'll see it way more often than the direct method in practice. Why? Because it's easier! Companies already track net income, so it's less of a hassle. Think of it like this: Net income is the starting point, and we're just backing out all the non-cash stuff to get to the actual cash flow. Master this, and you'll be golden on the exam (and in your career!). Don't underestimate the power of depreciation expense!

sections.title: Working Capital: The Sign is Key!
sections.content: Students often struggle with the sign conventions for working capital changes in the indirect method. Remember: An increase in a current asset (like accounts receivable) DECREASES cash flow from operations. Think of it this way: if A/R goes up, you recorded revenue but haven't collected the cash yet. Conversely, an increase in a current liability (like accounts payable) INCREASES cash flow. You got the benefit of the expense, but haven't paid cash yet. Getting the signs wrong is a guaranteed point-loser!

sections.title: Sarah's SCF Struggle
sections.content: Sarah, an accounting intern, was preparing the statement of cash flows for a small retail company using the indirect method. She noticed a significant increase in inventory but added it to net income instead of subtracting it. This resulted in a vastly overstated cash flow from operations. The moral of the story? Always remember that an increase in inventory means the company spent cash to purchase more goods, decreasing cash flow available from operating activities.

sections.title: SCF: Key to Financial Health
sections.content: In practice, the Statement of Cash Flows, especially the operating section, is crucial for assessing a company's short-term financial health. For example, if you're analyzing a potential investment, consistently negative CFO might signal trouble, even if net income looks good. It means the company isn't generating enough cash from its core business to cover its expenses. Understanding the drivers of CFO, whether it's changes in working capital or significant non-cash charges, is essential for making informed decisions.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Indirect: Net income → Adjustments → CFO
Add back non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization)
Remove gains (subtract), add back losses
Current asset increase = Subtract; Decrease = Add
Current liability increase = Add; Decrease = Subtract
Direct: Gross receipts and payments shown
Interest paid/received = Operating (GAAP)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X reported net income of $500,000. Depreciation expense was $50,000, accounts receivable increased by $30,000, and accounts payable increased by $20,000. Using the indirect method, what is the cash flow from operating activities?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is $440,000. To calculate cash flow from operating activities using the indirect method: Start with net income ($500,000), add back depreciation ($50,000), subtract the increase in accounts receivable ($30,000), and add the increase in accounts payable ($20,000). $500,000 + $50,000 - $30,000 + $20,000 = $540,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that increases in asset accounts decrease cash flow, and increases in liability accounts increase cash flow when using the indirect method.

reveal.question: What are the four main categories of cash payments under the direct method for the operating section of the statement of cash flows?
reveal.answer: The four main categories are: 1. Cash paid to suppliers, 2. Cash paid to employees, 3. Interest paid, and 4. Income taxes paid.

rows.label: Presentation
values: Adjusts net income for non-cash items and working capital changes
Shows actual cash inflows and outflows

rows.label: Supplemental Schedule
values: Required (Reconciliation of Net Income to CFO)
